But if what he genuinely wants is an end to private schools’ dominance of British life he would surely need to engage with what Prof Kynaston says: that unless something is done to stop them being so exclusive and so good at what they do, then “the current political consensus is … almost meaningless.” • Lord Ashcroft at ConservativeHome says he can understand why Ed Miliband wants leaders' debate before the election, but that they could backfire. The risk for Mr Miliband is that far from giving him a chance to explain why he thinks price controls are workable, TV debates would further expose the idea’s superficiality. As President Bartlet observed in the West Wing, the ten-word answer can be deadly in politics, but where are the next ten words, and the ten after that? And as Henrique Capriles, the Venezuelan opposition leader, said of President Maduro, “every time he opens his mouth, he scares away the investments that create employment and worsens the crisis”. Rather than transforming perceptions of Miliband after five years as Labour leader, TV debates could confirm them. Meanwhile, they would allow Cameron and Clegg to argue at greater length that higher living standards are achieved by proper economic management, not least controlling the deficit, and by encouraging businesses rather than threatening them. These are risks, not certainties. A gamble on TV debates may yet help Labour, if the Tories can be persuaded to take part despite having more to lose. But Miliband’s eagerness to hold them is not a signal of confidence. It’s the opposite. • Grant Shapps has used a Buzzfeed list to attack Labour. He has produced a Buzzfeed posted entitled: 12 Facts - Why Energy Bills Are Sky High And What We Could Do About It. • Philip Cowley and Mark Stuart on their Revolts blog list the 10 most interesting votes in the Commons since 2010. The Hansard for the opening of the bedroom tax debate is now on the Hansard website. In the Commons the Tory MP David TC Davies defended the government's bedroom tax. He even suggested that the welfare cuts did not go far enough. He made the point when he told an anecdote about doing some filming with the BBC with people affected by the policy. I met a young couple, a teenage couple, 17 years old, neither of whom had ever worked in their lives, they were in a two or a one-bedroom flat and were being expected to suffer some inconvenience, perhaps move into a studio flat. And again I say to the ministers on my front-bench, you are being far too generous in many instances. Why should the state be paying for two people to set up in, frankly, a teenage love nest? When I was 17 years old, if I wanted to see my girlfriend at the age of 17, I would go and see her on a park bench in Newport. Why is the government paying for them to have a flat all by themselves at all, never-mind whether it's one-bedroom or two-bedroom? And I got into a lot a of trouble because I suggested to the young man that perhaps he should go out and find a job himself. And he said there weren't any... I said, 'Well why don't you move to where the work is' and I immediately ran into a whole load of criticism for that. I even had someone email me here. He said, 'You're a Christian, you should be serving the Lord, one day you will stand by the Lord and account for this hardship'. And I wrote back and said, 'I read my bible, I don't see anywhere in the bible where it says 17 year olds should be given a flat but I see plenty of examples of people who have had to move for a better way of life, whether it was Abraham going off to the Promised Land or Moses or the disciples who toured all over Europe'. They all moved. I've taken the quote from the Press Association. Four Lib Dem MPs have tabled a Commons early day motion saying that the bedroom tax is a mistake and that, unless there are extensive changes - including a wider range of exemptions, and the removal of penalties from people who do not have the option of moving to a smaller home - it should be abolished. You can read the text of the motion here. Broadly it reflects the motion passed at the Lib Dem conference in September. The four Lib Dem MPs are: Greg Mulholland, Ian Swales, Adrian Sanders and Roger Williams. Caroline Lucas, the Green MP, used an intervention at the start of the bedroom tax debate to condemn the policy. She has sent out a news release saying that 1,270 people in Brighton and Hove are affected by the policy, of whom 300 are now behind with their rent. Of those 300 people, 205 are disabled. The Green-led council in Brighton and Hove has a policy of not evicting people who fall behind with their rent because of the bedroom tax, she says. This is a brutal policy, and the government simply doesn’t care that some of the most vulnerable in our society – many of whom have disabilities – are being hit hard. It’s worse than Victorian that the poor are being targeted by this tax, especially when the richest are benefiting from tax cuts. And it’s horrifying that such a high proportion of those affected are disabled. The policy risks pushing people who are already desperate into the hands of pay day loan sharks. The bedroom tax is a cruel and counterproductive measure from a government that seems completely indifferent to the impact of its policies. They say that people can get money from the discretionary housing payments fund but that just isn’t enough. It has to help people affected by all the welfare cuts. Brighton and Hove City Council’s DHP allocation for this year is just over £1m. That’s a drop in the ocean compared to the total reduction in benefit expenditure resulting from all the government’s cuts, which is over £13m. Citizens Advice does not get a vote in this evening's division on the bedroom tax. But it has issued a statement supporting the Labour position and saying the policy is a mistake. This is from Gillian Guy, its chief executive. The Government’s solution to spiraling Housing Benefit costs is simply creating more problems. Thousands are being pushed into arrears, 96 per cent of people affected have no alternative smaller homes to move into and some housing associations say they are being forced to demolish homes whilst 1.8 million languish on waiting lists. The number of issues of rent arrears that Citizens Advice Bureaux deal with is rising fast. The sky-high cost of housing is a huge strain on our clients’ budgets and is pushing many into the arms of payday lenders. Ministers are right to seek value for money, but the rushed nature of these changes and the lack of alternative housing means many will be caught in a catch-22 and unable to avoid the extra cost. The fundamental problem with the high cost of Housing Benefit is the lack of houses, not the families who live in them. This upside-down approach to policy does nothing to tackle the chronic housing shortage we face in the UK. For many disabled people and their families, having a second bedroom is not a luxury but an indispensible necessity. The under-occupancy penalty risks forcing a small number of families to bear the burden of years of under-investment in our housing stock. And here's what the Labour MP Stephen Pound said about his brother being a victim of the bedroom tax in the debate. There is a young man who lives in Earls Court who is in total renal failure. May I tell you that this man's spare bedroom is a dialysis unit. He has been told that he now has to pay the bedroom tax. He is very happy with the efforts of his MP, not of my political persuasion, to attempt to free him from the chains of the bedroom tax, but my brother faces losing his home of 20 years for being a kidney patient. Do you not agree with me that this is beyond disgrace? The Lib Dem MP Andrew George is also voting against the government on the bedroom tax, as George Eaton reports at the Staggers. Here's an extract from Rachel Reeves' opening speech. I've taken the quote from the Press Association. It's a shame that the pensions minister [Steve Webb] does not listen to his own party who only in September at the Liberal Democrat party conference voted overwhelmingly against the bedroom tax, saying that it is 'discriminating against the most vulnerable in society' and noted that the government had shown 'a lack of appreciation of the housing requirements of children and adults with disabilities and care needs'. But I am afraid that's what you get with the Liberal Democrats. They say one thing at their conference and when they are out on the doorsteps but they vote another way in here when it really counts. When they could make a difference they turn the other way. So while the work and pensions secretary [Iain Duncan Smith] scuttles off to Paris, he gets his Liberal Democrat pensions minister to defend a policy that isn't even part of his brief and is in contradiction with his own party's policy. I say shame on him and shame on his party. Tim Farron, the Lib Dem president, is set to vote against the government on the bedroom tax tonight, I'm told. A friend of Farron's tells me: "Party conference in Glasgow expressed its will very strongly against the bedroom tax and so Tim is listening to party members and will probably be voting against the government tonight. Tim is the voice of the party members, they have expressed their view and Tim wants to make sure that their voice is heard." Here's the motion on the bedroom tax that the Lib Dems passed at their party conference (pdf). It criticised the policy, and called for an immediate review of its impact. Rachel Reeves v Steve Webb - Verdict: Labour MPs have been claiming that Iain Duncan Smith stayed away today because he was too embarrassed to defend his bedroom tax policy. Having just heard the opening of the debate, another possibility occurs; perhaps he just realised that Steve Webb would do a better job. There is virtually nothing in the welfare portfolio harder to defend than the bedroom tax, and Duncan Smith (who is easily riled) would have probably ended up sounding shouty and belligerent if he had been here to respond to Rachel Reeves. Webb, though, was good. Defending sound policies is fairly easily, but the real test in the Commons comes when you are asked to defend the indefensible, and Webb managed about as well anyone could in the circumstances. But ultimately he was on a hopeless mission. Reeves hammered away at the injustices and inconsistencies of the bedroom with brisk, machine-gun efficiency, but it was the Labour interventions, and the stories they contained, that really sunk the government. (The Tory interventions were particularly poor.) The problems with the bedroom tax have been extensively documented already, but this was still an unusually moving opening to a debate. Webb tells MPs that, if someone comes to see them with a hard case, they should ask the council if it has spent all the money available for discretionary payments. And, if it has, they should ask it if it has asked the government for more money. Labour has nothing to say on the deficit, he says again. And that's it. His speech is over. Robert Flello, the Labour MP, says there are just no smaller homes available in his constituency. Webb says he is not talking about empty homes. He is talking about trying to get tenants to swap with other social housing tenants. Simon Hughes, the Lib Dem deputy leader, says he supported the Lib Dem motion on the bedroom tax passed at the party conference. Will the government exempt people who have applied to swap homes but are still waiting for a smaller property to become available? Webb says the Lib Dem motion on the bedroom tax was better than Labour. In response to a question about Manchester, he says Manchester returned £595,000 last year which it received for discretionary housing payments (DHP) but did not use. If Manchester is short of money this year, it can apply for more money, he says. Webb says he cannot commit the government to more money for remote rural communities. But Danny Alexander, the chief secretary to the Treasury, tends to be quite sensitive to the needs of communities like this, he says. Webb says Reeves did not mention the need to address the deficit. The welfare budget is the biggest in Whitehall. And housing benefit for social tenants is an area you have to look at. So spare rooms are an obvious issue to consider. Webb says there is an issue about adapted accommodation. The government could have tried writing down complex rules. But instead it decided it would be easier to allocate the discretionary money it would need to spend to help people with adapted homes and just give it to councils. Webb says last year, when the government gave councils money to make discretionary payments to help people with housing payments, Labour said it was not enough. But at the end of the year councils returned £10m. Webb says last year the government allocated £60m. This year it has trebled the sum, taking it up to £180m. And councils have the option of asking for more. But so far councils have not been asking for more, he says. Charles Kennedy, the former Lib Dem leader, says he cannot support the bedroom tax. Will the government considering exempting small communities. Webb says Kennedy has been lobbying on behalf of remote rural communities. The government has allocated an extra £5m to help these communities, he says. Labour's Emily Thornberry says, if the policy is designed to address over-crowding, why doesn't it apply to pensioners? Webb says he is not sure if Thornberry wants it to apply to pensioners. Thornberry says he is just probing, to find out what the purpose of the policy is. Webb says at least Labour is acknowledging over-crowding is a problem. Steve Webb, the Lib Dem pensions minister, is responding for the government. Iain Duncan Smith is at a conference on youth unemployment. Labour may not care about youth unemployment, but the government does. Webb says Rachel Reeves did not mention the concerns of tenants in over-crowded housing. He says he wants to take the start of Labour's motion and changing one one. That this House regrets the pernicious effect on vulnerable and in many cases disabled people of deductions being made from housing benefit paid to working age tenants in the private [not social, as the motion says] housing sector deemed to have an excess number of bedrooms in their homes. Webb says Labour's position would be consistent if it was opposed to people being penalised for having a spare room in public sector housing and private sector housing. But Labour introduced measures to cut housing benefit for people with a spare room in private housing. If people in private housing have to pay more for a room to store a dialysis machine, why shouldn't the same apply to people in social housing. Labour's Sheila Gilmore says this is a bogus point. Labour, when it introduced the private housing rule, did not introduce it without warning. Webb says Labour also talked about imposing the same rules on tenants in the public sector (ie, what the government has done). He quotes from a speech Malcolm Wicks gave when he was a minister. Reeves says it is time for the Commons to make a stand. MPs should stand with Danny Alexander's father on this, she says. Di Alexander says this policy is particularly unfair. And Danny Alexander should listen to the Lib Dem conference, which condemned the scheme. But that's what you get with the Lib Dems, she says. They say one thing in one place, and another in another. Labour would reverse it. It would fund this by: cutting down on bogus self-employment in the construction industry; reversing the tax cut for hedge funds, and cancelling the Treasury's shares-for-rights scheme, she says. And that's it. Her speech is over. Labour's Stephen Pound says his brother faces losing his home of 20 years because he is a kidney patient. He has a spare room which he uses for a dialysis machine. But he is not covered by an exemption, he says. Reeves mentions research from the University of York suggestion the savings will be £160m less than anticipated. There is a story about this research here. And here's the full report (pdf). Reeves says there is a contradiction at the heart of the policy. The government says the policy is designed to get people to move, so they free up large homes for larger families. Yet its projected savings figure assumes that families do not move, she says. So what is the purpose of this policy: to free up space, or to save money? John Hemming, a Lib Dem MP, says Labour introduced similar rules for the private sector. And the Labour government actually talked about introducing these principles for public sector housing, he says. When did Labour change its mind? Reeves says it will be interesting to see if Hemming votes with Labour, because the Lib Dem conference condemned the policy. If she becomes work and pension secretary, she will abolish the bedroom tax on day one, she says. Labour's Huw Irranca-Davies says this is a stupid policy. In south Wales, housing associations have three-bedroom homes lying empty because of the bedroom tax. And housing association arrears are going up. Reeves agrees. Labour's Madeleine Moon says people on dialysis moved into a home with a spare room to store their dialysis equipment. That means they do not have to go to hospital. It is not a spare room. It's a hospital room. Reeves says Moon's is making a good point. Margot James, a Conservative, says many of the cases cited by Reeves are covered by exemptions. Labour penalised claimants on housing benefit in private housing with spare rooms. So why is it unfair if the government imposes the same rules for public sector housing. Reeves says Labour's measure was not retrospective, whereas the government's is. Reeves says the policy is not just cruel; it is unworkable. Some 660,000 households are affected. Many have conditions that mean they need a second bedroom. And many are in poverty, he says. Labour's Robert Flello mentions a constituent who need a second bedroom for when his children come to visit. That is a condition for their being able to visit. The bedroom tax will prevent this, he says. Reeves agrees. This is an anti-family policy, as well as an anti-disabled policy, she says. Tobias Ellwood, a Conservative, asks what Reeves would say to people in overcrowded social housing who see other social housing tenants with spare rooms. Reeves says the government should build more homes. Back in the chamber, Reeves says many of the so-called spare bedrooms are rooms used by carers. Iain Duncan Smith is causing "hardship and heartbreak". Labour's Andrew Gwynne says in his constituency the discretionary budget allocated to the council to allow it to help people affected by the policy is not remotely large enough to meet the need. Reeves says she is not surprised. In Leeds, where she is an MP, two thirds of the discretionary budget has already been spent, she says. On the subject of the impact of the bedroom tax on the disabled, Demos sent me this earlier today. The government’s controversial ‘bedroom tax’ is costing disabled people over £200 million a year, according to analysis by the think-tank Demos. Researchers found 420,000 disabled people are currently being affected by the cut in what Coalition politicians are keen to label a ‘spare room subsidy’. The figures come from Demos analysis into the cumulative impact of the Government’s welfare reforms. It revealed an astonishing £28.3bn being taken out of disabled people’s pockets by 2018 – the date of the next spending review – due to a multitude of cuts including Disability Living Allowance (DLA), Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) and housing benefit. Caroline Lucas, the Green MP, says this is a "despicable" policy having a particular effect on disabled people. Reeves says she is absolutely right. Rachel Reeves, the shadow work and pensions secretary, is opening the debate. She says she is glad to see so many Labour MPs in the chamber for the debate. And disappointed to see so few Tories in the chamber. And she is disappointed that Iain Duncan Smith is not here. He is at a conference in Paris. But, she say, she is glad to see that one of the cabinet's strongest Eurosceptics has discovered an interest in Europe. Duncan Smith should have been here to hear about the impact of his policies, she says. The bedroom tax has had a particular impact on more than 400,000 disabled people, she says. Here's the Labour motion that MPs are debating. That this House regrets the pernicious effect on vulnerable and in many cases disabled people of deductions being made from housing benefit paid to working age tenants in the social housing sector deemed to have an excess number of bedrooms in their homes; calls on the Government to end these deductions with immediate effect; furthermore calls for any cost of ending them to be covered by reversing tax cuts which will benefit the wealthiest and promote avoidance, and addressing the tax loss from disguised employment in construction; and further calls on the Government to use the funding set aside for discretionary housing payments to deal with under-occupation by funding local authorities so that they are better able to help people with the cost of moving to suitable accommodation. And here's the government amendment. Line 1, leave out from ‘House’ to end and add ‘notes the substantial structural deficit which was inherited from the previous Government and the need to get the nation’s finances back into shape; further notes the need to bring expenditure on housing benefit under control; further notes that the proposed reversal of this policy would cost the Exchequer around half a billion pounds a year; regrets any exaggeration and misrepresentation of the effects of the policy; recognises the inequality of allowing social tenants to receive benefit for a spare bedroom whilst denying this opportunity to private tenants; supports the Government’s action to deal with this unfairness whilst protecting vulnerable groups such as pensioners and providing substantial funding through Discretionary Housing Payments to local authorities to support other tenants who would otherwise be adversely affected; further notes the Government’s continuing commitment to monitor the effects of the policy and the use of Discretionary Housing Payments; and welcomes the potential beneficial impact of this policy on those living in overcrowded accommodation and the 2.1 million families on waiting lists.’. The Commons debate on the bedroom tax will start shortly. Here's a short bedroom tax reading list. • An article by Iain Duncan Smith for ConservativeHome defending the bedroom tax (although, of course, he does not call it that.) The public can see how ridiculous it is that the benefit system was funding extra bedrooms, while hundreds of thousands of families are squeezed into cramped, overcrowded accommodation that is too small for their needs. Latest ONS figures show that around 375,000 households across England and Wales are living in overcrowded homes in the social rented sector, a fact hardly ever referred to by Labour who left us with this problem. And it is also clearly unfair to treat people differently in the social and private rented sectors. • A Daily Mirror article citing 25 reasons why the bedroom tax should go. • A post for Political Scrapbook explaining why Duncan Smith is not speaking in the debate. (He's at a conference in Paris.) Labour's Andy Slaughter uses a point of order to complain about journalists being briefed about the contents of Sir Bruce Keogh's report today (on an embargoed basis), when MPs will not see it until tomorrow. John Bercow points out that he did grant an urgent question on this. The statement is now over. The debate on the bedroom tax will start shortly. There's a 10-minute rule bill first. Following a point of order, John Bercow has just criticised the government for announcing a one-day delay in the timing of the autumn statement last night on Twitter, and not via a statement to MPs. Hunt says Labour cannot have it both ways; it cannot claim to be in favour of fiscal responsibility, but then complain about every cut. Labour's John Woodcock says ambulance response times have increased in 11 out of 12 trusts. Why is that? Hunt says, just as there is more pressure on A&E services, there is more pressure on ambulance services too. The government is trying to address this. Labour's Bill Esterson asks Hunt why he doesn't invest the NHS underspend in social care. Hunt says the government has already increased social care funding. But it is hard to invest an underspend because you cannot predict what that will be, he says. Labour's Virendra Sharma asks about the future of the A&E department at Ealing hospital. Hunt says an A&E unit at Ealing will stay. But the shape and size of it may change in the light of tomorrow's announcement. Labour's Kevin Brennan asks Hunt to apologise for calling people who visit A&E regularly "frequent fliers". (See 12.51pm.) Hunt says Brennan's question is "ridiculous". Labour's Andrew Slaughter asks why Hunt announced a few weeks ago that 45% A&E departments in West London would close. Hunt says services are being improved in the area. Labour's Jenny Chapman says it is harder to get a GP appointment in her area. A police superintendent has complained to her about his officers having to take patients to hospital because there are no ambulances. Hunt says that Chapman is the first Labour MP to acknowledge that it is hard to see a GP. The government is determined to address the problems created by the GP contract, he says. Here's a Guardian story about research that Jeremy Hunt cited earlier (see 12.54pm) about concentrating stroke services in specialist centres saving lives. Here is the full text of Jeremy Hunt's response to the urgent question. Mr Speaker, in January this year the Board of NHS England launched a review of urgent and emergency care in England. Urgent and Emergency Care covers a range of areas including: A&E departments, NHS 111 centres and other emergency telephone services, ambulances, minor injury units, and urgent care centres. The review is being led by Professor Sir Bruce Keogh, NHS England’s Medical Director. A report on Phase 1 of the review is being published tomorrow and it is embargoed until then. This is an NHS England report. NHS England is an independent body, accountable to me through the Mandate. The report being published tomorrow is a preliminary one, setting out initial thinking. A final version will be published in the new year. Sir Bruce has said that he will outline initial proposals and recommendations for the future of urgent and emergency care services in England. These have been informed by an engagement exercise that took place between June and August this year. These proposals will be further consulted upon through a number of channels including commissioning guidance and demonstrator sites. Spring 2014 will see another progress report. Decisions on changing services are taken at a local level by commissioners and providers, in consultation with all interested parties. That is exactly as it should be, Mr Speaker, as only then can the system be responsive to local needs. It is vital to ensure that both urgent and emergency care and the wider health and care system remain sustainable and readily understandable for patients. A&E performance levels have largely been maintained thanks to the expertise and dedication of NHS staff. A&E departments see 95% of patients within four hours and this figure hasn’t dropped below the 95% target since the end of April. But urgent and emergency care is falling behind the public’s needs and expectations. The number of people going to A&E departments has risen historically, not least because of an ageing population. A million more people are coming through the doors than in 2010. Winter inevitably further challenges the system, which is why we are supporting the most under-pressure A&Es with £250 million. Planning has started earlier than ever before this year, and the NHS has been extremely focused on preparing for additional pressure. Mr Speaker, we will look at Sir Bruce’s report extremely carefully. Reform of the urgent and emergency care system may take years to complete, but that does not mean that it is not achievable. We are exceptionally fortunate in this country to have in the NHS one of the world’s great institutions. Patients need to know that it will continue to be there for them whenever they need it. Labour's Graham Jones says 36,000 people used his local walk-in centre. Why does Hunt think it will be sensible to close it down? Hunt says Jones question highlights the problem. Labour opened walk-in centres in many places to solve the problems caused by their GP contract. Hunt says the government will address this. But where walk-in centres are valuable, they will be kept. Hunt says stroke survival rates in London have improved because the number of stroke centres has been cut from over 30 to eight (allowing patients to receive more specialist treatment). Hunt says an increasing number of people are coming from "Labour-run Wales" to seek NHS treatment in England. That's because they know where they can get better treatment. Hunt says some A&E patients are "frequent fliers". They keep coming back because there is nowhere else to go. Tomorrow's announcement will help to address this, he says. Hunt says the most senior A&E doctor in the country says between 15 and 30% of A&E patients could be treated elsewhere. Labour's Kevin Barron says competition law is causing chaos in the NHS. The government was warned about this. Hunt says many of the competition problems come from the Enterprise Act 2002. David Tredinnick, a Conservative, asks how many lives will be saved through the consolidation of services in London. Hunt says the measures in London will save hundreds of lives a year. Sir Bruce Keogh will say more about this tomorrow. Hunt is responding to Burnham. Real complacency was refusing to hold an inquiry into Mid-Staffs, he says. He says the A&E review is there to clear up the mess left by Labour. Walk-in centres were introduced because Labour messed up the GP contract, he says. The government will take difficult decisions. They are decisions Labour ducked. Burnham talked about a recruitment crisis. But there are 300 more A&E consultants than there were under Labour. And 2,000 more people are seen every four hours in A&E every day than under Labour. Under Burnham, the government made "so many wrong decisions about A&E", he says. Andy Burnham is responding. Rarely has the Commons been treated to such a disrespectful and complacent statement, he says. A&E services are in crisis already. David Cameron has taken personal control of the NHS crisis. And journalists have been given a private briefing this morning. They should not be given a briefing on material not available to MPs, he says. Burnham says weekend reports suggested Keogh is proposing alternatives to A&E, such as walk-in centres. Will Hunt put nurses back on the end of the phone for the 111 service? Burnham said Cameron misled the Commons last week when he said A&E waiting times were not getting worse. He says he has figures to disprove this. Hunt says emergency care performance levels are holding up. But demand for A&E is going up. Winter puts more pressure on the system. Another £250m has been allocated to A&E, he says. Hunt says the government will look at Keogh's recommendations carefully. Implementing them could take years. Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, says a preliminary report will be published tomorrow. It is under embargo, he says. MPs are protesting. (They hate journalists being told about these announcements first.) Hunt says the final report will be published next year. Andy Burnham asks Jeremy Hunt if he will update the Commons on Sir Bruce Keogh's A&E review "following this morning's breifing for the media". Andy Burnham, the shadow health secretary, has tabled an emergency question on A&E services. It relates to a review of A&E service by Sir Bruce Keogh, the NHS medical director, due to be published tomorrow. I'll be covering it in detail. • Energy UK, the body representing energy companies, has criticised Ed Davey, the energy secretary, for deploying "Punch and Judy ... insults". This language was not productive, it said. The organisation, which is hosting a conference today, issued its statement in response to reports that Davey would use his speech to tell energy companies that people "look at the big suppliers and they see a reflection of the greed that consumed the banks". (See 9.26am.) • EDF has become the latest energy company to raise prices for its customers, announcing an average price rise of 3.9% on its dual fuel tariffs. • Inflation has dropped to 2.2%, its lowest rate in more than a year, catching financial markets offguard and pushing back bets that interest rates in the UK will rise any time soon. • Probation trusts are suffering amid uncertainty and anxiety over government plans to shake-up the service, MPs have been told. As the Press Association reports, Sue Hall, chair of the Probation Chiefs Association, which represents leadership across the service, told the justice committee performance is starting to "look a bit rocky" in some parts of the country as the Government presses ahead with its reforms. And Hall warned the changes are being pushed through too quickly, presenting a "huge, huge" challenge for the probation trusts in England and Wales. • Lord Heseltine, the Conservative former deputy prime minister, has said that some estimates of the costs and benefits of the HS2 high-speed rail line are "mumbo jumbo" calculated by men with slide rules. He wants the project to go ahead. • The former SDLP MP Eddie McGrady has died. Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, is speaking at the Energy UK conference. She told the BBC that, even though EDF is putting up its prices less than some of the other big energy companies, its increases were still not justifiable. Labour has been saying for some time now the wholesale cost doesn’t reflect the rise in our bills. We shouldn’t let EDF off the hook either because actually the green levies on our bills cannot account for these price rises ... We’ve been looking back over the last four or five years and what we’ve seen is wholesale prices have not resulted in the sort of bill increases that we’ve seen. We think the people have been overcharged; they’ve been over charged in terms of what the wholesale costs have been and also we know that these companies have also been sitting on our money gaining from that when they should have been paying it back to us. So there has been overcharging going on and the government, and Ed Davey talks about cash cows, but I’m afraid those fine words don’t add up to action, and that’s what we need right now. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. Here's a statement from Ed Davey, the energy secretary, on the EDF energy price rise. Any price rise is disappointing but I’m encouraged b that EDF have kept their price rise much closer to inflation than some of their competitors. The competition we’ve introduced to the energy market means people have a choice. They can look for the best deal available; including from smaller suppliers, with the confidence that switching will make an immediate difference to their bills and force the Big 6 to compete on price. You can read all today's Guardian politics stories here. And all the politics stories filed yesterday, including some in today's papers, are here. As for the rest of the papers, here's the PoliticsHome list of top 10 must reads, here's the ConservativeHome round-up of today's political stories and here's the New Statesman's list of top 10 comment articles. As for the rest of the papers, here are four articles I found particularly interesting. • Helen Warrell and Jim Pickard in the Financial Times (subscription) say Theresa May is investigating ways of making British terror suspects stateless. Theresa May is seeking to change the law so that terror suspects can be deprived of their British citizenship and made “stateless” in a bid to make it easier to deport people deemed a security risk. The home secretary is already able to strip passports from those with dual nationality and has repeatedly said British citizenship is a “privilege, not a right”. Since coming to office, she has exercised this power on at least 16 individuals alleged to have links to terrorist groups. But the Financial Times understands Ms May has asked officials to find a way of overturning international human rights conventions which prevent individuals with only one citizenship being made stateless. The change – which could be tabled as an amendment to the forthcoming immigration bill – comes as ministers impose tougher restrictions on terror suspects after Mohammed Ahmed Mohamed escaped from a London mosque earlier this month dressed in a burka. • Steve Hawkes in the Daily Telegraph says E.ON is planning to raise energy prices by 6.6%. More than four million more households face spiralling energy bills this winter as another of the Big Six prepares to push up prices amid accusations from the Government that they risk being seen in the same light as “greedy” bankers. E.ON is preparing to push up charges by more than double the rate of inflation. The German-owned energy supplier is planning to unveil a rise of 6.6 per cent in gas and electricity rates later this month - with the increase set to take effect in early January. Dual-fuel bills will rise by nearly £90 a year to £1,425. • Michael Savage in the Times (paywall) says three of David Cameron's allies have been snubbed by a Conservative association selecting a candidate for a safe seat. Three of David Cameron’s allies have failed to be selected for a safe Tory seat, it has emerged. In an apparent snub by grassroots activists, two of the Prime Minister’s advisers and a former aide were left off the four-person shortlist for the coveted seat of Croydon South, which the Tories held with a majority of 16,000 at the last election.. Mr Cameron was understood to have been keen to see Oliver Dowden, his £125,000 deputy chief of staff, installed in the seat. Also on the longlist were Laura Trott, one of his special advisers, and Shaun Bailey, the Government’s “youth and engagement champion”. • Benedict Brogan in the Telegraph says George Osborne would like to be foreign secretary after 2015. For a start, there is a growing confidence on the Tory side that, one way or another, the Prime Minister will carry on in office after May 2015. The feeling is that, as the economy strengthens, so does his position. The same can be said of Mr Osborne, who has regained the ground he lost after his omnishambles Budget. He is being credited with the recovery, even if most Tory MPs are privately uneasy about one built on what looks suspiciously like an unsustainable credit boom. The attitude seems to be that anything that gets them into power is worth doing; they can argue about the consequences later. Whether Mr Osborne would then stay at the Treasury is a matter for conjecture: he is said to be eyeing up the Foreign Office, on the – untested – assumption that William Hague will take his leave of politics in 2015. There's an urgent question in the Commons on A&E care at 12.30am. David Cameron has been tweeting on the inflation figures. (See 9.37am.) There are also house price figures out today. This is from the Press Association. House prices surged at their fastest annual rate in almost three years in September and remained close to an all-time high recorded the previous month, official figures have shown. Prices rose by 3.8% year-on-year to reach £245,000 on average, marking a slight decrease on the peak of £246,000 recorded in August, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS). The annual uplift is the highest seen since October 2010 and comes amid calls for the Government to ease the upward pressure on house prices being fuelled by its new Help to Buy scheme by building more homes to meet demand. The figures were released as the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (Rics) called for urgent action to address the supply of homes, which it said is "nowhere near" that which is needed to cope with "soaring" demand from buyers. Rics said the number of surveyors reporting house prices lifting across the country has surged to an 11-year high as the Government's new Help to Buy scheme fuels "soaring" demand from buyers. The ONS said that on a seasonally-adjusted basis, UK house prices were unchanged between August and September. House prices in London have soared by 9.4% over the year to reach £434,000 typically. The year-on-year increase reflected growth of 4.2% in England, where typical prices reached £255,000, and 1.4% in Wales, where prices were £163,000 on average. Prices in England have fallen slightly from record levels seen the previous month but are still 0.8% higher than their previous 2008 peak, the ONS said. The rises were offset by annual falls of 1.1% in Scotland, where typical prices fell to £181,000 and 1.5% in Northern Ireland, where the average price edged down to £127,000. Prices in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland are still well below the peaks seen in the property boom. Northern Ireland has seen the sharpest decline and prices are still at 50% below their previous peak. Here's Catherine McKinnell, the shadow Treasury minister, on the inflation figures. (See 9.37am.) This fall in the inflation rate is welcome, but with prices still rising much faster than wages the cost-of-living crisis under David Cameron continues. And with inflation-busting hikes in energy prices in the pipeline families and pensioners will carry on feeling the squeeze. Here's Hilary Osborne's Guardian story about the EDF price rise. (See 9.27am.) EDF says it is increasing prices by less than other firms because it is assuming that the government will take some green levies out of bills. If the government does not do that, prices could rise further, it says. Here's an extract from Hilary's story. EDF said it had taken action ahead of the outcome of the government's review of the costs of the ECO scheme to help vulnerable households and other green schemes, designed to bring down costs for consumers. It said if the government makes bigger changes to the costs of its social and environmental schemes than it has anticipated, it would pass these savings on to customers, but if the changes were smaller there could be further price rises. Angela Knight, the head of Energy UK, has been speaking to the BBC from the organisation's conference in London. Here are the main points. • Knight said the industry "absolutely understands the concerns that are being raised by their customers". • She said energy firms were not to blame for prices going up. Overwhelmingly the reason why prices are going up are not because the energy company has got a real contribution in that price increase. It’s pass-throughs onto the bills – the policies that come through because of the government policies and because of the need to invest in networks and in distributions. • She rejected a suggestion that the energy companies wanted green levies to be scrapped. What they wanted was a debate over whether those measures should be funded through bills, or whether they should be funded by central government, she said. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. And here is some Twitter coverage of her speech to the conference. There are two polls around today. For the record, here are the figures. ICM for the Guardian Labour: 38% (no change from ICM last month) Conservatives: 30% (down 4) Lib Dems: 13% (up 1) Ukip: 10% (up 2) Labour lead: 8 points (up 4) According to Electoral Calculus, this would give Labour a majority of 96. YouGov for the Sun Labour: 40% (up 1 from YouGov in the Sunday Times) Conservatives: 33% (down 1) Ukip: 11% (no change) Lib Dems: 9% (down 1) Labour lead: 7 points (up 2) Government approval: -31 (down 1) According to Electoral Calculus, this would give Labour a majority of 88. Here are the headline inflation figures. • The rate of consumer price index (CPI) inflation fell to 2.2% in October - its lowest level in more than a year - from 2.7% in September. • The headline rate of retail price index (RPI) inflation fell to 2.6% from 3.2% in September. The Office for National Statistics news release about the figures is here. Here's the summary explanation for the fall. The largest contributions to the fall in the rate came from the transport (notably motor fuels) and education (tuition fees) sectors. And here's the statistical bulletin with full details (pdf). The Press Association has just snapped this. EDF Energy said today that average gas and electricity bills will rise by 3.9% from January 3. Here's the extract from Ed Davey's speech to the Energy UK conference that was released in advance overnight. We need our energy companies to be profitable so they can invest in our energy infrastructure, secure the energy supplies of the future, develop more energy efficient technologies, and create jobs. But those profits cannot come at the expense of the elderly, the vulnerable, and the poorest in our society. Customers are not just cash cows to be squeezed in the pursuit of a higher return for shareholders. Trust between those who supply energy and those who use it is breaking down. It is so difficult for people to work out what exactly they are paying for that they fear the big energy companies are taking them for a ride when bills go up. Fair or not, they look at the big suppliers and they see a reflection of the greed that consumed the banks. So this is a ‘fred the shred’ moment for the industry. You deliver an essential public service, so your industry must serve the public – and the public must have trust in what you do. So we have opened up our books and we are looking at how we can reduce the impact of government policies on bills. But our commitment must be matched by a commitment in industry to open up your books and set out exactly how you are bearing down on your own costs to make bills as low as possible. The industry must be much more transparent and Ofgem will have our full support to introduce whatever regulations are necessary to deliver that greater transparency. Comparing the energy firms to the banks may not go down particularly well, but at least Angela Knight, Energy UK's chief executive, will feel at home. In her last job she was chief executive at the British Bankers' Association, the trade body for banks. (Don't be surprised if her next job involves defending payday lenders." Energy UK has already hit back at Davey. This is what a spokesman has been saying: The energy industry is already working hard to ensure everyone can keep the lights on and stay warm this winter. The best way to do this is for everyone to work together, which is why this tit-for-tat Punch and Judy show of insults is so unproductive. The energy industry is vital to the UK. It is a major employer, a serious investor and a significant taxpayer. As analysis from UBS shows, about 95% of rising energy costs are out of the hands of the energy companies and can be attributed to government policies and other network, social and environmental costs. Here's the timetable for the conference. Davey will be speaking at 11.50am, not 9.30am as I thought earlier. It's busy today, but without any one political story dominating the news. I will concentrate particularly on the Ed Davey speech, and the opening of the Commons debate on the bedroom tax, but there is plenty of other politics to keep an eye on too. Here's the agenda for the day. 9.30am: Inflation figures are released. 10.15am: The Probation Chiefs Association and the Probation Association give evidence to the Commons justice committee. 11am: Ed Miliband gives a speech announcing a Labour taskforce looking at digital skills. As Patrick Wintour reports, Miliband will say that it will be headed by the former Tomorrow's World presenter Maggie Philbin. 11.15am: Justine Greening, the international development secretary, gives evidence to the Commons international development committee about the development implications of Scottish independence. 11.30am: Michael Gove, the education secretary, gives a speech on children's services. As Randeep Ramesh reports, Gove will announce a "personal" mission for a radical plan to reform social work on Monday – citing his own life which was "transformed because of the skill of social workers and the love of parents who were not my biological mother and father" as proof of the profession's power to change lives. 11.50am: Ed Davey, the energy secretary, speaks at the Energy UK annual conference. As Damian Carrington reports, Davey will say that energy companies are facing are facing a "Fred the Shred" moment because the public sees in them "a reflection of the greed that consumed the banks". 12.30pm: MPs begin a debate on a Labour motion calling for the repeal of the bedroom tax. Rachel Reeves, the shadow work and pensions secretary, will open for Labour, but Iain Duncan Smith, the work and pensions secretary, will not be responding. Instead it will be another minister. 2.30pm: Norman Lamb, the care minister, gives evidence to the Commons health committee on managing long-term care conditions. 2.45pm: David Anderson QC, the independent reviewer of terrorist legislation, gives evidence to the Commons home affairs committee. My colleague Paul Owen will be covering the hearing on his NSA files live blog. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary before 12.30pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

tags.description: Rachel Reeves has been Labour MP for Leeds West since 2010.

results.webTitle: A hostile environment: Immigration and British politics | Editorial

fields.headline: A hostile environment: Immigration and British politics
fields.trailText: Editorial: Immigration was until recently running at historic highs, and any politician needs to face up to public unease
fields.bodyText: There is a hard business case for immigration, and if you don't want to take it from meticulous empirical economists, take it from America's mainstream right. "Immigrants come with new skills and new ideas. They fill a critical part in our labour market," said George W Bush. "They work hard for a better life." George Osborne's fast-track Chinese visas reflect this, but Britain's right more often relegates financial concerns below the emotive dimension. The "immigration bill for hard-working people", which Theresa May published last week, is a ragbag of measures to make outlaws of individuals striving for a better life. Immigration was until recently running at historic highs, and any politician needs to face up to public unease. The polling, however, does not point to unremitting hostility, but rather a mood of fluidity, even confusion. Voters rank immigration highly among the issues "facing the country", but only as a marginal concern in their own lives. They judge it to be bad for the economy, and yet are inclined to believe that individual migrants work harder than average. The perceived difficulties bear no relation to the number of newcomers within each region, and are everywhere imagined to bite more at the national than the local level. It is almost as if everybody believes that immigration is someone else's problem. With experience counting for less than impressions, the power of suggestion is enhanced. The insinuations in the home secretary's bill are alarming; the spin centred on making immigrants pay for the NHS, a goal at once superfluous (immigrants can already be billed for non-emergency care) and beside the point (immigrants are healthier than most). The actual proposal here is for Mrs May to assume sweeping enabling powers to charge people wishing to come or to stay as much as she likes, for any reason. The legislation also seeks to tie the hands of judges weighing immigrants' right to a family life, a dubious restriction which could fall foul of Strasbourg, if it does not first create a clash in the British courts. Traditional conservatives should be horrified by a wheeze designed to short-circuit the constitutional wiring, but a crisis would further Mrs May's dream of turning back the clock on two-thirds of a century of civilisation by quitting the European convention. The real heart of the bill is the abolition of most appeals. Few bureaucratic decisions have the power to break families or fortunes in the manner of the choice to admit or expel from these shores; and yet – as the supreme court has noted – this is a field in which official process is marked by box-ticking "rigidity". Without appeals, more bad decisions will go unchallenged. One perverse consequence will be an increase in costly judicial reviews, the final buck stop that cannot be scrapped, which Whitehall has spent years trying to restrict. During the long, dragging process of such reviews, a second perversity will be to criminalise applicants who are legal today by virtue of an active appeal. This is part of a broader agenda which also includes barring irregular migrants from bank accounts and driving licences. Cash-in-hand work and uninsured driving will increase, with attendant effect on the public purse and public safety. Mrs May has obviously decided this price is worth paying for her overriding stated aim of making Britain "a hostile environment" for outsiders. The lure of the rich world to the poor is such that migrants' hopes are not easily deterred, as dreadful tales of watery graves in the Mediterranean underline. Many immigrants will not go home, but live here hidden, hopeless, and beyond the reach of the law; that will make British communities a more hostile environment for natives and newcomers alike. For Labour, which has a mixed record in this area, Yvette Cooper pledges to stand firm against "National Front" language. The Lib Dems have kicked back against fines for landlords housing irregular migrants, but now need to widen their attack. As it stands, this is a bill to stir searching Lib Dem questions about exactly what sort of government they are in.

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 11 October

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 11 October
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

results.webTitle: Politics and the market: more smoke than light | Editorial

fields.headline: Politics and the market: more smoke than light
fields.trailText: Editorial: Battle over energy prices first of what may be many that pitch politicians against suppliers of essential services voters have come to mistrust
fields.bodyText: Politicians are feeling the pain of families suffering from what one pay-day borrower described as the problem of the money running out before the week does. Labour's latest party political broadcast might have taken its script from the Office for National Statistics' monthly economic review, published on Wednesday. Disposable income hasn't grown since 2009. What's even more revealing is the change in where the money goes. Ten years ago, essential spending took just under a fifth of disposable income. Now it's more than a quarter. And while the consumer price index has risen by 30%, gas and electricity prices have soared by 120% and 190% respectively. So the share they take of household spending is up too, and for many homes – families with small children, pensioner households – it'll really matter. The recovery has a long way to go before, in Ed Miliband's telling conference metaphor, it lifts the little boats along with the yachts. And now that the pensions minister Steve Webb wants to cap pension fund fees, it's clear the intensifying political contest over energy prices is just the first of what may be many battles that pitches politicians against suppliers of essential services that voters have come to mistrust. The danger is that in fixing one thing, another gets broken. The energy market is a good example. The impact of carbon pricing, for example, which is meant simply to ensure the polluter pays, is confused by being applied differentially to gas and electricity and between business and domestic users. Labour allowed vertical integration between energy generation and supply in the belief it could hold down prices, but instead it seems to allow the big six companies to make even more profits. The introduction of an annual competition review, details of which the energy secretary Ed Davey is due to announce in the commons on Thursday, is unlikely to make a difference without a wider reform of the market structure, something Labour now promises would follow after its price freeze. Meanwhile, there's a growing sense that the suppliers have managed to capture the debate, and make it not about their price rises but about the impact of green levies instead. And although Mr Davey promised to "fight like a tiger" to defend the levies, Nick Clegg suggests the same funding could come out of general taxation; that might be popular, but would sacrifice the power of levies to nudge consumers into cutting back. The trust-busting mood of the times finds a more straightforward target in occupational pensions, where – as tough Office of Fair Trading analysis has confirmed – a conspiracy against the public has been indulged in for far too long. Just as with energy, vertical integration between different parts of the business – saving, managing investment and annuitisation – obscures the underlying economics to the detriment of the customer. There are no environmental complications here: this is a simpler case of savers getting fleeced. Desperate to get his semi-compulsory occupational pensions up and running without operational chaos, Mr Webb had pursued a quiet life with the industry. As recently as January, he likened the idea of capping charges to fixing the price of baked beans. Now, however, he has launched "a full-frontal assault", publishing calculations showing how innocuous-sounding annual levies of 1.5% can disguise the swiping of a third of a pension pot, and he talks of imposing a comprehensive cap of 0.75% of fund. Bravo. That word "comprehensive" is all important: barring the financial services industry from any particular levy is merely to squeeze the balloon. As Labour's Gregg McClymont has pointed out for years, charges such as transaction costs are so hidden that not even the insurers record them. Having neglected to enforce openness, the government could now discover – as the consultants warn – that if you can't measure it, you can't manage it. Scepticism in government about the market is overdue. Part of the answer is better regulation, but that's not going to happen until we have real transparency.

results.webTitle: Prescott, Blair, Straw – the next generation of UK politics?

fields.headline: Prescott, Blair, Straw – the next generation of UK politics?
fields.trailText: David Prescott is one of many with familiar surnames hoping to make it into parliament. Emine Saner looks at our Westminster dynasties and what they say about politics today
fields.bodyText: David Prescott was born just a few days after his father John became MP for Hull East. "So I've known nothing else," he says. Before he was four years old, he was on the campaign trail in the 1974 general election – "I used to go round in the car saying 'vote Yaybour'; I couldn't pronounce the letter L" – and he has volunteered in every election since 1983. Politics was in the family. "Labour activism didn't start with John," he says. "My grandmother was a member of Jack Jones's pensioners' parliament [the National Pensioners' Convention] and going back, there were politically active miners as well. If you're the son or daughter of an MP, people think it's just because of your father or mother. But actually it goes much further back and you find activism goes through a whole family." Prescott is standing for selection to be Labour's prospective parliamentary candidate (PPC) for south London's Greenwich and Woolwich seat tomorrow, after its current MP, Labour's Nick Raynsford, announced he would stand down at the next election. It's the third time he has sought selection – in 2008, he stood for his father's Hull East seat, two years later he sought to become Labour's PPC in Weaver Vale, Runcorn. Now he's trying again in the constituency where he has lived for the past 13 years. "I've never known a selection process that's had such a strong list of candidates," he says by way of countering any accusation he has been parachuted in. "I don't look round and find where my best chance of trying to get in is." Did he grow up wanting to become an MP? "Not really. I've always been active, but I made a very clear decision that I wanted to have a life outside politics." Prescott became a journalist, and now runs a PR company. "When Nick announced he was standing down, I had a long chat with my wife about it because I know the impact it can have on families." He describes rarely seeing his father, who would return home on Thursday and then spend the next couple of days on constituency business before heading back to Westminster. But it was an early life filled with political giants – Barbara Castle and the trade unionist Rodney Bickerstaffe were family friends. When he moved to London, he moved into a flat with his father and Dennis Skinner. "So when you grow up in that environment you can see the good that politics can do, on a very local level, how it can change people's lives. So it's hard not to think that would be a decent and honourable thing to do. I've always made it clear in my own mind that if the time was right, I'd find my own way." If Prescott were to become Labour's PPC – and Greenwich and Woolwich is a safe Labour seat – he will join a political class where family ties are not exactly rare. It almost feels as if it could take less time to run through the politicians, or potential politicians, who aren't related. There are siblings – Keith and Valerie Vaz, Maria and Angela Eagle, Boris and Jo Johnson (whose father Stanley was an MEP, and in a reversal to the usual pattern, attempted to succeed his son in Henley when Boris stepped down). Until recently, there were the Milibands. And there are the couples – Ed Balls and Yvette Cooper, Harriet Harman and Jack Dromey, Jo Swinson and Duncan Hames. Then there are the numerous children of politicians. The Conservatives have Francis Maude (son of Angus). Bernard Jenkin (whose father Charles, now Lord Jenkin, was an MP for more than 20 years), Nick Hurd (son of Douglas), Ben Gummer (son of John) and Andrew Mitchell (son of David). Dominic Grieve, the attorney general, is the son of Percy Grieve, who was a Conservative MP for nearly 20 years. How far back should we trace Nicholas Soames's political lineage? He is the greatgrandson of chancellor Randolph Churchill, and grandson of Winston Churchill; both his father, Christopher Soames, and uncle, Duncan Sandys, were members of parliament. Labour looks just as cosy. The MSP Paul Martin is the son of former speaker Michael Martin. Anas Sarwar took his father's vacated Glasgow Central seat in 2010. Andy Sawford, recently elected MP for Corby, is the son of Phil Sawford, who had the neighbouring Kettering constituency until 2005. And those are just the current MPs. There are also those who tried and failed to win seats, such as Tamsin Dunwoody, who contested her mother's seat in 2008. And then there are the potentials – Will Straw recently became Labour's PPC for Rossendale and Darwen, next to his father Jack Straw's Blackburn constituency. There was much excitement a few months ago when it was rumoured that Euan Blair, the eldest son of the former prime minister, was being lined up for a soon-to-be-vacated Coventry seat, although that is now looking unlikely. Labour looks set to have an all-women shortlist for Bob Ainsworth's Coventry North East seat, while the longstanding Coventry North West MP Geoffrey Robinson – whom, it had been reported, had received a phone call from Cherie Blair inquiring about the seat – has said he has no intention of standing down. Still, the rumours persist. As a recent piece in the Independent pointed out, if Euan Blair really were to stand and get himself elected, we could be looking at another generation of Blair, Straw and Prescott. Joe Dromey, the son of Harriet Harman and Jack Dromey, has said it is untrue he was seeking a seat, but it's clear he has a strong political interest – he studied politics at university and is a Labour council candidate for New Cross. Emily Benn, granddaughter of Tony and niece of Hillary, who ran in the 2010 general election at the age of 20, looks set to join Croydon council next year. Meanwhile, Georgia Gould, the daughter of the late Labour strategist Philip Gould, who sought to become Labour's PPC in Erith and Thamesmead in 2009, is already a Camden councillor. "I don't think we should ever condemn anybody who wants to give public service, especially at a time when politicians aren't especially liked by the public," says Katie Ghose, chief executive of the Electoral Reform Society. "But I think there's a bigger issue: that parties are shedding members, and that means there are fewer people putting themselves forward for election, and fewer in the business of choosing them. Parties are shrinking and you get less and less competition. When we look at family members, what we'd say is politics should be open to all – everybody should leave school feeling that elected office could be something they contribute in their lifetime, whether it's the parish council or MEP or any other level. "I don't think it's surprising that family members who have been exposed to politics have that shared passion and do want to go into politics. But we're in a worrying position now as a society where people feel more cut off, and we need to find ways for parties to connect to a much broader range of people, so we end up with a diverse institution." Prof Steven Fielding, director of the Centre for British Politics at Nottingham University, says the number of political families could be compared to "the later 19th century and early 20th century, when access to politics was restricted, and you couldn't afford to be in politics unless you had quite a lot of money, and it was expected that the aristocracy played a certain role. That diminished in the 20th century but it has reasserted itself for slightly different reasons. Class plays a role, but these people are more middle-class and they have the skills of their parents and networks. They're not from grand families, but they are from well-connected families." He adds that the political world isn't the only place where this is happening – journalism is another, and law. "It's not a good thing that politics, or any profession, is open to certain people and not others." But perhaps being the son of John Prescott, or any other politician, doesn't always help anyway. "People may be almost twice as concerned to make sure they're selecting you because you're good, as opposed to what your surname is," says David Prescott. When Tal Michael, son of the Welsh first minister Alun, stood for the Ynys Môn Welsh assembly byelection in the summer, he came under criticism. "There were some Plaid Cymru bloggers who were making accusations. It was strange because I had deliberately gone outside of politics and outside of Wales, because it meant I was being employed by people who weren't making connections [to his father]. I am on the side of the spectrum that thinks it would be wrong to be inheriting position – it's about doing things on merit." Lindsay Hoyle, the well-liked deputy speaker who has been MP for Chorley since 1997, served as a councillor in the town for 18 years, becoming deputy council leader, before being persuaded by his Labour colleagues to stand against the Tories. "They wanted to pick somebody local and that was me. This is my home town, it's where I was born and brought up – there aren't many MPs who can say that, and it's a real privilege to represent my town." He is also the son of Doug Hoyle, the former MP who now sits in the House of Lords. Did anyone ever accuse him of benefiting from that? "He had a very safe Labour seat, I had a marginal seat. People asked me to stand, which I did. It was nothing to do with nepotism, but I suppose it was being in the right place at the right time." Like Prescott, Hoyle says he was "born into politics. The greats of the Labour movement were at my first conference." He was barely four months old when he was taken to the 1957 conference, where Nye Bevan was speaking. "In 1964 I was delivering leaflets as a young child ready for the election. So you're brought up in it. You live within a political household – my mother was on the council as well – and as you get into your teens you turn off a little bit. But somehow I came back." There are plenty of examples of politicians' children who have political talent, and perhaps years of public service behind them: Will Straw, who founded the influential blog Left Foot Forward and works for the thinktank Institute for Public Policy Research, has been described as a "gifted political player"; for the past few years David Prescott has been the chair of governors which turned around a failing school; Estelle Morris (daughter of MP Charles Morris), like Hoyle and others, served in local government for years before entering parliament. Being the son or daughter of a politician shouldn't be a bar to entry, should it? "Absolutely not," says Fielding. "They're being judged by party members and activists and you would think that the criteria are exactly the same. But no matter how they get selected, if it is a trend and is getting more pervasive that is not a good thing in itself – it's just showing a wider evidence that the avenues to get involved in politics at the highest and most significant level are shutting down for more ordinary people." One question is why, when so many other people are put off by the idea of a political career, the children of politicians are seemingly running towards one. David Prescott saw what his family went through at the hands of the media – there was the tabloid frenzy after his father's affair and the way he was generally undermined for years by newspapers who mocked his grammar, his weight, his perceived vulgarity. Did that not put David off? "Not really. If you believe in something and you want to fight for social justice, you're open to that level of scrutiny. It was always difficult to see [him] going through that, but he survived because he's got a passion, and he knows that if you're in power you can achieve an awful lot of good."

results.webTitle: Global politics: always the national angle | Editorial

fields.headline: Global politics: always the national angle
fields.trailText: Editorial: Authoritative attempts to put every country in the same explanatory frame should come with a health warning
fields.bodyText: Norway and Australia are about as far apart geographically as two countries can be. But both have significant things in common all the same. Both are developed nations with established welfare states that have come through the global economic downturn in unusually good order. Norway's economy is growing at 2.6%, while Australia's has now notched 22 successive years of expansion. Many other developed nations can only look on in envy. Unusually, Norway and Australia have also been governed throughout this period of growth by centre-left governments. Both governments face general elections this weekend. Electoral politics in these, as in other, developed countries, are dominated by the adage that "It's the economy, stupid." Win the economic argument, the conventional wisdom goes, and you will win the election, too. If that suggests that Norwegian and Australian voters are poised to reward these centre-left incumbents for their management, think again. Opinion polls suggest that the centre-right is heading for victory in both countries, not the centre-left. If that turns out to be correct, therefore, any connection between economic performance and electoral success may not be as mechanistic as some believe. But what else might explain these putative results? If Jens Stoltenberg and Kevin Rudd are both shown the door, some will conclude that incumbency, not economic performance, holds the key. Ever since the financial meltdown, voters in many countries have punished governing parties. In Britain and Spain, the left was thrown out; in France and Italy, the right. By that reasoning, it's the simple bad luck that the downturn happened on their watch that may doom Messrs Stoltenberg and Rudd. In that case, though, how does one explain the fact that Angela Merkel's centre-right government in Germany seems to be processing serenely toward re-election in two weeks' time? The downturn has happened on her watch, too. Yet Mrs Merkel, like Stephen Harper in Canada, seems to be unaffected by the anti–incumbency factor. Others may conclude that the problem is on the centre-left. Maybe voters in Norway, Germany and Australia, like others before them, have lost confidence in the parties of the left, whether they are in office or not. Yet this, in turn, fails to explain Barack Obama's re-election or Enrico Letta's government in Italy. There is a commonsense conclusion to this. Authoritative attempts to put every country in the same explanatory frame should come with a health warning. A global crisis takes national forms, in which economic success, incumbency in the downturn and ideological credibility all matter, but in changing combinations. This may be intellectually frustrating, but it is also reassuringly messy and human.

results.webTitle: Heathrow expansion - Airports Commission publishes interim report: Politics live blog

fields.headline: Heathrow expansion - Airports Commission publishes interim report: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the day's political developments
fields.bodyText: • Campaigners living near Heathrow and Gatwick have said that they will fight plans to extend those airports. A new runway at one of the two sites now seems very likely to be recommended by the Airports Commission in 2015. Brendon Sewill, chairman of the Gatwick Area Conservation Campaign, said: Now the battle is for real. The battle lines are drawn. Now the spotlight is on Gatwick, the next step will be to examine the runway plans in detail, and it will be found that Gatwick is an unsuitable site. And John Stewart, chairman of Heathrow residents group Hacan, said: Although [Sir Howard] Davies's proposals focus less on Heathrow than had been rumoured, there is little doubt they will act as the trigger to 18 months of intense campaigning against Heathrow expansion. A new runway will increase planes from 480,000 a year to 560,000. There is no way overall noise levels will decrease. The scale of the opposition will be great. We believe that they [the proposals] are politically undeliverable and should have been dropped at this stage. In the Commons there were particularly strong warnings from MPs representing constituencies near Heathrow. Hayes and Harlington Labour MP John McDonnell, whose seat includes Heathrow, said: At the last election David Cameron advised people to vote blue to go green. He used the blocking of a third runway at Heathrow as the prime example of the Conservatives' conversion to green policies Setting up the Davies inquiry has been a cynical and obvious ploy to allow him to drop his commitment to my constituents and the people of west London. Many of my constituents whose homes, school and even church will be bulldozed by a third runway will view this as a disgraceful betrayal. We are a community betrayed. • Patrick McLoughlin, the transport secretary, has said that Network Rail will be reclassified as a government body in 2014 as part of a package of changes to the public finances, adding £30bn to Britain's national debt. • Inflation has fallen back to its slowest pace in four years thanks to steadying food prices and the fact that energy price hikes have yet to come in. As Katie Allen reports, the consumer prices measure of inflation slowed to 2.1% in November from 2.2% in October and was the lowest since November 2009, according to the Office for National Statistics. • Tom Winsor, the chief inspector of constabulary, has told MPs that it is "almost certain that some manipulation is going on" in the recording of crime statistics by officers. As the Press Association reports, Winsor told the home affairs committee said "in anything that gets measured" there is an "incentive, resisted by many, to manipulate the process to make your own performance look good". • The National Audit Office has said that just 26p of every £100 of the proceeds of crime is being confiscated from offenders. As the Press Association reports, the NAO said that despite £102m a year being spent operating the confiscation orders system by the police, prosecutors and the courts service, it recouped only £133m in 2012-13. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. Here's an Airports Commission reading list. • Jonathan Freedland at Comment is free says David Cameron is constrained on two sides over airports. So the political cost of a third runway is high, but so is the price of inaction. The business lobby is strong within the Conservative tribe and, apparently with George Osborne as its spokesman, it demands new airport capacity, ideally at Heathrow. So Cameron is caught between two unpalatable options. Which is why Davies's commission exists, to take a decision too hard for politicians to take by themselves. The "independent commission" is a favourite device, supplying that little dab of non-democracy – a smidgeon of China – needed to get things done. It allows a government torn in two to put power in the hands of those who will never have to face the voters – and to make sure they do nothing till after the next election, just to be on the safe side. When Friedman made his "China for a day" wish, he was bemoaning the democratic world's inability to take the action necessary to save the planet. The Heathrow row surely prompts even greater pessimism on that score.
