If it's this hard to agree on a move that will only add to our carbon emissions, how much harder to do what's needed to bring them down? • James Landale at the BBC says it will be hard for the main parties to continue dodging the issue of airport expansion until the election. Some Conservative ministers might want to cut their losses and express their private support for Heathrow expansion publicly. Can the Tories, they argue, really go into the next election as the pro-growth party without saying what they would do on one of the biggest long term investment issues this country faces? George Osborne, we are told, is much more supportive of Heathrow expansion than he once was. And, these ministers point out, if you want to drive airport expansion through, it would be better to have put it to the electorate first so you can claim some kind of mandate when the going gets rough. • James Kirkup at the Telegraph admires Howard Davies's deft handling of the "Boris Island" issue. • George Eaton at the New Statesman says Heathrow could become the tuition fees of the 2015 election. David Cameron (who declared in 2009: "the third runway at Heathrow is not going ahead, no ifs, no buts") and Ed Miliband (who nearly resigned as energy secretary over the issue and opposed a third Heathrow runway after becoming Labour leader) can bat away questions about aviation expansion during the election campaign by stating that no decision will be taken until after the final report has been delivered. This conspiracy of silence is reminiscent of that over tuition fees in 2010. Both Labour and the Tories knew the review of university funding chaired by Lord Browne would propose an increase in fees (and that they would support it) but it suited them to avoid acknowledging as much. Neither party outlined a position on tuition fees, with both merely stating that they would respond to Browne's report. • Mark Wallace at ConservativeHome says ruling out a third runway at Heathrow before 2010 was a "disastrous" mistake by Cameron. • Iain Martin at the Telegraph says that if Boris Johnson really wants his airport, he is going to have to become prime minister. Unite, which describes itself as Britain's biggest aviation union, says Britain must keep a hub airport. This is from Oliver Richardson, a Unite national officer. We welcome Sir Howard Davies’ positive approach to airport expansion but any option which involves a move away from a single hub airport would damage the UK’s competitive edge and hit aviation jobs. Losing a single hub airport would lead to global businesses reassessing their presence in the UK. Employers could relocate their European headquarters to other European nations like France and Germany which maintain single hub airports in Paris and Frankfurt. The Airports Commission has also made the wise decision to kick Boris Johnson’s hare-brained ideas into the long grass. We can now get on with the serious business of devising a workable plan for airport expansion to support jobs and the UK economy. That means the union is in favour of a third runway at Heathrow. Channel 4 News's Faisal Islam has some video from the village that will be destroyed if the main Heathrow extension plan goes ahead. Boris Johnson has been speaking to the Commons press gallery. Here are some of the highlights from Twitter. • Britain has come closer to committing itself to building a new runway in the south east of England following the publication of a report saying that one was needed and that it should go either at Heathrow or Gatwick. Sir Howard Davies, who chaired the Airports Commission, said his team favoured either building a new runway at Heathrow, extending Heathrow's northern runway to create a new one, or building a new runway at Gatwick. Some campaigners have claimed that Heathrow is now the clear favourite (see 11.25am) although, at his news conference, Davies certainly implied that Gatwick remained a strong contender. As the Guardian's Gwyn Topham explains in his story: In a huge boost to Gatwick's chances, Davies made clear he had not been convinced by the arguments made by many in aviation, not least by Heathrow and the London mayor, Boris Johnson, that expansion should only occur at one major "hub" – an airport with enough connecting flights and passengers to support other routes. Davies said London was a different case to other European hubs due to its size and popularity as a destination, and he did not accept there was a simple "hub or no hub" debate. However, the report is just an interim report. The commission is not due to publish its final recommendations until after the 2015 general election. • Ministers have refused to commit themselves to building a new runway. David Cameron fought the 2010 general election opposed to a third runway at Heathrow and it is widely assumed that he set up the Airports Commission to provide political cover for a U-turn to be performed after 2015. (On other issues Cameron has complained about the amount of time spent commissioning major infrastructure projects, but, on airports, he has been very happy to wait until after 2015.) In his statement to MPs Patrick McLoughlin, the transport secretary, said the government would not express a preference until after it receives the commission's final report. My principal concern as Secretary of State for Transport is to protect the integrity and independence of the Commission’s process through to the final report, which we expect to be delivered in summer 2015. So the government will not be commenting either today (17 December 2013) or in responding to the interim report, on the respective merits of the options which have and have not been shortlisted. In the Commons the Conservative MP Sir Edward Leigh asked McLoughlin explicitly if the government accepted the commission's contention that a new runway had to be built in the south east before 2030. McLoughlin would not even accept this. As I have said, I'm not actually going to say - the commision have said that, I believe what the commission has said is important, and we've got to see what their final proposals are and see if we can work to that particular deadline. The Labour MP Chris Bryant issued a damning verdict on this. Won't most ordinary people listening to this debate conclude that a politician that can't make a decision is no more use than a chocolate teapot? But, in truth, Labour has also been non-committal. And the Lib Dems have too. All three main parties have MPs representing West London seats who are very strongly opposed to Heathrow expansion. • Boris Johnson has welcomed the news that the commission has not yet ruled out his plan for a new airport on the Thames Estuary - but he seems to be finding a diplomatic way of accepting defeat. The commission's report does not shortlist the Thames Estuary idea, but says more work needs to be down before a final decision on its viability can be taken. It will rule on this next year. Davies made it fairly clear at his news conference that he thinks the idea is flawed, and he did not challenge claims that he only chose not to rule it out now in response to lobbying from the government. Johnson says he will carry on campaigning for the Thames Estuary option, but, in interviews, he sounded like a man accepting that he is losing the argument. Interviewed on the Today programme, he said a new runway at Gatwick would be the alternative least injurious to Londoners. (See 8.25am.) • A former Conservative peer has said that up to 50 peers are regularly "clocking in" to the House of Lords in order to claim a £300 daily attendance allowance. As Rajeev Syal reports, Lord Hanningfield – who served nine weeks of a nine-month sentence in 2011 for falsely claiming £28,000 in parliamentary expenses – suggested it was normal practice. He made his comments after the Daily Mirror alleged that it had followed Hanningfield in July and found that on 11 of 19 days he spent less than 40 minutes in the Lords before returning to his home in Essex. Hanningfield said the money went on "entertaining, meeting people, employing people", adding that he was a "full-time peer" who needed to be able to pay his electricity bill and buy food. Marcus Jones, the Conservative MP for Nuneaton, says Birmingham should feature far more heavily in the final Davies commission report. McLoughlin says he hopes more services can be attracted to Birmingham. That's it. The statement is over. I will post a summary soon. Rehman Chishti, the Conservative MP for Gillingham and Rainham, says a new Thames Estuary airport would cost more than £100bn. Does the government have that amount of money to spend? McLoughlin says it is not unusual for the price tag on transport infrastructure to rise. At one point it was only expected to cost £75bn. David Nuttall, the Conservative MP for Bury North, says airport passenger duty should be cut for new long-haul flights from regional airports. That would not cost anything because those services currently don't exist. McLoughlin says when he approaches the Treasury with schemes that supposedly do not cost anything, they often take a different view. Stuart Andrew, the Conservative MP for Pudsey, says Leeds-Bradford airport should have been given more consideration. Mark Reckless, the Conservative MP for Rochester and Strood, asks if Boris Johnson will get any public money to promote his Thames Estuary scheme. And he asks if meetings with the prime minister and the chancellor played any part in the decision to include it as a viable option at the last minute. McLoughlin says it is up to Johnson do decide how he spends the money he gets from the government. He does not address the second question. Stephen McCabe, the Labour MP for Birmingham Selly Oak, says a new runway at Heathrow would cost £6bn. A new runway at Birmingham could take two thirds of those passengers, at just 2% of the cost. McLoughlin says the commission did look at the case for Birmingham. Labour's Ian Lucas asks McLoughlin to explore the idea of getting all the three main parties to commit to accepting the Davies commission's final recommendation. McLoughlin says yes, he will. Sir John Randall, the Conservative MP for Uxbridge and South Ruislip, says expanding Heathrow is politically and environmentally unacceptable. Labour's Paul Goggins asks McLoughlin to ensure that the idea of using air passenger duty to encourage airlines to move away from London remains on the table. (But Sir Howard Davies said it wouldn't work. See 9.57am.) Labour's Chris Byrant says a politician who cannot make a decision is no more use than a chocolate teapot. Given that the government can't even rule out Boris Island, doesn't that show it has run out of steam. McLoughlin says Labour spent 13 years avoiding major decisions on infrastructure. Michael Fabricant, the Conservative MP for Lichfied, says HS2 should link directly to whichever airport is chosen for expansion. Labour's Ian Austin, MP for Dudley North, says people in the West Midlands will be staggered that they are being asked to wait until 2050 until Birmingham airport can be expanded. McLoughlin says there is a huge amount going on at Birmingham airport already. The runway has been extended. Labour's Gisela Stuart, MP for Birmingham Edgbaston, says she is not reassured by the news that Birmingham may get a new runway by 2050. (See 1.11pm.) She will be 95 by then, she says. Bernard Jenkin, the Conservative MP for Harwich and North Essex, says the Thames Estuary proposal cannot be lightly dismissed. Putting an airport there would shift the economic geography of the south east of England. Is that within the remit of the commission? McLoughlin says the commission is looking at this issue. Sir Edward Leigh, a Conservative, asks if the government agrees a new runway has to be build in the south east by 2030. McLoughlin says the commission has said that. It's an important statement, he says. Jason McCartney, the Conservative MP for Colne Valley, says there should be more long-haul flights from regional airports. Here is the full text of McLoughlin's statement. Zac Goldsmith, the Conservative MP for Richmond Park, says it will not be credible for a political party to go into the election without a clear position on this. McLoughlin says the commission is studying the evidence. It will produce a recommendation by the summer of 2015. Jack Dromey, the Labour MP for Birmingham Erdington, says Britain cannot succeed through London and the south east alone. There should have been more focus on expanding Birmingham, he says. McLoughlin says the commission says Birmingham could get a new airport after 2030 Crispin Blunt, the Conservative MP for Reigate, says the Thames Estuary option is the only viable one. McLoughlin says the government set up the commission so it could base its decision on solid evidence. Labour's Graham Stringer, MP for Blackley and Broughton, says both parties should give a commitment to accept the conclusions of the Davies report. Successive governments have ducked this issue, he says. McLoughlin says he hopes there will be a consensus on this. Angie Bray, the Conservative MP for Ealing Central and Acton, says any expansion of Heathrow would be intolerable. Labour's Andrew Slaughter, MP for Hammersmith, says Heathrow's grip on the Conservative party is as strong as ever. One third of people in Europe affected by aircraft noise live near Heathrow. McLoughlin says Slaughter fought an election on a manifesto calling for a third runway at Heathrow. Sir Roger Gale, the Conservative MP for Thanet North, says the commission did not consider other airports seriously. It should look at Manston airport in Kent, he says. Caroline Lucas, the Green MP, says some businesses are cutting their use of aviation. McLoughlin says passenger numbers are rising sharply. People do want to travel. Perhaps Lucas only has holidays in the UK. But other people like to travel abroad. Alistair Darling, the Labour former chancellor and former transport secretary, says we are back where we were 10 years ago, considering a third runway at Heathrow. Does the government think the commission is on the right track? Darling says the government needs to take a decision quickly. McLoughlin says the 2003 white paper on aviation only mentioned Dubai once. That shows how much circumstances change. Julian Huppert, the Lib Dem MP, says he welcomed David Cameron's "no ifs, no buts" statement ruling out a Heathrow. What will be done to ensure that Britain meets its climate change commitments? McLoughlin says it is bad for emissions to have aircraft circling London because they cannot land. Louise Ellman, the Labour chair of the transport committee, says Britain will continue to lose out if no action is taken. McLoughlin says Sir Howard Davies will give evidence to the committee early next year. Sir Alan Haselhurst, the Conservative MP for Saffron Walden, urges McLoughlin to get a bipartisan agreement for any final decision. McLoughlin says he will be trying for a tripartisan agreement. John McDonnell, the Labour MP for Hayes and Harlington, says at least 2,000 of his constituents could lose their homes under these plans. That figure could rise to 10,000. And a cemetery may have to be dug up too. McLoughlin says McDonnell is prejudging the report. McLoughlin is responding to Creagh. She quotes from Creagh's press release issued earlier. (See 10.47am.) Creagh said Labour was sceptical of the Heathrow third runway idea. But this proposal was in Labour's 2010 election manifesto, written by Ed Miliband, he says. He says he is glad welcomes the fact that Labour supports the commission. As for legislation, he says he cannot say what will be in the Queen's Speech. Mary Creagh, the shadow transport secretary, says Labour is glad the original plan for a third runway at Heathrow has been abandoned. She asks for details of when legislation will be passed setting up an independent aviation noise authority. (See 9.28am.) McLoughlin says the government will be working with the promoters of extension schemes on compensation schemes for people affected. It will set out its proposals when it responds formally to the interim report. But, when it issues this response, it will not say which of the options it favours. It will let the Airports Commission produce its final recommendations before responding. • McLoughlin confirms that the government won't express a preference for a new runway in the south east until after the Airports Commission produces its final report (by the summer of 2015 - after the general election.) McLoughlin is now summarising the recommendations on making better use of aviation industry now. (See 7.17am.) Many of these recommendations are aimed at the industry, he says. But the government will consider these proposals too, and respond to them by the spring of next year. Patrick McLoughlin say he knows this issue is important not just to the country's economy, but to people who work at airports and who live near them. The airport sector contributes £18bn a year to the economy. It employs 220,000 people, he says. Many MPs hold strong views on this, he says. MPs will be invited to a briefing with Sir Howard Davies on this in the New Year. The interim report is professional and detailed, he says. It provides new evidence on the airport capacity the UK will require in the future. It says there is a need for new runway capacity in the medium term. The government will want to take a decision in the best interests of the country in the long term. Davies says building a new runway for 2030, and another for 2050, can be consistent with Britain meeting its climate change targets. McLoughlin is summarising the proposals now. (See 7.05am.) If the Thames Estuary option is added to the shortlist next year, it will be subject to detailed consideration, he says. Patrick McLoughlin, the transport secretary, is about to make a Commons statement on the Airports Commission report. I will be covering it in detail. Ladbrokes have issued odds on what the final recommendation of the Davies commission will be. They think the new runway at Heathrow is most likely. Here are the odds on the four options. Third runway at Heathrow Evens Extend existing runway at Heathrow 9/4 New runway at Gatwick 4/1 Construction of Boris Island 6/1 (I think Ladbrokes are over-estimating the chances of "Boris Island" going ahead. And, having been at the Davies press conference, I came away with the impression that he regarded Gatwick as a better option than extending the northern runway at Heathrow. See 9.36am.) Here is some more reaction to the report from environmental groups. (I quoted WWF earlier - see 9.21.am.) From John Sauven, executive director of Greenpeace Once you consider the noise, air pollution, community destruction, and the climate change impact of expanding Heathrow, no genuinely independent commission could come to the conclusion that a third runway could ever be a sensible idea. David Cameron told voters, “no ifs, no buts, no third runway” and when standing for leadership of the Labour Party Ed Miliband described his party’s previous support for expansion at Heathrow as “a mistake.” Since then nothing has changed except that millions of pounds have been spent by the aviation industry rehashing and re-spinning the same flawed arguments that were made before the last election. Aviation is still outside of the national and international legal frameworks on cutting carbon emissions yet the industry remains one of the fastest growing contributors to climate change. Heathrow expansion would also add to local air pollution already exceeding EU legal limits, and would add to already severe road and rail congestion in the area. The industry has produced no answers to this Party leaders must come clean with voters about their real positions on this issue before the general election – and explain how expanding one of the world’s busiest airports could ever be consistent with building a low carbon economy and staying within air pollution limits. From Jane Thomas, a senior campaigner at Friends of the Earth Building new airports and runways will have a huge impact on local communities and their environment, and pump more climate-changing pollution into our atmosphere. If our airports are allowed to expand, other sectors of the economy will have to make even bigger carbon cuts to enable the UK to play its part in tackling climate change. The south east doesn’t need aviation expansion - London has more flights to the world’s business centres than its European competitors. From Sue Armstrong-Brown, head of policy at the Royal Society for the Protection of Birds We have always said that the Thames Estuary is a disastrous place to put an airport. It supports many thousands of wintering birds and other wildlife. Every time a spotlight is put on the Thames Estuary as a potential site for an airport it is revealed to be both an environmental disaster and economic lunacy. The more scrutiny put on this proposal, the more clear it will be for all concerned that it is a non starter. However climate change remains the greatest long term threat to wildlife. We believe there should be no further airports in this country until the Government can demonstrate how they can be built and operated without busting our legally binding climate targets. Emissions from aircraft are one of the fastest increasing sources of greenhouse gases. The impacts of climate change on wildlife in the UK and abroad are already being felt with seabirds struggling to find food as sea temperatures increase. Evidence shows that climate change could lead to up to a third of land-based species committed to extinction by 2050. From Georgia Wrighton, from the Campaign to Protect Rural England in Sussex A second runway at Gatwick, together with sprawling development and car parks anticipated on a massive scale, would concrete over cherished open countryside. A heady cocktail of increased flights, HGV traffic and cars would erode the tranquillity of rural communities, and the health and quality of life of people living under its shadow. My colleague Mona Chalabi has been using the Guardian's data blog to show how the different London airports compare on matters like delays and passenger numbers. Here's the text of Boris Johnson's full, formal response to the Howard Davies report. Johnson is giving a speech to the press gallery in the Commons at lunchtime, so I'm sure we haven't heard the last from him on this. And here is some more reaction from MPs to the report. From Andy Slaughter, the Labour MP for Hammersmith From John McDonnell, the Labour MP for Hayes and Harlington From Simon Danczuk, the Labour MP for Rochdale From Mary Macleod, the Conservative MP for Brentford and Isleworth From Mark Reckless, the Conservative MP for Rochester and Strood. Here's an extract from a post on his blog. I welcome the fact that a Thames Estuary Airport has not been shortlisted, but I am concerned that the commission’s study of a Grain option next year will cause further unnecessary blight and worry for local residents. I am also concerned that improper political pressure from London’s mayor or from within government on an independent commission may have led to “further study” of a Grain option, rather than it being ruled out in the Interim Report, as I believe it should have been. I will be asking further questions to clarify how this decision was made. Although a study on the feasibility of a Grain option will be undertaken next year, I believe that further examination by the Airports Commission will further highlight how unworkable this pie in the sky proposal is, and that it will be rejected. From Tracey Crouch, the Conservative MP for Chatham and Aylesford. Here is an extract from a post on her blog. The fact that proposals for a new hub airport in the Thames Estuary have not been shortlisted is welcome. While it is frustrating that the commission will look again at the Isle of Grain before its final report in 2015, the interim report published today identifies some of the significant fundamental challenges associated with such an option, including the enormous environmental and economic cost and its deliverability, which have rightly kept it off the shortlist. These challenges will not go away, and neither will local opposition when it is examined again by the commission next year and I call on it to reject the proposal as soon as possible. From Michael Fabricant, the Conservative MP for Lichfield From Cheryl Gillan, the Conservative MP for Chesham and Amersham (and a campaigner against HS2) From Ed Vaizey, the Conservative MP for Wantage From Nick de Bois, the Conservative MP for Enfield North Zac Goldsmith, the Conservative MP for Richmond Park, has been speaking to BBC News about the Davies report. Here are the main points. • Goldsmith said that for David Cameron to back Heathrow expansion would be "monumental" breach of promise. This kind of U-turn on a monumental, flagship promise – a black-and-white, unambiguous promise before the election – would send shivers throughout the entire party. People will wonder ‘What’s next? Can we trust the manifesto? Is there any point even reading the manifesto?’, because political promises delivered by my party – potentially, if we do a U-turn on this issue – are things that can be dropped very casually. • Goldsmith reaffirmed his pledge to resign and trigger a byelection if the Conservative party backed a new runway at Heathrow. • He said that he thought Howard Davies favoured expanding Heathrow - the option championed by BAA. • He said the Conservatives were too sympathetic to BAA. The BAA machine is incredibly impressive - I take my hat off to their PR capabilities - but what surprises me is that there are so many people in my party – Conservatives – who seem to favour what is effectively a taxpayer-funded monopoly on one end of London. That has never served the interests of customers Competition has always been a better alternative, and we have three airports in London which, if they were allowed to compete properly, would deliver the best customer experience. Boris Johnson, the mayor of London, has now given various interviews about the Davies report. Here's a summary of the main points. • Johnson welcomed the news that his plan for a new Thames Estuary airport has not been rejected. The report was "very, very good news", he said. • He revealed that within the last few days he had persuaded Sir Howard Davies to agree to further research into the viability of the Thames Estuary option. One of the great things that we’ve got from Sir Howard over the last couple of days is an agreement that the option of an estuary can be taken forward and properly financed, we can properly study exactly what the cost would be, and don’t forget the thing we’re leaving out of this equation is also the huge regeneration benefits to the east of the city, an area which fell into post-industrial decline and we think could support hundreds of thousands of new jobs. It has been reported that originally the Davies commission planned to rule out the Thames Estuary option, and that it was only after pressure from the Treasury that it was decided not to reject it at this stage. Johnson's comments seem to confirm that the commission had a last-minute change of heart. At his news conference Davies said that the shortlist had not changed, but he refused to comment on his discussions with the Treasury, or to deny that the commission had changed its stance on the Thames Estuary option in response to lobbying. (See 9.44am.) • Johnson said that Gatwick expansion was not a serious option. If you have an extra runway at Gatwick then it won’t make a bean of difference because the airlines will still want to go to Heathrow ... There is no power on earth, except perhaps legal coercion, that is going to get the airlines to migrate from Heathrow to Gatwick because Heathrow is where they want to go to. That meant, realistically, the choice was between Heathrow expansion and the new Thames Estuary airport, he said. • He said that expanding Heathrow was "completely crackers". Our contention, Londoners' contention is that building another runway in the west London suburbs is completely crackers and all it will do, of course, is simply feed the beast, it will get bigger and bigger and as soon as you’ve completed a third runway, which wouldn’t be until 2030 at the very earliest, or 2029, 2030, there would be insatiable demands for a fourth runway ... It would be catastrophic for London, it would be catastrophic for quality of life. All our policies about improving quality of life in this city, you’d be consigning millions of people to extra noise pollution ... It is very important for our listeners to realise that what is being proposed at Heathrow is not some oven-ready, peasy scheme. Both the Heathrow options involve concreting over the M25, probably closing it for at least five years, doing major realignments of the M4, of the A113, we are talking about tens of billions and so my question to the world is why on earth entrench a huge planning error and expand Heathrow and consign future generations to misery when we could go for the right option? • He said London needed a hub airport. "If you look at what everybody else in Europe is doing, to say nothing of what’s happening in Dubai, around the world in China, in Istanbul, in India everybody is building bigger hub airports," he said. • He said he would not resign from the Conservative party over Heathrow expansion. I believe in going on and winning fights, rather than flouncing out. I believe this is an argument that I believe will eventually be won by the iron logic of the case which is that basically you cannot continue to expand a 21st century hub right in the middle of the western suburbs of the greatest city on earth. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. Here's Mary Creagh, Labour's transport spokeswoman, on the Davies report. We will scrutinise closely the important work that Sir Howard Davies is doing, including his report today. It is vital that we take decisions about our airport capacity, including in the South-East, which are important for Britain's competitiveness. It is good to see that the original Heathrow proposal for a third runway, of which we were sceptical, has been taken off the table, and that this work is taking place within the context of our ongoing climate change commitments. As the commission now looks in greater detail at specific proposals it remains crucial that they take into account the need to minimise local and environmental impacts of increased capacity. Vince Cable, the Lib Dem business secretary and MP for Twickenham, has posted a statement on his blog saying he is firmly opposed to Heathrow expansion. The Davies Commission interim report has put Heathrow at the front of its thinking which is questionable economically, damaging environmentally and probably undeliverable politically. My strong view is that the Davies review should not decide to press ahead with Heathrow options and I will be making that view known in the strongest terms. I fully support the need to improve UK business links with airports in the emerging markets of Asia which is important for jobs, but this could be achieved more quickly by reforming and reallocating airport slots; by building up point to point services; and by strengthening the capacity of UK regional airports. Such is the impact of noise on hundreds of thousands of people in London that pushing ahead with Heathrow expansion is almost certainly the worst option for expanding airport capacity. Here is some Twitter comment on the Howard Davies press conference. From the Guardian's Gwyn Topham From Channel 4 News's Faisal Islam I lost my internet connection, but the press conference is now over and I'm back in the Commons. Here is the final question. Q: Why did you reject the south-west option for a new runway proposed by Heathrow? Davies says they decided the water options would be very hard to overcome. Essentially, a large reservoir would have to be built. Finding a location would be very hard. Building new reservoirs is almost as hard as building new airports. Q: Are there different options being considered for Gatwick? Davies says it is possible that the consultation might propose minor changes to the new runway at Gatwick. Q: What will your new work on the Thames Estuary project involve? Isn't the information out there already? Davies says the commission has been aware of the risk of blight. A key issue relates to the sites of special scientific interest. What would you have to do to provide compensatory habitats? Davies says it is not clear yet. And there are still questions about the cost of installing surface transport. One proposal involves a tunnel to Waterloo. And there has not been much analysis of the impact on other other areas. Q: Did you consider increasing air passenger duty? Yes, says Davies. The commission looked at proposals to encourage airlines to move to other, regional airports, using air passenger duty as a lever. There could be a higher charge on congested airports. It modelled what would happen. And it concluded this would not improve connectivity. And it would increase emissions. Some new routes might be set up to popular destinations, but with smaller planes. There would be no net increase in connectivity. Q: Who will pay for the new work on the feasibility of the Thames Estuary project? Davies says the commission will fund its own research. It has a budget, although it is not lavish. Q: Are you having further discussions with Heathrow about the plan to extend the runway? It was not their idea. Davies says there will be further talks with them on this. They did not throw up their hands in opposition to the idea. Q: What attracted you to the idea of expanding the northern runway at Heathrow? Davies says in some ways it is simpler. It does not deliver as much extra capacity as a new runways (190,000 extra movements, compared to 250,000). But it has some interesting features. Q: Are we moving more towards New York-style provision, where you have three decent-sized airports? And, if so, how do you respond to the charge that it is not as well served as other US cities? Davies said they spent quite a lot of time looking at New York. The hub airports in New York have hub airlines. In New York they are looking to built a new runway somewhere too. London is big enough to have competing airports. It has done well from that, he says. But it is not clear how the balance will shift in the future. Davies says all options would involve some public money. While the airports would fund improvements themselves, the options would require changes to transport. The public purse would have to meet those costs. Given that a large proportion of the costs of a Thames Estuary options would come from the need to install transport links, the costs to the taxpayer would be higher. At Heathrow the transport infrastructure is already in place. Q: Are you effectively ruling out the Thames Estuary option? Davies says the commission will make its decision next year. But the hurdles for the Thames Estuary option are higher than for the others. Q: What can be done to help homeowners affected by blight while the commission waits to produce its final conclusions? Davies says the commission is sensitive to this issue. But it does not have a budget to address this. This is an issue for airport operators. Q: Did your report change after your conversations with the Treasury? Davies says they finalised the report last week. The shortlist did not change. And they went backwards and forwards on the Thames Estuary issue. But they finalised the report last week. Q: Did the Treasury ask you to keep the Thames Estuary option in play? Davies says he will not comment on his discussions with minis Davies says you could do Heathrow now, and Gatwick after 2030, or vice versa. But the commission does not favour both at the same time. Davies says more than all of the increased demand over recent years has come from low-cost fliers. Demand for flights from "legacy fliers" (ie, the traditional airlines) has gone down. In the future the demand will remain for long haul. It may be the case that low-cost fliers provide more of these flights. In the long run, it is an open question whether London will be better served by a different constellation of airports, or by one big hub. That would involve "putting all your eggs in one basket". That could be risky. It is not clear that London needs one big hub, he says. In other places, like Frankfurt or Schiphol, that is not the case. That is because they do not need to serve so many local connections. It is "simplistic" to suggest otherwise, Davies says. I lost my wifi connection for a moment, so missed the last few minutes of Davies's opening presentation. He is now taking questions. Q: Did you come under pressure to keep the Thames Estuary option on the table? Davies says the commission drew up its shortlist some time ago. It went back and forth on the Thames Estuary option. It has decided to allow more time for it to be evaluated. But these measures do not avoid the need for more capacity, Davies says. The commission reviewed more than 40 options, he says. The sifting process led to the conclusion that there are two credible locations for expansion within the timescale needed. Gatwick's claims for expansion were not backed in the 2003 white paper, because there was a promise it would not expand for a certain period. But now the commission is looking beyond that timescale. Expansion would be feasible. The noise impact of Gatwick expansion would be less than Heathrow. At Heathrow, two options are being considered. These are not the same as the new short runway proposal advocated before the general election. Davies says building a new runway is the most attractive option. But expanding the existing northern runway is also worthy of consideration. Both options would lead the number of people affected by noise being lower than today, because aircraft are getting quieter, he says. Davies says there are many inefficiencies in the way airspace is managed. The commission proposes various ways to improve this. These could bring economic advantages worth £2bn. The commission is recommending the creating of an aviation noise authority. The government should legislate for this as soon as possible. And Davies says he wrote to the chancellor before the autumn statement asking him to improve transport facilities at airports. He was glad George Osborne responded in the autumn statement. Davies says aviation forecasting is not an exact science. The commission has looked at the impact on climate change. Aviation is not easy to decarbonise, and it is envisaged that emissions will rise in the future. More capacity will be needed in the future, he says. The options for avoiding capacity increases do not look realistic, he says. The press conference is starting now. Sir Howard Davies is starting now. This is just an interim report, he says. It contains recommendations for what should be done in the short term, plus a shortlist of options. The first conclusion is that airports have managed well. The UK does not face an immediate crisis of capacity, although that is partly because of the recession. But there are problems in the longer term. And Gulf airports have been able to pick up some transfer traffic that could have gone to the UK. Because of capacity shortages, airports are not well equipped to cope with delays. And there is some evidence that passengers are paying for lack of capacity. And here's what WWF, the environmental group, is saying about this report. This is from Jean Leston, transport policy manager at WWF-UK. It's a shame that the Airports Commission thinks we need more runways, and probably at Heathrow, to provide more routes to emerging markets. We think you can do this without pouring more concrete and wrecking our climate targets. The fact is, business is flying less and expansion at Heathrow will be used mostly for leisure travel. Expanding Heathrow for more leisure flying is an environmental travesty. There's ample spare capacity at other airports to for holidaymakers to use. WWF says that the number of business flights from Heathrow has fallen by 23% since 2000, and that business flights across the whole of the UK are down 13% over the same period. And here's some business reaction to the report. From Colin Stanbridge, chief executive of the London Chamber of Commerce and Industry (LCCI) It has been apparent for a long time that the south east needs more airport capacity and so we welcome the announcement today from the Davies Commission that more runways could be built both at Heathrow and Gatwick. Heathrow is a world recognised hub airport and should be the priority for growth. It makes most sense to expand there in order to meet the pressing needs of both London companies as well as those wishing to come and do business here. Now that we’ve had these recommendations the Government should just get on and act on them, particularly the short-terms measures like the changes to early morning arrivals. It makes no sense whatsoever to put this decision off until after the general election when businesses are crying out for action now. This political posturing puts our economic recovery at risk and threatens London’s reputation as a world leading city. The options are now very clear, the Government needs to act and produce a clear timetable for delivering on the recommendations made today. From John Longworth, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce Businesses across Britain need the very best air links if they are to deliver growth, jobs, and investment. Companies have long since understood that the south-east of England requires additional runway capacity, and we are happy that the Davies Commission has recognised this. It is vital that we have sufficient aviation capacity if we are to rebalance the economy towards exports, as well as encourage inward investment and tourism. For far too long Britain has dithered on capacity while others have delivered, and as a consequence we are rapidly losing ground to competitor countries. Any option that is funded by the private sector, and can be delivered in a short timeframe, should be allowed to proceed. A decision simply must be implemented as soon as possible – playing politics and putting off important decisions will only place the UK at a further global disadvantage. I'm at the press conference. It's in a smallish meeting room at the Department for Education. It should be starting in about 10 minutes. Here is some more early reaction to the Airports Commission report. From the Green party. This is from Keith Taylor, a Green MEP for the South East The political opposition to airport expansion in South East England is sadly melting away. There's no doubt that the Government will be pleased with this report. It gives them the cover they need to go on avoiding answering difficult questions on airport expansion and to prepare themselves for a colossal u-turn on Heathrow expansion. This report will be of great concern to my constituents near Gatwick and Heathrow. We know that any new runways at either airport will increase air pollution, destroy homes and countryside and mean more people’s lives are blighted by flight noise. With the exception of a handful of local MPs it looks likes the residents near Gatwick and Heathrow will be utterly abandoned by the Westminster parties. The good news for my constituents is that the ‘Boris Island’ airport looks to be dead in the water. This report also clears a path for expansion of one of the fastest growing sources of climate changing carbon emissions. If we are serious about tacking climate change we need to radically re-think the way we travel, not just tarmac over our countryside and knock down homes in pursuit of evermore business as usual. From Stop Stansted Expansion We are obviously greatly relieved that Stansted is not on the shortlist. The environmental consequences of even one extra runway would have been catastrophic and the fact is that there has never been a viable business case for any extra runways at Stansted. The airport currently operates at less than half of its potential capacity. We must now show solidarity with the communities around Heathrow and Gatwick and support their efforts in resisting the threat that they now face. SSE expects to issue further press comment this afternoon, after Sir Howard Davies’s presentation in London and the opportunity to consider his interim report in more detail. I'm off to the press conference now. I will post again as soon as I've fired up my laptop at the press conference venue in Westminster. And this is from Dale Keller, chief executive of the Board of Airline Representatives in the UK (BAR UK), an industry body representing airlines. Despite the inevitable challenges and disagreement that will follow, the vast majority of airlines believe that expanding Heathrow is now the only sensible way forward for the UK. Sir Howard and his team have correctly identified that other proposals would cost billions in public funding and could create mass relocations and associated social upheaval and economic pain, or not deliver on the core objectives. Unlike rail, it’s the world’s airlines and their customers who will ultimately pay for the new airport infrastructure through airport charges. Since aircraft are a moveable asset, the UK cannot risk building capacity where the airlines and their customers don’t want it. Airlines are also highly supportive of the inclusion of an optimisation strategy [see 7.17am], with Sir Howard acknowledging that this is not only an issue of airlines adding more flights to meet increasing demand, but also about improving resilience and the customer experience. The travelling public want the industry and the government to reduce delays caused by congestion and to fully meet their future travelling needs. This can only be achieved by having three or more runways together in the right location. Here is some reaction to the report from airports From Heathrow. This is from Colin Matthews, Heathrow’s chief executive, Britain is better off when we’re connected. The world economy is changing fast and Britain needs a world-class hub airport with the capacity to compete against Paris, Frankfurt and Amsterdam. A third runway is the quickest, cheapest and surest way of connecting the UK to growth.” We have thought afresh about how a third runway can be delivered. Our new option is different from the previous proposal for a third runway and will deliver the flights Britain needs while continuing to reduce the total number of people affected by aircraft noise.” We do not oppose other airports being permitted to grow or add new runways. Heathrow is the UK’s only hub airport and competes with Frankfurt, Paris and Amsterdam for transfer passengers to support long haul routes. Point-to-point airports like Gatwick serve a different market. Heathrow does not agree with Gatwick’s stance that there can only be one new runway in the South East. We are pleased that the Commission recognises that there is not a binary choice between providing additional hub capacity or additional point-to-point capacity. We would welcome a solution in which point to point airports were allowed to grow alongside the UK’s only hub airport to deliver choice for passengers and airlines. From Heathrow Hub, the group backing the idea to expand the northern runway at Heathrow We are delighted to have been shortlisted by the Airports Commission. This is an important endorsement of our innovative solution to Heathrow expansion. The Commission have conducted a thorough and transparent process and we look forward to a continuing dialogue with them and other stakeholders as they undertake a detailed appraisal before a public consultation in autumn next year. We will also continue to advocate the extension and splitting of the southern runway; and the development of an integrated rail and passenger facility north of Heathrow in order to create an integrated hub airport for the whole country. We would like to emphasise that our proposal is not only the cheapest on offer, it also has the most comprehensive noise reduction strategy. From Gatwick Airport. This is from Stewart Wingate, Gatwick's chief executive officer. I am very pleased that the strength of London Gatwick's case has been recognised by the Airports Commission and that the short list has been narrowed down to two main locations. Expansion at London Gatwick can give the country the economic benefits it needs at an environmental cost it can afford with the lower fares and greater choice that passengers want. It can be delivered more quickly and at lower cost. London Gatwick is also uniquely placed to help meet all the different aviation needs of the future. It is the only option which can the deliver the right type of capacity for the UK as it is the only airport serving all airlines models including both low cost and legacy airlines as well as both transfer passengers and people flying direct. I believe the critical importance of this to the aviation debate will become far more widely recognised over the coming months. Q: If David Cameron says he would go into the election looking at a Heathrow third runway, where would you put that in the pantheon of Cameron mistakes? Johnson says there is no such category. He will carry on arguing for his preferred option. He thinks it is important to remain in a fight to win it. And that's it. The interview is over. Evan Davis is interviewing Boris Johnson, who has turned up at last. Q: What's your reaction? Johnson says his idea is "not dead yet". It is "good news" that people have a choice - either Heathrow or a new option. The Gatwick option will not work, because the airlines will not want to go there. If a third runway were to be built at Heathrow, but 2030 there would be pressure for a fourth runway. He says he will work with Howard Davies to allay some of his concerns about the cost of the airport. It would not cost as much as Davies suggests. Private finance could fund much of it. Both Heathrow options involve concreting over the M25, and probably closing it, he says. So why entrench a huge planning error when you could go for the right option. Q: He thinks you have underestimated the cost? Johnson says he has got an agreement that a proper study into the cost of the Estuary airport can be carried out. And Davies has not taken into account the huge regeneration benefits. Q: He says you are overestimating the importance of a four-runway hub. He thinks a third one would be enough at Heathrow. After that a new runway could go to Birmingham or Stansted. Johnson says that is what the Heathrow lobby says. "We have no further territorial demands." Q: It's not the Heathrow lobby, it's Davies. He says you are wrong to assume that a four-runway hub is needed? Johnson says he is not proposing a nine-runway hub, like they one they are building in Beijing. But if a third runway is built at Heathrow, there would be pressure for another. Q: Of the three shortlisted options, which do you prefer? Johnson says a new runway at Gatwick would be the "least injurious" to Londoners. But he does not think that would work, because airlines would not want to go there. This is what Number 10 is saying about the report (not much). The Today programme are hoping to interview Boris Johnson. But he hasn't turned up yet, so that will have to wait. Boris Johnson is about to be interviewed on the Today programme. Here is more from the Airports Commission's report on why his plan for a Thames Estuary airport has not been shortlisted at this stage. An Isle of Grain Airport would be some 33 miles from central London (compared to 15 for Heathrow and 25 for Gatwick) with no direct surface access links in place. Its easterly location makes it less convenient than Heathrow for the majority of UK travellers. The Commission’s analysis suggests that a number of major transport enhancements would be required to support an Isle of Grain airport, including new high speed links to central London, extensions to Crossrail and local conventional rail lines and a range of road improvements. Securing planning permission for and delivering surface transport investment of this scale to support an airport opening date prior to 2030 would be extremely challenging, particularly given the overlap with the construction period for HS2. These surface transport enhancements would also be very costly (estimated at more than £24 billion before any adjustment for risk or optimism bias) and would present their own planning, delivery and environmental challenges. They may, however, provide additional benefits through increasing transport capacity for other users in Kent and East London. Even with these improvements in place the population likely to be living within 45, 60 and 120 minutes’ travel of the airport would be lower than around Heathrow and broadly similar to Gatwick. The overall costs of an Isle of Grain airport would be extremely high. The Commission’s independent assessment suggests that they could total as much as £82-112 billion by 2030, including surface access costs and allowances for risk and optimism bias. This is around five times the estimated cost of a new runway at Heathrow, and does not include any costs related to the acquisition or closure of Heathrow. Here is some early Twitter reaction to the report. From Zac Goldsmith, the Conservative MP for Richmond Park, an environmentalist and a strong opponent of a third runway at Heathrow From David Lammy, the Labour MP for Tottenham From the BBC's James Landale From the New Statesman's George Eaton From ITV's Chris Ship From the BBC's Norman Smith From the ConservativeHome founder Tim Montgomerie And here is what the Airports Commission report (pdf) says on why the Thames Estuary options - favoured by Boris Johnson - have not, at this stage, been shortlisted. The Thames Estuary airport options were not at this stage shortlisted. While the potential they offered to reduce aviation noise impacts in the South East of England and to support economic development on the eastern side of London was attractive, they presented many challenges and uncertainties. They would be extremely expensive, with the cost of an Isle of Grain airport (the most viable of those presented) around five times that of the three short-listed options at up to £112 billion. They would present major environmental issues, especially around impacts on protected sites. The new surface access infrastructure required would be very substantial, with potential cost, deliverability and environmental challenges of its own. And the overall balance of economic impacts would be uncertain – particularly as an Estuary airport would require the closure of Heathrow for commercial reasons and London City for airspace reasons. The Commission intends to carry out additional analysis in respect of the Isle of Grain option in the first half of 2014. On this basis, it will reach a view before the end of the year as to whether such an option would offer a credible proposal for consideration alongside the short-listed options. If so, it will be subject to a similar appraisal and consultation process as for those options, although not necessarily to the same timetable. Evan Davis is interviewing Sir Howard Davies now. Davies says that, to be fair to the Thames Estuary idea, he does not want to rule it out now. Further work will be done to see whether it is feasible. And he also wants to consider whether airlines would move to it. Q: Were you under any pressure not to drop that idea from the report? Did David Cameron and Boris Johnson say you had to keep this option until until the election? Davies says that the commission will decide by the summer of next year whether not to keep that as an option. Q: At Heathrow one scheme involves a double-lenght runway. Isn't that dangerous? Davies says they thought that too at first. But the Civil Aviation Authority said it was viable. Q: Boris Johnson says you need a hub airport in London, and they have to be Heathrow or the Thames Estuary. Davies says he does not agree. London has a constellation of airports. The question is, what will happen in the future. If there new flights to China, they will be at Heathrow. But do all new flights to New York have to go from Heathrow, as current ones do. Q: Why was Stansted left out? It is closer to the rest of the country. Davies says there used to be a ban on expansion at Gatwick. But demand shows that Gatwick is much more popular with airlines than Stansted. Q: Is the commission unanimous on this? Yes, says Davies. This is what the Airports Commission says about why airport expansion is necessary. I've taken it from the report's executive summary. It says failing to expand airport capacity could cost the wider economy up to £45bn over a 60-year period. Heathrow is now effectively full. Gatwick is operating at more than 85% of its maximum capacity and is completely full at peak times. It is becoming more and more difficult for airports and airlines to operate efficiently within the constraints of their existing infrastructure. Smaller airports have been successful at attracting some forms of traffic, but many services – particularly in long-haul markets – rely on the volumes of demand that only exist at the country’s largest airports. As a result, the UK appears to be reaching the limits of what can be achieved within its existing airport infrastructure. Passengers at Heathrow suffer from a high level of delay and unreliability, as a result of capacity constraints limiting the airport’s day-to-day efficiency and its ability to respond to one-off events. These issues do not only affect passengers; they also limit the airport’s ability to offer predictable patterns of respite from noise for local communities. As other airports reach capacity, similar impacts can be foreseen. In terms of connectivity, Heathrow continues to have a dominant position amongst European hubs on routes to North America and other established aviation markets. However, it has not been able to build on this and establish a similar position of strength in routes to emerging economies. And the number of domestic routes to the airport is declining, restricting access from other UK regions to Heathrow’s network of international services. The current approach of forcing ever greater volumes of traffic through the existing infrastructure, if continued, would therefore have increasingly detrimental effects on the national economy, businesses, and air passengers. The Commission’s analysis suggests that the costs of failing to address these issues could amount, over a sixty-year time period, to: ● £18-20 billion of costs to users and providers of airport infrastructure ● £30-45 billion of costs to the wider economy. It is not possible to predict exactly when these problems will come to a head. There are major uncertainties involved in forecasting aviation demand, and any forecasts are sensitive to assumptions around how the economy and society will develop in future. Governments, however, have a responsibility to plan ahead for the future, particularly in the case of long-lived infrastructure like runways, which take many years to plan and build. To do so, policymakers need to consider a range of future scenarios and their implications for the amount and type of infrastructure that may be needed. The Commission has developed a new set of forecasts which address many of the key concerns about the way the Department for Transport has previously forecast aviation demand. It has used these forecasts to test a range of scenarios for the future of the aviation sector. Across all scenarios considered, including where the UK is meeting its climate change targets, there is significant growth in demand for aviation between now and 2050, placing additional pressure on already stressed airport infrastructure in London and the South East. The London airport system is forecast to be under very substantial pressure in 2030, and by 2050 sees demand significantly in excess of the total available capacity, even when aviation emissions are constrained to 2005 levels. In his foreword to the report (pdf), Sir Howard Davies admits he does not expect to "miraculously deliver a consensus". My colleagues and I are not naïve enough to think that the analysis in this report will miraculously deliver a consensus on the way forward. But we hope that the many people who have responded to our consultations – and we are grateful to all of them – will accept that we have made an effort to grapple with the conflicting points of view and to produce reasonable responses to their points. We hope we have clarified the arguments, updated earlier analysis, assessed the way the aviation world is changing and identified a realistic set of options for future expansion. We were conscious of the importance of avoiding unnecessary costs and anxiety for communities across the South East, unless we saw a credible case for a commercially viable and sustainable development at a proposed site. Sir Howard Davies has been giving interviews already. Here are some of the points he has been making. And here's a quote from Sir Howard Davies. Decisions on airport capacity are important national strategic choices and must be based upon the best evidence available. The Commission has undertaken a fresh, comprehensive and transparent study of the issues. This report is the product of extensive consultation, independent analysis and careful consideration by the commissioners. The UK enjoys excellent connectivity today. The capacity challenge is not yet critical but it will become so if no action is taken soon and our analysis clearly supports the provision of one net additional runway by 2030. In the meantime we encourage the government to act on our recommendations to make the best of our existing capacity. The Commission will now focus on the challenge of appraising the three options, further assessing the case for a new airport in the Thames Estuary, and delivering a robust final recommendation to government in summer 2015. The commission is also recommending measures for "immediate action" to make better use of the airport capacity in the south east of England that already exists. Here's what the news release says about this. The report also contains recommendations to the government for immediate action to improve the use of existing runway capacity. an ‘optimisation strategy’ to improve the operational efficiency of UK airports and airspace, including airport collaborative decision making airspace changes supporting performance based navigation enhanced en-route traffic management to drive tighter adherence to schedules time based separation a package of surface transport improvements to make airports with spare capacity more attractive to airlines and passengers, including the enhancement of Gatwick Airport Station further work to develop a strategy for enhancing Gatwick’s road and rail access work on developing proposals to improve the rail link between London and Stansted work to provide rail access into Heathrow from the south the provision of smart ticketing facilities at airport stations trials at Heathrow of measures to smooth the early morning arrival schedule to minimise stacking and delays and to provide more predictable respite for local people the establishment of an Independent Noise Authority to provide expert and impartial advice about the noise impacts of aviation and to facilitate the delivery of future improvements to airspace operations Here's the interim report (pdf). It's 228 pages long. So, here's a quick summary. • Airport Commission shortlists two plans for an extra runway at Heathrow, and one plan for an extra runway at Gatwick, for 2030. • Boris Johnson's preferred option, an airport in the Thames Estuary, has not been shortlisted because of "too many uncertainties", although the commission has not ruled out the idea for good and further work will be carried out. • The commission also says a further runway will be needed by 2050. Stansted or Birmingham could be in line for this, it says. And here are the main points from the news release. The Airports Commission’s independent review into airport capacity and connectivity in the UK has concluded that there is a need for one net additional runway to be in operation in the south east by 2030. Its analysis also indicates that there is likely to be a demand case for a second additional runway to be operational by 2050. These conclusions are valid across a range of assumptions about future demand growth, and are consistent with the Committee of Climate Change’s advice to government on meeting its legislated climate change targets. The Airports Commission’s interim report published today (17 December 2013) has announced that it will be taking forward for further detailed study proposals for new runways at two locations: Gatwick Airport Gatwick Airport Ltd’s proposal for a new runway to the south of the existing runway Heathrow Airport (two options) Heathrow Airport Ltd’s proposal for one new 3,500m runway to the northwest Heathrow Hub’s proposal to extend the existing northern runway to at least 6,000m, enabling the extended runway to operate as two independent runways. The next phase of its work will see the Commission undertaking a detailed appraisal of the three options identified before a public consultation in autumn next year. The Commission has not shortlisted any of the Thames Estuary options because there are too many uncertainties and challenges surrounding them at this stage. It will undertake further study of the Isle of Grain option in the first half of 2014 and will reach a view later next year on whether that option offers a credible proposal for consideration alongside the other short-listed options. The Commission has not shortlisted proposals for expansion at Stansted or Birmingham, however, there is likely to be a case for considering them as potential options for any second new runway by 2050. In its final report the Commission will set out its recommendations on the process for decision making on additional capacity beyond 2030. Here's the Airports Commission's press notice. The Press Association has snapped this about the report. Extra runways at Heathrow and Gatwick airports are among the options put forward by the government-appointed Airports Commission in its first report today. I can't find it on the Airport Commission's website yet. Sir Howard Davies, who chairs the Airports Commission, is due to publish his interim report at 7am. You will be able to read it on the commission's website. Here's Patrick Wintour's preview story for the Guardian and here's an extract. Sir Howard Davies is due to publish his interim report on Tuesday amid warnings by local MPs of street protests, direct action and MPs resigning from parliament if Heathrow expansion is the favoured option. Davies is due to set out his short-list of options for long term extra capacity, including at Heathrow and Gatwick, but is likely also to keep alive London mayor Boris Johnson's long cherished 5 year plan for a multi-billion hub along the Thames estuary. Johnson recently suggested the estuary airport should be named after Margaret Thatcher ... Cameron promised before the 2010 election "No ifs, no buts, no third runway", and the transport secretary Patrick McCloughlin is expected to make a holding Commons statement at lunchtime after the 7 am release of the report. He will stress that the report is not designed as an elaborate cover to press ahead with Heathrow expansion, the suspicion of many anti-Heathrow campaigners. Labour has yet to resolve its internal differences on the issue. It went into the election supporting a third runway, but Ed Miliband and the former shadow transport secretary Maria Eagle were opponents on environmental grounds. Although this is only an interim report, it will provide a clear guide to what the commission is likely to say when it produces its final report in 2015. This is an announcement with important implications for all the main political parties, as well, of course, for people living in London. Here's the timetable. 7am: Publication of the Airports Commission's interim report. 7.30am: Sir Howard Davies is interviewed on the Today programme. 8.10am: Boris Johnson, the mayor of London, is interviewed on the Today programme. 9.15am: Sir Howard Davies holds a press conference. 12.30pm: Patrick McLoughlin, the transport secretary, is expected to make a statement in the Commons. I will be focusing on this issue today, although I will cover other political news in my summaries. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: David Miliband: no place for unions' old politics

fields.headline: David Miliband: no place for unions' old politics
fields.trailText: Former foreign secretary says brother right to change way unions fund Labour party, as fresh Falkirk allegations emerge
fields.bodyText: There is no place left for "old politics" in the trade unions, David Miliband has said, as he backed his brother's efforts to reform Labour's relationship with its biggest backers. The former foreign secretary said Ed Miliband was doing the right thing as Labour leader by changing the way unions fund the party, as new allegations surfaced about Unite's tactics in the Scottish constituency of Falkirk. Asked about the reforms, David Miliband told the BBC's Andrew Marr Show: "It's so big that Ed was right to say that he wants to reform the relationship with the unions. Because the truth is the leadership of the party has got to represent the whole country. Working people have an important stake, it's important to bring them in, but what you call 'old politics', rightly has no place, obviously." Ed Miliband is facing fresh pressure to publish a report on Unite's actions during the process to select a Labour candidate in the Falkirk byelection. Labour commissioned an investigation into allegations of vote-rigging but concluded that there was no wrongdoing and cleared all Unite officials. Unite has repeatedly denied any wrongdoing. However, the Sunday Times has obtained a Unite document designed to rebut allegations of vote-rigging, which suggests party investigators found "evidence that signatures were forged" and potential new members being allegedly coerced or "badgered" into signing direct debit bank forms for joining Labour. On Sunday, Len McCLuskey, the general secretary of Unite, condemned the "disgraceful and despicable attacks on his union following publication of the allegations. He told the BBC's Sunday Politics that it had already been shown there was no case to answer. "The truth of the matter is this is a track being laid by Tory central office," he said. Speaking on the same programme, Chuka Umunna, the shadow business secretary, said police were now looking at fresh allegations about Falkirk and Labour would look at what to do after that. However, he rejected calls for the report on Falkirk to be published, saying it was not right to ask people to give evidence to investigators in confidence and then reveal their disclosures. A senior Labour source said no one should forget that Miliband was implementing "the biggest single reform in a generation" to the party's relationship with the unions. Last week, the Labour leader was forced to condemn any "intimidatory tactics" used by unions in industrial disputes, after Unite members were accused of personally targeting Ineos bosses during the row over pay and conditions at Grangemouth. Miliband said the same codes of behaviour were true for unions and employers, after the Daily Mail published allegations that family members of Ineos directors were intimidated by union tactics. Unite has defended its right to use "leverage" tactics against companies, and McCluskey has dismissed any suggestion that some of his union's members were involved in bullying. McCluskey said his union believed it was legitimate to confront "faceless directors … who destroy communities". However, the incident has sparked a debate about the use of leverage tactics, which the union said involve asking "all interested parties to make moral and ethical decisions about their future relations with an employer who we believe is acting immorally".

results.webTitle: Modern politics: show business for ugly people | Jonathan Green

fields.headline: Modern politics: show business for ugly people
fields.trailText: Jonathan Green: Our politicians take what we think, feel and fear, and then turn that to their own advantage. Australia's most basic needs shouldn't be so cynically treated – we need and deserve better
fields.bodyText: At some point they refined the art of politics, whittling it down to a nub of cynical ambition couched in something that from the middle distance might pass for belief. Show business for ugly people, that’s the joke. Funny, but also terribly true: the same obsessive drive for attention, the same deluded sense of influence and importance, except that for a politician, unlike your run-of-the-mill Home and Away starlet, the potential to do actual social good – and of course harm – does exist. It’s a confounding and confusing business. Isn’t that the overwhelming impression of these past few years? That we elect people to power whose primary interest is simply succeeding in that democratic transaction and then doing what they must to repeat the experience. If it involves a system of belief at any point, it is a simple expedient that is either adapted to need or abandoned when surplus to the fundamental requirement of obtaining and maintaining power. Perhaps this is the true story of this period, a narrative that might explain how Kevin Rudd and Julia Gillard could come and go, how Tony Abbott might ascend almost without trace to sudden electability. It might be a simple aberration, when by dumb, unfortunate luck we are faced with political choices that put the empty promises of personality politics ahead of the considered and complex policy prescriptions that might be better suited to the unique challenges of our moment. Because it does seem that we are in flux, with an economy hesitating on the brink of a changing and uncertain future in which it needs to do more than ship and quarry, an ecology transforming as it charges toward the brutal inevitability of global warming, and our slender hold on the arable fringes of the Australian continent already challenged by a growing population and by the demands of others from around the world who see the place’s new-world possibilities and would like to share them. Our systems of information and community are moving through epochal change, change with the potential to remake the standing mechanisms of media and politics that have survived the last era of communications technology but will undoubtedly be utterly transformed by the current one. In the midst of all of this, the reaction of our political players seems to have been to shrug at the enormity of these tasks and challenges and focus instead on what might be achieved on the smaller, more containable canvas of pure, unattached politics. They need us for our votes and will twist reality and perception to acquire them, but they look unlikely to give us more of the leadership, thought and challenging vision that should be ours by right in return. The normal laws of political nature – the equation that regards a vote as the reward for a policy position and vision– no longer apply, replaced by the simpler laws of celebrity. Show business for ugly people? It is now, almost to a fault. Our political characters inhabit the closed system of national politics, a world that carries on with a strangely divided attitude to the universe that surrounds it, sometimes in blithe isolation from the greater world and its aspirations, sometimes driven utterly by its obsessive sampling of that public’s true systems of need and belief. They take what we think, feel and fear, and then consider how they might best turn that to their own advantage. It’s hard to say which is the greater scandal: that the legitimate aspirations of voters and the most basic needs of the country should be so cynically treated by the political class, or that we so unthinkingly accept this self-serving reality. It’s circular and presumably ultimately destructive, and our politics is increasingly at a tangent to reality as a result. For the most part, and for most people, politics can be safely ignored as a fundamentally technocratic exercise that will deliver a bipartisan consensus around most of the substantial issues and points of policy that confront us: we want a low-inflation, low-unemployment, market-based but increasingly carbon-free economy, with growing trade and opportunity, secure borders, good schooling and a health system that will not fail us in need. Both sides agree; both will in all likelihood provide outcomes that will deliver pretty much that same set of broad goals, despite each and every party’s insistence that they and they alone have the policy settings to make any or all of it possible. In part this speaks to the amount of influence any Australian government can exert over key economic circumstances, the great tides of the global economy that carry us—hopefully—like a bobbing cork. In part it speaks of the simple necessity of most major policy planks, of the things that just need doing, regardless of political conviction. The result: most of what we hear from all parties is not much advanced beyond statements of the necessary and obvious. There are moments of difference and brilliance, rare gems of provocative forward thinking and political purpose, but too often the vast bulk of politics, certainly the greater part in these past three years, is an almost entirely internal beauty contest staged by and for an inner circle of political, parliamentary and media professionals. Only in this world does the triptych of Rudd, Gillard and Abbott make sense: two leaders disliked by the majority of the population, but favoured thanks to the internal dynamics of party power play; one leader beloved publicly but despised bitterly from within, a man who could only find the necessary internal favour for a last desperate fling by throwing his panicked party some sort of lifebelt. The sum of it is not edifying. It would be fair to say that we are either living through a low point, a moment in which people increasingly are disenchanted and disengaged with a political system that purports to serve them, or, worse than that, through the escalating decay of a political system that is failing to meet even the most basic markers of public policy performance. And there we have the sum of modern politics: on the one hand, good policy diluted through fear of adverse reaction, squandered through a focus not on its practical fundamentals but on its inter-party or intra-party effect, and on the other, simple bad policy, sculpted—think of border security—from fear, apprehension and myth with the entire aim of creating politically useful division. A solution that might heal this division would be to stand strongly for points of policy and principle, to see the political process as the reverse of its modern state: a process aimed more or less at simple re-election. This is not a system driven by belief; it is one that adapts belief reactively to the political circumstance. We need and deserve something better.
