If our increasingly professionalised political contest is doing little to remedy the pressing issues and dilemmas that confront us, how can it be described as anything but a failure? Its purpose must be broader than providing work, interest, power and reward for its small inner circle. What is Australian politics doing, for example, to meet the urgent need for a changed economy that does not drive us to ecological instability and catastrophe? How is it addressing the fundamentally dysfunctional infrastructure of our fast expanding cities, our reliance on systems of transport that simply ingrain congestion and inefficient chaos? How is it going about improving the circumstances of the working poor? What does it offer the increasing and unhappy mass of the mentally ill? How will it house and comfort the homeless? How does it propose to allocate resources for a health system that delivers the full potential of modern methods, science and technology to the greatest number of people, while effectively triaging the modern inclination to overservicing? What is the road map to heal the soul-deep division between white and indigenous Australians? The list goes on ... and as the new world of instant electronic community, communication, expanding knowledge and understanding enfolds us, we are left with a political process that happily rewards three years of assiduous bastardry and disloyalty with power, and that apparently sees no issue with handing that power to another party whose greatest virtue is that publicly they stand for nothing. Why should we be engaged? Why should we not hope for better? Why should we not conclude that our politics– a system that looks among us for the most deluded egotists, people whose true belief it is that they among all of us would be best placed to run an entire country and who then waste that democratic gift on the aggrandisement not of us but of self– why should we not conclude that our politics is well and truly broken? And having reached this realisation, why would we not look among ourselves, and the expanding resources and opportunities of our interconnected age, for the tools to fix it? • This is an edited except from Jonathan Green’s latest book, The Year My Politics Broke.

tags.description: Julia Gillard is a former Australian politician who was country's 27th prime minister and the leader of the Australian Labor party. She was the first woman to hold either position

results.webTitle: Government and Labour reshuffle: Politics live blog

fields.headline: Government and Labour reshuffle: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the government and Labour party reshuffles expected today
fields.bodyText: • Ed Miliband has demoted three of the most prominent "Blairites" left in the shadow cabinet in a reshuffle promoting women and members of the 2010 intake. Liam Byrne and Stephen Twigg have left the shadow cabinet, and Jim Murphy has been given the backwater of international development. The Conservatives depicted these moves (see 4.43pm) as a shift to the left, or a sop to Len McCluskey, but Labour sources have robustly rejected this pointing out that Douglas Alexander, who ran David Miliband's campaign for the Labour leadership, has now been put in charge of the general election. In truth, the generational shift may count for more than the ideological shift. Now around a third of shadow cabinet seats are filled by people who were not even in parliament before the 2010 election. There may also be interesting policy implications. Labour does not have a particularly distinctive position on education, but Tristram Hunt, a clever newcomer, now has the job of finding one. And the removal of Maria Eagle from the transport brief may signal a further weakening of support for HS2 (she was notably more supportive of it than Ed Balls has been), although party sources insist the policy has not changed. • Nick Clegg has provocatively put a radical, liberal, conspiracy theorist, Norman Baker, into the Home Office. It is the most surprising appointment of the day, and it suggests that Clegg wants to adopt a more adversarial approach on civil liberties within the coalition. When Baker joined the government in 2010, there were doubts that someone with such a non-conformist bent could last as a minister. But now he is becoming one of the party's great ministerial survivors. • David Cameron has taken some modest steps towards getting more women into his government. Number 10 does not have any figures yet for what proportion of the government is now female, although a spokesman said that, as a result of today's moves, Cameron was "making progress" towards his goal of having a third of ministers women. From today the government will sound a little more feminine, a little less posh, and a little more northern. But since most of the changes involve junior ministers who are not well known to the public, the impact of this will be relatively modest. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. I'm not sure Tony McNulty, the former Labour MP, is entirely right about this, but he certainly has a point. Here's a short reshuffle reading list. • Benedict Brogan at the Telegraph says that Nick Clegg and Ed Miliband's reshuffles were more interesting than David Cameron's - but that that might not be a good thing for them. Both have produced reshuffles that will keep heads being scratched for some time. For the Lib Dems the good achieved by promoting Alistair Carmichael to defend the Union in Scotland was comprehensively undone by the dismissal of Jeremy Browne and his replacement with the conspiracy theorist Norman Baker at the Home Office. Mr Browne was one of the successes of the Lib Dem end of the Coalition, and an exemplar of the party's seriousness in government. His sacking is baffling, but not nearly as baffling as his replacement by Mr Baker. The MP for Lewes is a media friendly operator who has made a name for himself as an eclectic campaigner. But to put in the Home Office someone who argued with a straight face that David Kelly was the victim if an Iraqi plot that was covered up by the authorities frankly diminishes the office. As for Mr Miliband, there are two elements to the reshuffle that will fascinate and keep the village talking. First, the purge of the remaining Blairites. It does credit to Liam Byrne and Stephen Twigg that they have accepted their demotions with good grace. Ditto Jim Murphy. But it will have been noticed by the modernisers, who will give up looking for evidence that Mr Miliband isn't taking the party on a long march to the left. Alongside that, the appointment of Spencer Livermore as campaigns director, and Douglas Alexander as campaigns coordinator, is equally interesting. It's a big moment for two Labour operators whose common experience is to have fallen foul of Damian McBride, and by implication of Ed Balls. It will be tempting to conclude that their apointment is designed to marginalise the Shadow chancellor or at least keep him well clear of election planning. • Stephen Tall at Lib Dem Voice offers four thoughts on the Lib Dem reshuffle. 1. Nick feels secure enough to be ruthless. Sacking both Michael Moore and Jeremy Browne is not something Nick would have been able to contemplate a year ago. Then – with the economy still mired in recession, his apology video still fresh in the memory, and Vince reminding everyone he stood ready, willing and able should the need arise – Nick was vulnerable, in need of allies. Now – with the economy recovering, Eastleigh defended and all key conference votes won – Nick feels able to asset himself. • Sam Macrory and Anoosh Chakelian at Total Politics have 10 lessons from the reshuffles. • Alex Massie at the Spectator says it has been a day of low drama. And here are two alternate views on the Labour reshuffle. From Eric Pickles, the communities secretary From Luke Akehurst, a Labour blogger Ken Livingstone has been tweeting on the Labour reshuffle. Fiona Syms, whose ex-husband Robert Syms was sacked as a whip today, has taken to Twitter to vent her anger with David Cameron. Alistair Burt, the Conservative Foreign Office minister, has confirmed that he is leaving the government. There have been some warm tributes to him on Twitter, and not just from Conservatives. His opponents like him too. From Ben Bradshaw, a Labour former Foreign Office minister From Tom Price, a former Labour adviser From Tim Farron, the Lib Dem president And the press like him too. And here's the full list of today's appointments from Number 10. There's been a slew of government appointments while I've been tied up with Labour and the briefings. That's Tina Stowell, the Lords whip who took the gay marriage legislation through the Lords. Dan Rogerson is the Lib Dem MP for North Cornwall. The Department of the Environment is becoming a Cornish stronghold Stephen Williams is another Lib Dem MP joining government. It will be interesting to see the proportion of the Lib Dem parliamentary party who have served in government by 2015. Jane Ellison is the Conservative MP for Battersea. That's a sideways move for Anna Soubry, the health minister who spoke out in favour of plain packaging for cigarettes before Number 10 decided (to her embarrassment) to drop the idea. Kris Hopkins is Conservative MP for Keighley in Yorkshire. He's also a former army private. He ticks two boxes for those keen to show that the Tories are not just posh southerners. Karen Bradley was already a whip. She has just been promoted. And Sam Gymiah was Cameron's PPS. Amber Rudd, Claire Perry, and Gavin Barwell. John Penrose was a culture minister until he was sacked last year. After Shailesh Vara (see 3.23pm), he is the second person (I think) to rejoin the government a year after being dropped. Judith Jolly is a Lib Dem peer. Desmond Swayne was already a whip, but now he is a more senior one. Number 10 officials have been briefing on the government reshuffle. It is not quite over, and they haven't yet got a final list to give us. The spokesman played down the suggestion that it was all about promoting women, or northerners, or George Osborne's allies. "It is not about camps and cabals," he said. "It is about giving jobs to people who can do the job well." Most of the questions, though, were about Norman Baker going to the Home Office. Downing Street would not say that Theresa May was consulted (which implies that she was not). The prime minister's spokesman also played down the involvement of Cameron himself. This was a Lib Dem appointment and Nick Clegg was responsible for Lib Dem ministerial appointments, he said. But Cameron was consulted about it, he added. Grant Shapps, the Conservative chairman, has just picked up the point about Len McCluskey. He has sent out this statement. Len McCluskey is the real winner of this reshuffle. Ed Miliband’s purged the moderates and promoted those who want the same old Labour policy of more spending, more borrowing and more debt. That’s exactly what got us into a mess in the first place And it’s hardworking people who would pay the price through higher taxes and higher mortgage rates. If Ed Miliband is too weak to stand up to Len McCluskey, he’s too weak to stand up for hardworking people. I'm off to another briefing. I will post again after 5.30pm. The Labour MP Paul Flynn clearly has not heard the line from Labour HQ about factionalism. (See 4.52pm.) Len McCluskey, the Unite general secretary, should be happy with the Labour reshuffle. In an interview earlier this year, he cited Liam Byrne and Jim Murphy as ministers who should be moved. A Labour spokesman dismissed the suggestion that the reshuffle was a sop to the left. The days of factionalism in the party were over, he said. To be fair, McCluskey also criticised Douglas Alexander. But, in one of the most significant appointments of the day. Alexander has been put in charge of the election Labour has just released details of Ed Miliband's shadow cabinet reshuffle. A spokesman says Miliband is keen to point out that he is promoting talented young women. Here are the details. • Rachel Reeves becomes shadow work and pensions secretary. She was shadow chief secretary to the Treasury. • Gloria De Piero becomes shadow minister for women and equalities. • Emma Reynolds becomes shadow housing minister. She will attend shadow cabinet. She was shadow Europe minister. • Tristram Hunt becomes shadow education secretary. He was a shadow education minister. • Stephen Twigg, the former shadow education secretary, becomes a shadow minister in the justice team, responsible for constitutional affairs. • Chris Leslie becomes shadow chief secretary to the Treasury. He was a shadow Treasury minister. • Vernon Coaker becomes shadow defence secretary. He was shadow Northern Ireland secretary. • Jim Murphy becomes shadow international development secretary. He was shadow defence secretary. • Ivan Lewis becomes shadow Northern Ireland secretary. He was shadow international development secretary. • Maria Eagle becomes shadow environment secretary. She was shadow transport secretary. • Mary Creagh becomes shadow transport secretary. She was shadow environment secretary. • Michael Dugher becomes shadow Cabinet Office minister. He also takes charge of political and campaign communications. • Douglas Alexander, the shadow foreign secretary, also becomes chair of general election strategy. He will be responsible for election strategy and planning. • Spencer Livermore, a former aide to Gordon Brown, has been appointed general election campaign director. He will start work later this year. • Liam Byrne, the former shadow work and pensions secretary, joins the shadow business team, with responsibility for higher education and emerging markets. • Lord Falconer, the former Lord Chancellor, will advise on planning and transition into government. • Wayne David becomes parliamentary private secretary to Ed Miliband. Karen Buck is already his other PPS. A Labour spokesman said that of the 32 people who now attend shadow cabinet, 14 (or 44% are women). And around a third of those attending shadow cabinet are from the 2010 intake. Iain Dale has taken Grant Shapps to task over the reshuffle for hardworking people. (See 3.39pm.) And Shapps has replied. I've got to attend a briefing now. I will post again after 4.30pm. As a minister leaving the government, you can write a letter to the prime minister. Alternatively, you can just post a note on Twitter. This is from Mark Prisk, who has been sacked as housing minister. Here's the Staggers on Tristram Hunt. More from the Labour reshuffle. That's Tristram Hunt, the historian and Stoke-on-Trent MP who was only elected in 2010. Earlier I asked when was the last time there was a minister in the Home Office as liberal/libertarian as Norman Baker. (See 2.43pm.) I've had a very good reply. Alex Lyon was married to Clare Short. There is a short Wikipedia entry on him here. I'd like to think this is a parody ... Earlier I posted Nick Clegg's letter to Jeremy Browne about Browne losing his job as Home Office minister. Here's Browne's reply. Dear Nick, Thank you for your letter. I has been a privilege to serve in a rejuvenated Foreign Office and in a Home Office which has presided over a fall in crime at the same time as achieving necessary budget reductions. I remain supportive of the government, which has given Britain political stability and a credible economic recovery plan. These may be the minimum requirements of a government, but they are essential and deserve not to be taken for granted. I hope the government will continue to strive to be reforming and innovative and avoid the danger of lapsing into transactional trade-offs and deferred decision making. Britain's medium-term prosperity and status is at stake. It is crucial that we continue to reduce the budget deficit, maintain stability in monetary policy, keep our business and personal tax rates internationally competitive, raise school standards, tackle endemic inter-generational welfare dependency, invest in infrastructure and extend personal freedom in both the private realm and in public service provision. I supported your election as party leader and I admired your decision to take our party into government. Easy and permanent opposition may be alluring to some but you are right not to find it seductive. Our party should aspire to be in government, not for its own sake but because our country will benefit hugely from an infusion of authentic liberalism as we seek to remain relevant and successful in a fast-changing world. Yours sincerely, Jeremy Browne My colleague Nicholas Watt has filed a story on how George Osborne's allies are prospering in the reshuffle. Back to the Conservative reshuffle. Shailesh Vara was a whip until he was sacked in last year's reshuffle. After Sajid Javid, he is the second ethnic minority Conserative to gain a promotion today. George Eustice used to be Cameron's press secretary before he became an MP. His parents run a fruit farm in Cornwall, and so he should feel at home in the environment department. Robert Goodwill is MP for Scarborough and Whitby, so he ticks the "northern" box. Until today he was a whip. Here's more from the Labour reshuffle. Here's the text of Nick Clegg's letter to Jeremy Browne, who has been sacked as a Home Office minister. Clegg tells Browne that he wants as many Lib Dem MPs as possible to have a chance at being a minister. Dear Jeremy I want to thank you for the key role you have played in government over the past three years, first as Minister of State at the Foreign Office and latterly as Minister of State in the Home Office. You have made a hugely valuable political contribution to the coalition over the past three years both as a highly able representative of the UK to other nations and more recently dealing with the many domestic challenges that face the Home Office. It is always very difficult to move colleagues out of government but as you know, I have always been keen that we provide the opportunity for as many in our ranks as possible to contribute their skills to ministerial office during this Parliament so that, just as the government has benefited from your contribution over the past three years, it can also gain from those of other colleagues in the remaining years of this parliament. I am immensely grateful to you for your commitment and support over the past few years. You have made a major contribution to this historic coalition government and as one of the very few ministers who have served in two departments, I have no doubt there will be an opportunity for your experience to be deployed in government in the future. Yours sincerely Nick Clegg And more from Labour. Sky has some news from Labour. And Downing Street has confirmed that Norman Baker. That will be interesting. Baker is a serious libertarian. He's probably the most liberal minister to serve in the Home Office since ... well, I've no idea. (I'll consult Twitter.) Before he became a minister Baker also wrote a book suggesting David Kelly was murdered. If he has to have any dealings with MI5 in his new job, those conversations should be interesting. Another Conservative move. Hugh Robertson was sports minister. Presumably he will be replacing either David Lidington or Hugo Swire, the two ministers of state in the Foreign Office from the Commons. More news from Number 10. That's a sideways move for Helen Grant, who until today was justice minister. That's Susan Kramer, who was once the Lib Dem candidate for London mayor. She is joining the government for the first time. She is effectively taking Norman Baker's Lib Dem slot at transport, although Baker was only a parliamentary under-secretary. As minister of state she will be more senior. Earlier I included a tweet from Patrick Wintour saying Dominic Cummings, one of Michael Gove's most important advisers, is leaving the Department for Education. (See 11.26am.) Patrick has now filed a story with more detail. Matthew Hancock, the business minister, has been promoted. He was just a parliamentary under-secretary at the Department for Business, but not he is a minister of state. But he seems to be keeping the same portfolio (skills). The Chartered Institute for Housing has issued a tribute to Mark Prisk, who has reportedly left his post as housing minister. This is from Graina Long, its chief executive. Mark Prisk's tenure as housing minister has been marked by a commendable focus on delivery. Although progress on numbers of new homes has been disappointingly slow it is clear that the minister has been working hard to explore different delivery options and taking a personal interest in work to unlock stalled sites. He also brought a good deal of personal integrity to the role and a careful and thoughtful approach informed by a real understanding of the housing system and housing markets reflecting his background as a property professional. His personal style was highly non-partisan demonstrating a willingness to search for solutions rather than a desire to play politics with something as important to peoples’ lives as housing. • Michael Moore, the Lib Dem Scottish secretary, has been sacked in a government reshuffle currently underway. He will be the only cabinet minister to be moved. Other changes affect the junior ranks of the government. Moore has been replaced by Alistair Carmichael, the Lib Dem chief whip. Moore said he was "disappointed". I’m disappointed to be leaving office right now, but I’m very pleased at what I’ve been able to achieve in the last couple of years, particularly the constitutional debate with the Scotland Act and the Edinburgh Agreement. But this big decision that we’re taking as a country is bigger than one individual, bigger than one party, and I look forward to continuing to play a really big role in the constitutional debate over the course of the next 12 months. • David Cameron has been seeking to refresh the image of his party by promoting women, non-southerners and people with modest backgrounds. Those who have benefited so far include: Esther McVey (a woman, obviously, and an MP from the Wirral, who has moved up at DWP from disabilities minister to employment minister); Sajid Javid (the Lancashire-born son of a bus driver, who has moved up at the Treasury from economic secretary to financial secretary); Nicky Morgan (the MP for Loughborough in the Midlands who is moving from the whips office to the Treasury); Mike Penning (the former fireman who has become a minister of state at the DWP, having held the same rank at the more obscure Northern Ireland Office); and Greg Clark (the Middlesbrough-born son of a milkman, who moves sides from the Treasury to the Cabinet Office but who will now attend cabinet). Other people who have been promoted include Greg Hands, who moves up in the whips office to become deputy chief whip. • Theresa May, the home secretary, has welcomed the promotion of women. I’m very pleased to see women being promoted. I think it is important that we get that mixture across ministerial positions. And of course we had a step change in the number of women on the Conservative benches in the House of Commons as a result of the general election in 2010, and I’m very pleased to say a number of those women have been getting very good experience and have been coming through the ranks. • A string of middle-ranking ministers are leaving government. Simon Burns (the transport minister), John Randall, (the deputy chief whip) and Chloe Smith (the Cabinet Office minister) announced their departure before today. So far Richard Benyon (the fisheries minister) and Mark Hoban (the employment minister) have confirmed today they are leaving the government. Mark Prisk, the housing minister, is also reported to be leaving. Smith has explained that it was her decision to leave government. This is entirely my decision, it’s a very positive one for me because it allows me to focus on the things that are most important to me, which is my work for the constituency and work that I can do to also promote the cause of young people. • Don Foster has become Lib Dem deputy chief whip. He was a communities minister. In other Lib Dem moves, it is also being reported that Norman Baker will replace Jeremy Browne at the Home Office. • Ed Miliband has begun a reshuffle of his frontbench. But he is keeping the details to himself for the moment. Another Conservative move. Jeremy Browne, the Lib Dem minister, is out of the Home Office, apparently. Mark Prisk has been sacked as housing minister, Guido Fawkes claims. The new employment minister, Esther McVey, replaces Mark Hoban, who has been sacked. Guido Fawkes says this runs counter to the supposed theme of the reshuffle. More on Sajid Javid's promotion. This is from the FT's Beth Rigby. There is now a female minister back in the Treasury team. Nicky Morgan was a whip. More on Greg Clark's move. So this is being billed as a promotion. But being in charge of the constitution at the Cabinet Office, when the government's constitutional reform programme has virtually evaporated, is not a move to boast about. Apparently they have had to summon an ambulance to Number 10. The new appointments are coming thick and fast now. Mike Penning, the Northern Ireland minister, has been promoted. (See 12.05pm.) He was a minister of state at the Northern Ireland Office, but having the same rank at the Department for Work and Pensions counts as a bigger job. Esther McVey, the minister for the disabled, has been promoted. She is now employment minister. Here's the story my colleague Severin Carrell has filed on Alistair Carmichael replacing Michael Moore. The SNP's Angus Robertson points out that Carmichael once said the Scotland Office should be abolished. And an SNP adviser, Elizabeth Lloyd, says she thought Jo Swinson was supposed to get that job. The Lib Dem MEP Chris Davies has paid tribute to the outgoing fisheries minister, Richard Benyon. Davies founded the "Fish for the future" group in the European parliament. Greg Hands used to be George Osborne's parliamentary private secretary (PPS). Osborne has a reputation for being particularly good at looking after his political supporters. More on Michael Moore. Nicola Sturgeon, Scotland's deputy first minister, sends her best wishes. My colleague Severin Carrell points out that Sturgeon was the Scottish government's main negotiator over the Edinburgh agreement, which transferred temporary legal power to hold the independence referendum to Holyrood last year. She is also widely seen as having bested Moore in an STV debate on independence earlier this year. As I mentioned earlier (see 10.11am) having good relations with the SNP seems to have been Moore's undoing. And Magnus Linklater, the former Scotsman editor, thinks dropping Moore will be a disaster. My colleague Nicholas Watt predicts that Michael Penning, the Northern Ireland minister (and the only former fireman in government, I think) will get a promotion. Richard Benyon has confirmed his departure on Twitter. PoliticsHome's Paul Waugh has the name of another reshuffle casualty. That's Richard Benyon, the fisheries minister, who is going. More speculation on the Labour reshuffle. If so, that seems a bit harsh for standing up to Ed Balls over HS2. At this moment it might be worth recalling what Jonathan Powell wrote about the way junior ministers are reshuffled in his memoir about working for Tony Blair, The New Machiavelli. The appointment of junior ministers is a mass production exercise. "Switch" [the Downing Street switchboard] gets five or six about-to-be-ministers stacked up on the phone, and then they are put through to the prime minister one after another. The private secretary's job is to stick the right bit of paper in front of the prime minister to make sure that he appoints the right person to the right job. There were frequent shouts from the den asking what on earth the job was that the next caller was supposed to be doing. And here's Willie Rennie, the Scottish Lib Dem leader, on the sacking of Michael Moore and his replacement by Alistair Carmichael. Michael Moore is leaving on a high after three very good years as Scottish Secretary. He persuaded the nationalist Government to approve the Scotland Act they previously condemned as a poison pill. Against the odds he secured an agreement with the nationalists on the conduct of the referendum. Michael Moore repeatedly outwitted Alex Salmond. As we move to the next stage of the campaign I am looking forward to working with Alistair Carmichael. His feisty style combined with his charm, wit and intelligence is just what we need for the last twelve months in our efforts to safeguard our partnership with the rest of the United Kingdom. And here's some Twitter comment on the Alistair Carmichael appointment from Scottish journalists. From James Maxwell From Magus Linklater, the former Scotsman editor For a clue as to how Alistair Carmichael, the new Scottish secretary, may adopt a more aggressive tone towards Alex Salmond, just take a look at this press statement he put out last month (about a comment by Salmond). Carmichael manages to include the words "narrow", "cheap", "nonsensical", "ludicrous" and "offensive" all into the same release. My colleague Patrick Wintour has some good reshuffle intelligence. Women are expected to do well in today's government reshuffle. To help to explain why, it's worth quoting what Yvette Cooper, the shadow home secretary (at least, for now - the Labour reshuffle is definitely going ahead too, so that may have changed by the end of the day) and shadow equalities minister said in her speech to the Labour women's conference. David Cameron seems surprised that he has such a problem with women. Yet look at his policies, and look at his approach No women in any of the top economic posts: no women Treasury Ministers, no women on the Monetary Policy Committee. In David Cameron’s last reshuffle he sacked 0% of the men and 60% of the women in his cabinet – and there are fewer women in the cabinet as a result. Sir George Young, the chief whip, has just left Downing Street. More Twitter comment on the reshuffle. Here's some Twitter comment on the reshuffle. (It may well have been a journalist.) Here's Nick Clegg's letter to Michael Moore (sacking him). Clegg says that he expects Moore to serve in government again in the future. (But this sounds like a reference to a possible coalition government after 2015, which may not be a great consolation to Moore.) Dear Mike I want to thank you for the vital role you have played as Secretary of State for Scotland over the past three years. You became Scottish Secretary in 2010 at a critical time in Scotland's relationship with the rest of the United Kingdom and you have managed the challenges of the situation with great skill and effectiveness. Not only have you successfully piloted through legislation to enable Scotland to take a major step towards the party's long held goal of 'Home Rule'. but you have also ensured that the referendum next year will give the Scottish people a clear and decisive question on which to cast their vote. It should be recognised that you secured both the Scotland Act and the Edinburgh Agreement in the context of a majority SNP government at Holyrood, and against a backdrop of an external political narrative that often suggested the legislation would fail and a referendum agreement could not be secured. You have achieved all of this while working ceaselessly for the interests of the Scottish people within the United Kingdom. As we discussed when we spoke on Friday, I believe we now need to draw on different experience in the final year running up to the referendum itself and I am keen that just as we have benefited from your formidable skills over the past three years that we take advantage of other experience within our ranks during this period. I am immensely grateful for all the work you have done at the Scotland Office and for the very significant contribution you have made to the first coalition government in 70 years. I have no doubt that there will be an opportunity for your talents to be deployed in government in the future. Yours sincerely, Nick Clegg And here's Moore's reply. Dear Nick Thank you for your letter. I am grateful to have had the opportunity to be part of the cabinet. Putting Liberal Democrat policies into practice in government has been our party's ambition for a very long time and I have valued the chance to play a key role in it. This has been, and will continue to be, a hugely important time in Scottish politics and that has made it a challenging and rewarding time to be Secretary of State for Scotland. Taking the Scotland Act through Parliament and negotiating the Edinburgh Agreement have been the highlights of my time in office, as well as, more recently, making the case for Scotland to stay part of the United Kingdom. However, I have also valued the chance to get out and about around Scotland week after week to listen to the challenges facing people and make sure those are understood by colleagues elsewhere in government. I am glad that there are now early signs of economic recovery, but we must not lose sight of the huge difficulties many people still face. Over the last few years I have worked with a superb team of civil servants and advisers in the Scotland Office, and other government departments, in very challenging circumstances. I do not think the support teams for ministers always get the credit they deserve: I am very grateful for the support they have given me. In leaving the Scotland Office I am pleased that Alistair will be succeeding me. As a good friend and long time colleague, I believe he will do a superb job. I wish him all the best. Yours sincerely, Michael Moore The SNP's Angus Robertson has a kind word for Michael Moore. (I suppose this might help to explain why Moore was sacked. See 10.06am.) David Cameron has been tweeting about the reshuffle. Alistair Carmichael, the new Lib Dem Scottish secretary, has told the BBC that he is up for a fight with Alex Salmond ahead of the referendum on Scottish independence next year. Apparently Michael Moore, the outgoing Scottish secretary, was sacked because it was felt that Carmichael would be a better campaigner in the referendum campaign. Last night Downing Street revealed that John Randall, the Conservative deputy chief whip, and Chloe Smith, the Cabinet Office minister,had resigned. Here's an extract from the letter Randall wrote to David Cameron offering his resignation. I have nothing but the deepest admiration for you as a person, leader and prime minister. I will never forget the kind note that you wrote to me when my mother died last year. You can be assured that I will do whatever I can for you personally as well as for the party, the Government and of course the country. In his reply, Cameron said he "could not have wished for a more loyal, discreet, patient, trustworthy and committed colleague" and that he "had rather hoped this day would never come". You have been a rock, not just in the whips office since 2000 where you have served with great distinction as assistant and then deputy chief whip, but for the whole parliamentary party. Your wit and humour are well known across the party, but so too is your compassion; your dedication to the party and to parliament; and your steadfast reliability in good times and bad. And here's an extract from the resignation letter than Smith wrote. I have been privileged to serve my country and my party under your leadership. However, for the remainder of the parliament I want to be able to spend more time serving my constituents. My constituents have always come first for me, and they know the high standards and hard work that I ask of myself as the member of parliament for Norwich North. As you know, I was only 27 when I was lucky enough to be elected as an MP, and of my four years in Parliament so far, I have spent three as a minister. I would welcome the opportunity to develop other ways of giving public service, both inside and outside parliament, while continuing to work hard for my constituents. Smith won her seat, Norwich North, from Labour in a byelection and in 2010 she had a majority of just 3,901. She may have a good reason for wanting to focus on her constituency. According to the BBC, the replacement of Michael Moore will be the only cabinet-level move in the reshuffle. Here's a reading list with some of the most interesting speculation/comment published ahead of the reshuffle. Conservatives • Paul Goodman at ConservativeHome earlier this year said women would be promoted in the reshuffle. I suspect that the proportion of women on Cameron's Commons front bench will be larger than it is now once the reshuffle has taken place. The Prime Minister would do well to stick with rather than sack the women he has in Cabinet already. But if there were vacancies, the women Ministers below Cabinet level are: Helen Grant, Esther McVey, Chloe Smith, Anna Soubry and Elizabeth Truss. Cameron will have an eye to regional balance as well as political outlook if he is looking to promote one or more of them. McVey and Truss probably top the list. McVey is good at presenting, is handling a difficult brief deftly (she is Minister for Disabled People at a time of spending retrenchment), and, uniquely, is a Tory woman Minister from the most crucial swing area of all – the north-west. Truss may have lost a battle over childcare policy with Nick Clegg, but she hasn't lost her standing in government: she fronted the case for Michael Gove's GCSE changes last week. Soubry may be promoted if Cameron and the whips want to please the Party's left and like the way she fires from the hip. • The Mail on Sunday predicted that the Treasury minister Sajid Javid would be promoted to near "the door of the cabinet" in the reshuffle. • The Sunday Mirror suggested that Kenneth Clarke, the veteran minister without portfolio, will be sacked. • The Independent on Sunday said David Cameron would promote women. While one or two cabinet ministers may see their jobs change, the main shake-up, expected tomorrow, will be in the junior ministerial ranks, with Liz Truss, Anna Soubry and Helen Grant tipped for promotion, along with Jessica Lee, a relatively unknown MP who is parliamentary aide to Dominic Grieve. The Prime Minister was heavily criticised a year ago for a reshuffle which saw the number of women in the Cabinet decrease from five to four. There were rumours in Whitehall this weekend that Transport Secretary Patrick McLoughlin will be moved from his post – although not out of the Cabinet altogether – after complaints that the case for HS2 has not been made clear enough to voters. Mr Cameron and George Osborne are considering giving the job to someone who can better argue the case in the media and in Parliament. • George Parker in the Financial Times (subscription) says Tory MPs who are female and northern are particularly likely to do well. The Tory reshuffle is not expected to extend to the cabinet but those tipped for promotion include Liz Truss, children’s minister; Amber Rudd, George Osborne’s parliamentary aide; and Claire Perry, another close ally of the chancellor. Those few Tory MPs who happen to be women and northerners are likely to be especially blessed: Jane Ellison, a Yorkshire-born former manager at John Lewis, and Esther McVey, a Liverpudlian former television presenter and businesswoman, are well-regarded in Number 10. • James Forsyth at Coffee House says Chloe Smith wanted to stand down to concentrate on holding her seat. Chloe Smith quitting is going to tempt everyone to dust off the footage of that Paxman interview with her. But, I hear, that Smith’s desire to protect her own marginal seat was more important to the decision. There was actually a sense that Smith had recovered relatively well from that disastrous interview. So, Number 10 was fairly surprised when she wrote to the Prime Minister last month saying she wanted to step down. Tomorrow, we’ll hear who has been promoted. It is expected to be a good day for women and northern MPs. As I say in the Mail on Sunday, Esther McVey is tipped for a second successive promotion. Amber Rudd, George Osborne’s PPS, is another who’d be well advised to make sure her phone is fully charged tomorrow. While the straight talking, Cameron loyalist Kris Hopkins is almost expected to do well. The promotion of this Yorkshire MP and former army private would help counteract the idea that the Tories are just a posh, southern party. Labour • The Daily Mirror suggested that Rachel Reeves could become the new shadow work and pensions secretary and Tristram Hunt the new shadow education secretary. • LabourList says two Labour shadow ministers, Robert Flello and Anne Maguire, have already stepped down. There is definitely a Lib Dem reshuffle happening today. According to the BBC, the Lib Dem Scottish secretary, Michael Moore, is being replaced by the Lib Dem chief whip, Alistair Carmichael. These reshuffles, like pregnancy, dentistry and exams were further proof that pain has no memory. I don't know how many we have done now, but until a new one starts, you forget how awful the process is. That's Alastair Campbell, writing in his diary on in May 2002. With two reshuffles expected today, that's a good place to start. Neither Downing Street nor the Labour party have formally confirmed that they are reshuffling today, and so there's a possibility that we could get to 6pm and with the headline I've put on this blog looking premature (like all those headlines about the "pregnant" panda in Edinburgh zoo - whatever happened to her). But Labour sources are saying that their reshuffle will be over before the Commons returns from its recess which suggests that, unless Ed Miliband wants to wrap it all up before lunch tomorrow, he's going to go today. And in some respects the government one has already started, because, as Rowena Mason reports on today's Guardian, three ministers have already resigned. David Cameron's long-serving deputy whip, John Randall, and his youngest minister, Chloe Smith, have resigned ahead of a reshuffle in which the prime minister is expected to promote a number of women. Downing Street's surprise announcements sparked speculation that Cameron will shake up junior roles within his team as early as Monday. They follow the resignation of transport minister Simon Burns, who wants to stand as deputy Commons speaker, with rising stars such as Sajid Javid, Esther McVey and Nicky Morgan tipped for bigger jobs. Randall, who has served in the Tory whips' office for 13 years, said he informed the prime minister last year that he wanted to return to the backbenches. He is best known for his role in the resignation of his boss, Andrew Mitchell, the former chief whip, over the "plebgate" scandal just over a year ago. And even if we don't get all the names today, there's not much point blogging about anything else, because the two reshuffles may the the only things people are talking about at Westminster today. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary at about 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 11 October

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 11 October
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

results.webTitle: Jack Straw: a life in politics – in pictures

fields.headline: Jack Straw: a life in politics – in pictures
fields.trailText: A look back at the Labour politician's career, which included stints as foreign secretary and home secretary

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened - Canberra reacts to Indonesia crisis

fields.headline: Politics as it happened - Canberra reacts to Indonesia crisis
fields.trailText: Australia's relationship with Indonesia sinks with ambassador recalled as fallout continues over spying allegations. Follow all the latest with politics live
fields.bodyText: It has been a weird day down this end of the press gallery. It has been characterised by the fallout from the Guardian/ABC's Indonesia allegations, scrambled by Senate estimates committees, party room meetings in the Coalition and caucus, Clive Palmer, Christine Milne, Bob Katter and capped off by Kamahl. It is not too much of a stretch to say your correspondent needed Five Eyes to keep on top of it. But let's be methodical. The Indonesian president spoke out for the first time on allegations that his phone and that of his wife had been tapped by Australian intelligence agencies. SBY said Tony Abbott responded too "lightly" and promptly recalled the Indonesian ambassador. Tony Abbott said the government always acted in the national interest and "others" should not expect anything else. He again underlined the importance of the Australian Indonesian relationship. Finance minister Mathias Cormann and former finance minister Penny Wong sparred over who was guilty of the biggest job cuts in the public service. Notwithstanding the Coalition commitment to cut 12,000 through natural attrition, the government will refer the whole lot to the Commission of Audit for advice. Clive Palmer urged Treasurer Joe Hockey not to allow Archer Daniels Midland to takeover GrainCorp and Labor members expressed their concerns about the sale in the party room, even though shadow treasurer Chris Bowen has supported the sale. Operation Sovereign Borders chief Angus Campbell revealed that he had come up with the media strategy of weekly briefings, rather than immigration minister Scott Morrison. Immigration officials also confirmed that the term "illegals" had been instituted across the department after notification by Morrison. Until tomorrow, good night. Meanwhile in Senate estimates, Martin Bowles, immigration department secretary is very nervous about talking about individual cases of young children. We operate in the best interests of all the people in the detention system. I know it makes no sense at all but just so you know, the entertainer Kamahl, has visited Parliament to recite Abraham Lincoln's Gettysburg address to mark its 150th anniversary. Why? Not sure but Barnaby Joyce was there. Immigration officials confirm there are eight pregnant women on Nauru among asylum seekers. Martin Bowles, immigration department secretary is saying generally the policy is not to separate families but it depends on the circumstances.. Campbell has confirmed OSB is not trying to purchase fishing boats "at this time". He defines an "operational matter" for the benefit of Senator Kim Carr. Those issues that might prejudice the conduct of current and future operations. That is, anything that: Provides an advantage to people smugglers Provides information to allow the manipulation of passengers. Has the potential to damage bilateral relations Compromises the safety of our people. Martin Bowles, secretary of immigration, says the PNG solution has been "effective" and it has been "part of the the solution" which has seen a drop in numbers of asylum seeker arrivals. Parliament has moved into hyperdrive for the afternoon.... A division to gag the debate tomorrow (Wednesday) afternoon on the Mineral Resource Rent Tax repeal bills has been won by the government 80 - 54. In Senate, Angus Campbell is telling Greens senator Hanson-Young that he, not his minister Scott Morrison, came up with the media strategy which sees the careful control of information on arrivals. Chairman of the National Broadband Network Ziggy Switkowski is answering questions from Labor's Kate Lundy on the limitations (or not) of the Telstra copper system. Oh and Kamahl is in the building. Carr asks Angus Campbell how he feels about being involved in the political process? Campbell: I am not involved in the political process. My job is to provide confidential advice to the government of the day and to implement those policies of the government. General Campbell used the example of the recent Canadian mounted police operation to break a global pedophile ring. That was not reported on an incidental basis, says Campbell. In estimates, Senator Kim Carr asks the head of Operation Sovereign Borders, Angus Campbell, about an alleged incident in which a customs boat sought to tow a vessel and ripped the bow out of the vessel last Friday night. Labor also raised the incident in question time. Campbell: I would respectfully offer that it is not appropriate to be giving incidental advice of arrivals or on-water. Carr: The sinking of a vessel by an Australian vessel, the rescuing of 40 asylum seekers, could be considered a serious incident. Campbell says he makes recommendations to minister Scott Morrison about revealing information on serious incidents. There is a sound basis for not discussing events that occur on water. A series of tweets by the Indonesian president in English earlier today. Lieutenant-General Angus Campbell, head of the government's Operation Sovereign Borders, is up in front of the Senate estimates committee. He is explaining his information protocols. Katharine Murphy was watching the ABC managing director Mark Scott in the Senate estimates as he was questioned about the publication of revelations which exposed Australia’s attempts to spy on Indonesia. Scott faced questions during a senate estimates hearing in Canberra on Tuesday which required him to explain why the national broadcaster allowed the publication of documents with a top secret security classification. Scott was asked by the Liberal senator Anne Ruston whether any money had changed hands concerning the stories that were published simultaneously by both outlets on Monday. “No, certainly not,” he said. He said there was nothing unusual in the ABC collaborating with other media outlets on investigations - and he pointed to the Guardian’s policy of collaborating with other respected media partners around the world on the Snowden revelations. Joint ventures for the ABC, Scott said, were “not atypical.” Scott said Guardian Australia had supplied the documents to the ABC, and the broadcaster had embarked on an independent investigation. The ABC had sought legal advice, the stories were reported independently, and were published as soon as practicable, he said. He suggested the ABC was able to offer Guardian Australia reporting depth and a broadcast platform. Labor is trying to pin down the assistant education minister Sussan Ley on benefits around childcare, ahead of the findings of the Productivity Commission. Bill Shorten attempts to elicit a promise from Abbott not to cut childcare payments over the next term of government. We will fully honour our election commitments, says Abbott. Question time is over and the Opposition shadow Kate Ellis is now speaking on childcare as a Matter of Public Importance. Re my earlier comment on the plural of booby, thanks to Brian Dodd. Communications minister Malcolm Turnbull is in full flight, taking the mickey out of the National Broadband Network. Turnbull describes the visits to the Northern Territory by Labor members, including the member for chiefly Ed Husic and the former minister Stephen Conroy. Turnbull says Husic employed his "powerful charisma" to switch on the NBN yet in that territory, only 322 houses are capable of getting a broadband service and only five have connected. Conroy went to the Northern Territory more often than there are active connections. The Northern Territory News should have the number of connections on the front page. They like UFOs and crocodiles. What is a bigger crock than connections to the NBN. At it's current rate, it is about $2m per connection. The Prime Minister is asked by Chris Bowen about the special dividend of $8.8bn given to the Reserve Bank. Abbott says the government topped up the capital of the Reserve Bank because capital had been stripped by the former government. Morrison is talking about his visit to an asylum seeker family in Townsville who "came the right way", waiting in a UN camp "for decades". They have established a home and educated their kids. Morrison is making the point that of the Coalition's 13,750 places under the special humanitarian program - not one will go to those "who arrive illegally by boat". Labor, he says, increased the humanitarian program to 20,000, of which 7000 were going to be protection visas for those who came by boat. Those who come the right way can expect the generous spirit and heart of the Australian people. Shorten asks the prime minister whether he stood buy the asylum seeker boat buy back scheme? Abbott says he stands by all election commitments. Education minister Christopher Pyne is talking about the public service job cuts. This was one of the booby traps left by the boobies opposite for the government. Spelling note: unsure of the plural of booby. The government's not-so-secret weapon, Joe "hasta la vista" Hockey, has been unleashed against Labor on the public service job cuts. The Coalition has promised 12,000 job cuts but say Labor's previous cuts were causing 14,500 cuts. This is about nailing the former Labor government for redundancies as a result of their efficiency dividend. The Coalition is getting the Commission of Audit to look at the matter. The PM's office has released the full statement made by Tony Abbott on Indonesia at the start of question time. In the past 24 hours there have been calls for Australia to detail our intelligence operations and to apologise for them. Madam Speaker, the first duty of every government is to protect the country and to advance its national interests. That’s why every government gathers information and why every government knows that every other government gathers information. Madam Speaker, there is no greater responsibility for a prime minister than ensuring the safety of Australian citizens and the security our borders and that, indeed, is why we do collect intelligence. National security, Madam Speaker, requires a consistent determination to do what’s best for Australia and that’s why this government will support the national security decisions of previous ones as we will expect future governments to respect ours. Madam Speaker, Australia should not be expected to apologise for the steps we take to protect our country now or in the past, any more than other governments should be expected to apologise for the similar steps that they have taken. Importantly, in Australia’s case, we use all our resources, including information, to help our friends and allies, not to harm them. Similarly, Madam Speaker, Australia shouldn’t be expected to detail what we do to protect our country any more than other governments should be expected to detail what they do to protect theirs. Others should ask of us no more than they are prepared to do themselves. Madam Speaker, I want to make it absolutely crystal clear that Australia has deep respect for Indonesia, for its government and for its people. I regard President Yudhoyono as a good friend of Australia, indeed as one of the very best friends that we have anywhere in the world. That’s why, Madam Speaker, I sincerely regret any embarrassment that recent media reports have caused him. But Madam Speaker, it is in everyone’s interests – Indonesia’s no less than Australia’s – that cool heads prevail and that our relationship grows closer, not more distant. I pledge myself to build the strongest possible relationship with Indonesia. After all, due to its size, proximity and potential to be an emerging democratic superpower of Asia, it is the most important single relationship that we have. Speaker Bishop mistakenly asks the member for Hunter "Joel" Fitzgibbon to sit down. "What's going on here?" jokes Treasurer Joe Hockey, regarding the special relationship between Fitzgibbon and the Speaker, who is supposed to refer to his title: the member for Hunter. General jocularity erupts. Warren Truss gets his turn to answer a question on the evils of the carbon tax. Marles asking Morrison about the effectiveness of the Iranian visa change in Indonesia and can he assure the House it will continue. I blinked and missed the answer as the minister sat down immediately. Independent Bob Katter asks how Abbott would feel if he and his wife's phones were tapped. Katter says "surely an apology is necessary" to the Indonesian president. Abbott says he is not going to support every aspect of previous government's foreign policy, saying he deplores the Oceanic Viking stand off and live export ban. But he is not going to be critical in respect of intelligence gathering. The prime minister says he does not believe Australia should be expected to apologise for "reasonable intelligence gathering operations". Scott Morrison in answer to Marles: The government works cooperatively with Indonesia (to stop people smuggling) in all measures. Tony Abbott earlier: Australia should not be expected to apologise for the steps we take to protect our country now or in the past anymore than other governments should be expected to apologise for the similar steps that they have taken. Importantly in Australia's case, we use all our resources including information to help our friends and allies and not to harm them. Australia shouldn't be expected to detail what we do to protect our country any more than other countries should be expected to detail what they do to protect theirs. Others should ask of us no more than they are prepared to do themselves. Richard Marles to Scott Morrison: were all those on board (towed boat) rescued, where were they taken and where are they now? Morrison: I've answered this question, I know the Opposition would like us to hand over the pin number to Operation Sovereign Borders. Labor is championing the interests of people smugglers, says Morrison. Labor's Tony Burke has asked for the imputation to be withdrawn. Morrison withdraws. More from Tony Abbott earlier at the start of question time on Indonesia: National security requires a consistent determination to do what's best for Australia and that's why this government will support the decisions of previous ones and expects future governments to respect ours. Dixer to Tony Abbott on the advantages to Australia of scrapping the carbon tax. Question one to Scott Morrison from Richard Marles who asks him to confirm last Friday a customs vessel attempted to tow an asylum seeker boat and it "broke up". Morrison says the government will not be supporting the Opposition's campaign for the the people smugglers right to know. More from Tony Abbott: The first duty of every government is to protect the country and advance its interests, that's why every government gathers information and why every government knows every other government gathers information. There is no greater responsibility for a prime minister than ensuring the safety of Australian citizens and the security of our borders and that indeed is why we do collect intelligence. At the start of question time, Tony Abbott has expressed his sincere regret for any embarrassment caused to Indonesia over spying allegations. He says it is the government's first duty to act in the national interest and says it's important for "cool heads to prevail". Abbott says the Australian government should not be expected to detail what it does to protect Australian interests and "others" should not expect them to. Bill Shorten says no one side of politics owns the relationship with Indonesia and calls on the government "not to allow this matter to fester". ABC managing director Mark Scott is before Senate estimates, saying he put Indonesian spying allegations to air as soon as practical. Regarding the partnership with The Guardian, Scott says the ABC has worked with Fairfax and News Corp on stories in the past. SA Liberal Senator Anne Ruston asked: is it reasonable to publish something with Top Secret written on it? Scott says the story was in the public interest and goes to the nature and extent of how intelligence gathering activities are undertaken and how material is shared. He says the discussion is similar to debates in the US after Edward Snowden released the documents. Scott made the comparison with Wheat Board scandal which also attracted criticism that the story was against the national interest. The Coalition party room has also met this morning. The prime minister told his troops it had been a good two weeks for the new government - getting on with business and "intelligently responding to the exingencies of the day." (A spokesman later clarified that the exigencies of the day included, in Abbott's account, the small matter of Indonesia.) Labor by contrast, Abbott reported, was consumed with self referential nonsense - determining who was the worst prime minister - Kevin Rudd or Julia Gillard - a "doleful duo." (Just for those interested, synonyms for doleful include wretched, forlorn, woebegone.) The prime minister reminded his marginal seats holders their first duty was to their electorate. He also praised colleagues, and further, encouraged them, to engage with ethnic communities. People needed to feel part of the family, Abbott reasoned. A number of colleagues took the opportunity of the meeting to praise Abbott for his deft handling of the issue of human rights whilst in Sri Lanka recently. The prime minister, by way of reply, noted he was not one of those politicans inclined to lecture neighbours on human rights; and he had no interest in taking sides in foreign conflicts. Abbott made a passing allusion to the spirit of Cardinal Moran - but if I go there in detail, it wil require a large digression that I won't quite pull off given the proximity of Question Time. Back to the questions. Liberal Andrew Nikolic made a big pitch for a defence centre at Scottsdale to deal with not only defence provisions but also food aid in the event of natural disasters. This, apparently, went down well. There was a negative reference to a looming documentary from John Pilger (described in the briefing as "that awful John Pilger") that would apparently make a negative contribution to the cause of constitutional recognition of Indigenous Australians. There was concern about the housing crisis in Sydney that was triggered by green regulations locking up land. And GrainCorp got a brief mention: one MP noted that lazy journalists were framing the whole transaction as Liberals versus Nationals. Foolishness. This was a matter of grain seats versus metropolitan seats. (Fair cop, that analysis.) This has been Katharine Murphy, for politicslive .. Labor senator Kim Carr asks for an explanation of the removal of the former secretary Andrew Metcalfe. Assistant immigration minister Michaelia Cash takes the question on notice. What's in a name? It cost $195,000 for the name change of the department from Immigration and Citizenship to Immigration and Border Protection. Martin Bowles, secretary of immigration department, will be answering questions on Operation Sovereign Borders any minute now. He is now giving his opening address to Senate estimates. Finance minister Mathias Cormann, says he has just discovered that Labor had planned job cuts to the tune of 14,500 jobs in the Australian public service. As a result, Cormann says the government has to reassess and will consider advice from Commission of Audit but they remain committed to achieving the committed 12,000 cuts through natural attrition. He says Labor only funded redundancies for some 800 jobs. Greens leader Christine Milne and fellow senator Scott Ludlam are speaking about diplomatic incidents including the Australian-Indonesian spy allegations, the jailing of Greenpeace activist Colin Russell in Russia and the gifting of two patrol boats to Sri Lanka. Milne says Tony Abbott's response to the Indonesian spying allegations, "trying to brush it away is only going to escalate matters". Acknowledge the (spying) mistake, it was clearly the last government and it won't happen again. Hello all, Katharine Murphy back again. (Quite liking this roving live blog correspondent gig, just quietly.) It being Tuesday, the Labor caucus has gathered in Canberra. The Labor leader Bill Shorten gave the troops a gee up - this new Coalition government was secretive and arrogant and not what they told the public they were pre-election. There were 'well dones' to Tony Burke, Richard Marles, and Chris Bowen - settling into post election roles. Legislation was considered. Questions were posed, including about the issue of the moment - GrainCorp. Shadow treasurer Chris Bowen has been trying to give the treasurer Joe Hockey some help recently about the controversial GrainCorp takeover. Bowen has been talking up the general national interest associated with foreign investment. Labor colleagues, however, seem less convinced. Bowen took some questions this morning from Labor folks concerned about whether the Americans would honour their commitments about investment should the takeover succeed. Concern is not just limited to the Coalition MPs, it would seem. And of course, Clive Palmer - who spoke out against the takeover this morning. Okedoky, while the Senate estimates continue, the lower house is preparing to meet at midday. We can expect more debate on the carbon tax repeal bills ahead of the vote expected on Thursday. In the meantime, Graham Readfearn reports that Australia has slipped down the climate change performance index as a result of trying to repeal the emission trading scheme. Australia has become an "anti-climate" influence on international efforts to slow global warming after dropping close to the bottom of a ranking of the world's largest greenhouse gas emitters. Australia's ranking on the climate change performance index fell from 51 to 57 out of 61, as the United Kingdom became the second highest ranked country behind Denmark. Our political editor Lenore Taylor: The Australian prime minister has refused to apologise for, or even talk about, the revelations of attempted spying against Indonesia revealed on Monday by Guardian Australia and the ABC, saying to do so was not in the interests of the bilateral relationship. Immigration officials are providing the nuanced arguments around the government's change in language from "irregular maritime arrivals" to "illegal maritime arrivals" when describing asylum seekers. Michael Pezzullo, the customs chief, at the Senate's Legal and Constitutional Affairs legislation committee, says "illegal" is a term that the department and government agencies took on after the election. We didn't generate it within the (public) service. Labor's Kim Carr: When you arrive by boat and seek asylum, how can you be illegal? Assistant immigration minister Michaelia Cash says the term "illegal refers specifically to that persons mode of arrival" without a proper visa, even though they do not commit an unlawful act when seeking asylum. Cash says turning up on our shores without a visa does breach the Migration Act and reminds estimates that many Labor ministers used the term "illegal". The problem appears to be that the term "unauthorised maritime arrival" is in the act. Labor senators want to know whether the government will create an offence for asylum seekers that will qualify the government to use the term "illegal". Martin Bowles, secretary of the immigration department, says he received the notification to use the term "illegal" in a letter from immigration minister Scott Morrison. Morning all, Katharine Murphy breaking in briefly on Gabi's live call of the day. While Ms Chan is monitoring simultaneously, Penny Wong's senate estimates mendacity crackdown, the increasing outrage of Jakarta and the world according to Clive Palmer .. and translating this in a flow of elegant prose .. I'm listening in currently on the customs service. Michael Pezzullo, the customs chief, is before the Senate's Legal and Constitutional Affairs legislation committee this morning. Pezzullo is updating senators on the efforts by customs management to root out corruption in the service. He's outlined the steps that have been taken to discipline or move on staff, the drug testing regime - and the current effort to crackdown on "hard to detect corruption and serious misconduct." Pezzullo says he's currently in discussion with the integrity commissioner about how to get to a bunch of folks he suggests are hiding, hoping to ride out recent scandals. He suggests those trying to wait it out should instead come forward. Pezzullo, who is known in Canberra for his straight talk, is also telling it like it is in terms of the customs budget. They are through the fat, through the muscle, and are in danger of breaching bone. "Our current operating model is not sustainble. This year the service will run at an operating loss for the scond year in a row," he says. "That's a very sad and frightening account you've just given," notes the Liberal senator Ian Macdonald. Photographer Lukas Coch caught the Indonesian ambassador before he flew back to Indonesia, under orders from his government. From Senate estimates, we discover that Australian public service natural attrition rates currently sit at 4.12 per cent, which suggests the numbers of public servants leaving and retiring add up to 26,069 over the next four years. Senator Wong believes the Coalition cannot achieve their 12,000 planned cuts completely through natural attrition and rejected suggestions that Labor had not disclosed their plans. Finance minister, Mathias Cormann: "Your cuts were not disclosed. Former finance minister, Penny Wong: "I will decline to respond to more mendacity. Endy Bayuni has written a comment piece for Guardian Australia regarding the spying allegations. He has translated comments made on Twitter by the Indonesian President SBY. Readers can find SBY's account on Twitter and then see his comments in Indonesian. Press "view translation" for an English version. Twitter's translation is slightly different in words to the translation below however the message remains the same. I regret the Australian PM statement belittling the phone-tapping in Indonesia without feeling guilty.We are reviewing a number of cooperation agenda because of the damaging Australian behaviour. Today, I have ordered FM Marty Natalegawa to recall the Indonesian ambassador to Australia. This is a decisive diplomatic step. The action by the US and Australia is damaging the strategic partnership with Indonesia, a democratic nation. One of SBY's tweet follows. Senate estimates continue with Penny Wong interrogating the department of finance officials and finance minister Mathais Cormann. Senator Wong is trying to nail down exactly how many public servants will go under the Coalition government given their stated plans to cut 12,000 jobs and minister Cormann is suggesting the former Labor government already had its own plans for widespread job cuts to the tune of 14,000. Cormann and Wong have a long sparring history, which is leaking into the hearings. Wong tells Cormann she is totally aware the Coalition won the election and gloating about it is not going to add value. My colleague Paul Farrell is reporting moves by the Australian Privacy Foundation in the wake of spying allegations. The Australian Privacy Foundation has urged the federal government to support a United Nations resolution bolstering international privacy protection in the wake of more revelations about Australia’s surveillance of Indonesian ministers. "As we find out what our national security agencies are doing overseas, it makes you wonder what they're doing within Australia,” the chairman of the foundation’s board, Roger Clarke, told Guardian Australia. Over at Fairfax, Philip Dorling has delivered a forthright message on Australia's efforts to bug the Indonesian leadership, Why do we do it? Behind all the declarations of friendship and good neighbourliness by successive Australian governments, Canberra just doesn't trust Jakarta. We work closely with Indonesia, including in the fields of security and intelligence, but we don't trust them. We never have, and probably never will. Greens Senator Scott Ludlum has called for an inquiry into Australia's electronic surveillance following the allegations that Australia spied on Indonesia. Ludlam says the spy agencies are not accountable to the Australian Parliament. Ludlum says the Australian Signals Directorate, allegedly responsible for the Indonesian surveillance in 2009, cannot be called before estimates committees, such as the ones held by the Senate this week. Ludlam says the public receives no annual report and we do not even know how many staff ASD employs. Ludlam lost a bid for a wide ranging Senate inquiry last week but in the light of the Indonesian revelations, he will have another crack at getting it up next week. Tony Abbott announced the government will provide $1 million to the White Ribbon campaign to help reduce violence against women, particularly in culturally and linguistically diverse communities. Clive Palmer has been haunting the courtyards of Parliament House to urge Treasurer Joe Hockey not to approve $3.4bn takeover by Archer Daniels Midland of Australian trader GrainCorp. He has called on the National Party, including deputy prime minister Warren Truss and agriculture minister Barnaby Joyce to sway their colleague against the decision, due on December 17. Good morning, The fallout from the Guardian/ABC revelations continues to spread across Australian politics like a creeping fog. The allegations that in 2009 under the Rudd government, Australian intelligence agencies were spying on the Indonesian President Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono and his wife Ani Yudhoyono were contained in documents leaked by NSA whistleblower, Edward Snowden. For a full round up over developments overnight, check out the report by Guardian correspondent Oliver Laugh land: Indonesia has recalled its ambassador to Australia and is reviewing all co-operation with the country after revelations that Australian spy agencies attempted to listen in to the phone calls of the Indonesian president and his inner circle. For the Indonesian perspective, check out Indonesia's top English language newspaper, The Jakarta Post: Foreign Minister Marty Natalegawa confirmed on Monday the government would recall Indonesian Ambassador to Australia Nadjib Riphat Kesoema following news reports suggesting that Australia had tried to listen to the phone calls of President Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono and his wife Ani Yudhoyono. Prime Minister Tony Abbott has been out early this morning, repeating his comments from Monday that Indonesia remains a great friend. I am not going to say anything or do anything to damage relations with our good friend Indonesia ... today may not be the best day in this relationship ... nevertheless we have a good relationship. While the Indonesian president has remained ominously silent, the Indonesian foreign minister Marty Natalegawa used a clear analogy for a sports loving nation like Australia. The ball is very much in Australia's court. Responding to Abbott's claims on Monday that all governments gather information on other governments, Natalegawa said "I've got news for you. We don't do it." Former foreign minister Bob Carr has been speaking to the ABC about the way forward, describing the public allegations as catastrophic and urged the Abbott government to apologise immediately. Carr refused to speculate on the specific allegations which occurred under Labor's watch. One of the more interesting perspectives this morning came from the Tasmanian independent member and former intelligence officer Andrew Wilkie who said it actually helps cement better relations for countries to know exactly what other leaders of thinking. He says when you don't know what they are thinking, it can make them worse. However he applauded Snowden's release of documents because "on balance it is in the public interest to know better what our agencies do." Wilkie says of what he knows of the business, he would be more surprised if Australia was not spying on Indonesia and "so much of what is going on in Indonesia is about the coming election".

results.webTitle: This payday loans decision gives me faith in politics | Serai Hann

fields.headline: This payday loans decision gives me faith in politics
fields.trailText: Serai Hann: I won't stop campaigning, but George Osborne's curbing of the cost of credit may mean others won't be harassed like I was
fields.bodyText: I was not interested in politics. I had never voted before. But earlier this year, I started campaigning with a group of friends in Swansea against the way we were being exploited by payday lenders. After taking a small loan to buy my kids Christmas presents, I ended up stuck in £1,000 of debt. And, being out of work, I was constantly worried about paying it back, with collectors ringing me daily for the money. Our campaign joined up with a national one, Sharkstoppers; a movement of communities organised by Movement for Change, fighting for fair credit in alliance with Labour MPs. Today, the chancellor, George Osborne, gave recognition to the campaign and the MPs, like Stella Creasy, who were behind it, announcing that the government will cap the interest rates that payday lenders can charge, and cap the total cost of credit. We don't have the details yet, but thsi decision has given me hope that my friends and people like us will be freed from the grip of these money lenders. And it's shown me that politics can work. This time last year, I had never bothered with politics. I didn't see the point. All it was to me was a bunch of people – mainly men – in suits, on TV, behaving worse than my two kids. But now I've seen it is much more than that. In Swansea, we brought together local people and institutions, like LGBT Unity, churches, trade unions, the Labour party and the credit union. We took action, got into negotiations and got our local council to act. We got our assembly members to act. We got the regional newspaper to give free coverage to fair credit and to consider banning high interest lenders from its pages. This month I went to London for the first time. There, I met people from Dundee to Southampton who had been running similar campaigns, and I saw just how powerful we are when we make politics work for us. On stage at the Labour party conference, my fellow activist Trina Burman and I asked the shadow minister for work and pensions, Rachel Reeves, to commit Labour to capping the cost of credit.
