When she did, we thanked her – and then Ed Miliband confirmed Labour would do it. The government has today responded as well, so it has our gratitude too. But we are going to keep campaigning. We want to end the way people are harassed by doorstep lenders. And we want to make sure people like me have access to fair credit, so that this Christmas I don't have to pay 5,000% interest just to get turkey on the table.

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 27 September

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 27 September
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened - Kevin Rudd's return - politics live blog

fields.headline: Politics as it happened - Kevin Rudd's return - politics live blog
fields.trailText: Kevin Rudd to be sworn in as prime minister, all the fallout from Labor's leadership change
fields.bodyText: The senate goes on sitting tomorrow. but as we've noted, the green chamber is up. The end of the 43rd parliament has been much like the parliament itself: exceptional, a bit wild, oversized, fabulous. It has been a privilege for me to cover this parliament - and I want to thank readers of politics live for such strong support since our launch in May. And hats of to Mike Bowers for exceptional work, always; but absolutely over the past few days. Let's say goodnight. Today opened with Tony Abbott signalling he would not subject Kevin Rudd to a no confidence motion. Kevin Rudd went to see the Governor-General and was sworn in as prime minister, alongside his deputy Anthony Albanese, and new Treasurer Chris Bowen. The last element of Julia Gillard's legislative legacy, the 457 visa legislation, passed the House of Representatives. MPs continued to bid politics farewell in emotional speeches. Abbott declared meanwhile the Coalition would hold Rudd to his record, not allow him to hit reset without a murmur. Fairfax Media's Jonathan Swan has filed a news update on the Slipper allegations and the latest responses. You can read that here. My colleague Lenore Taylor has just spoken again to Clive Palmer's spokesman on the issue of whether Joe Hockey or Mal Brough raised James Ashby's legal fees - would Palmer pay for Ashby's legal fees in a legal action taken against Slipper. (See posts just before and just after Question Time today for particulars.) Palmer's spokesman has switched course entirely from his previous advice to Lenore. He now says that the issue of payment of Ashby's legal fees was raised with Palmer by either Joe Hockey or Mal Brough - but he can't recall which person raised it. Hockey denied that he had raised the issue in a statement just after Question Time. For the history books. Julia Gillard takes leave of politics, and the chamber. That's it. Kevin Rudd exits the chamber and the 43rd parliament. Got to zip. Greens Senator Lee Rhiannon is speaking now in the Senate in the abortion debate. She's argues this is thin edge of the wedge stuff. She praises Julia Gillard for raising abortion rights in the final weeks of her prime ministership. This bill shows how right Ms Gillard was. This bill is a shot across the bows of Mr Abbott. She says it is yet to be seen whether Madigan would seek to use his influence with Tony Abbott post-election. She says this issue has flushed Abbott out. She notes Abbott's recent commitments not to change the abortion law. But she says his previous statements on women's reproductive rights are troubling. That's the end of the 43rd parliament in the House of Representatives. The House stands adjourned until it meets again for the 44th. That's it. Cash is the Coalition's spokesperson for the status of women. She argues gender selection abortion - a practice which occurs in China and in India - is a means of entrenching gender inequality. She says it occurs in countries where male children are prized above female children. She says there's no reliable evidence that gender selection abortions are occurring in this country. If it is happening, it is rare. Most abortions occur before the sex of the foetus is known. Cash refers to Abbott's recent commitment not to revisit the abortion law. She says it is a matter for the states predominantly. Now I apologise, I've had to chamber surf. I've tuned in now to the senate debate we foreshadowed earlier today on the Democratic Labor Party Senator John Madigan's bill to remove Medicare funding for gender selection abortions. Speaking presently is the Liberal Senator Michaelia Cash. I support Senator Madigan's right to stand up for his principles. Garrett closes on China. It's such an important relationship, but Australia needs to stand up for democratic values and human rights. And on growth versus sustainability. It can't be all about growth, it's got to be about the quality of growth. We have to protect the eco-system. Now to education reform. Garrett says he's grateful to Julia Gillard for the big task she gave him with Gonski. If we can put aisde the partisanship and the rancour, this package will deliver for kids, Garrett says. (Emerson - it is that kind of day.) Former Trade Minister Craig Emerson is sitting, listening, in a t-shirt. That kind of day. And shorts! When you come here you start from the ground up. I really wanted to learn the craft and apply myself to it. Garrett, on that transition. He came here with a great passion on the environment. What we've done on climate change will be regarded as a substantial transforming reform. He suggests the clean energy policy might be too modest, but it's rational and considered. He says the Greens haven't delivered much on conservation reform - the party that has done most for the environment is the Labor Party. I came into this place as a frontman and I became a team player. Peter Garrett reflects on the transformation from rock star to Cabinet minister. Stunts, he says, don't help you in the long term. Playing air guitar on the desk would dishonour his previous calling and his current one. You don't come here out of self interest, you come here out of public interest. Manager of Opposition Business Christopher Pyne, on Kevin Rudd's return. Mr Smug is back, writ large. Self indulgent, self regarding .. Pyne is on a Rudd roll. Pyne is asked to withdraw his remarks. I'm shocked but I withdraw. (He's shocked that self indulgent and self indulgent is unparliamentary.) The Liberals don't waste any time either. Here's the latest attack ad against Kevin Rudd. Colleagues reflect on his character. Kevin Rudd in his reply has challenged Abbott to some policy debates. Rudd says he's about building the house, not about knocking it down. Abbott suggests he's happy to have debates. Name the date, don't be scared, Abbott says, as Rudd exits the chamber. Tony Abbott is leading the matter of public importance debate this afternoon. Labor would like the public to move on from the extraordinary events of the past 24 hours - but the Coalition won't be moving on. The Coalition will be holding Rudd to account. He can't whitewash his record. Abbott would like to know when the election will be. Julia Gillard was brave enough to tell the public - why can't he? You should name the date. And what are your policies? Meanwhile, coming to an inbox near you, this salutation from the new Prime Minister. A reader has passed it on. The ALP are quick off the mark. Dear XXX In recent years, politics has failed the Australian people. There has just been too much negativity all round. There’s been an erosion of trust. Negative personal politics has done much to bring dishonour to our parliament but done nothing to address the urgent challenges facing our country, our communities and our families. In fact it has been holding our country back. This must stop, and with all my heart that is the purpose that I intend to pursue as Prime Minister. I want to acknowledge the achievements of my predecessor, Julia Gillard. She is a woman of extraordinary intelligence, of great strength and energy. She has achieved much under the difficult circumstances of minority government. Every effort I have in my being will be dedicated to uniting the Australian Labor Party. No retributions, no pay backs, none of that stuff. It is pointless, it is old politics. Together, we can win and defend all that Labor has achieved in the past six years. Join the campaign today or click here to donate. Thanks, Kevin Hockey is making a personal explanation concerning Peter Slipper's farewell speech just before Question Time. Slipper said of a meeting he had with mining magnate Clive Palmer earlier this year: I do recall that Mr (Clive) Palmer mentioned to me at that time that about Easter last year, Mr Brough, accompanied by the Member for North Sydney, came to see Mr Palmer to ask him to fund James Ashby's legal fees with respect to the litigation which people listening would be aware of. (We covered this statement from Slipper just before 2pm.) Mr Hockey did meet Palmer at that time. But he says Slipper's claims concerning Ashby and the legal fees are patently untrue. Hockey says he has never met Mr Ashby - and the discussion Slipper alluded to did not take place. He asks Slipper to correct the record. Slipper is now on his feet. He's attempting to repeat the allegation. But he's been sat down by Acting Speaker Bruce Scott. Slipper seeks indulgence. There is nothing to withdraw. I am simply repeating a conversation. Tony Abbott on Stephen Smith. Kevin Rudd. What do you say about Smithy? You are even more anally retentive than I am. Smith pays tribute to his core group of friends in politics. Anthony Albanese. A hero of the Labor movement in this parliament. Jenny Macklin, the Families Minister. Stephen Conroy, the Communications Minister. Conroy is misunderstood by many. (There's much laughter about that in the chamber.) Conroy is the bravest member of the parliament that I know. A man, he says, of raw courage, raw integrity. Wayne Swan. His closest friend. Swan, he says, put Labor in a position to win the election in 2007. He positioned Labor to take down John Howard on policy grounds, Smith says. Little bit gold, this picture. Rudd reacts to Stephen Smith's herogram for Stephen Conroy. (Rudd and Conroy are, let's put this politely, not very close.) Smith thanks Kevin Rudd and Julia Gillard for the opportunities he has been given in these two terms. I am very proud of what I have sought to do ... with regard to defence culture and the treatment of women. Smith says he will serve on the frontbench until September. But he's telling parliament on indulgence that he won't contest the election. It's time to retire. Twenty years I can do, twenty three years I can't. Rudd has placed further questions on the notice paper. Shadow Climate Action Minister Greg Hunt wants Kevin Rudd to account for deaths that occurred related to the controversial pink batts scheme - a government stimulus scheme that was poorly administered. Hunt wants to know if Rudd will table the warnings he got when he was previously prime minister about problems associated with the scheme. Rudd: Any industrial accident is one too many. Childcare Minister Kate Ellis is raising Judtih Sloan's recent remarks about the intelligence of childcare workers. (We covered the intervention by Professor Sloan - a columnist for The Australian, on the blog earlier this week. I believe she called childcare workers dim wits.) Minister Ellis is unhappy with Professor Sloan. On indulgence Tony Abbott makes his first remarks on this subject. I support moves to improve the professionalism of childcare centre workers. After all my wife runs a childcare centre. Tony Abbott lists Kevin Rudd's policy failures on boat arrivals. Kevin Rudd says Abbott's tow back policy will not work. Rudd is quoting the Indonesian Amabassador's position in late May - he says the Indonesians won't do what Abbott suggests they will do. Australian defence officials have said tow back doesn't work. Rudd: If we are having a debate in this country it should be about policies that work rather than soundbites which sound good. Rudd says he's seeking security briefings on the latest concerning boat arrivals. He suggests Abbott should do the same. Or is he frightened of facing the facts? Shadow treasurer Joe Hockey wants to know if Kevin Rudd will scrap the carbon tax and the increase due next Monday. Rudd: I have long been committed to a carbon price. Those opposite have a little to explain on this question. Rudd would like to know why the Opposition has departed from its previous support for emissions trading under the Howard Government. He points to global action on climate change. Kevin Rudd is telling the shadow treasurer Joe Hockey that Cabinet will meet next week, most likely. And he'll be seeking briefings from Treasury. Tony Abbot is on Kevin Rudd's broken promises. Kevin Rudd is on message - sorry, on policy. Why don't you debate me about policy Tony? Rudd: All we get here is politics politics politics. My colleague Lenore Taylor has checked Peter Slipper's claim just before Question Time that Mal Brough and Joe Hockey approached Clive Palmer concerning the payment of James Ashby's legal fees. Lenore has spoken to Mr Palmer's spokesman. This is his quote: Clive has denied this about a thousand times. One more quick back track. Here's Mike Bowers beautiful frame of Julia Gillard's reaction to Rob Oakeshott's valedictory speech. Your father would have been proud of you. Superb stuff. And his lovely version for Twitter. The new backbench. I need to back track briefly. There was that Slipper squeeze just before Question Time, apologies. Rudd in his first answer has thrown the election date up in the air. He's said he will revert to the practice of previous prime ministers - meaning he doesn't intend to telegraph the date in advance. Ah no. Deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop inquires why the Australian people can trust Rudd when he promised not to challenge Julia Gillard for the Labor leadership. Rudd says he's explained why he changed his mind. Bishop is back with a supplementary. Why didn't you keep your word to the Member for Lalor? I refer her to my statement from yesterday. I have nothing further to add. Rudd: It's time for the old politics of negativity to be dead and buried. Having observed from the nether regions these past few years .. Prime Minister Kevin Rudd, back at the dispatch box, for his first Question Time. I am seeking comment from Joe Hockey's spokesman concerning Peter Slipper's allegation in that valedictory. Slipper says he signed up the former Howard Government minister and LNP candidate, Mal Brough. We helped him. We signed him up. In a sense, I am responsible for my own predicament. Slipper says the mining magnate Clive Palmer invited him recently to breakfast at his resort. I think it was held in the Titanic room. A very large breakfast for two people and we had a very interesting conversation. I do recall that Mr Palmer mentioned to me at that time that about Easter last year, Mr Brough, accompanied by the Member for North Sydney, came to see Mr Palmer to ask him to fund James Ashby's legal fees with respect to the litigation which people listening would be aware of. The former Attorney-General, Ms Roxon, the Member for Gellibrand, mentioned the possibility of an Ashbygate royal commission. I do understand that matter is under active consideration. I would hope the government moves to see the involvement of members of the oppositon in particular in what has resulted in a situation where there was an attempt to not only remove shall we say the speaker of the parliament, but also bring down the government of this nation. Ah, the meaning of this intervention is becoming clear. This is a speech about James Ashby. This is somewhat unusual. Former Speaker Peter Slipper is giving a farewell speech just in case he decides not to run, or in case he loses. Oakeshott tells the chamber about a text message he sent Gillard last night, He said your father would be proud of you. Gillard is struggling to keep her emotions in check. It is a very profound moment, the respect that has passed between these two people. Oakeshott says some truly disgusting things have been said over this past three years. We've got to deal with ugly Australia. We've got to deal with it somehow. I adore this picture from Mike Bowers. Suspect my blog format doesn't do it justice, but it's full of atmosphere. Julia Gillard enters the chamber for the first time as a backbencher to listen to Rob Oakeshott make his farewell. On his infamous 17 minute statement before backing Julia Gillard and Labor in 2010 - Oakeshott makes no apology. I own that 17 minutes for the work contained within it. He says everything he said in that 17 minutes has been delivered. Joke away world if you like. (The world does joke away on the 17 minutes, it's true). On the first minority parliament in Canberra since World War Two. I think it has been an enriching experience to be part of it. I hope the convenient rhetoric doesn't kill the reality. Oakshott says more than 80 per cent of the legislation in this parliament has been bipartisan. Confidence has been maintained. Three budgets passed. I leave optimistic and I leave confident. Parliament and democracy is bigger than its intrigues. Thank you prime minister. Oakeshott pauses, horrified at his faux pas. Julia Gillard has entered the chamber to listen. Thank you Julia. Crossbencher Rob Oakeshott has begun his farewell speech. The moment's right for me. I'm basically a bit tired. There we go. The last bit of Julia GIllard's legislative legacy is through the lower house. This is a prime minister who has failed the first test of decency. Scott Morrison, on the 457 bill. Kevin Rudd has rolled over to the unions is his argument. The House is now dividing for the third reading. This bill is just about to pass the House of Representatives. Once were ministers. Team Gillard. Here comes the vote on the 457 legislation. The procedural skirmishes are continuing. The government is attempting to push through on 457 visas. Scott Morrison is trying to head that off. He's attempting to bring on a valedictory speech from Rob Oakeshott. The Speaker Anna Burke is unimpressed. I'm loath to use a Member's valedictory in this way. The Speaker guides the House back to 457 visas. The Opposition is objecting. Another division is underway. Deputy Prime Minister Albo. Welcome back Kevin. The sequel. Welcome back Kevin. Firmly into the procedural fray now. The government is attempting to bring the 457 legislation back on. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison is objecting. At some length. Ah yes, here's the attack line. The new prime minister started the boats. He can't be trusted to stop the boats. Morrison says the 457 visa legislation is a smoke screen by the government to distract the public from its failures on border protection. And who was responsible for the failures? One KRudd (as one KRudd is inclined to say). The Opposition leader congratulates the prime minister on achieving his ambition to return to The Lodge. Tony Abbott: May he elevate that office, and he would best elevate that office by explaining the events of last night. Abbott adds that Rudd would also elevate the office by telling the Australian voters when they will get an election. Abbott makes the reflection that politics is sometimes far more brutal than it needs to be. There is some murmuring at that observation. Rudd shook hands with Tony Abbott as he entered the chamber for the first time as prime minister .. well of course .. the first time as prime minister since 2010. Rudd informs the House there has been a leadership change. He's been commissioned as the prime minister. I would like to acknowledge the achievements of the former prime minister and the former treasurer. Rudd repeats his remarks about Julia Gillard's positive policy legacy from last night - and he acknowledges her as a standard bearer for women. Australia's first female prime minister. Swan gets a positive wrap for his work during the global financial crisis. Rudd informs the House that Anthony Albanese will take questions during question time on behalf of the ministers who exited their posts overnight. There will be announcements to follow. Rudd says politics is a hard game. Let us try, try .. and be a little kinder and gentler to each other in the deliberations of this parliament. Shadow Treasurer Joe Hockey has just shaken hands with Chris Bowen down in the chamber. Let the new joust begin. I think we'll take stock before the tempo of the political day accelerates at noon. The Canberra day thus far, let's keep this simple: Tony Abbott is signalling he doesn't intend to move a no-confidence motion in the wake of the Labor leadership change. Kevin Rudd is back in the The Lodge, he was sworn in at Government House this morning with his new deputy, Anthony Albanese, and his new Treasurer, Chris Bowen. Current advice suggests the 457 legislation will be debated later on. There will be a senate debate this afternoon about John Madigan's private member's bill on gender selection abortions. Question Time - how Rudd will manage that in a logistical sense given a bunch of frontbenchers have stood down and are yet to be replaced - is a bit of a mystery to me. We shall see. Rudd statement to parliament coming right up. Greens deputy leader Adam Bandt is already in campaign mode in the Mural Hall of parliament house. The Greens are stable, united and progressive. Unlike .. Well, you can fill in the blanks. David Marr brings his wonderful facility to the subject of Kevin Rudd. You can read the full piece here. Here's a taste. The plotters who pulled Kevin Rudd down in 2010 didn’t know their man. They knew he was making a mess of government and had lost support in the caucus. They could see his immense popularity fading. But they didn’t realise that Rudd would never give up, the wounds would never heal and he would never go away. His self-belief is bottomless. There were dark nights for Rudd after his defenestration but it remained a constant comfort that he had never been rejected by the Australian people. His quarrel was with his party, a quarrel he was ready to resume whenever the opportunity allowed. 457s - Rudd's office guidance evidently remains as it was to me earlier. This legislation will be considered today. That's the plan. Thanks to Fairfax Media's Daniel Hurst. A lot going on today around the place. The Senate will this afternoon reflect on the Democratic Labor Senator John Madigan's private member's bill which seeks to remove Medicare funding for "gender selection" abortions. The debate will take place in private member's time, which is after 4.30pm. At this stage, the debate has three speakers. Here's Guardian Australia political editor Lenore Taylor's news lead. The day thus far. Kevin Rudd has been sworn in as prime minister for a second time, three years after being deposed, and now faces the daunting task of reworking policy and replacing more than one-third of the cabinet just months before an election. Rudd has not said when he will call the poll or whether he will stick with Julia Gillard’s nominated election date of 14 September, but appears to have the confidence of the hung parliament. The Coalition is indicating it will not move a no confidence motion. As the nation woke, yet again, to find the prime minister had been changed overnight and without reference to the voters, Labor struggled to work through the divisions that resulted in the overthrow of Australia’s first female prime minister in a ballot won by Rudd, 57 votes to 45. Rudd backer Chris Bowen who resigned from the cabinet after the last leadership crisis in March, has been confirmed as treasurer. Gillard backer Gary Gray said he had agreed to stay on as resources minister, but was still blunt in his assessment that Gillard’s leadership had been killed off by a constant campaign of destabilisation by Rudd supporters. Gray only last week said Rudd was able to “get himself into the media … what he can’t do is govern and what he can’t do is lead the Labor party”. He has told ABC radio he made the remarks “in the heat of anger and frustration”. Speaking after the ballot on Wednesday night, a composed Gillard said she was proud of her achievements in what “has not been an easy environment to work in” because of the minority parliament, the internal Labor divisions and the Coalition’s fierce anti-carbon tax campaign. I've already referenced Penny Wong's interview this morning on Radio National Breakfast. It was compelling listening. Here's an excerpt - Wong on Gillard and the gender question. FRAN KELLY: Julia Gillard was Australia’s first female Prime Minister. How hard was it for you to vote against Australia’s first female Prime Minister? Here you are, a senior woman in a Labor Cabinet. PENNY WONG: And a feminist. And that’s been extremely hard. But, ultimately, as I said, you have to weigh your loyalties and your different principles. And I do not believe the feminist principles which I hold dear would be served by Tony Abbott becoming Prime Minister with the views that he has expressed on women and women’s capacity and because of the ways in which he has implicitly allowed the sexism in this country that we have seen on display. I do not believe a leopard changes his spots. FRAN KELLY: In terms of the sexism which has been on display, as you say, the Prime Minister made some reference to that last night too – the former Prime Minister, Julia Gillard, in her speech after the vote. She said: “The reaction to being the first woman Prime Minister does not explain everything about my Prime Ministership, nor does it explain nothing. It explains some things and it’s for the nation to think about those things in a sophisticated way”. Do you think the reaction to having a female Prime Minister ... how much of it does explain about Julia Gillard’s position in the polls with the electorate? PENNY WONG: I think Julia put it very well – “it explains some things, but not everything”. And I think her suggestion that we collectively consider as a nation the response and, in particular, the way in which things were said, the sorts of views and personal attacks that became legitimised, we should think about whether that is what we want for our national politics. As Prime Minister Rudd said last night, that is not what we want for our national politics. We should have a very different approach to our political life and I hope that we do consider that. FRAN KELLY: Julia Gillard also said last night that she thinks her doing this job will make it easier for the next woman. You could argue it could make it harder, given the reaction? PENNY WONG: Well, it depends what we do with it. I think she’s right. The important thing about being the first is that there’s another, and then another. There's fifty of his closest friends waiting outside the ABC studios in parliament house. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten is doing a round of interviews after his decision to back Kevin Rudd in last night's leadership ballot. He's being asked, in different constructions, how it feels to be a traitor. Shorten's playing a dead bat on that. Kevin Rudd gives Labor its best chance of saving its policy legacy, Shorten says. Bit of confusion around about the fate of the 457 visa legislation - the proposal Julia Gillard championed to limit employer's use of temporary skilled migration. It's not on the list for consideration by parliament today. There's some suggestions around that the legislation has been pulled - but Kevin Rudd's people are telling me it is meant to be on the list. Clear as mud. When I know one way or the other, I will tell you. Here's the official photo. The Rudds are back in The Lodge. Ms Josephine. And her parents - Albert Tse and Jessica Rudd. This is the current guidance: We expect Rudd to make a statement to parliament at noon today. That will be followed by Question Time. We expect Rudd to unveil the remainder of his ministry tomorrow. I will do my very best. That's what Rudd just told the Governor-General Quentin Bryce. Aussies update. Julia Gillard left the cafe just as Kevin Rudd exchanged the paperwork to become prime minister. That's just happened by the way. It's offical. It's Prime Minister Rudd. Now it's Deputy Prime MInister Albanese. He's just been sworn in. Baby Josephine is burbling away contentedly. Underway now at Government House. Rudd is looking very attentive, bolt upright in his seat, clutching his bible. Now down at Aussies Cafe in parliament house, a different scene. Julia Gillard and Wayne Swan are having a coffee. Swan is wearing a hoodie. They are sitting at a table in front of a television which is broadcasting today's swearing in ceremony. My spies at Aussie Cafe tell me they are having a laugh and a chat with staff - and ignoring the pictures. A bible for Kevin. Anthony Albanese's wife Carmel Tebbutt - a former deputy Labor leader herself - is at Government House with her husband. Mike Bowers on the spot. The Rudd family have just pulled up at Government House. Therese Rein. Daughter Jessica. Grand-daughter Josephine. All smiles. I'm absolutely confident that Kevin Rudd will be making some key policy announcements over the coming days. That's Rudd supporter and Labor backbencher Stephen Jones, on Sky News. Policy, policy, policy. Here comes Kevin Rudd, up Dunrossil Drive. I do love that avenue. Just recapping in case you watched the football last night. Julia Gillard's farewell press conference. Greens leader Christine Milne, flanked by her deputy Adam Bandt, is in the courtyard telling reporters she has informed Kevin Rudd he has confidence provided there is an election by the end of September. Bandt is reflecting on Gillard's exit. I don't think her own side ever gave her a fair go. There's been years of destabilisation and back stabbing. The ultimate winner out of that has been Tony Abbott. Nick Champion, Labor backbencher, is on Sky News arguing there is a deep reservoir of affection for Kevin Rudd out there in voter-land. Moving forward. Order of the day. Who says there's no gratitude in politics? Penny for your thoughts Tony. Abbott on the lawns for the breakfast TV parade this morning. The swearing in will also include Anthony Albanese as Rudd's deputy - and Chris Bowen, who returns to the frontbench as Treasurer. Bowen is a strong performer who has been wasted on the backbench courtesy of Labor's civil war. It will be good to see him step up again. It appears from what the crossbenchers have been saying this morning that Rudd would have had the numbers on the floor of the parliament to survive any confidence test. The legal advice underpinning Kevin Rudd's swearing in as prime minister has also been released this morning. If you want to have a look at the relevant correspondence, look here. We expect Rudd to be sworn in at 9.30am this morning. One of the big questions hanging over any transition by Labor back to Kevin Rudd as leader and prime minister has been whether the new government would survive a no-confidence motion. Rather a big deal, that issue. But Tony Abbott this morning is clearly walking back from the idea that he would move a motion of no confidence in Rudd. Here's Abbott on breakfast television: KARL STEFANOVIC: As you were walking over, I was saying that you seem to be moving away from the no confidence motion. Is that the case this morning? TONY ABBOTT: Look, plainly one-third of the Cabinet has no confidence in Mr Rudd. They have resigned. It’s the biggest exodus from the Cabinet in memory. But we are not into parliamentary games. There have been far too many parliamentary games, there’s been far too much backroom dealing and double crossing. Let's let the people decide. That’s what we need. KARL STEFANOVIC: You’ve talked about that, though, in the past. You’ve obviously changed your mind on that. Why have you have changed your mind on the no confidence motion? TONY ABBOTT: Well, I’ve always said it’s the people's responsibility to choose the Prime Minister and to choose the government. All we have had for the last three years are backroom deals and back-stabbing orchestrated by the faceless men who are not interested in the welfare of the Australian people, not interested in the all the battlers out there who are struggling with the cost of living pressures, who wonder why our borders are in chaos. They’re just interested in protecting their own power and prerogatives. An air of exhaustion hangs over the place today - but that hasn't stopped the new guard hitting the airwaves this morning. Penny Wong and Mark Butler - two prominent ministers and two key players in Labor's transition back to Rudd - have confirmed this morning that they voted for Rudd in yesterday's ballot. Wong obviously faced a very difficult personal decision. She told Fran Kelly on Radio National breakfast it was a testing proposition for a feminist - walking away from the country's first female prime minister. But Wong said her feminist principles were best served ultimately by ensuring Labor had the best strategy to keep Tony Abbott from winning the election. Good morning and welcome to the day after the night before. Mike Bowers has been out early documenting the transition to a new prime minister. He's captured a handshake between two of Kevin Rudd's great supporters, lobbyist and strategist Bruce Hawker and Senator Doug Cameron. It's like a circus down on the front lawns of the parliament - all the TV networks have set up their breakfast shows to broadcast from Canberra. Our two protagonists met up after some punditry on morning TV. I won’t bombard you all with an exhaustive list of stories about the leadership in our news wrap this morning - I’m going to assume, given the readership yesterday, that you are across what happened, more or less. But I will point you in the direction of a few pieces by my favourite writers on a completely extraordinary day. Michael Gordon: The Age Julia Gillard has delivered the ultimate act of leadership and paid the ultimate price, ending the most poisonous, inglorious chapter in modern Labor Party history. But whether Kevin Rudd transforms Labor's prospects at the election depends on whether he can re-unite a fractured party that he helped divide, re-connect with a hostile electorate and challenge a rampant opposition. Gillard's magnanimity in the end has made the transition easier; her decisiveness ensured that this was the last stand. Yes, her flaws made her vulnerable, but she will be remembered as one of the nation's most tenacious leaders. Paul Kelly: The Australian (paywalled) Kevin Rudd is resurrected, Julia Gillard's career is destroyed and Rudd has launched a new crusade to halt the Tony Abbott bandwagon. This is the dramatic trifecta that pivots the 2013 election upon an unpredictable new axis. In grim desperation the Labor Party has voted for its self-survival in a bid to deny or cripple an Abbott government. Lenore Taylor: Guardian Australia The future of the party now hinges on whether voters still like Rudd when he is leader again, or whether their professed preference for him was really a hankering for the way they felt when they elected him as "Kevin07", and politics still felt hopeful and positive – in contrast to the down and dirty shouting match it has been for the past three years. Laura Tingle: The Australian Financial Review (paywalled) Kevin Rudd finally returns to a prime ministership diminished by the Labor civil war that first removed him, helped create minority government and then replaced him. And in which he, of course, was a major protagonist. His will to power, and all the flaws it has exposed along the way, now turn on Tony Abbott. His goal will be an election victory – not saving the furniture – though the timing of that election must now be in doubt. Michelle Grattan: The Conversation Rudd has been restored to the leadership three years to the week after he was pushed out. In his mind, his return journey has all been about righting a wrong, seizing back what was his – the power, the Prime Ministerial Office, the Lodge. This rang through his news conference tonight, when he said: “In 2007 the Australian people elected me to be their PM. That is the task that I resume today …” Rudd’s tortuous course back has been costly to the party and contributed to, although is not responsible for, Gillard’s failures. We'll be following all today's events live. You can join in the conversation on Twitter by using the hashtag #politicslive or follow me there @murpharoo

results.webTitle: Politics Weekly podcast: Labour party conference 2013

fields.headline: Politics Weekly podcast: Labour party conference 2013
fields.trailText: Michael White, Heather Stewart and Mark Ferguson join Hugh Muir to discuss Ed Miliband's various pledges
fields.bodyText: Ed Miliband promised to stand up for consumers against the big six energy companies with a pledge to freeze gas and electricity prices for the 20 months following the next election. Labour says the move would save the average household £120 and forms a major part of the party's vow to help with what it calls a "cost of living crisis". In a speech delivered without notes, Miliband also pledged to support voting rights for 16- and 17-year-olds and build 200,000 new homes. Joining Hugh Muir this week to discuss it all are the Guardian's columnist Michael White; the Observer's economics editor Heather Stewart and the editor of Labour List Mark Ferguson. Also this week: we pick through the revelations in Damian McBride's book on his time at the heart of Gordon Brown's communications team. And we look ahead to David Cameron's challenge at his party's conference in Manchester next week. Please leave your thoughts below.

results.webTitle: Prescott, Blair, Straw – the next generation of UK politics?

fields.headline: Prescott, Blair, Straw – the next generation of UK politics?
fields.trailText: David Prescott is one of many with familiar surnames hoping to make it into parliament. Emine Saner looks at our Westminster dynasties and what they say about politics today
fields.bodyText: David Prescott was born just a few days after his father John became MP for Hull East. "So I've known nothing else," he says. Before he was four years old, he was on the campaign trail in the 1974 general election – "I used to go round in the car saying 'vote Yaybour'; I couldn't pronounce the letter L" – and he has volunteered in every election since 1983. Politics was in the family. "Labour activism didn't start with John," he says. "My grandmother was a member of Jack Jones's pensioners' parliament [the National Pensioners' Convention] and going back, there were politically active miners as well. If you're the son or daughter of an MP, people think it's just because of your father or mother. But actually it goes much further back and you find activism goes through a whole family." Prescott is standing for selection to be Labour's prospective parliamentary candidate (PPC) for south London's Greenwich and Woolwich seat tomorrow, after its current MP, Labour's Nick Raynsford, announced he would stand down at the next election. It's the third time he has sought selection – in 2008, he stood for his father's Hull East seat, two years later he sought to become Labour's PPC in Weaver Vale, Runcorn. Now he's trying again in the constituency where he has lived for the past 13 years. "I've never known a selection process that's had such a strong list of candidates," he says by way of countering any accusation he has been parachuted in. "I don't look round and find where my best chance of trying to get in is." Did he grow up wanting to become an MP? "Not really. I've always been active, but I made a very clear decision that I wanted to have a life outside politics." Prescott became a journalist, and now runs a PR company. "When Nick announced he was standing down, I had a long chat with my wife about it because I know the impact it can have on families." He describes rarely seeing his father, who would return home on Thursday and then spend the next couple of days on constituency business before heading back to Westminster. But it was an early life filled with political giants – Barbara Castle and the trade unionist Rodney Bickerstaffe were family friends. When he moved to London, he moved into a flat with his father and Dennis Skinner. "So when you grow up in that environment you can see the good that politics can do, on a very local level, how it can change people's lives. So it's hard not to think that would be a decent and honourable thing to do. I've always made it clear in my own mind that if the time was right, I'd find my own way." If Prescott were to become Labour's PPC – and Greenwich and Woolwich is a safe Labour seat – he will join a political class where family ties are not exactly rare. It almost feels as if it could take less time to run through the politicians, or potential politicians, who aren't related. There are siblings – Keith and Valerie Vaz, Maria and Angela Eagle, Boris and Jo Johnson (whose father Stanley was an MEP, and in a reversal to the usual pattern, attempted to succeed his son in Henley when Boris stepped down). Until recently, there were the Milibands. And there are the couples – Ed Balls and Yvette Cooper, Harriet Harman and Jack Dromey, Jo Swinson and Duncan Hames. Then there are the numerous children of politicians. The Conservatives have Francis Maude (son of Angus). Bernard Jenkin (whose father Charles, now Lord Jenkin, was an MP for more than 20 years), Nick Hurd (son of Douglas), Ben Gummer (son of John) and Andrew Mitchell (son of David). Dominic Grieve, the attorney general, is the son of Percy Grieve, who was a Conservative MP for nearly 20 years. How far back should we trace Nicholas Soames's political lineage? He is the greatgrandson of chancellor Randolph Churchill, and grandson of Winston Churchill; both his father, Christopher Soames, and uncle, Duncan Sandys, were members of parliament. Labour looks just as cosy. The MSP Paul Martin is the son of former speaker Michael Martin. Anas Sarwar took his father's vacated Glasgow Central seat in 2010. Andy Sawford, recently elected MP for Corby, is the son of Phil Sawford, who had the neighbouring Kettering constituency until 2005. And those are just the current MPs. There are also those who tried and failed to win seats, such as Tamsin Dunwoody, who contested her mother's seat in 2008. And then there are the potentials – Will Straw recently became Labour's PPC for Rossendale and Darwen, next to his father Jack Straw's Blackburn constituency. There was much excitement a few months ago when it was rumoured that Euan Blair, the eldest son of the former prime minister, was being lined up for a soon-to-be-vacated Coventry seat, although that is now looking unlikely. Labour looks set to have an all-women shortlist for Bob Ainsworth's Coventry North East seat, while the longstanding Coventry North West MP Geoffrey Robinson – whom, it had been reported, had received a phone call from Cherie Blair inquiring about the seat – has said he has no intention of standing down. Still, the rumours persist. As a recent piece in the Independent pointed out, if Euan Blair really were to stand and get himself elected, we could be looking at another generation of Blair, Straw and Prescott. Joe Dromey, the son of Harriet Harman and Jack Dromey, has said it is untrue he was seeking a seat, but it's clear he has a strong political interest – he studied politics at university and is a Labour council candidate for New Cross. Emily Benn, granddaughter of Tony and niece of Hillary, who ran in the 2010 general election at the age of 20, looks set to join Croydon council next year. Meanwhile, Georgia Gould, the daughter of the late Labour strategist Philip Gould, who sought to become Labour's PPC in Erith and Thamesmead in 2009, is already a Camden councillor. "I don't think we should ever condemn anybody who wants to give public service, especially at a time when politicians aren't especially liked by the public," says Katie Ghose, chief executive of the Electoral Reform Society. "But I think there's a bigger issue: that parties are shedding members, and that means there are fewer people putting themselves forward for election, and fewer in the business of choosing them. Parties are shrinking and you get less and less competition. When we look at family members, what we'd say is politics should be open to all – everybody should leave school feeling that elected office could be something they contribute in their lifetime, whether it's the parish council or MEP or any other level. "I don't think it's surprising that family members who have been exposed to politics have that shared passion and do want to go into politics. But we're in a worrying position now as a society where people feel more cut off, and we need to find ways for parties to connect to a much broader range of people, so we end up with a diverse institution." Prof Steven Fielding, director of the Centre for British Politics at Nottingham University, says the number of political families could be compared to "the later 19th century and early 20th century, when access to politics was restricted, and you couldn't afford to be in politics unless you had quite a lot of money, and it was expected that the aristocracy played a certain role. That diminished in the 20th century but it has reasserted itself for slightly different reasons. Class plays a role, but these people are more middle-class and they have the skills of their parents and networks. They're not from grand families, but they are from well-connected families." He adds that the political world isn't the only place where this is happening – journalism is another, and law. "It's not a good thing that politics, or any profession, is open to certain people and not others." But perhaps being the son of John Prescott, or any other politician, doesn't always help anyway. "People may be almost twice as concerned to make sure they're selecting you because you're good, as opposed to what your surname is," says David Prescott. When Tal Michael, son of the Welsh first minister Alun, stood for the Ynys Môn Welsh assembly byelection in the summer, he came under criticism. "There were some Plaid Cymru bloggers who were making accusations. It was strange because I had deliberately gone outside of politics and outside of Wales, because it meant I was being employed by people who weren't making connections [to his father]. I am on the side of the spectrum that thinks it would be wrong to be inheriting position – it's about doing things on merit." Lindsay Hoyle, the well-liked deputy speaker who has been MP for Chorley since 1997, served as a councillor in the town for 18 years, becoming deputy council leader, before being persuaded by his Labour colleagues to stand against the Tories. "They wanted to pick somebody local and that was me. This is my home town, it's where I was born and brought up – there aren't many MPs who can say that, and it's a real privilege to represent my town." He is also the son of Doug Hoyle, the former MP who now sits in the House of Lords. Did anyone ever accuse him of benefiting from that? "He had a very safe Labour seat, I had a marginal seat. People asked me to stand, which I did. It was nothing to do with nepotism, but I suppose it was being in the right place at the right time." Like Prescott, Hoyle says he was "born into politics. The greats of the Labour movement were at my first conference." He was barely four months old when he was taken to the 1957 conference, where Nye Bevan was speaking. "In 1964 I was delivering leaflets as a young child ready for the election. So you're brought up in it. You live within a political household – my mother was on the council as well – and as you get into your teens you turn off a little bit. But somehow I came back." There are plenty of examples of politicians' children who have political talent, and perhaps years of public service behind them: Will Straw, who founded the influential blog Left Foot Forward and works for the thinktank Institute for Public Policy Research, has been described as a "gifted political player"; for the past few years David Prescott has been the chair of governors which turned around a failing school; Estelle Morris (daughter of MP Charles Morris), like Hoyle and others, served in local government for years before entering parliament. Being the son or daughter of a politician shouldn't be a bar to entry, should it? "Absolutely not," says Fielding. "They're being judged by party members and activists and you would think that the criteria are exactly the same. But no matter how they get selected, if it is a trend and is getting more pervasive that is not a good thing in itself – it's just showing a wider evidence that the avenues to get involved in politics at the highest and most significant level are shutting down for more ordinary people." One question is why, when so many other people are put off by the idea of a political career, the children of politicians are seemingly running towards one. David Prescott saw what his family went through at the hands of the media – there was the tabloid frenzy after his father's affair and the way he was generally undermined for years by newspapers who mocked his grammar, his weight, his perceived vulgarity. Did that not put David off? "Not really. If you believe in something and you want to fight for social justice, you're open to that level of scrutiny. It was always difficult to see [him] going through that, but he survived because he's got a passion, and he knows that if you're in power you can achieve an awful lot of good."

results.webTitle: Modern politics: show business for ugly people | Jonathan Green

fields.headline: Modern politics: show business for ugly people
fields.trailText: Jonathan Green: Our politicians take what we think, feel and fear, and then turn that to their own advantage. Australia's most basic needs shouldn't be so cynically treated – we need and deserve better
fields.bodyText: At some point they refined the art of politics, whittling it down to a nub of cynical ambition couched in something that from the middle distance might pass for belief. Show business for ugly people, that’s the joke. Funny, but also terribly true: the same obsessive drive for attention, the same deluded sense of influence and importance, except that for a politician, unlike your run-of-the-mill Home and Away starlet, the potential to do actual social good – and of course harm – does exist. It’s a confounding and confusing business. Isn’t that the overwhelming impression of these past few years? That we elect people to power whose primary interest is simply succeeding in that democratic transaction and then doing what they must to repeat the experience. If it involves a system of belief at any point, it is a simple expedient that is either adapted to need or abandoned when surplus to the fundamental requirement of obtaining and maintaining power. Perhaps this is the true story of this period, a narrative that might explain how Kevin Rudd and Julia Gillard could come and go, how Tony Abbott might ascend almost without trace to sudden electability. It might be a simple aberration, when by dumb, unfortunate luck we are faced with political choices that put the empty promises of personality politics ahead of the considered and complex policy prescriptions that might be better suited to the unique challenges of our moment. Because it does seem that we are in flux, with an economy hesitating on the brink of a changing and uncertain future in which it needs to do more than ship and quarry, an ecology transforming as it charges toward the brutal inevitability of global warming, and our slender hold on the arable fringes of the Australian continent already challenged by a growing population and by the demands of others from around the world who see the place’s new-world possibilities and would like to share them. Our systems of information and community are moving through epochal change, change with the potential to remake the standing mechanisms of media and politics that have survived the last era of communications technology but will undoubtedly be utterly transformed by the current one. In the midst of all of this, the reaction of our political players seems to have been to shrug at the enormity of these tasks and challenges and focus instead on what might be achieved on the smaller, more containable canvas of pure, unattached politics. They need us for our votes and will twist reality and perception to acquire them, but they look unlikely to give us more of the leadership, thought and challenging vision that should be ours by right in return. The normal laws of political nature – the equation that regards a vote as the reward for a policy position and vision– no longer apply, replaced by the simpler laws of celebrity. Show business for ugly people? It is now, almost to a fault. Our political characters inhabit the closed system of national politics, a world that carries on with a strangely divided attitude to the universe that surrounds it, sometimes in blithe isolation from the greater world and its aspirations, sometimes driven utterly by its obsessive sampling of that public’s true systems of need and belief. They take what we think, feel and fear, and then consider how they might best turn that to their own advantage. It’s hard to say which is the greater scandal: that the legitimate aspirations of voters and the most basic needs of the country should be so cynically treated by the political class, or that we so unthinkingly accept this self-serving reality. It’s circular and presumably ultimately destructive, and our politics is increasingly at a tangent to reality as a result. For the most part, and for most people, politics can be safely ignored as a fundamentally technocratic exercise that will deliver a bipartisan consensus around most of the substantial issues and points of policy that confront us: we want a low-inflation, low-unemployment, market-based but increasingly carbon-free economy, with growing trade and opportunity, secure borders, good schooling and a health system that will not fail us in need. Both sides agree; both will in all likelihood provide outcomes that will deliver pretty much that same set of broad goals, despite each and every party’s insistence that they and they alone have the policy settings to make any or all of it possible. In part this speaks to the amount of influence any Australian government can exert over key economic circumstances, the great tides of the global economy that carry us—hopefully—like a bobbing cork. In part it speaks of the simple necessity of most major policy planks, of the things that just need doing, regardless of political conviction. The result: most of what we hear from all parties is not much advanced beyond statements of the necessary and obvious. There are moments of difference and brilliance, rare gems of provocative forward thinking and political purpose, but too often the vast bulk of politics, certainly the greater part in these past three years, is an almost entirely internal beauty contest staged by and for an inner circle of political, parliamentary and media professionals. Only in this world does the triptych of Rudd, Gillard and Abbott make sense: two leaders disliked by the majority of the population, but favoured thanks to the internal dynamics of party power play; one leader beloved publicly but despised bitterly from within, a man who could only find the necessary internal favour for a last desperate fling by throwing his panicked party some sort of lifebelt. The sum of it is not edifying. It would be fair to say that we are either living through a low point, a moment in which people increasingly are disenchanted and disengaged with a political system that purports to serve them, or, worse than that, through the escalating decay of a political system that is failing to meet even the most basic markers of public policy performance. And there we have the sum of modern politics: on the one hand, good policy diluted through fear of adverse reaction, squandered through a focus not on its practical fundamentals but on its inter-party or intra-party effect, and on the other, simple bad policy, sculpted—think of border security—from fear, apprehension and myth with the entire aim of creating politically useful division. A solution that might heal this division would be to stand strongly for points of policy and principle, to see the political process as the reverse of its modern state: a process aimed more or less at simple re-election. This is not a system driven by belief; it is one that adapts belief reactively to the political circumstance. We need and deserve something better. If our increasingly professionalised political contest is doing little to remedy the pressing issues and dilemmas that confront us, how can it be described as anything but a failure? Its purpose must be broader than providing work, interest, power and reward for its small inner circle. What is Australian politics doing, for example, to meet the urgent need for a changed economy that does not drive us to ecological instability and catastrophe? How is it addressing the fundamentally dysfunctional infrastructure of our fast expanding cities, our reliance on systems of transport that simply ingrain congestion and inefficient chaos? How is it going about improving the circumstances of the working poor? What does it offer the increasing and unhappy mass of the mentally ill? How will it house and comfort the homeless? How does it propose to allocate resources for a health system that delivers the full potential of modern methods, science and technology to the greatest number of people, while effectively triaging the modern inclination to overservicing? What is the road map to heal the soul-deep division between white and indigenous Australians? The list goes on ... and as the new world of instant electronic community, communication, expanding knowledge and understanding enfolds us, we are left with a political process that happily rewards three years of assiduous bastardry and disloyalty with power, and that apparently sees no issue with handing that power to another party whose greatest virtue is that publicly they stand for nothing. Why should we be engaged? Why should we not hope for better? Why should we not conclude that our politics– a system that looks among us for the most deluded egotists, people whose true belief it is that they among all of us would be best placed to run an entire country and who then waste that democratic gift on the aggrandisement not of us but of self– why should we not conclude that our politics is well and truly broken? And having reached this realisation, why would we not look among ourselves, and the expanding resources and opportunities of our interconnected age, for the tools to fix it? • This is an edited except from Jonathan Green’s latest book, The Year My Politics Broke.

tags.description: Julia Gillard is a former Australian politician who was country's 27th prime minister and the leader of the Australian Labor party. She was the first woman to hold either position

results.webTitle: Government and Labour reshuffle: Politics live blog

fields.headline: Government and Labour reshuffle: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the government and Labour party reshuffles expected today
fields.bodyText: • Ed Miliband has demoted three of the most prominent "Blairites" left in the shadow cabinet in a reshuffle promoting women and members of the 2010 intake. Liam Byrne and Stephen Twigg have left the shadow cabinet, and Jim Murphy has been given the backwater of international development. The Conservatives depicted these moves (see 4.43pm) as a shift to the left, or a sop to Len McCluskey, but Labour sources have robustly rejected this pointing out that Douglas Alexander, who ran David Miliband's campaign for the Labour leadership, has now been put in charge of the general election. In truth, the generational shift may count for more than the ideological shift. Now around a third of shadow cabinet seats are filled by people who were not even in parliament before the 2010 election. There may also be interesting policy implications. Labour does not have a particularly distinctive position on education, but Tristram Hunt, a clever newcomer, now has the job of finding one. And the removal of Maria Eagle from the transport brief may signal a further weakening of support for HS2 (she was notably more supportive of it than Ed Balls has been), although party sources insist the policy has not changed. • Nick Clegg has provocatively put a radical, liberal, conspiracy theorist, Norman Baker, into the Home Office. It is the most surprising appointment of the day, and it suggests that Clegg wants to adopt a more adversarial approach on civil liberties within the coalition. When Baker joined the government in 2010, there were doubts that someone with such a non-conformist bent could last as a minister. But now he is becoming one of the party's great ministerial survivors. • David Cameron has taken some modest steps towards getting more women into his government. Number 10 does not have any figures yet for what proportion of the government is now female, although a spokesman said that, as a result of today's moves, Cameron was "making progress" towards his goal of having a third of ministers women. From today the government will sound a little more feminine, a little less posh, and a little more northern. But since most of the changes involve junior ministers who are not well known to the public, the impact of this will be relatively modest. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. I'm not sure Tony McNulty, the former Labour MP, is entirely right about this, but he certainly has a point. Here's a short reshuffle reading list. • Benedict Brogan at the Telegraph says that Nick Clegg and Ed Miliband's reshuffles were more interesting than David Cameron's - but that that might not be a good thing for them. Both have produced reshuffles that will keep heads being scratched for some time. For the Lib Dems the good achieved by promoting Alistair Carmichael to defend the Union in Scotland was comprehensively undone by the dismissal of Jeremy Browne and his replacement with the conspiracy theorist Norman Baker at the Home Office. Mr Browne was one of the successes of the Lib Dem end of the Coalition, and an exemplar of the party's seriousness in government. His sacking is baffling, but not nearly as baffling as his replacement by Mr Baker. The MP for Lewes is a media friendly operator who has made a name for himself as an eclectic campaigner. But to put in the Home Office someone who argued with a straight face that David Kelly was the victim if an Iraqi plot that was covered up by the authorities frankly diminishes the office. As for Mr Miliband, there are two elements to the reshuffle that will fascinate and keep the village talking. First, the purge of the remaining Blairites. It does credit to Liam Byrne and Stephen Twigg that they have accepted their demotions with good grace. Ditto Jim Murphy. But it will have been noticed by the modernisers, who will give up looking for evidence that Mr Miliband isn't taking the party on a long march to the left. Alongside that, the appointment of Spencer Livermore as campaigns director, and Douglas Alexander as campaigns coordinator, is equally interesting. It's a big moment for two Labour operators whose common experience is to have fallen foul of Damian McBride, and by implication of Ed Balls. It will be tempting to conclude that their apointment is designed to marginalise the Shadow chancellor or at least keep him well clear of election planning. • Stephen Tall at Lib Dem Voice offers four thoughts on the Lib Dem reshuffle. 1. Nick feels secure enough to be ruthless. Sacking both Michael Moore and Jeremy Browne is not something Nick would have been able to contemplate a year ago. Then – with the economy still mired in recession, his apology video still fresh in the memory, and Vince reminding everyone he stood ready, willing and able should the need arise – Nick was vulnerable, in need of allies. Now – with the economy recovering, Eastleigh defended and all key conference votes won – Nick feels able to asset himself. • Sam Macrory and Anoosh Chakelian at Total Politics have 10 lessons from the reshuffles. • Alex Massie at the Spectator says it has been a day of low drama. And here are two alternate views on the Labour reshuffle. From Eric Pickles, the communities secretary From Luke Akehurst, a Labour blogger Ken Livingstone has been tweeting on the Labour reshuffle. Fiona Syms, whose ex-husband Robert Syms was sacked as a whip today, has taken to Twitter to vent her anger with David Cameron. Alistair Burt, the Conservative Foreign Office minister, has confirmed that he is leaving the government. There have been some warm tributes to him on Twitter, and not just from Conservatives. His opponents like him too. From Ben Bradshaw, a Labour former Foreign Office minister From Tom Price, a former Labour adviser From Tim Farron, the Lib Dem president And the press like him too. And here's the full list of today's appointments from Number 10. There's been a slew of government appointments while I've been tied up with Labour and the briefings. That's Tina Stowell, the Lords whip who took the gay marriage legislation through the Lords. Dan Rogerson is the Lib Dem MP for North Cornwall. The Department of the Environment is becoming a Cornish stronghold Stephen Williams is another Lib Dem MP joining government. It will be interesting to see the proportion of the Lib Dem parliamentary party who have served in government by 2015. Jane Ellison is the Conservative MP for Battersea. That's a sideways move for Anna Soubry, the health minister who spoke out in favour of plain packaging for cigarettes before Number 10 decided (to her embarrassment) to drop the idea. Kris Hopkins is Conservative MP for Keighley in Yorkshire. He's also a former army private. He ticks two boxes for those keen to show that the Tories are not just posh southerners. Karen Bradley was already a whip. She has just been promoted. And Sam Gymiah was Cameron's PPS. Amber Rudd, Claire Perry, and Gavin Barwell. John Penrose was a culture minister until he was sacked last year. After Shailesh Vara (see 3.23pm), he is the second person (I think) to rejoin the government a year after being dropped. Judith Jolly is a Lib Dem peer. Desmond Swayne was already a whip, but now he is a more senior one. Number 10 officials have been briefing on the government reshuffle. It is not quite over, and they haven't yet got a final list to give us. The spokesman played down the suggestion that it was all about promoting women, or northerners, or George Osborne's allies. "It is not about camps and cabals," he said. "It is about giving jobs to people who can do the job well." Most of the questions, though, were about Norman Baker going to the Home Office. Downing Street would not say that Theresa May was consulted (which implies that she was not). The prime minister's spokesman also played down the involvement of Cameron himself. This was a Lib Dem appointment and Nick Clegg was responsible for Lib Dem ministerial appointments, he said. But Cameron was consulted about it, he added. Grant Shapps, the Conservative chairman, has just picked up the point about Len McCluskey. He has sent out this statement. Len McCluskey is the real winner of this reshuffle. Ed Miliband’s purged the moderates and promoted those who want the same old Labour policy of more spending, more borrowing and more debt. That’s exactly what got us into a mess in the first place And it’s hardworking people who would pay the price through higher taxes and higher mortgage rates. If Ed Miliband is too weak to stand up to Len McCluskey, he’s too weak to stand up for hardworking people. I'm off to another briefing. I will post again after 5.30pm. The Labour MP Paul Flynn clearly has not heard the line from Labour HQ about factionalism. (See 4.52pm.) Len McCluskey, the Unite general secretary, should be happy with the Labour reshuffle. In an interview earlier this year, he cited Liam Byrne and Jim Murphy as ministers who should be moved. A Labour spokesman dismissed the suggestion that the reshuffle was a sop to the left. The days of factionalism in the party were over, he said. To be fair, McCluskey also criticised Douglas Alexander. But, in one of the most significant appointments of the day. Alexander has been put in charge of the election Labour has just released details of Ed Miliband's shadow cabinet reshuffle. A spokesman says Miliband is keen to point out that he is promoting talented young women. Here are the details. • Rachel Reeves becomes shadow work and pensions secretary. She was shadow chief secretary to the Treasury. • Gloria De Piero becomes shadow minister for women and equalities. • Emma Reynolds becomes shadow housing minister. She will attend shadow cabinet. She was shadow Europe minister. • Tristram Hunt becomes shadow education secretary. He was a shadow education minister. • Stephen Twigg, the former shadow education secretary, becomes a shadow minister in the justice team, responsible for constitutional affairs. • Chris Leslie becomes shadow chief secretary to the Treasury. He was a shadow Treasury minister. • Vernon Coaker becomes shadow defence secretary. He was shadow Northern Ireland secretary. • Jim Murphy becomes shadow international development secretary. He was shadow defence secretary. • Ivan Lewis becomes shadow Northern Ireland secretary. He was shadow international development secretary. • Maria Eagle becomes shadow environment secretary. She was shadow transport secretary. • Mary Creagh becomes shadow transport secretary. She was shadow environment secretary. • Michael Dugher becomes shadow Cabinet Office minister. He also takes charge of political and campaign communications. • Douglas Alexander, the shadow foreign secretary, also becomes chair of general election strategy. He will be responsible for election strategy and planning. • Spencer Livermore, a former aide to Gordon Brown, has been appointed general election campaign director. He will start work later this year. • Liam Byrne, the former shadow work and pensions secretary, joins the shadow business team, with responsibility for higher education and emerging markets. • Lord Falconer, the former Lord Chancellor, will advise on planning and transition into government. • Wayne David becomes parliamentary private secretary to Ed Miliband. Karen Buck is already his other PPS. A Labour spokesman said that of the 32 people who now attend shadow cabinet, 14 (or 44% are women). And around a third of those attending shadow cabinet are from the 2010 intake. Iain Dale has taken Grant Shapps to task over the reshuffle for hardworking people. (See 3.39pm.) And Shapps has replied. I've got to attend a briefing now. I will post again after 4.30pm. As a minister leaving the government, you can write a letter to the prime minister. Alternatively, you can just post a note on Twitter. This is from Mark Prisk, who has been sacked as housing minister. Here's the Staggers on Tristram Hunt. More from the Labour reshuffle. That's Tristram Hunt, the historian and Stoke-on-Trent MP who was only elected in 2010. Earlier I asked when was the last time there was a minister in the Home Office as liberal/libertarian as Norman Baker. (See 2.43pm.) I've had a very good reply. Alex Lyon was married to Clare Short. There is a short Wikipedia entry on him here. I'd like to think this is a parody ... Earlier I posted Nick Clegg's letter to Jeremy Browne about Browne losing his job as Home Office minister. Here's Browne's reply. Dear Nick, Thank you for your letter. I has been a privilege to serve in a rejuvenated Foreign Office and in a Home Office which has presided over a fall in crime at the same time as achieving necessary budget reductions. I remain supportive of the government, which has given Britain political stability and a credible economic recovery plan. These may be the minimum requirements of a government, but they are essential and deserve not to be taken for granted. I hope the government will continue to strive to be reforming and innovative and avoid the danger of lapsing into transactional trade-offs and deferred decision making. Britain's medium-term prosperity and status is at stake. It is crucial that we continue to reduce the budget deficit, maintain stability in monetary policy, keep our business and personal tax rates internationally competitive, raise school standards, tackle endemic inter-generational welfare dependency, invest in infrastructure and extend personal freedom in both the private realm and in public service provision. I supported your election as party leader and I admired your decision to take our party into government. Easy and permanent opposition may be alluring to some but you are right not to find it seductive. Our party should aspire to be in government, not for its own sake but because our country will benefit hugely from an infusion of authentic liberalism as we seek to remain relevant and successful in a fast-changing world. Yours sincerely, Jeremy Browne My colleague Nicholas Watt has filed a story on how George Osborne's allies are prospering in the reshuffle. Back to the Conservative reshuffle. Shailesh Vara was a whip until he was sacked in last year's reshuffle. After Sajid Javid, he is the second ethnic minority Conserative to gain a promotion today. George Eustice used to be Cameron's press secretary before he became an MP. His parents run a fruit farm in Cornwall, and so he should feel at home in the environment department. Robert Goodwill is MP for Scarborough and Whitby, so he ticks the "northern" box. Until today he was a whip. Here's more from the Labour reshuffle. Here's the text of Nick Clegg's letter to Jeremy Browne, who has been sacked as a Home Office minister. Clegg tells Browne that he wants as many Lib Dem MPs as possible to have a chance at being a minister. Dear Jeremy I want to thank you for the key role you have played in government over the past three years, first as Minister of State at the Foreign Office and latterly as Minister of State in the Home Office. You have made a hugely valuable political contribution to the coalition over the past three years both as a highly able representative of the UK to other nations and more recently dealing with the many domestic challenges that face the Home Office. It is always very difficult to move colleagues out of government but as you know, I have always been keen that we provide the opportunity for as many in our ranks as possible to contribute their skills to ministerial office during this Parliament so that, just as the government has benefited from your contribution over the past three years, it can also gain from those of other colleagues in the remaining years of this parliament. I am immensely grateful to you for your commitment and support over the past few years. You have made a major contribution to this historic coalition government and as one of the very few ministers who have served in two departments, I have no doubt there will be an opportunity for your experience to be deployed in government in the future. Yours sincerely Nick Clegg And more from Labour. Sky has some news from Labour. And Downing Street has confirmed that Norman Baker. That will be interesting. Baker is a serious libertarian. He's probably the most liberal minister to serve in the Home Office since ... well, I've no idea. (I'll consult Twitter.) Before he became a minister Baker also wrote a book suggesting David Kelly was murdered. If he has to have any dealings with MI5 in his new job, those conversations should be interesting. Another Conservative move. Hugh Robertson was sports minister. Presumably he will be replacing either David Lidington or Hugo Swire, the two ministers of state in the Foreign Office from the Commons. More news from Number 10. That's a sideways move for Helen Grant, who until today was justice minister. That's Susan Kramer, who was once the Lib Dem candidate for London mayor. She is joining the government for the first time. She is effectively taking Norman Baker's Lib Dem slot at transport, although Baker was only a parliamentary under-secretary. As minister of state she will be more senior. Earlier I included a tweet from Patrick Wintour saying Dominic Cummings, one of Michael Gove's most important advisers, is leaving the Department for Education. (See 11.26am.) Patrick has now filed a story with more detail. Matthew Hancock, the business minister, has been promoted. He was just a parliamentary under-secretary at the Department for Business, but not he is a minister of state. But he seems to be keeping the same portfolio (skills). The Chartered Institute for Housing has issued a tribute to Mark Prisk, who has reportedly left his post as housing minister. This is from Graina Long, its chief executive. Mark Prisk's tenure as housing minister has been marked by a commendable focus on delivery. Although progress on numbers of new homes has been disappointingly slow it is clear that the minister has been working hard to explore different delivery options and taking a personal interest in work to unlock stalled sites. He also brought a good deal of personal integrity to the role and a careful and thoughtful approach informed by a real understanding of the housing system and housing markets reflecting his background as a property professional. His personal style was highly non-partisan demonstrating a willingness to search for solutions rather than a desire to play politics with something as important to peoples’ lives as housing. • Michael Moore, the Lib Dem Scottish secretary, has been sacked in a government reshuffle currently underway. He will be the only cabinet minister to be moved. Other changes affect the junior ranks of the government. Moore has been replaced by Alistair Carmichael, the Lib Dem chief whip. Moore said he was "disappointed". I’m disappointed to be leaving office right now, but I’m very pleased at what I’ve been able to achieve in the last couple of years, particularly the constitutional debate with the Scotland Act and the Edinburgh Agreement.
