Ed Miliband held private talks in his Commons office with the disgraced Co-op Bank boss embroiled in a drugs scandal, it emerged last night ... Labour sources confirmed that Mr Miliband had personally appointed Flowers to his elite Business and Industry Advisory Group in 2010 — and even invited him for private talks in his Commons office last year. It also emerged that Flowers described Shadow Chancellor Ed Balls as a ‘political friend’ and boasted of helping to arrange a £50,000 donation for him from the Co-op last year. Both Mr Miliband and Mr Balls deny being close to Flowers, who was suspended by Labour last night for ‘bringing the party into disrepute.’ • James Chapman in the Daily Mail says David Cameron has decided to install porn filters on his computers at home. The OECD has published its global economic outlook report today. As Katie Allen reports for the Guardian, the OECD is gloomy about the prospects for the world economy, but more optimistic about the UK. The outlook for the global economy has darkened as the prospects for emerging markets deteriorate and the United States approaches another potentially catastrophic debt deadline, according to the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development. The thinktank upgraded its outlook for the UK but cut its forecasts for the global economy. The Paris-based group forecast that unemployment would remain stubbornly high in several of its 34 mainly rich countries, that growth would slow in many emerging markets and that there was a significant threat to financial stability from the US Federal Reserve unwinding its stimulus programme – a process known as tapering ... For the UK, however, the Paris-based thinktank is more optimistic than many other forecasters and has revised up forecasts made in May. Predicting that stronger investment and household spending will drive faster growth, the OECD now forecasts the UK economy will grow by 2.4% in 2014. In May it had forecast 1.5%. That outlook for next year is more optimistic than the 1.9% growth forecast by the International Monetary Fund and 1.8% predicted in March by the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR), the Treasury's independent forecaster. Here's what the Treasury has been tweeting. And here's what the OECD says about the UK in summary. Economic activity has picked up and broadened, supported by a turnaround in private sector confidence, continued monetary stimulus, a policy-induced recovery in the housing market and a more gradual pace of household and public sector deleveraging as automatic stabilisers operate. Growth is projected to strengthen further in 2014 and 2015, mainly supported by an upturn in gross fixed investment and exports. Despite exceeding the inflation target of 2%, headline inflation is projected to fall gradually in the next two years. Consistent with its newly adopted state-contingent forward guidance, the Bank of England has announced its intention to keep interest rates low to support the recovery. The welcome efforts to speed up the recapitalisation of the banking sector should underpin financial stability. While headline deficits are expected to shrink as growth recovers, it is important to maintain existing consolidation plans to restore fiscal sustainability. Further relaxing the barriers holding back housing supply is an important policy priority to contain house price inflation. Unison has criticised Jeremy Hunt for not introducing mandatory staff:patient ratios. This is from Christina McAnea, head of health at the union. The government has missed another opportunity to introduce fixed, safe nurse-to-patient ratios. There is safety in numbers when it comes to caring for patients and this one step would bring about a revolutionary change on the wards. They are recommending a 'toolkit' to set minimum staffing levels but what will happen if these are ignored when wards are under pressure, which is almost a daily occurrence in today's NHS? It shames this government that they have waited so long to announce that they are to recruit 3,700 more nurses - which still falls short of the 6,000 that have disappeared from the NHS since the Coalition came to power. There is no doubt that some hospitals have become dangerously under-staffed because of the government cuts. They have ignored the warning signs and the calls from hospital managers, staff, patients and unions that more nursing staff were needed urgently. For the record, here are today's YouGov GB polling figures. Labour: 39% (no change from YouGov in the Sunday Times) Conservatives: 32% (down 1) Ukip: 12% (no change) Lib Dems: 11% (up 1) Labour lead: 7 points (up 1) Government approval: -26 (up 4) According to Electoral Calculus, this would give Labour a majority of 90. According to James Chapman at the Mail, Jeremy Hunt is going to announce plans to make it easier for poor managers to be blocked from getting new posts in the NHS. Here's an extract from his story. Blundering hospital bosses are to be barred from working in the NHS. Health Secretary Jeremy Hunt will today unveil plans to prevent a ‘merry-go-round’ of executives who move from job to job despite disastrous performances. Senior managers will also be assessed according to the success of their hospital – making it easier to sack failures without giving them huge pay-offs. In the wake of the Francis Inquiry into appalling care at the Mid Staffordshire trust, hospitals will be given Ofsted-style quality ratings and told to publish staffing levels. The new barring system will mean that managers – like failed doctors – can be effectively ‘struck off’ and prevented from ever moving elsewhere in the NHS. Andy Burnham, the shadow health secretary, has been using Twitter this morning to criticise the government for cutting nursing posts in the NHS. He has also posted this to rebut claims that hospital standards got worse under Labour. Jane Cummings, the chief nursing officer for England, has been giving interviews this morning about today's announcement. She explained why the government was not setting a mandatory staff:patient ratio for nurses. That figure could become a maximum, she claimed. The real danger is that we stick to a particular number and it doesn’t reflect the differing needs and the different services in different wards. I think the risk of doing anything that sets a target or a number like that is it could become a maximum. Some people do talk about a minimum of one to eight, and that may be appropriate on some wards, but for many it won’t ... What we’re saying is that you need to determine [the right staffing level] depending on the type of ward that you work on. So for some it may well be one to eight; for others it may be one nurse to four patients or one nurse to six patients. The other thing you need to think about is the whole team. So for example, if you’re working in a stroke ward, you might need to have occupational therapy, speech therapy and physiotherapists working alongside nurses and doctors. What we’re talking about here is actually making that decision locally and actually making sure that we use evidence to determine that. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. It is more than nine months since Robert Francis QC published his report into the Mid Staffs hospital scandal and today we are finally getting the government's full response. The Department for Health has been briefing overnight and we know that Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, is going to focus on staffing levels when he responds. Here is some background. • A summary of the key recommendations in the Francis report, published in February. • A summary of the government's preliminary response, published in March. • Patrick Wintour's story previewing what Hunt will say today. Here's how it starts. Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, will on Tuesday introduce monthly mandatory reporting of numbers of staff on hospital wards but will reject a fixed minimum nurse-patient ratio. Hunt's proposal is in response to the Francis report commissioned in the wake of the Mid Staffordshire scandal, where hundreds of patients died amid appalling failings in care. He will propose that the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) be required to draw up a "toolkit" suggesting minimum staffing levels in wards according to the size of ward, acuteness of patient illness, age profile and other factors. NHS trusts will then be required by law to publish the staffing in each ward – and will be subject to an immediate health inspection by the Care Quality Commission if they are not meeting the guidelines. The Safe Staffing Alliance, which includes the Royal College of Nursing, Unison and the Patients Association, backs the idea of a fixed staff ratio of no more than eight patients to one nurse. But Hunt's aides regard support for a fixed ratio as a mistake since they believe it was "Labour's target culture" that led to failures at institutions such as Mid Staffs. The shadow health secretary, Andy Burnham, has not backed a fixed ratio. Proposals for regular publication of ward staffing levels were backed by the health select committee. I will be covering the statement at 12.30pm in detail, as well as all the reaction. Here is the full agenda for the day. 9.30am: Police officers and police and crime commissioners give evidence to the Commons public administration committee about crime statistics. 9.45am: Jo Swinson, the business minister, launches an employee ownership report. 10am: David Anderson, the former Co-operative Financial Services chief executive, gives evidence to the Commons Treasury committee. 11.30pm: Nick Clegg takes questions in the Commons. 11.30am: Facebook and Twitter give evidence to the Commons culture committee on online safety. At 1pm Damian Green, the Home Office minister, Ed Vaizey, the culture minister, and Claire Perry, the prime minister's adviser on the commercialisation and sexualisation of childhoold, will give evidence. 12.30pm: Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, makes a statement in the Commons anouncing the government's final response to the Francis report on the Mid-Staffs hospital scandal. 1pm: Nick Boles, the planning minister, gives a speech to Bright Blue, the modernising Conservative pressure group, on “Who should a liberal vote for in 2015?” 2.45pm: Chief Constable Andy Bliss, the Association of Chief Police Officers lead on drugs, and Commander Simon Bray, the ACPO lead on psychoactive substances, give evidence to the Commons home affairs committee on drugs. Also at some point today Alex Salmond, Scotland's first minster, and John Swinney, Scotland's finance minister, will be publishing with their economic plans for an independent Scotland. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary before the Hunt statement at 12.30pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Thursday 19 September

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Thursday 19 September
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Wednesday including some in today's paper, are here.

results.webTitle: Tory MP Chloe Smith: we have to show why politics matters

fields.headline: Tory MP Chloe Smith: we have to show why politics matters
fields.trailText: Smith says it is in the interests of all democrats for young people to vote and be interested in politics
fields.bodyText: Chloe Smith is not a politician given to dramatic statements. Serious and super-loyal, the former minister for political reform cautions me from the outset not to interpret any of remarks as being remotely at odds with Tory policy. There doesn't seem to be much danger of that, as the 31-year-old is prone to sticking warnings about the urgent need for deficit reduction and following the economic plan on to the end of her sentences. But scratch beneath the surface, and Smith has some profound criticisms of the broader political system that may come as a surprise. There is a significant problem about to hit Westminster, she believes. The old language of the parties may not be so relevant any more. And there is a risk to the whole legitimacy of parliament if it is not sorted out. The issue? Millions of British young people are not voting, have never voted and are unlikely to do so unless there is reversal of a downward trend in electoral participation that seems to have started 50 years ago and accelerated in the last two decades. "I think there is an existential problem coming here for traditional forms of British democracy, which it is in everyone's interests, all of us as democrats to respond to," she said. "We have to demonstrate what politics is for, why a young person's individual action in voting matters and we have to explain the ideas we commonly use in politics more clearly. Some of the old shorthand about left and right for example doesn't necessarily work any more. "It's absolutely necessary to use clear new language to talk about big issues that face this generation perhaps even more starkly." She is most troubled by the idea it could start to lead to "generations far apart and not talking to each other". It is a further worry that those who do not vote first time may never get into the habit, turning young non-voters into older ones as time goes on – a pattern that already appears to have begun. "The statistics show quite clearly that young people are turning out much less than previously and it's a worse problem than at the equivalent points in previous generations," she said. Smith has a particular interest in the issue as one of the youngest MPs in the House of Commons. Voted into parliament in a by-election at 27, the MP became what is known as the "baby of the house" – in common with Charles Kennedy, Sarah Teather, David Lammy and Jo Swinson before her. Since then, she has had a curious career of swift promotionthrough the ranks of government, before taking the surprise decision to resign as the minister in charge of political and constitutional reform this autumn. During those three years, she braved much media speculation about her relative inexperience, with one story emerging that she was promoted to the Treasury by the prime minister on the mistaken premise that she was a former accountant. Later, her stint in George Osborne's team came to an end not long after what was described as a "car crash" interview with Jeremy Paxman on the BBC's Newsnight, defending a fuel duty policy, which ended with him asking whether she ever thought she was incompetent. From there, Smith was shifted sideways into the Cabinet Office, taking on responsibility for electoral issues within her brief. When she stepped down out of the blue in September, the point did not go unnoticed that one of the reasons was a desire to spend more time encouraging young people to make a difference in politics. However, Smith firmly denies that her youth was anything to do with the briefness of her career in government, insisting all her colleagues were "warm and welcoming from the prime minister down". She brushes off the idea that any of them were resentful of her early success, insists no one was ever dismissive of her age and points out that she had a job in the private sector at Deloitte before running for parliament.So why didn't she stay on and help achieve the change she wanted to see as a minister responsible for electoral reform? Smith, who got engaged after a whirlwind romance last year andmarried in November, says her priority in stepping down was to spend more time on constituency work. In her Norwich North seat, where she has a slim majority after booting out Labour in 2009,she is running an ambitious campaign to halve youth unemployment and engage young people in the problems that affect them. However, she rejects some of the policies that Labour argues could help engage young people, such as lowering the age of voting to 16.Instead, Smith argues the best solution is making young adults realise what they are missing out on. "Young people have to come and say this for themselves and it's all up for grabs. Most important of all is encouraging people into the public sphere to say this for themselves," she says. But what about those who feel like the system is stacked against them, who don't know how to vote and feel the world of Westminster is a foreign country?"I turn that question back to those who choose not to participate in traditional politics. My message would be get involved and change it. Politics will go ahead without you if you're not in it," she said. After a moment of contemplation, she softens the argument slightly, acknowledging the role of this generation of politicians in motivating future ones. She would love to see more young people in parliament -in fact sees it as necessary to have a more youthful, energetic voice in the chamber if it is going to be more reflective of society as a whole. "One of the most important things we can do as those who are currently elected representativesis to reach a hand out to others and explain why politics is worth being involved in," she said. "We must at all costs avert a generation of divergence where, to borrow from Disraeli, you have two nations who have no sympathy and no intercourse and might think they have nothing to do with each other at all. That isn't true yet in our society and we mustn't let it become true." Her devotion to the Conservative party, which started age 18, is also more practical and less tribal than it first appears. The way to explain parties to young people is simply as a way of "working together to get things done," she says. "Do we need political parties? I think it's the ideas that are the most important and we need to explain those clearly," she adds. Smith acknowledges some of the headlines about lack of voting among the young make for "depressing" reading but urges everyone not to see an inevitable decline. She wants the public and politicians alike to wake up to the problem and take action to change it – for the sake of future generations. "The next election is crucially important for young people. This is about real people, not numbers, and the choices we can all take for the future of our country.".

results.webTitle: Politics Weekly podcast: David Runciman on democracy in crisis

fields.headline: Politics Weekly podcast: David Runciman on democracy in crisis
fields.trailText: Cambridge political scientist David Runciman says the survival of liberal democracy is not inevitable. He joins Anne Perkins to discuss his new book The Confidence Trap
fields.bodyText: Western democracy has not been in good health since the economic crash of 2008. In the eurozone, elected politicians have struggled to force through austerity budgets demanded by the officials offering bailouts. Incumbents from across the political spectrum have been booted out of office, while others rise to power only to confront the same short-term problems of attempting to a balance the books without ruining their own hopes for re-election. Meanwhile, in states such as China that continue to eschew democracy, there is continuing economic growth and growing global power. It all adds up to a cause for great concern, according to the political scientist David Runciman. His new book is The Confidence Trap: A History of Democracy in Crisis from World War I to the Present - a critique of the complacency of many in the west and the assumption that democracy is inevitable. And it's not just economic factors that threaten democracy. For Runciman, The Guardian's revelations of massive state surveillance show another drawback of complacency by electorates - allowing security apparatus to grow without sufficient oversight. Leave your thoughts below - or follow Politics Weekly on Soundcloud.

results.webTitle: Politics Weekly podcast: Douglas Alexander on Labour's foreign policy

fields.headline: Politics Weekly podcast: Douglas Alexander on Labour's foreign policy
fields.trailText: Labour's shadow foreign secretary Douglas Alexander discusses his party's crucial intervention in the parliamentary debate on Syria, Britain's future relations with the European Union, the emerging power dominance of China and his role as Labour's election co-ordinator
fields.bodyText: Labour's foreign secretary Douglas Alexander joins Jonathan Freedland to discuss the major issues facing Britain in the coming years: the rise of China, mass surveilance by intelligence agencies, the European Union and the Middle East. His new book Influencing Tomorrow: Future Challenges For British Foreign Policy takes in contributions from a selection of authors. Back in August the government was reportedly furious that Labour refused to back David Cameron's motion paving the way for military strikes on Syria - a move that can plausibly be said to have altered history, as the Barack Obama appeared to have second thoughts on intervention once Britain had ruled out taking part. Another issue a future Labour government would be forced to address is Britain's place in the European Union - and how to frame the debate during any future referendum campaign. Leave your thoughts below.

results.webTitle: Obama: rebranding healthcare will be difficult given partisan politics

fields.headline: Obama: rebranding healthcare will be difficult given partisan politics
fields.trailText: President conceded that government-run IT is inefficient, but said glitches hard to fix when 'one side of Capital Hill invested in failure'
fields.bodyText: Barack Obama admitted on Tuesday that the task of rebranding his troubled healthcare reforms will be challenging because of the partisan nature of Washington politics, with Republicans "invested in failure". Speaking before an audience of business leaders, the president acknowledged he had underestimated how fierce opposition would prove to be, after forcing the Affordable Care Act through congress with only Democrat votes. “We should have anticipated that [the ideological resistance] would result in a rockier roll-out than if Republicans and Democrats had both been invested in success,” he told the Wall Street Journal conference. “One side of Capital Hill is invested in failure and that makes the iterative process of fixing glitches as they come up and fine-tuning the law more difficult. We are going to have to rebrand and re-market and that will be challenging in this political environment.” In the latest of a series of mea culpas over the troubled launch of an online exchange for health insurance, Obama also admitted the government's record in delivering complex computer projects should have rung alarm bells earlier. “There is a larger problem that I could have identified,” he added. “The way the government does procurement and IT is generally very inefficient. There is probably no bigger gap with the private sector than the way we do IT.” However, Obama continued his recent efforts to diversify support outside Washington with a direct appeal to corporate financial interests. “The thing that hasn't been talked about a lot is cost and that affects the bottom lines of everybody here,” he said. “Over the last three years we have seen healthcare costs grow at the slowest rate for 50 years and that's saving us money.” On Monday night, Obama reached in the opposite direction with a direct appeal to 200,000 grassroots supporters from his election campaign for their help in bolstering health reform, but neither strategy has yet succeeded in overcoming what is fast becoming the biggest political test of his presidency. Earlier on Tuesday, the latest in a series of hearings in Congress produced more testimony suggesting that remaining technical problems with the website were substantial. Henry Chao, a top official in charge of managing the construction of healthcare.gov, revealed that the section of the healthcare web site that processes subsidy payments to insurers might not be built until mid-January. Only 60% to 70% of the back end of the web site has been built and incomplete functionalities include "the back office systems, the accounting systems, the payment systems," Chao told the hearing. Efforts to show the positive results of health reforms suffered another blow when CNN reported that a woman used by the White House to demonstrate insurance savings during a launch event in the Rose Garden had now been told her premiums would go up. After initially writing a heartfelt thank-you letter to the White House, Jessica Sanford said she received another letter informing her the Washington state health exchange had miscalculated her eligibility for a tax credit. Speaking in Florida, health and human services secretary Kathleen Sebelius on Tuesday encouraged people to return to the HealthCare.gov website, promising them a better experience. "It is far faster than it was when we first opened," Sebelius said, in remarks reported by Reuters. "The error rate is down to less than 1% and the volume capacity is continuing to be added to." With opinion polls suggesting Obama's approval ratings have slumped to their lowest level yet over the affair, it may take more than speeches to business leaders and campaign supporters to help overcome the political damage from such high-profile setbacks. But Obama also received a rare vote of support from a prominent Republican during the WSJ conference as New Jersey government Chris Christie, a front-runner for the party's 2016 nomination, said Obama should be allowed to govern. "He just won [re-election] a year ago, and as we shove him out the door, we minimize his ability to be an effective executive," Christie said. The moderate Republican also mocked those in is party who led the fight to de-fund the Affordable Care Act and led to a 16-day federal government shutdown. “[Republicans] just did not have an endgame to a strategy,” said Christie. “Since Obamacare is still currently being funded and the government is re-opened, maybe I’m too simple, but it appears to me that the strategy of de-funding it by closing the government failed.”

results.webTitle: MPs question police over Plebgate: Politics live blog

fields.headline: MPs question police over Plebgate: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including the Commons home affairs committee taking evidence from the Police Federation and chief constables over Plebgate and David Cameron and Ed Miliband at PMQs
fields.bodyText: Well, what have we learnt from that? The first point is that Deborah Glass should probably be running a police force. She seemed far more impressive than the three chief constables giving evidence to the committee. And as for the Police Federation trio? Well, it's already clear they are going to get a bit of kicking when the committee reports next week. It has not been a good afternoon for the police. Here are the main news points. • The three Police Federation officers accused of giving a misleading account of their meeting with Andrew Mitchell have refused to give him a proper apology. They would not go beyond the generalised apology offered earlier this week, and refused to accept they offered a misleading account of what was said. • But the chief constables of the three forces employing the three officers have apologised to Mitchell for what happened. • David Shaw, the West Mercia chief constable, has said he wants a fresh inquiry into the allegation that the three officers were guilty of misconduct. But his two chief constable colleagues, Andy Parker from Warwickshire and Chris Sims from West Midlands, have rejected calls for a new inquiry. Keith Vaz, the home affairs committee chairman, has described this as "very disappointing". • Vaz has described the evidence of the three Police Federation officers as "most unsatisfactory". • Deborah Glass, the IPCC deputy chair, has said that she thought what the three officers did amounted to gross misconduct, not just misconduct. The first draft of the investigation said they had a case to answer for misconduct, but this recommendations was ignored. That's all from me. Thanks for the comments. Vaz concludes. He says he is glad all three chief constables have apologised to Andrew Mitchell. Members of the committee thinks David Shaw was right to reopen the investigation, he says. He says the MPs think the other two other chief constables are wrong not to support that. And that's it. I will post a short summary in a moment. Sims says the MPs have not seen the best of the Police Federation officers. His officer (Christopher Jones) is a good officer, he says. Sims says this episode has highlighted difficulties with the status of the Police Federation. He also addressed this in his written evidence. One of the key issues arising from this case is the role of the Police Federation. It has been remarked that police leaders are in some way ‘afraid’ of the Federation. Let me say from a West Midlands perspective, where I have imposed Regulation A19, introduced wide ranging changes to working practices and taken more than £100m from our budget, I would not be regarded as a “Federation Poodle”. Nor are we soft on discipline as 21 officers have been dismissed without notice in the past 18 months. The Police Federation’s status, however, is ambiguous and unsatisfactory. The 1969 legislation is virtually silent on the right of the organisation and its members to campaign. While condemning wholeheartedly the events of 12 October, I do recognise that, in the absence of formal union rights, officers need to be able to highlight issues that impact upon pay, status and conditions. The Review by Sir David Normington may shed light on this issue. In the meantime, I have focused the ‘management action’ in the West Midlands at the conclusion of this investigation on changing the ‘Facilities Agreement’ the force has with the Federation to make the repeat of such an incident impossible. Sims says it would have been "instructive" if Deborah Glass had had to go through the rigour of taking a decision herself. But he says his officers should never have got involved in this political campaign. Parker says officers from Warwickshire Police Federation will not be campaigning like this again. Michael Ellis, the Conservative, is asking questions now. Q: Is it right for the police to investigate themselves? Sims says the force had to follow a legal process. Shaw says Jerry Reakes-Williams told the committee that he came under no pressure over his conclusions. Ellis says it looks as if the favourable version of the report has been chosen over the unfavourable one. The Police Federation evidence clearly did not impress the Tory MP Mark Reckless. Sims says the assistant chief inspector who looked at the case on his behalf thought there was no need for misconduct proceedings. Mark Reckless, the Conservative MP, is asking questions now. Parker says he would not want to spend any more money on this case when those who reviewed the paperwork thought there was not enough evidence to justify misconduct proceedings. But, Reckless says, the investigating officer, Jerry Reakes-Williams, thought there was a case for misconduct proceedings. Labour's Steve McCabe is asking the questions now. McCabe says Sims and Parker do not see the case for new investigation. But Shaw does. Back to the hearing, Chris Sims, the West Midlands chief constable, and Andy Parker, the Warwickshire chief constable, are both giving evidence now with David Shaw. They are asked if they will apologise to Andrew Mitchell. Sims and Parker do both offer apologies. But Sims and Parker both say they do not agree with Shaw about the need for a new investigation. Q: So why are you apologising? Because of the hurt to Mitchell, Sims says. He also says Chris Jones, the West Midlands officers involved, was only involved to a limited extent. Q: You must be aware of what people like the prime minister have said. But it has no impact on you. Sims says he must act on legal grounds. Q: How many officers have been dismissed for misconduct? Nine in five years, says Parker. He says Warwickshire is the smallest force. Shaw says six people have been dismissed from West Midlands in the last two and a half years. The investigation into the three officers was carried out by two officers. The most senior was Jerry Reakes-Williams, who gave evidence earlier. He was assisted by a Detective Inspector Smith. The first draft of their report contained conflicting recommendations. Smith thought the three did not have a case to answer for misconduct. But Reakes-Williams did. Here is the passage from the section written by Reakes-Williams explaining why he thought they had a case to answer for misconduct. I have read the IO’s report, and understand the conclusions he has reached and his rationale. However, my view is that the officers do have a case to answer for misconduct. Without repeating unnecessarily aspects already covered in detail, I set out below my rationale ... I have already stated that Mr MITCHELL later provided more detail than he did to the Officers at the meeting on 12th October. However, I would make the following comments in support of my view about a case to answer for misconduct, bearing in mind the points from the Standards of Professional Behaviour listed above. Why, if he was not satisfied that Mr MITCHELL had given a full account, was Sergeant HINTON ready to ‘move on’? (Page 6 of the meeting transcript) This surely implies that he was happy with, or at least accepted, the explanation provided. Why did three experienced police officers not press Mr MITCHELL for the full explanation they wanted during the meeting? He gave an answer, but they do not go on to indicate that they are not satisfied that he has given a full account. Sergeant JONES does not challenge Mr MITCHELL when the latter says ‘You asked me in good faith what I said and I told you’ (Page 11). Sergeant HINTON also says ‘....I understand that you are saying what you said now.....’ (page 18) and ‘I appreciate your candour....’ (page 19). These statements are difficult to align with later statements to the press that claim that Mr MITCHELL did not tell them what he had said. I cannot see how the Officers can claim that Mr MITCHELL ‘refused’ to tell them what he said, when, as outlined above, they did not seek clarification from him during the meeting. Indeed, they appear to have accepted that he had given them his account. I think that an ordinary member of the public, listening to the Officers speaking to the media after the meeting, would have interpreted their words as meaning that Mr MITCHELL would not give any account of what occurred in Downing Street on 19th September 2012. This is clearly not the case. 7. The Officers have therefore given an account of the meeting to the media that was inaccurate and misleading and contrary to the elements of the Standard of Professional Behaviour listed above. What I do not see from the information gained through the investigation is a malicious attempt to deliberately misrepresent what took place at the meeting. It seems to me that it was more a case of being poorly prepared, badly advised and inexperienced in dealing with such intense media scrutiny. The Officers were somewhat ‘caught in the headlights’, coming straight out of the meeting to face the assembled media without taking any time to discuss what had taken place between themselves and to agree a reasoned, balanced and accurate response. There is an element of recklessness here because it is inconceivable to me that the Officers would not have been aware that the media were gathered and would have wanted an account to go out on the 6 o’clock news. I have considered whether ALL of the officers have a case to answer. Although it was Inspector MacKaill who acted as the main spokesman after the meeting, it is clear that all the Officers spoke to the media either immediately after the meeting or subsequently, and all gave the same message. In interview, they have all stated their agreement with the information given to the media. In my view, this makes them all jointly responsible. By giving a misleading account of what took place at the meeting, I believe the Officers have a case to answer for misconduct and bringing discredit on the Police Service. And in his written evidence to the committee Chris Sims, the West Midlands chief constable, implied he would be apologising to Andrew Mitchell. Finally, and most importantly, I would turn to Mr Mitchell who although not a complainant, is very much at the heart of this incident. I would not wish to use the impersonal medium of a Select Committee to issue an apology, but I fully recognise the personal and professional cost this has had on him and his family. I have today written to Mr Mitchell seeking an early opportunity to meet with him to discuss these issues and to thank him for continuing to work with the local police in his constituency capacity. Sims also defended the original decision not to find the officers guilty of misconduct. In his written evidence Andy Parker, the Warwickshire chief constable, said he stood by the investigation's conclusion that the officers were not guilty of misconduct. But he said the events were "deeply regrettable". It is deeply regrettable for all involved, that this situation has been reached. It is my view that the role of both the Federation and the media in choosing to significantly and deliberately escalate an incident in Downing Street (where an apology had already been offered and accepted) was badly misjudged and extremely regrettable. I believe there are clear lessons to be learnt for both the police service and, in particular, the Police Federation. Whilst they have every right to campaign on behalf of their members and oppose measures which they believe will be damaging for the police service, the way they exercise that responsibility clearly needs to be subject to close and careful review, and I welcome the fact that the national Federation are currently in the process of doing that. I also regret the significant impact that this incident has undoubtedly had upon Mr Mitchell and his family and the role of a Warwickshire Police officer in adding to the pressure brought to bear upon him at an already very difficult time. Here is the conclusion from David Shaw's written evidence to the committee. The vast majority of police officers and staff in West Mercia Police conduct themselves with the utmost integrity. As with any Police Force, a very small minority do not live up to the standards that I and the public rightly expect of them. When that occurs, it is a huge disappointment to me and to the public. West Mercia Police is committed to identifying and dealing robustly with misconduct by police officers. We have a very strong track record of doing so. Whether or not the IPCC and others agree with the conclusions reached by the West Mercia Police Investigators and the Appropriate Authority in this case, I am entirely satisfied that those who reached them acted rationally and entirely in good faith. I acknowledge that there have been procedural errors in the context of an extremely complex statutory regime for dealing with police misconduct. I therefore intend to commission an independent peer review into what went wrong with the processes in this case and how our processes can be improved. The committee has now released a large file of written evidence (153 pages) on its website (pdf). I will pick out the highlights in a moment. Shaw says Glass wrote a letter alluding to two versions of the report. He says he reviewed the process behind the investigation. There was a "flaw" in the process. The report presented to the three forces did not have recommendations. And it should have done. On that basis he can sent the whole thing back to someone else for a complete redetermination. Vaz says the committee has seen three versions of the report: a draft one (a), which contains Jerry Reakes-Williams' recommendations; and two others. Shaw says he has approached HMIC about this. They are looking for another chief constable to oversee a new inquiry. Q: Why don't you look at this yourself? This decision will prolong the process. Shaw says he could have re-opened this himself. But he chose not to. Q: Does this open you up to judicial review? Shaw says he hopes not. But there is a risk of this, he says. David Shaw, the West Mercia chief constable, starts with an apology to Andrew Mitchell. It is a "profound, unreserved apology", he says. He wrote to Mitchell on Monday to offer his apology, he says. • The three Police Federation officers refused to issue a personal apology to Andrew Mitchell. They would not go beyond the generalised apology they offered earlier this week. • They rejected claims that they lied about their meeting with Mitchell. • Keith Vaz said their evidence was "most unsatisfactory". Keith Vaz says the committee has found the evidence from the three "most unsatisfactory". He reminds them that giving false evidence to a committee is a contempt of parliament. As the hearing wraps up, MacKaill says he wants to clarify one answer he gave earlier. There was a misconduct complaint against him eight years ago, he says. Nicola Blackwood, the Conservative, goes next. Q: The nature of your apology will be confusing to people. You told Mitchell you would have to report his implication that Scotland Yard officers lied. You suggested that you had no problem with his account. Then you went outside and said he should resign. That must have been "very confusing" for Mitchell. Why won't you apologise? Blackwood has to put the question three times before she gets an answer. Jones finally says he does not think she has set out the position. Vaz intervenes. Can MacKaill answer? MacKaill says there was no intention to resign. Hinton says, with hindsight, Mitchell should have been included in the apology issued this seek. But that should be offered to him personally. Hinton says Mitchell should be included in the apology issued on Monday. Hinton says he cannot apologise for something he did not do. Hinton says honesty and integrity is central to the police. It is central to his values, And to Jones's and MacKaill's too. Julian Huppert, the Lib Dem MP goes next. Q: Did you prepare what you were going to say to the media? Hinton says they did not. That was the problem. He had not had media training, he says. But Jones and MacKaill had had media training. James Clappison goes next. He says Mitchell's attitude at the meeting was "contrite". Chris Ruane, the Labour MP, goes next. Q: Do you think Mitchell was right to resign? Yes, says MacKaill. Q: Don't you feel any sympathy for him? Don't you think you owe him an apology? MacKaill says Mitchell had dismissed police integrity. At the time MacKaill did not know what subsequently emerged about the Number 10 incident. Vaz goes next. Q: Did you know that the National Federation had stopped using John Gaunt because he had stopped following the instructions of his client? Hinton says he did not know that. Q: You had a PR person with you. He drove to the meeting with you. He was receiving calls from the media. And he told you to finish the meeting before the 6pm news. You were acting with a view to discredit a senior minister. MacKaill says he refutes that. Q: When you spoke to BBC Midlands you were not telling the truth. Hinton says he does not accept that. Q: You said Mitchell spoke with candour. Then you said he had not given a full account. Hinton says the reference to "candour" was a reference to the way Mitchell made it clear he had not used the word "pleb". Q: How did the media find out about the location of the meeting? Mitchell would not have told them. Hinton says the Police Federation's PR adviser, John Gaunt, was under orders not to tell the media about the location of the meeting. Vaz says Gaunt will be called to give evidence. Michael Ellis says Hinton's use of the phrase "that woman" was a clear reference to Theresa May. It is disgraceful. Hinton should apologise, he says. Keith Vaz goes next. He quotes from the transcript. Hinton said the Police Federation had problems with "that woman" from the Conservatives. Who was that? Hinton says he does not recall using the phrase "that woman". He thinks there is a mistake in the transcript. Labour's David Winnick goes next. Jones says the campaign against cuts was a legitimate one. Q: Haven't your actions undermined the campaign against the cuts? Jones says things have been taken out of context. If they were to run the campaign again, they would do it in a different way. At the time his members were very supportive, Jones says. Mark Reckless, the Conservative MP, goes next. Hinton says he was subject to the investigation, but not involved in it. Q: Were you there on Police Federation time? Yes, says Hinton. He says full-time federation officials are paid by the police to carry out union duties. Q: Should taxpayers' money be spent on this? Hinton says they should be able to represent their members. Police officers do not have normal employment rights, he says. Steve McCable, the Labour MP, goes next. He says the "plebgate" affair has distracted attention from the campaign against cuts. Keith Vaz is asking the questions now. Q: This was about the cuts. Did you get a letter from the chairman of the national Police Federation (the late Paul McKeever) asking you not to carry on with your campaign. MacKaill said he did get a letter. But the Police Federation had no power to tell them to stop. Q: The national federation was concerned about the personal nature of your campaign. MacKaill says they had the support of their local members, and the public. Vaz says the meeting ended at 5.45pm, even though they had not yet discussed the cuts. He suggests that was because he wanted to get on the evening news. Hinton says he was no there to conduct an interview with Mitchell. He was there to clear the air. He says that when Mitchell said he did not use the words "pleb" or "moron", that was the first time he had been that specific. Clappison says the officers said that Mitchell did not tell them what he said. But he had done. James Clappison, the Conservative, goes next. Q: You said Andrew Mitchell did not tell you want he said. But he did. MacKaill says his comment has been taken out of context. He reads a full version of what he said. MacKail goes on: He gave an account but he did not tell us exactly what he said. Q: Do you think it is in your interests for this to be redetermined? Jones says the chief constables have reviewed this. Q: So you think all is fine now? Hinton says they submitted themselves to an inquiry. Q: But the chief constable now wants this reopened. Hinton says he did not know that. MacKaill says he would be surprised by that. He would like to know the reasons. Jones says the police should follow due process. He thinks due process has been followed. Jones has served 28 years in the police, MacKaill 22 years, and Hinton 21 years. Q: Do you know that the report said you gave a misleading account? Jones says he knows that now. The three Police Federation officers are here. They are: Sergeant Chris Jones from West Midlands Police Federation, Detective Sergeant Stuart Hinton from Warwickshire Police Federation and Inspector Ken MacKaill from West Mercia Police Federation. Keith Vaz starts. Q: You issues an apology. It seems very half-hearted. Is it an apology to Mitchell. Jones says the officers did represent that meeting correctly. Q: So there is no apology to Mitchell? Jones says is an apology for the choreography. Q: You think you have done nothing wrong. That's right, says Jones. MacKaill says he gave what he thought was an accurate account to the media. • Deborah Glass, the IPCC deputy chair, has said that she thought the three officers should have been found guilty of gross misconduct. She said she was surprised when the final report dropped the conclusion that they were guilty of misconduct and instead said there was no case to answer. • She said that she would consider whether there were legal grounds for the IPCC to reopen the inquiry. Keith Vaz is asking questions now. Q: Is it a good diea for police officers to carry recording cameras, as David Davis recommends? Owers says she would not like to extrapolate from one case. But she would point out that when the PACE said interviews should be recorded the police objected. Yet now it is recognised as as good idea. Steve McCabe, the Labour MP, goes next. Q: Would you says it is "almost impossible" to understand how the police arrived at this decision. Glass says the evidence and the conclusion are so at odd she felt the need to issue a statement. Q: Do you think Mitchell deserves an apology? Glass says she has focused on the misconduct. Q: Were you surprised how the police reacted to the Glass statement last week. Some officers said the IPCC should be abolished? Owers says she did not hear that. Owers says the IPCC can only supervise certain investigations because of its limited resources. That is unsatisfactory, she says. Julian Huppert, the Lib Dem MP, goes next. Q: Why did the IPCC go for a supervised investigation (ie, letting the police investigate this themselves, with supervision from the IPCC), instead of doing it themselves? Glass says the IPCC has to live in the real world. It has limited resources. Unlike the police, it cannot just borrow officers from forces. I would have loved to have done it. But it just was not practical. Mark Reckless, a Conservative, goes next. Q: The final report says the IO [investigation officer] thought there was no case to answer. Who did you take that to be? Glass says she would have taken that to refer to Jerry Reakes-Williams. She says the authorities would have been entitled to ignore his recommendation. The conclusions were "extraordinary", she says. Q: Why did the IPCC take over the investigation? Glass says that to do that just to rewrite the conclusions would have been an abuse of process. She says if there is a way of reopening it lawfully, she would. Q: If the chief constables say to us they would like the IPCC to now deal with this, you will take legal advice to see if you can do this? Glass says she will look into this. The chief constables themselves definitely can reopen this investigation, she says. Nicola Blackwood, a Conservative, goes next. Glass says nothing gave her concern until she saw the final report on 28 August recommending no further action. Michael Ellis, a Conservative, goes next. Q: The investigating officer came to a conclusion. But he was usurped. Is this a whitewash? Glass says we are certainly dealing with confusion. Q: But do you feel that this is an attempt at a whitewash? In your statement last week you said the Police Federation were running a political campaign. Glass says she thinks the conclusions were wrong. Chris Ruane, the Labour MP, goes next. Q: Mitchell had a 45-second conversation. This has now gone on for a year. Is that too long? Glass says the IPCC did not deal with the Number 10 incident. That was investigated by the Metropolitan Police. Owers says the IPCC has limited reources. Owers says the appropriate authorities knew what Glass's view was. James Clappison, a Conservative, goes next. He quotes from a letter from Andy Parker, the Warwickshire chief constable, saying there were irregularities with the investigation. Glass says she was not aware of that at the time. Asked if she thought Andrew Mitchell gave a full account of what happened to the officers, she says he answered all the questions he was asked. The point about not giving a full account sounds like an ex post facto argument, she says. Dame Anne Owers, the IPCC chair, and Deborah Glass, the IPCC deputy chair, are giving evidence now. Glass says she thinks the report should have found the three guilty of gross misconduct. Q: Do you think they lied? Glass reads from the statement she put out last week. A misconduct should have determined that, she says. Q: You have been doing this for 13 years. Is there any precedent for a draft report saying misconduct, your advising gross misconduct, and then the final report being even weaker. Glass says it is not unusual for reports to change from draft to final version. But in this case she could not see how the conclusion was justified. Q: Do you think the IPCC should have carried out the investigation itself? Glass says she had no problem with the way the inquiry was carried out. It was just the conclusion she could not accept. She took legal advice on what she could do. That led to her putting out her statement. Here is a snap summary, • Keith Vaz has said that David Shaw, the West Mercia chief constable, has now changed his mind about whether the three officers should face disciplinary action. • Jerry Reakes-Williams, the Professional Standards officer who investigated the three officers, has confirmed that he originally concluded the officers were guilty of misconduct, but that he was overruled. • Reakes-Williams has said that he did not think the officers deliberately lied about their meeting with Andrew Mitchell. We are now on to the IPCC witnesses. Keith Vaz says RW has been very open. But the public will conclude you thought there was misconduct, you had a meeting with two deputy chief constables, and then that recommendation was removed. He says the chief constable of West Mercia has submitted evidence to the committee saying there was evidence of misconduct. It backs up what RW said. That will be published by the committee at 5.30, Vaz says. RW says no one put pressure on him to change his stance. Labour's Steve McCabe goes next. Q: How would you rate yourself as an investigator? RW says there may have been procedural issues. But at the time no one picked these up at the time, including the IPCC. Mark Reckless, a Conservative, goes next. Q: The IPCC told you not to share your conclusions with the authorities (representatives of the chief constable). But you did . Why? RW says he thought it was important that they were aware of the difference of opinion. Q: But you were the investigating officer? RW says it was clear he was the investigation officer. But in police terms his deputy may have been seen as the investigating officer. He says the IPCC told him to forward a copy of his report without the conclusions. Q: But you disobeyed that instruction? Yes, says RW. Q: And you are supposed to include all matters in a report that you see fit. RW says he was acting with the full knowledge of the IPCC, and of his legal adviser. Labour's Chris Ruane goes next. Q: Are you sure minutes were not taken at the meeting with the deputy chief constables? RW says he took action points at all his meetings with the IPCC. Q: It is extraordinary that no one took notes? RW says it is easy to be wise with hindsight. He took notes in his policy file. Keith Vaz asks for a copy of those notes. Labour's David Winnick goes next. Q: Is there anything else Mitchell could have said at the meeting? RW says Mitchell was clear about what he did not say. But there is "doubt" about whether he gave a full account of everything said between him and the officers at Downing Street at the meeting with the Police Federation. That is why RW concluded that the officers were not guilty of gross misconduct,he says. Q: So you are saying there remains a question mark over what happened in Downing Street? Absolutely, says RW. Q: The Gaunt Brothers (a PR firm) issued a statement on behalf of the Police Federation officers before the meeting with Mitchell. Does not that suggest premeditation? RW says his investigation had a very narrow remit. He was asked to establish if the offices gave a false account of their meeting with Mitchell. Q: What is the worst thing that can happen to someone guilty of misconduct? A final written warning, says RW. Q: And what is the worst punishment for gross misconduct? Dismissal, says RW. Q: And is there anything less serious than misconduct. Management action, says RW. Nicola Blackwood, a Conservative, goes next. RW says he thinks there is doubt as to whether Mitchell gave a "full account" of what he said at Number 10 in the meeting with the Police Federation. In a later interview in the Telegraph Mitchell provided details that he did not give to the three Police Federation offices. On that basis you could argue that he did not give them a "full account". RW says he is surprised that the officers did not press Mitchell for more detail of what happened in Downing Street. Vaz is going on about the lack of a note. He seems surprised. Keith Vaz goes next. He asks if there was a minute of the meeting between RW and representatives of the three chief constables. RW says he would have taken a note in his policy book. Q: Were official minutes taken? No, says RW. Vaz says for a meeting with two deputy chief constables, and a legal adviser, somebody would have made a note. RW consults his notebook. It's his policy book, he says. James Clappison, a Conservative, goes next. Q: Did you make it clear to all three forces (represented by deputy chief constables in two cases) that there should be disciplinary action? Yes, says RW. He says he does not agree with their decision. But he understands their reasons. Clappison quotes from RW's report. RW said the officers gave an account of the meeting with Mitchell that was "inaccurate and misleading". Q: Your views were left hanging in the wind? RW says he works in a disciplined organisation. Sometimes you do not agree with every decision. But a collective decision has to be taken. Julian Huppert, the Lib Dem MP, goes next. Q: Several changes were made to you report. Why? RW says it is common to change the words of a report. Q: In every single case the changes made had the effect of softening the report. RW says he could not be sure. But the final report had to reflect the views of the appropriate authorities. Michael Ellis, a Conservative, goes next. Q: You thought the three officers should face disciplinary action for misconduct. But your junior disagreed. Yes, says RW. Q: You say the officers did not lie, but that they misled. What is the difference? RW says the different amounts to intent. If they intended to mislead the press, that would have been gross misconduct. But he does not think that was the case. Q: Your report went to the officers before it went to the IPCC. That was illegal, wasn't it? RW says he was acting under the directions of the IPCC. But clearly if you look at the regulations that is an irregularity. Keith Vaz is asking the questions. Q: It is up to the chief constables to submit the final conclusion, isn't it? RW says he had a meeting with representatives from the three forces. The IPCC had directed that they were not share their conclusions with the officers. On 31 July a copy of the report, without the conclusions, was sent to the authorities. On the following day he made it clear that there was a difference opinion over the conclusions. The chief constables had the final say. West Mercia and Warwickshire senior officers came back within 24 hours with a recommended conclusion. The IPCC wanted just one conclusion. Q: Is Andrew Mitchell owed an apology? Yes, says RW. Q: Has this had an impact on the reputation of the forces? Yes, says RW. Reakes-Williams (or RW as as will call him from now) confims that the final report did not include his original conclusion that there should be disciplinary action. Democracy Live is not working, but the parliamentary website is. Jerry Reakes-Williams, the Professional Standards officer for West Mercia Police, is giving evidence. He wrote the initial report on the conduct of the three Police Federation officers. He says the comments made by the Police Federation representatives did have the effect of misleading the public about that meeting. If he had thought officers had lied, he would have concluded they were guilty of gross misconduct. But misconduct is not the same as gross misconduct, he says. There is room for doubt as to whether Andrew Mitchel gave a "full account". On the balance of probabilities, he does not think the officers lied, he says. The hearing has started, but the live feed is not working. I'm trying a different source now. Last year police and crime commissioners were elected. They were supposed to hold the police to account. But, in relation to this aspect of the "plegbate" affair, at least two of the three police and crime commissioners involved have generally sided with the police. Ron Ball, the independent Warwickshire police commissioner issued a press notice last Tuesday saying the IPCC's handling of the matter had been "manifestly unfair". Three days later he put out a statement saying the statement made by the three Police Federation officers was "at best an error of judgment", but also renewing his criticism of the IPCC. He subsequently accused Andrew Mitchell's friends of "baying for blood". Bob Jones, the Labour West Midlands police commissioner, said in a statement that the IPCC had led to the three Police Federation officers suffering trial by media. And Bill Longmore, the independent West Mercia police and crime commissioner, put out a statement saying he was "surprised" that the IPCC has not taken over the investigation into the three officers. He then put out another statement saying he was keen to get the facts and would not comment further until after today's hearing. The Commons home affairs committee hearing on Plebgate will be starting soon. Here's the timetable. 2.45pm: Dame Anne Owers, chair of the Independent Police Complaints Commission, and Deborah Glass, the IPCC's deputy chair, give evidence to the Commons home affairs committee about the Plebgate controversy. At 3.15pm Chief Inspector Jerry Reakes-Williams, from Professional Standards for Warwickshire and West Mercia Police, gives evidence. At 3.45pm Detective Sergeant Stuart Hinton from Warwickshire Police Federation, Sergeant Chris Jones from West Midlands Police Federation and Inspector Ken MacKaill from West Mercia Police Federation give evidence. And at 4.30pm Chief Constable Andy Parker from Warwickshire Police, Chief Constable David Shaw from West Mercia Police and Chief Constable Chris Sims from West Midlands Police give evidence. I say "plebgate", but the hearing will not really touch on what Andrew Mitchell did or did not say to the police officers at Downing Street when they refused to open the gates for him as he was leaving on his bicycle last year. That has been investigated by the police, and a file is now with the Crown Prosecution Service which will decide whether officers will be charged. Instead this hearing is just about the conduct of the three Police Federation officers who met Mitchell to discuss the case and who then told journalists that he had refused to give a full account of what happened. West Midlands Police, West Mercia Police and Warwickshire Police decided not to discipline them. But the IPCC criticised this decision on the grounds that an investigation seemed to show that they had not told the truth about what Mitchell said. It was a small incident, involving four people. But what the Police Federation officers said that day helped to contribute to Mitchell's decision to resign as chief whip and it has become a story about whether police officers can be trusted, whether the Police Federation behaves responsibly and whether it is right for the police to investigate misconduct allegations against officers itself. (In other circles this is called "marking your own homework". As we in the press have discovered, this has rather gone out of fashion.) Here is some background reading before the hearing starts. • The press statement from Deborah Glass, the Independent Police Complaints Commission deputy chair, last week criticising West Mercia Police for not taking disciplinary action against the three officers and calling for that decision to be reconsidered. Here's an extract. The investigation by West Mercia Police concluded that although the Police Federation undoubtedly contributed to the pressure on Mr Mitchell and his decision to resign, none of the officers had a case to answer for misconduct or gross misconduct. The investigating officer concluded that while the federation representatives' comments to the media could be viewed as ambiguous or misleading, there was no deliberate intention to lie. I disagree. In my view, the evidence is such that a panel should determine whether the three officers gave a false account of the meeting in a deliberate attempt to support their MPS colleague and discredit Mr Mitchell, in pursuit of a wider agenda. In my opinion the evidence indicates an issue of honesty and integrity, not merely naïve or poor professional judgment. • The full six-page statement from Glass setting out her case in more detail (pdf). This give more details of what happened when Mitchell met the three officers, and why their account of it afterwards was misleading. It is clear from the recording that at the start of the meeting Mr Mitchell makes his apology, and hopes he can “draw a line” under the incident. He answers the question of “what he did say”: “The incident was very brief I complied with the officer and I picked up my bicycle but I did say under my breath but audibly, in frustration, I thought you lot were supposed to f***ing help us and it is for that I apologise and I am grateful to that officer for accepting my apology and I should never have said it and I will never do it again...” ... Immediately after the meeting the West Mercia officer, who did not speak during the meeting, is the principal spokesman when the officers speak to the waiting media .... When asked by a reporter if Mr Mitchell should resign he says: “I think Mr Mitchell now has no option but to resign, he’s continuing to refuse to elaborate on what happened, I think his position is untenable.” Immediately following this interview all three officers gave separate interviews along similar lines to various media. • The transcript of Mitchell's 45-minute meeting with the three officers on 12 October 2012. • A joint statement from Warwickshire, West Midlands and West Mercia police responding to the IPCC statement. Despite a thorough investigation under the supervision of the IPCC we do not believe that there is sufficient evidence to support the view that the officers concerned should face misconduct proceedings. Our view is that the officers have demonstrated poor judgement in arranging and attending the meeting in the first place. In light of this our position is that management action is a proportionate response. It is important to emphasise these points; i) Mr Mitchell has not made a complaint against police; ii) Had the IPCC decided at any stage during the investigation to treat this as a managed or an independent investigation they had the power to direct the forces to convene misconduct proceedings. They have chosen not to exercise these powers despite being invited to reconsider and reminded of their options as recently as last week. iii) At any stage during a supervised investigation the IPCC can direct further work to be done and they have not done so in this case. • The Police Federation's initial response to the IPCC statement. The Police Federation of England and Wales welcome the conclusions of the IPCC-supervised investigation into the actions of three Police Federation representatives, which found no evidence of misconduct. The IPCC had the choice at the outset of this investigation either to conduct an independent, managed or supervised investigation, and it chose to supervise. The investigation team has reached its conclusion and in accordance with statutory regulations, a decision has been made by the appropriate authority that there is no case to answer. Against this factual background, we are therefore shocked that the IPCC Commissioner, Deborah Glass, would then appoint herself judge and jury by releasing her personal view that she disagrees with the findings and asks the public to decide. This is done with the full knowledge that her status and title will naturally undermine the considered findings of the investigation in the eyes of the public, whereas in fact those investigating and deciding the case are the proper arbiters in this matter. • An account of the partial apology that the three Police Federation officers issued on Monday. We acknowledge the investigation's criticism relating to our poor judgement in talking to the media following the meeting with Andrew Mitchell, for which we take this opportunity to apologise. We would like to emphasise (as we did to the investigation) that in no way did any of us ever plan or intend to mislead anyone about what occurred during this meeting or otherwise. Here are the main points from the urgent question on Grangemouth. I have taken it from Graeme Wearden's business live blog. Graeme has been covering the Grangemouth story in great detail. • The UK government hopes that the Grangemouth petrochemical plant can still be saved, despite its owner Ineos announcing plans today to shut it down. Energy and climate change secretary Ed Davey told MPs repeatedly that the government wants the plant to stay open if at all possible. It would still consider a business case to provide investment to help keep the plant running. Davey also confirmed that detailed contingency plans have been drawn up to protect firms and customers from running out of fuel and chemical supplies. He will meet with MPs at 4.15pm to discuss the issue in more detail. • Many MPs are very worried about the impact on the Scottish economy, and the knock-on effects on the UK chemicals industry. Michael Moore, Liberal Democrat MP, called it "An act of industrial vandalism not seen in decades". • MPs were critical of both Ineos and the unions, for allowing the dispute to reach this point. Alistair Darling, former chancellor, said it would be tragic to lose the plant to an industrial dispute reminiscent of the 1970s, while Sir Malcolm Bruce said both sides were guilty of "a degree of intransigence". Tom Watson MP called for an independent assessment of Grangemouth's finances. • David Cameron has announced a review of green energy taxes after saying they had helped push up household bills to "unacceptable" levels. He made the announcement at PMQs, after Ed Miliband challenged him to say if he agreed with Sir John Major and Labour about the need to recoup money from the energy companies. (See 1.08pm and 1.44pm.) The Conservative MP Zac Goldsmith, a strong environmentalist, took to Twitter to criticise Cameron for going back on green pledges he made before 2010. • John Bercow, the Commons speaker, has rebuked Cameron for calling Miliband a "conman". Bercow made the point in an intervention during PMQs. Let me just say, I let it go the first time, the word 'conman' is frankly unparliamentary. The prime minister is a man of great versatility in the use of language - it's a bit below the level. We'll leave it there, it's a bit below the level. • Ineos has announced it is closing the petrochemical plant at Grangemouth following a bitter row over pay and conditions, putting 800 workers' jobs at risk. My colleague Graeme Wearden has more details on the business live blog. • David Davis, an ally of Andrew Mitchell, has has called for a royal commission to investigate the "crisis of ethics" in the police, as three chief constables prepare for questioning by a committee of MPs over issues raised by the Plebgate affair. As Rowena Mason reports, Davis said police should wear microphones and cameras to record their actions to address a decline in public trust – an idea that has won the support of Nick Herbert, a former policing minister, who said the idea was "worth piloting". The call came as Sir Bernard Hogan-Howe, Britain's most senior officer, admitted the Plebgate row had clouded the public view of Scotland Yard and had taken too long to deal with.Speaking on LBC on Wednesday, he said: "During the time this thing has been an issue, the Met has been performing better than ever. We've just got to live with the reality - the newspaper headlines, the fact that you're talking about it, clouds the fact that crime's coming down at its fastest for 30 years. • Mike Penning, a welfare minister, has admitted that the government does not know how many people the benefit cap is forcing into work, after a new study said the flagship welfare policy was not helping the unemployed or saving money. • The Ministry of Justice has announced plans to help control the cost of motoring, including "a crackdown on whiplash fraud, a freeze on MOT test prices and a scheme designed to reduce the cost of fuel at motorway service stations". • Danny Alexander, the chief secretary to the Treasury, has said that British businesses should do their bit to revive the economy by investing some of the £500bn they have in their reserves. Here's an excerpt from a press notice about a speech he is giving tonight. He will call on business leaders to invest some of their £500 billion cash balances - enough for 50 Olympic games - now to ensure the economy hits ‘top gear’. Had businesses increased their investment by just 10% in 2012, he will say, then the level of GDP would be £12bn higher - almost a whole percentage point growth in GDP ... Cash balances in non-financial businesses have risen by £14 billion in the last quarter and £104 billion since pre crisis levels - a 26% increase since the financial crisis. Cash balances now stand at over £500 billion. At the same time business investment is currently 25% below its pre-recession peak. • Government plans to go ahead with the next election on the basis of an individual voter register, as opposed to the current household register, have been given a boost after an experiment suggested nearly 80% of the electorate could be transferred to an individual register automatically. • The Office for National Statistics has published figures showing that happiness increased between 2011-12 and 2012-13. • Justine Greening, the international development secretary, has announced Britain is to provide a further £2m in aid to provide water, sanitation and protection to vulnerable Syrian children who have fled to Iraq to escape their country's civil war. Here is more about energy initiatives announced (or half-announced - see 1.08pm) by David Cameron at PMQs. This is from the Press Association. Downing Street said that details of the competition review will be set out next week in the annual energy statement to the House of Commons by energy secretary Ed Davey. An annual review of competition in the energy sector will be conducted by regulator Ofgem, the Office of Fair Trading and the new Competition and Markets Authority. The first review is expected to begin within weeks and report next year, said the prime minister's official spokesman. It will look at issues affecting competition and consumers, ranging from prices and profit levels to barriers to new entrants in the market, as well as how companies engage with customers. "With regard to the charges that are part of people's bills, we are of course looking across the board at what more government can do to help customers and consumers," said the spokesman. The spokesman declined to give any detail of sanctions which energy companies might face as a result, but said: "It would be wrong at this stage to rule anything out in terms of how we get more competition into the market." The spokesman said the review was agreed by both Conservative and Liberal Democrat sides of the coalition and insisted that the Government was not giving up its claim to be "the greenest ever". The prime minister's ambition to "roll back" green levies has also been discussed with Liberal Democrats over recent weeks, and details will be released in Chancellor George Osborne's autumn statement on December 4. Downing Street sources said that, if there was no policy change, green levies could rise from the current £112 to £194 - or 14% of the typical household bill - by 2020. Cameron wants action to reduce the impact of the levies, the source said. And here is what Labour are saying. This is from a party source. The prime minister is clearly in a panic over energy prices. He is refusing to take real action to tackle overcharging now. He wants to wait another year when bills are going up 10% as we speak. The Labour party has a comprehensive plan to freeze prices and reset the market. David Cameron's response is panicked and totally inadequate. My colleague Patrick Wintour has a shrewd point about David Cameron's comment about "green" levies. I put "green" in inverted commas because some of the measures Cameron was referring to, such as the Energy Companies Obligation, involve funding energy saving measures for the poor. You could see them as much as welfare measures as climate change measures. PMQ's - Verdict: Earlier I said that was a clear win for Ed Miliband. Half an hour later, it looks even more significant - a big win, that left David Cameron. Wit does not always trump bluster, but it did today. Miliband's opening question was very neat. The prime miniser said that anyone who wanted to intervene in energy markets was living in a Marxist universe. Can he tell the House how does he feel now that the red peril has claimed Sir John Major? And Miliband continue to mock Cameron effectively, particularly with his question about someone taking Cameron's advice to switch from British Gas only to be hit with a price increase from npower. Cameron's front-foot aggression seemed misplaced, and his final jibe, about Major being a "good man" and Miliband being a "con man", was marked by a touch of desperation (although it was also noticeable that he was not going on about Miliband being "weak" as much as usual). However, tone is not particularly relevant. At PMQs even the best debating skills cannot disguise a policy lacuna, and Cameron's real problem was that he did not have an effective response to Miliband's best question: Major proposes recovering money from energy companies via a windfall tax, Labour propose using a price freeze, so how would you recoup that money? Cameron does not have a full answer yet, and that's why he lost. But Cameron did have half an answer. In fact, he made three significant concessions on energy policy. • He said that energy bills had reached a "completely unacceptable level". I don't think he has ever said this so bluntly before.
