As Severin Carrell reported yesterday, Osborne will say that Scottish households will be about £2,000 better off and freer to work and trade with the rest of the UK if voters reject independence. 10am: Jenny Watson, chair of the Electoral Commission, gives evidence to the political and constitutional reform committee about the lobbying bill. At 11.15am Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons, gives evidence. 11.30am: William Hague, the foreign secretary, takes questions in the Commons. 12.40pm: MPs start debating the second reading of the lobbying bill. 3.30pm: Sir Hugh Orde, president of the Association of Chief Police Officers, gives evidence to the Commons home affairs committee about EU justice and home affairs opt-outs. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary before the debate starts and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

tags.description: The latest news and comment on Angela Eagle

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened

fields.headline: Politics as it happened
fields.trailText: Inspector-General to examine complex asylum cases - Coalition declares national security failure
fields.bodyText: Labor's most famous backbencher is coming up very shortly. I've seen a preview of the interview, and having passed the embargo at 7.30pm, can now share. The key points of Rudd’s interview with host Leigh Sales are as follows. Rudd argues that Tony Abbott’s asylum policy is based on an absolute lie. The Opposition leader can’t stop the boats and tow them back to Indonesia. It's just not going to happen - and he knows it. Indonesia has made its position clear. It’s a slogan, not a substantive position. The Coalition’s campaign on debt deficits - the crisis in government finances - is another absolute lie, Rudd contends. Australia has a Triple A credit rating. Rudd mocks Abbott’s transformation in the Liberal leadership from conservative hardliner to policy moderate. Just because you put on a blue tie doesn’t mean you’ve changed your spots. He rejects suggestions he and his supporters are destabilising the Prime Minister. Rudd declares he’ll be campaigning his heart out for Labor. I’m going to take the argument up to the Australian people wherever I can. Leigh Sales asks Rudd whether voters have stopped listening to Julia Gillard? He reponds with an argument for collective responsibility. 100 days, he says, is an eternity in politics. Everyone has the opportunity to argue the case for Labor. He's asked will he stay the full term in parliament? The lips purse characteristically. Of course I will. I don’t believe in bringing on unnecessary by-elections. Sales presses on. Are there any circumstances in which you’d take on the Labor leadership? Rudd says the caucus voted and I accepted their response. The caucus affirmation was a convincing and strong win for Julia Gillard, he says. My verdict on this performance? A full trowel-load of helping. Let no-one say Kevin is not helping. Let noone say there's no 'K' in team. Good night to you all. See you again soon on Politics Live. If you'd prefer just the twenty questions to Christine Milne, and her twenty answers - without the journalistic filter - you can see the full transcript here. I see we've just posted the next instalment of one of my special projects for Guardian Australia - find a way of getting some oxygen and change-of-pace into a political interview. When we interviewed Prime Minister Julia Gillard last week we put 20 questions to her from a panel of Australians who were interested in the opportunity of an open-sourced interview. Our same group posed a question each to the Greens leader Christine Milne. I've written a piece of analysis weaving the questions and answers together. You can read that here if so inclined. Here's an excerpt to give you a flavour: Christine Milne remembers The Miracle of Our Lady of Fatima, the feature film made in 1952, because it was her first visit to the cinema. In response to a question put to her by film director Glendyn Ivin, Milne recalls: “It was a huge outing for us but I’ve never forgotten it because the children in the movie were threatened with being put in a big pot of boiling oil and it traumatised me for years.” Things improved after the advent of the Carry On films, which came with bonus carbohydrates. “After that, my father loved taking us to the Devonport drive-in in his old ute to watch the Carry On films and we would have hot chips at half time, which was always lots of fun.” From a man who ought to know. We'll take this as a comment on events of the evening and the week by Kevin Rudd's former press secretary, Lachlan Harris. Possibly a little eccentric, but I'll do the summary before we cover Kevin Rudd's interview at 7.30. We just need to stand still and work out where things stand with 100 days to polling day. It's very hard to believe there are only two more sitting weeks in this 43rd parliament. So, today: The Coalition continued to pressure the Gillard Government over the case of an Egyptian man detained in a low security immigration detention facility in the Adelaide Hills, even though he'd been flagged by Interpol. The government dug in, resisting calls to widen the inquiry it announced yesterday would be undertaken by the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security. The government argued that the IGIS (not the parliament) was the right body to examine the issues surrounding this case. It has not released any detailed terms of reference for the probe. The Prime Minister told parliament the IGIS could not be directed by the parliament - it was fully independent, and the government would give full cooperation. Julia Gillard accused Tony Abbott of substituting slogans for substance, and running a campaign of fear in relation to boat arrivals and national security. She continued to attack the Coalition's policy in this space as unworkable. That issue dominated the political day. The government also introduced legislation giving effect to its much telegraphed crackdown on the use of 457 visas. Rudd addresses the two subjects he's asked most often about in his 7.30 Report outing: China and the Labor leadership. The ABC has released a teaser of Kevin Rudd's interview on the 7.30 Report this evening. Labor should not be constructing alibis for defeat. I'll hang on this evening and cover that interview live. The questions relate to a story the newspaper is obviously pursuing in relation to asbestos and the NBN roll-out - an issue that we've covered extensively on the blog this week. Bit of background might help here. The Gillard Government has had it's up and downs with News Limited. I think that's fair to say. Senior ministers believe the Murdoch media has it in for the Labor government. There have been cycles of confrontation between Labor ministers and News running over this term in parliament. Labor went through a phase a while ago of releasing questions put to it by News Limited publications in order to highlight that answers had been supplied to journalists queries. It was an effort to highlight any unfairness in coverage. (Of course releasing answers to questions also had the effect of knocking off a "scoop". News Limited was furious about this escalation.) Then there was the whole cycle of confrontation around the Gillard Government's media reform package - tougher regulation for newspapers following the Finkelstein and Convergence Reviews. That package hit the fence after a huge push back from media companies - all of them really - but News was the loudest opponent. Interesting to see that Senator Conroy is back in the business of releasing questions and answers prior to publication. Bit of a theme going this afternoon it would seem. The Communications Minister Stephen Conroy has just issued a statement letting rip against The Daily Telegraph. It's headlined: Daily Telegraph: not interested in the facts Today I was contacted by the Daily Telegraph. I was asked seven questions (responses below). It is clear that the Daily Telegraph is not interested in the facts in this case. When dealing with something as serious as the safety of workers and the community, it is important that the facts are reported without misrepresentation. We must be absolutely vigilant each and every time we deal with asbestos. There can be no short cuts. This is the Tweet to which John Murphy refers. Murphy refers to a recent Tweet from Murdoch. Labor is not frightened of Rupert, he declares. How dare Rupert Murdoch tweet “Oz polls show nothing can save this miserable government. Election cannot come soon enough. People decided and tuned out months ago”. The reason people tune out Mr Murdoch is because of the tripe served up in your tabloids and your cross-promotion of that tripe. Everyone knows you twist the political agenda to suit your business agenda and in so doing you crush and corrupt our democracy. We are not frightened of you. And we will not surrender to your massive media power encapsulated in your tweet. Labor backbencher John Murphy is making a blistering contribution in parliament's adjournment debate on one of his favourtite subjects - Rupert Murdoch and his news organisation. His publications print nothing but "tripe", Murphy declares. Murphy: It fits Mr Murdoch’s political and business agenda to knock this Labor government. Make no mistake, Mr Murdoch’s political and business agenda are one and the same. If it’s good for Mr Murdoch’s business, he will support it. If it’s bad knock it down and destroy it. If citizenship gets in the way of business expediency, trade it in for another. If a newspaper and its staff get caught up in a phone-hacking scandal, don’t expect Mr Murdoch to accept personal responsibility. Blame the journalists, blame the editors, blame everyone else. Then shut the newspaper down. Australian employees of Mr Murdoch should not expect a better response when their turn to face the axe comes. And it will come soon. Why? Because it is expedient for business. The financial bottom line comes first and foremost ahead of all else. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor has just added to answer he made in Question Time today. There are no other people in immigration detention that are subject to Interpol notices he says - but there are 55 people in high security detention subject to adverse security assessments. Call me maybe. Kevin Rudd on his way out of the chamber. Picture worth a thousand words. Some stellar eye contact there between the Prime Minister and Kevin Rudd. Not a sight you see every day, Tony Abbott kneeling before Malcolm Turnbull. I've been chasing the terms of reference for the inquiry by the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security since the Prime Minister announced the initiative yesterday. The Prime Minister's office has responded just now. I'm told there are no terms of reference beyond what the Prime Minister said in the chamber yesterday. I'll repeat that for the benefit of Politics Live readers. The Prime Minister: Today I have directed the Inspector General of Intelligence and Security to examine the management of Australian government agencies of persons seeking asylum who present complex security issues, particularly this case. That's it. No further criteria, or dot points. For the record, that's your terms of reference. The general unwinding over the past few days extends to the House being unable to maintain a quorum after Question Time. Perhaps there is a jolly afternoon tea somewhere. Stink eye. Exhibit A. Liberal frontbencher Bronwyn Bishop, a lady who puts great store in the House of Representatives practice, would like Speaker Burke to reconsider. But the lady is not for turning. But some biffo nonetheless. The MP bringing forward the afternoon's Matter of Public Importance debate - Labor's Stephen Jones - is not in the chamber. The debate can't proceed as usual. Mr Abbott objects in strenuous terms. He wants Speaker Burke to bring forward another debate. Speaker Burke is annoyed at his reflection on the chair. Abbott explains that wasn't his intention, to reflect on the chair. He says he's trying to defend her position. I am embarrassed. I ashamed. Speaker Burke insists that is not within the standing orders to dial up another MPI debate. Liberal MPs continue to argue the toss. The Speaker is not amused. She pushes on. The Prime Minister has placed further questions on the notice paper. No suspension of the standing orders this afternoon. Tactics dear fellow, tactics. Tony Abbott and Christopher Pyne. Manager of Opposition business Christopher Pyne is raising other media reports of asylum seekers behaving badly. He wants to know whether the new inquiry will look at the full range of matters. The Prime Minister parodies Pyne's reliance on newspaper clippings as the source of his questions, and shouts about kooks and cranks on the internet being his next source of material. The Opposition benches are cracking up laughing. They are roundly unconvinced by the Prime Minister's line of attack. The body language suggests the Coalition knows it's thrashing Labor on the politics of this issue. Julia Gillard: It's just a game to you. It's been a game all week. The Health Minister wanted to know if her Queensland shadow, Peter Dutton, would stand up for the interests of his home state, or sit there silent, as he has done for years. Is this a pool fence I see before me? The Prime Minister declares in conclusion that Tony Abbott can be trusted with a slogan but not with national security, which is a complicated business. Health Minister Tanya Plibersek gets a Dorothy Dixer on Queensland health and has an uncharacteristic shout at those opposite. Tony Abbott would like the Prime Minister to guarantee that the Inspector-General's inquiry will be completed and made public before the election. Julia Gillard: It is impossible for me to give a direction to the Inspector-General. She's an independent office holder, Gillard says. It's the law. It passed through the parliament. We won't go around thugging people, the Prime Minister says. Gillard is asked to withdraw. She withdraws. Transport Minister Anthony Albanese lets fly with a morale builder for the backbench about a lack of spending by the Queensland Government on infrastructure. The Tokyo solution. The Indian solution. Albanese is making a point about Quensland transport officials cramming commuters onto trains and buses. Much chortling. Deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop asks a question of the Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus. Isn't the issue of whether the Egyptian man has committed offences in Australia rather irrelevant if he's committed serious offences elsewhere? Mark Dreyfus says the man in question remains in detention, has been in detention at all times, is now in detention, and in addition there is no evidence he has committed any offence while in Australia. Meanwhile, back at BobKat ranch. Croaky Scott Morrison is at the dispatch box. That voice will go very soon I suspect. Why, he asks, is the Prime Minister continuing to give the benefit of the doubt to a convicted jihadist terrorist? The Prime Minister says she will always put Australia's national security first. Morrison is back again. Will your inquiry also examine the conduct of your ministers? The Prime Minister says the Inspector-General is not subject to direction by her. The Inspector General has serious, wide-ranging and intrusive powers. The government will cooperate in every way with this inquiry. While Question Time is underway in the House, Bob Katter has his own personal Question Time, via social media, in his office. It's his practice on a Thursday. Mike Bowers went down to have a look. Here's Bob and his peeps, with the Prime Minister thundering away in the House on the TV in the background. Nationals leader Warren Truss would like the Prime Minister to agree that the mishandling of the Egyptian case has undermined public confidence in the management of border security. The Prime Minister contends that the Coalition has been the shambles this week, not the government. Australia is well served by our security agencies. This person was always in detention. She adds it was appropriate for the Inspector-General to look into the case, so she's been asked to do so. A Dorothy Dixer now on education. The Prime Minister is advocating the Gonski package and congratulating New South Wales for supporting the reforms. She notes it is possible to be a conservative and support changes beneficial for children. He (the NSW Education Minister) cares about Australia's children and Australia's future. Why can't those opposite do the same? Leader of the Opposition Tony Abbott asks the Prime Minister why she initially decried the Egyptian case as a beat up and has now launched an inquiry into the matter. The Prime Minister retorts that she's launched the inquiry becuase she is a supporter of facts. Julia Gillard: You have no solutions on border security. No answers, just a campaign of fear, where the facts have never mattered. A Dorothy Dixer now to allow Brendan O'Connor to argue the case for the changes he's introduced this morning to 457 visas. The Prime Minister subsequently asks the Manager of Opposition Business Christopher Pyne to withdraw an unparliamentary interjection. It would appear to be xenophobia. Pyne withdraws, but objects to having to withdraw. It being 2pm, Speaker Anna Burke is in the chair. Shadow Minister for Border Security Michael Keenan opens the batting. It's a question to Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor. Any update from your officials? O'Connor: These are of course very serious matters. The advice I've received is there are no other IMAs (irregular maritime arrivals) in the network that are the subject of an Interpol red notice. Keenan is back with a picture of a pool fence. (The Coalition's riff is the Egyptian was held behind a "pool fence" at Inverbrackie). Can the minister guarantee there are no more terrorists being held in detention? Brendan O'Connor essentially repeats his previous answer. Gallows humour from the Prime Minister's communications director, John McTernan? news.com has jumped in, boots and all. Everyone, and I mean everyone, should pull their heads in, Kevin Rudd said yesterday. Tonight, he'll stick his head out. Where has politics touched down this lunchtime? The Coalition is continuing to press for a wide-ranging parliamentary inquiry into the case of an Egyptian held in a low security immigration detention facility despite being flagged by Interpol. The government argues it is pursuing the appropriate course of action by handing this issue to the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security. The Greens have marked 100 days until the election campaign by launching their first political advertisement, which focuses on gay marriage. Queenslander Bob Katter has marked the day by announcing Peter Mailler as the Katter's Australia Party senate candidate for NSW. The government has introduced legislation giving effect to its proposed crackdown on 457 visas - documents that allow temporary skilled migration. The government claims some employers are rorting the scheme. Reverse ferret. ABC managing director Mark Scott issues statement declaring fruit to live another day. ABC TV confirms that it will continue to broadcast Bananas in Pyjamas, one of the globe’s most popular and iconic children’s programs. The latest series has 156 new episodes, with the current block of episodes premiering in May this year. In order to make the most of our significant investment in the hugely popular program, we will continue airing these episodes to a new generation of Australian pre-schoolers, as has been our standard practice in children’s television for more than two decades. Another series of Bananas in Pyjamas will go through the usual commissioning process to determine future production of the program. This process does not relate to current government funding. McKibben is speaking about growing resistance by activists to the expansion of fossil fuel industry in the United States, and around the world. We can't outspend them, but we can out organise them. Bill McKibben - global warming activist, scientist and Methodist Sunday school teacher. We are riding a carbon bubble, and either the bubble pops or the planet pops. Queensland MP Bob Katter is in the senate courtyard announcing the Katter Australia Party's NSW senate candidate for September. From one end of the spectrum to another, over at the National Press Club, scientist Bill McKibben is making the case for reducing carbon emissions. A new column from Guardian Australia political editor Lenore Taylor. After a week where some MPs seem intent on reducing Labor to a sideshow, here's the case for being a credible alternative. You can read Lenore's full post here. Here is an excerpt. Only 30% of the electorate say they will give their primary vote to Labor, according to Newspoll. Obviously, that represents electoral disaster for the ALP. But it also represents more than 4.6 million people who are backing Labor despite its leadership sideshow and its backflips on promises and policies. Those 4.6 million people want a choice. And political parties owe it to the electorate to offer a credible choice, even when it’s pretty damn clear an overwhelming majority of voters are preparing to vote for their opponents. Some within Labor still seem to get that. But over the past few days a number of them look like the bloodied remnants of an army where the soldiers have downed weapons, stopped fighting and started heading for the hills to save their own hides. Are you thinking what I'm thinking B1? What are you thinking B2? Labor men Simon Crean and Ed Husic have a pow pow in the chamber. Rudd men. Lot's of interesting groups around the place this week. Like ripples on a river. Are you thinking what I'm thinking B1? Absolutely no idea, B2. Bananas. The weeping. Does this make (the ABC managing director) Mark Scott the ultimate Fruit Ninja? Greens communications spokesman Scott Ludlam gets this question from a reporter in the Mural Hall. His response? I wish I'd thought of that line Jeremy. I'm un-Australian enough to be unmoved by the reported demise of the bananas. Senator Mark Bishop on the other hand is looking much cheerier today. The Labor Senator featured on this blog, and all the television news programs last night, banging the table in a committee hearing in total frustration. Bishop's outburst was an emblem of a crazy day. Better this morning, evidently. Politics will shortly express its grief that the ABC has axed Bananas in Pyjamas. (Yes, it will.) Get down B2. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison is seriously in danger of losing his voice. He's on ABC24 with political editor Lyndal Curtis. Morrison is being quizzed about how long it will take the Coalition to make a significant impact in stemming irregular maritime arrivals. Morrison: We will make a difference from day one. Curtis persists. You won't stop the boats on day one will you? Morrison: No-one has made that suggestion. Morrison says the Coalition will be accountable for its statements on this issue after the election. Lots of committee hearings going on around parliament this morning. The head of Treasury, Martin Parkinson, is giving testimony in the Senate Economics Legislation Committee. Dr Parkinson says we are in for a decade of macro-economic volatility on a scale not seen since the 1970s. Parkinson: It's going to be an interesting ride for us globally, and in Australia in particular. With reports this morning that some Labor MPs are already packing up their offices in preparation for the great wipe out of September 14 (according to the collective wisdom of opinion polls) - Mike Bowers paid a visit to Victorian Labor man Alan Griffin. Griffin was one of the MPs mentioned in disptaches this morning as packing his bat and ball and taking it home. Bowers recieved an unequivocal no to his polite request to document the dismantling. Something about legs was mentioned. Packing didn't interfere with parliamentary duty evidently. Here's Griffin with Robert McClelland. Another Rudd man. If you've never heard of the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security - the statutory office holder who will undertake this inquiry at the behest of the Gillard Government, here's a link to their website. The IGIS, according to their blurb: Monitors what the Australian intelligence and security agencies do. Conducts inquiries, either self-initiated or at the request of government. Investigates complaints about the agencies. Makes recommendations to government. Provides annual reports to the parliament. The IGIS has extensive powers to obtain information and can: require any person to answer questions and produce relevant documents take sworn evidence, and enter agency premises The IGIS' inquiries are conducted in private, but many of them are reported in annual reports. The current Inspector-General is Dr Vivienne Thom. I've been seeking terms of reference for this inquiry since it was announced in Question Time yesterday. So far, I haven't seen any. 100 days, and all is world-weary with readers of politics live. PWS. Here was the Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor on the ABC's AM program this morning with reporter, Samatha Hawley. Who is responsible? SAMANTHA HAWLEY: Does the Government take ultimate responsibility for this situation? BRENDAN O'CONNOR: Well, we've made clear at all times that the person in question has been in detention, there have been dealings, there has been issues between the, and a good working cooperation amongst the agencies. SAMANTHA HAWLEY: There hasn't been great work, has there, among the agencies and between the agencies and the Government? Chris Bowen for instance was sent a brief on this, as you have pointed out or corrected on the record, in September last year. Are you surprised at all that he never laid eyes on that, his staff did nothing about it and now it's lost? BRENDAN O'CONNOR: The department advised me that the submission held by the former minister's office was not returned to the department. But let's be very clear here … SAMANTHA HAWLEY: So who is responsible for that? BRENDAN O'CONNOR: Well, let' be very clear here. The department and the Australian Federal Police and ASIO have been dealing with this matter. It is agencies who deal with operational matters and indeed make decisions around the detention and security of detainees. SAMANTHA HAWLEY: And a senior minister in the Government was told that this man posed a risk and he never laid eyes on that letter, his staff did nothing with it and now evidently no-one can find it. BRENDAN O'CONNOR: What I've made clear to you is we've obviously announced a review. That's the right thing to do. There's been a round of duelling media interviews this morning about the Egyptian man, and the inquiry the Prime Minister set up yesterday by the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security. On the Coalition side, we've had Tony Abbott, Scott Morrison and Michael Keenan. For the government, we've had Brendan O'Connor and the Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison, by close of business, may set a new record in how many times he can utter the phrase convicted jihadist terrorist in front of a TV camera. Perhaps someone could keep count? Tony Abbott told reporters this morning he wanted a full independent inquiry into this case. (Which means a parliamentary probe, which would also examine the role of ministers in this case.) The Coalition would like the option of probing why the former Immigration Minister Chris Bowen got an officials briefing on the case of the Egyptian man late last year, and didn't tell Brendan O'Connor seemingly until this week. Partisan politcs aside, given the history in this portfolio of less than stellar administration, the gaps in this case study look worrying. The Attorney-General has said the inquiry the Prime Minister proposed yesterday will look at the national security implications of complex asylum assessments. Mark Dreyfus was not engaging on the question of why Bowen didn't bring O'Connor into the loop. Dreyfus: I don't know what happened there and I'm not going to speculate. Readers will be aware from our top story this morning that Guardian Australia's political editor Lenore Taylor has interviewed the Greens leader, Christine Milne. With senate balance of power very much in play in September, Milne talks about a possible shift in upper house king making. Have a watch. Legislation on the recognition of local government in the Constitution - a procedural step required before the question is put at a referendum later this year - passed the House of Representatives last night. Two Liberal MPs, Alex Hawke and Dennis Jensen, voted against this legislation. There is a growing conservative campaign against the proposed change. People like former Howard Government minister Peter Reith believe recognising local government in the Constitution doesn't help people in the regions, it merely centralises power in Canberra. One reader this week was concerned that there hasn't been enough coverage of one significant issue - the proposal to recognise local government in the Constitution - to enable him to make up his mind about which way to vote. With that in mind, I can point you to a Comment is Free piece that Local Government Minister Anthony Albanese has contributed to Guardian Australia this morning. You can read the full piece here. Here's an excerpt: The change being put to the Australian people is modest and practical. It simply adds into the Constitution the right of the Commonwealth to grant financial assistance to any state or to any local government body formed by a law of a state, under any terms or conditions as the Parliament thinks fit. It in no way changes the role of State Governments which will stay responsible for local councils. Under the Bill before the parliament, the Commonwealth couldn’t provide funding with terms and conditions that wouldn’t be valid under state law. It also does not interfere with the state’s rights to change local councils through, for example, amalgamations or by sacking them if the need arose. There's lots of ways to chime in to the daily discussion around politics live. You can comment here on the blog, or engage on Twitter, using the hashtag #politicslive You can get in touch with me there @murpharoo I've opened the batting on Twitter this morning by asking for thoughts on 100 days to go. This response from Andrew McCormack is fairly representative. In the House of Representatives, the Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor is speaking about the changes the government wants to the 457 visa program. In our wrap of the news, I've pointed you to an interesting story in The Age this morning which uncovered 200 cases of rorts in this scheme. O'Connor is telling the House the package of reforms aims to rebalance the system. The government has concerns that some employers are turning to overseas workers first without investing in training. The government believes Australian residents deserve the chance to get jobs on Australian projects. Labor has copped a lot of flack for this crackdown both externally and internally. The government stands accused of caving in to its trade union base (which has long opposed temporary skilled migration programs because of the risk such programs will drive down local wages and conditions); and playing to the politics of xenophobia. But this move is popular with Labor's base. There is a strong under current of economic nationalism in the electorate right now. Here was the Opposition leader, Tony Abbott, out with reporters just a little while ago with his talking point on 100 days to go. Have a listen. With 100 days to go until we all cast our votes, the Greens have this morning launched their first election ad. The pitch is gay marriage. We, the Greens, are into it. If you are into it, Vote 1 Green. Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of politics from the national capital. Given the mad day yesterday - the outbreaks of self indulgence and panic by various Labor figures - Mike Bowers went early this morning to visit two legends of Labor history. The wartime leaders: John Curtin and Ben Chifley, immortalised in bronze in the parliamentary triangle. As the walkers walked by, Curtin and Chif were keeping their own counsel about the circus on the hill. To our day. We are still firmly in the national security frame after the Prime Minister announced yesterday an inquiry by the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Security into complex asylum cases requiring security assessments. We’ve not yet seen terms of reference for this probe. The Coalition is displeased. It wants a parliamentary inquiry. The Opposition leader Tony Abbott has been out to the doors of parliament to declare the case of the Egyptian man a major breach of national security. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison - voice hoarse from the flu - has batted off questions from reporters about whether he is prejudicing this man’s claim for asylum by constantly declaring him “a convicted jihadist terrorist.” The Immigration Minister Brendan O’Connor has been on radio. Where does the buck stop with this case Minister? Clearly these are important matters. Let’s get into our parliamentary sitting day with a review of the main political stories. 100 days 'til poll day .. but who is counting? For more than two years, Rod Cameron's pessimism about Labor's prospects has been a strictly private affair. Although he dubbed Tony Abbott unelectable, the former ALP pollster remained circumspect on the challenge facing the party he served in more than 50, mostly winning, state and federal campaigns. ''I didn't want to throw any curve balls in while there was a prospect that the party would do what I thought it would do - and that's just act out of self-interest,'' Mr Cameron explains. Now, ''more in sorrow than anything'', he is predicting an epic Labor disaster. He is not alone. While the mood of the Labor caucus has been despondent for months, it seems that only now, 100 days from polling day, the gravity of what is in prospect is really sinking in. (Fairfax Media) Labor faces an unprecedented wipeout in five western Sydney and Central Coast seats, with more than twice as many voters claiming they are backing Tony Abbott over Julia Gillard. With 100 days to go before the election, western Sydney has spoken. And the message was clear. While there was little love for Mr Abbott, they have had enough of Ms Gillard and want to teach her government a painful lesson. A straw poll by The Daily Telegraph of more than 100 voters yesterday revealed that asylum seekers and Ms Gillard's leadership were the hot-button issues. (The Daily Telegraph) At least two veteran Labor MPs have packed up their Parliament House offices in anticipation they will not be returning to Canberra after the September 14 federal election. With widespread despondency pervading the government at all levels, Victorian MP Alan Griffin and south-western Sydney MP Daryl Melham packed up their Canberra offices. (The Australian Financial Review, paywalled) Boats and national security Julia Gillard has ordered an investigation into how an al-Qa'ida terrorist was housed for months in low-security immigration detention, as Labor seeks to fend off opposition attacks suggesting the flood of boat arrivals is jeopardising national security. The Prime Minister caved in yesterday after days of having resisted opposition demands for an inquiry into the case of convicted Egyptian jihadist Maksoud Abdel Latif, who Tony Abbott said was housed behind a "pool fence" in the Adelaide Hills. (The Australian, paywalled) A Sri Lankan asylum-seeker who committed suicide in Sydney's Villawood detention centre by consuming a fatal quantity of cyanide had been diagnosed just months earlier with leprosy. The death in 2011 of the Sri Lankan asylum-seeker known as Victor triggered a system-wide search for cyanide within the detention network. (The Australian, paywalled) 457 visas - rorts Hundreds of foreign workers and students are being forced into debt bondage after paying up to $40,000 for an Australian skilled worker visa, with some signing contracts stating they will be sacked if they engage in ''trade union activities''. A Fairfax Media investigation can reveal the most extensive rorting of the 457 and 187 visa schemes ever to be exposed, with up to 200 cases across Australia. (The Age) Peter Slipper - unpaid legal fees The law firm that defended Peter Slipper against a sexual harassment claim in the Federal Court is suing the former speaker for $237,000 over alleged unpaid legal fees. Maurice Blackburn has filed a claim in the Supreme Court in Brisbane, alleging Mr Slipper still owes the firm $231,762 in unpaid invoices, plus more than $5300 interest. Greens leader - interview The Greens leader, Christine Milne, has suggested Tony Abbott is not fit for leadership because of his stance on climate change, but she is preparing a Democrats-style “keep the bastards honest” election campaign to stop Abbott’s “excesses” in the Senate, on the assumption he will be prime minister. (Guardian Australia) I’ll also point you in the direction of a couple of interesting columns published this morning. Mark Latham - Labor’s micro-economic reform record After two terms in office, Labor … has developed a substantial environmental and social policy agenda, (but) micro-reform has been a black hole. History will record an extraordinary paradox: how the previous Labor government (1983-96) drove a hard-edged reform program, while the Rudd and Gillard cabinets were missing in action. The party, in effect, has never recovered from its first term in opposition after the 1996 election. A pair of ex-ACTU presidents, Simon Crean and Martin Ferguson, abandoned Paul Keating’s legacy by reviving an interventionist platform of corporate welfare and protectionism. No wonder conservative commentators now universally praise the two union leaders – they wrecked Labor’s economic credentials for a generation. (The Australian Financial Review, paywalled) Jonathan Green - the politics of xenophobia Politics is nothing if not a mirror of the society it serves … that it, in every sense, represents. We provide the clay they work with. If there wasn't a vote in hate, fear and prejudice then there would be no gain in pandering to any of them. The great Australian shame is that not only are there votes to be had here, but that this is the heartland in which our political game is lost and won. The likes of Eddie McGuire aren't even a pimple on its backside … and in many ways the star chambers that assemble around these public transgressions just blind us to the greater reality of a public whose blind-peeping anxieties breed an agenda that turns that suburban fear to populist political profit. (ABC - The Drum)

results.webTitle: Politics, pop, scoops and Sloanes: how magazines helped shape  society

fields.headline: Politics, pop, scoops and Sloanes: how magazines helped shape  society
fields.trailText: They may be known for gossip and fashion, but the UK's weeklies and glossies have also broken major news stories – and as the publishers' association celebrates its 100th anniversary, they seem to be in ruder health than ever
fields.bodyText: From home-baking recipes to luxury car reviews, from diet advice to celebrity interviews, British magazines continue to be the informed friends the public turns to in a quiet moment. This month sees a retrospective look at a century of titles: the good, the bad and the stylish. If the bleakest predictions had been proved right, the 100th anniversary of the industry's trade body, the Professional Publishers Association, would have been a mournful, nostalgic look back at a bygone business model. Instead, its celebration of key moments from magazine history shows how new trends have taken off while reliable old publications have frequently found a fresh audience. A brochure, put together by Gill Hudson of Reader's Digest – a former editor of the Radio Times, Company and Maxim – for the association's celebratory event on 21 November, kicks off its survey of 100 pivotal magazine moments with Margaret Thatcher's resonant interview from 1987, when the prime minister told Woman's Own: "There is no such thing as society. There is a living tapestry of men and women and people, and the beauty of that tapestry and the quality of our lives will depend upon how much each of us is prepared to take responsibility for ourselves." The scoop demonstrated that the more relaxed atmosphere of a feature interview can sometimes make more waves than the front pages of a newspaper. As Thatcher rang the bell for the start of the age of the individual, so Gordon Roddick, the co-founder of the Body Shop, and John Bird were preparing to help those individuals not likely to make it on their own with the launch of the Big Issue in 1991. Produced in response to the growing number of rough sleepers on the streets of the capital, Bird and Roddick's magazine offered an induction course and a fixed pitch on the pavement for an army of homeless sellers. It now works with around 2,000 vulnerable people. Women's magazines have undergone a design and content revolution in the last century, from the proliferation of catwalk coverage to the huge demand for celebrity-based photo-journalism. While the influential arrival in 1972 of both the feminist title Spare Rib and the straight-talking Cosmopolitan helped give women a stronger voice in society, it is pure fashion and gossip that now drive trade on an unimagined scale. An attempt to supply the everyday woman with a portion of advice and entertainment that would last no longer than a cup of tea came in 1990 with the launch of Take a Break. Still the bestselling women's magazine in the country, it created a new publishing genre: true-life weeklies. John Dale, who edited the title for 20 years, said: "I held certain beliefs – first, that working-class women, especially in the north, were the victims of institutional discrimination; second, that such women were ill-served by magazines produced by home counties types; third, that these women needed stories about themselves rather than the Queen Mum and Joan Collins; and fourth, that the journalist should get out of the way so women could speak directly to other women in their own voice." One of the best judged pieces of social class commentary since Nancy Mitford wrote about "U" and "non-U" manners for Encounter magazine in 1955 was hidden between the covers of the October 1975 edition of Harpers & Queen. This is where Peter York first identified the phenomenon of the Sloane Ranger. The label lived on long after Lady Diana Spencer and her tribe had stopped wearing strings of pearls and upturned collars. Style punditry quickly became a major engine of the industry as the 1980s came to an end. Alongside the Face and Esquire, GQ, a magazine that celebrated its 25th birthday last week, set out to target a sophisticated, young audience. The US parent magazine was bought by Condé Nast in 1983, and the company launched several international editions. In 1998, the UK edition began its enduring, high-profile quest to find the men "whose own peers expect to make a major impact in the year ahead" and its Men of the Year awards enjoyed their 16th outing two months ago. Past winners make an eclectic line-up: Michael Douglas, Russell Brand, Sir Bradley Wiggins, athlete Steve Backley, writer Kazuo Ishiguro and Patrick McLoughlin, now secretary of state for transport. In 1998, the event was marked with a drinks reception; this year there was a black-tie dinner at the Royal Opera House. The capacity for smaller publications to take the most important risks has been proved repeatedly over the last century, not least last year when the Oldie was the first to repeat in print the rumour that Jimmy Savile had committed child abuse, alluded to in Miles Goslett's feature about the BBC's aborted Newsnight item. Similarly, in 1992, the subversive Private Eye printed the allegation that the heart surgery unit at Bristol Royal Infirmary was known as the Killing Fields because of high death rates: "Despite a long crisis of morale among intensive-care staff, the surgeons persistently refused to publish their mortality rates in a manner comparable to other units." Editor Ian Hislop said: "It followed that pattern of Eye stories where you have to keep on telling it and eventually it comes good." In a Britain where many of the young men who once hunched over a copy of NME have now graduated to comparing dishwasher reviews in the consumer magazine Which?, Nicholas Coleridge of publisher Condé Nast has spoken of a potential new golden age for magazine journalism. Sales of printed copies may have dropped, but he believes the public are still devoted readers of their favourite titles. "Circulation figures don't show that people enjoy reading them on the new devices," he told BBC Radio 4's The Media Show last week, adding that his leading title, Vogue, has had its "most profitable year" yet, and that a veteran such as House and Garden sells in the same numbers it did 50 years ago. Talking to the Professional Publishers Association in the runup to its centenary, Coleridge recalled his early, independent days working for editor Tina Brown on Tatler. The publication was, he said, "so broke that, in order to pay the restaurant critic Julian Barnes, one of my jobs was to ring up publishers and pretend we were doing a feature on illustrated books. They would duly bike over all these fabulous volumes, which I'd then take in a taxi to a secondhand book dealer called D Levin of Grape Street, unload them, flog 'em, come back with the cash, and hand (most of it) to Tina. She'd then hand it to JB and he would go off somewhere like the Connaught for lunch and pontificate about it." Hudson, who compiled the PPA's 100 moments in consultation with contacts across the industry, believes the magazine business still relies on bringing content to people in a way they can enjoy, whether online or in print. While new electronic platforms allow for much more involvement with readers, she warned editors of the future to keep a clear idea what they want to create. "There is so much more customer feedback now, but then there is also a danger that you only give customers what they want and not what they don't realise they want yet," she said. Barry McIlheney, the PPA's chief executive and a former editor of music title Smash Hits and movie magazine Empire, is also heartened to see that reports of the death of print magazines have been exaggerated. "I kept being told that print was over at conferences, but it doesn't seem to be true," he said. "Things look very different now to even five years ago. Print magazines go very deep into the affections of their readers and many are also working really well on tablets: a high percentage of the top 50 rated apps are magazines." While McIlheney regrets that Smash Hits is no longer on the newsstands and selling a million copies an issue, he celebrates the survival of Empire, which he launched. "It is still going stronger than ever." • This article was amended on 20 November 2013 to make it clear that it was a Newsnight item about Jimmy Savile that was dropped, not a Panorama programme.

results.webTitle: Egg wars: how politics of influence block free-range labelling reform | John Kaye

fields.headline: Egg wars: how politics of influence block free-range labelling reform
fields.trailText: John Kaye: In their quest for even greater profits, Australian industrial egg producers are appropriating the free-range label while depriving their animals of room to move. Reform is needed
fields.bodyText: The chief consumer affairs bureaucrat of Australia's largest state has just done something quite unusual for a public official. NSW fair trading commissioner Rod Stowe has departed from script on free-range labelling – usually along the lines of "there’s nothing to see here" – to respond to a super complaint from national consumer advocacy giant CHOICE. In doing so, he has admitted to the major failure of the free-range egg market. Cases of false labelling, inconsistencies across standards and a proliferation of high-cost, low-welfare eggs led him to call for nation-wide legally enforceable standards. By doing so, he has now made it impossible for governments, the egg industry and large industrial producers to continue hiding behind their stone wall of denial. Despite the consumer tide rapidly turning against factory farmed foods and the grotesque levels of animal cruelty they involve, Australian governments have blocked, reversed or subverted almost every move to stop free-range being little more than a marketing tool. In August of this year, the NSW Coalition government used its numbers in the lower house to axe a Greens bill to impose real meaning on the free-range label. In Queensland, the Newman Liberal National party government quietly dumped that state's requirement that free-range egg farms stock no more than the industry standard of 1,500 birds per hectare. In both cases, Coalition governments danced to the tune of the big industrial producers. They allowed the ethical motivations of consumers to continue to be exploited and cashed in for massive profits. Yet the figures show just how out of touch they are. Increasing numbers of consumers are looking for a better animal welfare alternative to factory farmed meat and eggs. In 2011, 34%of all eggs sold in Australia were labelled free-range, representing 44% of the entire value of the market. If recent trends continue, the appetite for ethically produced eggs will overtake the caged products early next decade and it will spill over into other foods including poultry and pig products. While the statistics reveal an explosive growth in the demand for free-range eggs, they disguise a crisis in the authenticity of the products and the labels. In their quest for even greater profits, industrial egg producers are combining the pulling power of the free-range label with high stocking densities that deprive their animals of room to move and genuine access to a fresh range. In responding to CHOICE’s super complaint, the NSW department of fair trading found that egg farms with some of the highest stocking densities were actually charging the highest prices. The report states: The current lack of a consistent, standard definition of what is meant by ‘free-range’, coupled with the prevalence of varying industry certification schemes, makes it difficult for consumers to know whether the products they are purchasing meet their expectations. Since 2010, the Egg Corporation has been pushing for formal endorsement of the intensification of free-range standards. The egg lobby wants the current industry-accepted stocking density of 1,500 birds per hectare to be boosted by more than 1,200 per cent to a staggering 20,000. Most consumers expect free-range products to come from farms where the animals have the freedom to move about in the outdoors and express their "natural behaviours." Many would be appalled to learn that far from these basic expectations of animal welfare, many hens would not even make it out to the range, no matter how green and lush it might look in the marketing image on the carton. Despite the loud protests from consumer rights and animal welfare organisations, current laws fail to prohibit the Egg Corporation from trashing the term free-range. In the absence of any state or federal legally-enforceable definition, the big industrial producers are able to determine just how "free" free-range their hens need to be to justify the label and the price increases. It turns out to means not very free at all. While reform at a political generally lags community values and expectations, when it comes to the egg industry and labelling rorts, there are more sinister and active forces at play. In justifying her party’s opposition to the Greens NSW Truth in Labelling bill in NSW in August of this year, National party MP and primary industries minister Katrina Hodgkinson said: The Food Authority is also actively enforcing existing truth in labelling in relation to eggs… [it] already has the power under the Food Act 2003 to pursue food businesses if they are guilty of misleading or deceptive conduct … the system works and that there is nothing to be gained by duplicating regulatory powers that the authority already has and takes seriously. This is in stark contrast to the NSW fair trading commissioners assessment, where consumer expectations were so badly subverted that he called for binding national standards to end the inconsistency. Action on closing the gap between labelling and expectations has been stymied by the National party and their pro-cage and anti-regulation mantra. The big industrial egg producers are determined to keep churning out low-welfare high-cost "free-range" to consumers who don’t get to see behind the farm gate to tell the difference. The National party appear to have willingly taken up their cause, despite the consequences for consumers, chickens and, most surprisingly, small and medium-sized free range farmers. These National party priorities are making many of their Liberal colleagues quite uncomfortable. Truth in labelling is a small but persistent thorn in the relationship between the Coalition partners. The idea that the label free-range is being misused to gouge more money out of consumers would not sit well with the often well-education product-savvy constituents in Liberal electorates. Most Liberals would understand that for any market to operate will it needs high quality information. Misleading product labelling and advertising are the enemy of free-market capitalism, distorting the supply-demand chain. Extraordinarily, the then-NSW fair trading minister Anthony Roberts failed to even contribute to the debate on the Greens Truth in Labelling bill. He was wedged between protecting consumer rights and maintaining peace within the Coalition. Political convenience won out over his constituents and his commitment to ensure a well-functioning free-range market. Vested National party interests are poisoning the debate over free-range labelling and curtailing sensible efforts to introduce labelling reform. This is the case of minority power blocking reforms supported by the majority. The National party, with its 550,000 voters , is denying 10m free-range consumers their rights. The good news is that while the National party are a vocal minority, they are only a minority. The pressure for a fair go for genuine free range farmers, consumers and hens is growing daily. It is increasingly apparent to voters and law makers that industry self-regulation is failing, and that parliaments must step in and make free range mean free range.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened

fields.headline: Politics as it happened
fields.trailText: The Coalition campaigns on national security, Kevin Rudd advises MPs to pull their heads in
fields.bodyText: I didn't quite manage a lunchtime summary today. Let's rectify that this evening. Today in federal politics: The Coalition established the terms of the political face-off early in the day, signalling its intention to pursue the case of an Egyptian man held in a low security immigration detention facility in the Adelaide Hills despite being flagged by Interpol. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor said first that neither he, nor his predcessor Chris Bowen, were aware of the circumstances of this case until April this year. O'Connor later had to amend this statement when he learned that Bowen had been briefed while still in the portfolio. The Coalition grabbed the contradictions and ran with them, insisting this constituted a major national security failing on the part of the Gillard Government. The Prime Minister tried to circumvent the political attack by announcing in Question Time that the Director-General of Intelligence would hold an inquiry into this case, (and others involving complex security issues for would-be asylum seekers.) Not good enough thundered the Coalition - (and crossbenchers Andrew Wilkie and Rob Oakeshott) - who thought the multipartisan parliamentary committee on intelligence should conduct the probe. This proposal lacked the required parliamentary majority to proceed. In other issues, Julia GIllard got her landmark Gonski reforms through the House of Representatives. Despite the legislative victory, Labor continued to have a public dialogue with itself about its failings, with various people suggesting the government couldn't sell it's policies. Kevin Rudd went to the doors of parliament and advised people in the caucus to "pull their heads in." Treasurer Wayne Swan advised people to "put a sock in it." Labor Senator Mark Bishop, meanwhile, banged the table during a committee hearing, producing vision that became emblematic of Labor's internal ill-discipline and frustration. New national acocunts data confirmed the Australian economy grew by 0.6% in the March quarter. Climate Minister Greg Combet told the National Press Club Tony Abbott would not repeal the carbon price regardless of what he's saying now. That's essentially today, stripped of fog and moonstruck madness. Thanks so much to Mike Bowers for brilliant pictures. See you all tomorrow. Shadow Minister for Border Protection Michael Keenan is still going. David Speers, fresh from luring Liberal Greg Hunt away from a critical division, is now quizzing Keenan on Sky TV. Speers is putting a rebuttal from refugee advocates about this Egyptian - that he wasn't convicted in a legitimate court - to Keenan. (Are you confident that this man has been convicted in a legitimate court of these serious offences Speers inquires?) Keenan insists the Opposition is absolutely confident about the facts it is asserted about this case. After the chaos of the day, let's move in more tranquil fashion towards the evening. Can I point readers to Simon Jackman's new blog post? (Simon, Stanford Professor, is our resident, learned, utterly delightful, political numbers guy). He's written today on last week's political controversy around public funding for elections. You can read the full blog post here. Simon would cop more public funding for elections if the Australian system forced the political parties to actually tell us who is signing the cheques. Lots of meaty morsels in this piece. Here's an excerpt: We have very little information about the major parties’ expenditures, despite the parties taking considerable sums of public money via the “per vote” funding models. We know more about the relatively tiny expenditures of independents than the expenditures of the major parties: Tony Windsor spent $94,249.93 in the 2010 election, Rob Oakeshott $78,139.16 and Andrew Wilkie $35,924. Tony Abbott reported receiving nothing and spending nothing on his 2010 re-election in Warringah, the same for Julia Gillard in Lalor, and Adam Bandt in Melbourne – all true as a formal, legal matter, but quite the giggle otherwise. We only get a sense of where party money goes when the parties are giving money to entities required to report receipts to the AEC: for example, the parties transferring funds between federal and state branches, to various supporting foundations and thinktanks (some might say “front organisations”). This is all the more galling when the cross-partisan “deal” that was scuttled last week included public money to cover the parties’ “administrative costs”. This isn’t good enough. And this is why the politics of last week were so distressing. Morrison says this person should be deported. Doesn't matter if Egypt has the death penalty. Morrison: We shouldn't get distracted by (that) issue. Ever indefatigable. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison has called a press conference. This inquiry the Prime Minister has announced doesn't go nearly far enough. Morrison declares there's a don't ask, don't tell policy on national security. The vote on whether to recommit the vote failed to secure the absolute majority required. Wrong way. Go back. Anthony Albanese tries to convince the Independents to drop their motion. The ABC's Melissa Clarke puts this well. Speernapped. A brand new concept has entered the political discourse. Liberal Greg Hunt is now addressing the matter of his absence during the vote. He says he was trapped in a sound proof booth with the Sky political editor David Speers. Hunt: I will learn to run faster. Albanese: National security cannot be a political plaything. I say to the crossbenchers and everyone else, don't play politics. Think of the consequences of this motion. The Wilkie motion failed to secure the required absolute majority. Manager of Opposition business Christopher Pyne is now trying to recommit the vote. Pyne: There was great confusion. (Katharine: You don't say Christopher.) Pyne says Liberal MP Greg Hunt was trapped outside the glass doors. The Member for Fisher did not appear either. People didn't know this Wilkie motion was coming. They went on with other business, only to be called back urgently. He's pleading misadventure. Let's vote again, Pyne says. Anthony Albanese is not impressed. What a short memory the Member for Sturt has got. Albanese says the Coalition can't have procedural issues both ways. The fact is the Opposition was two votes short of a majority. He says if two genuine votes can present themselves, then let's talk. Otherwise this is a stunt. Albanese adds that the intelligence committee is not empowered to inquire into individuals. The standing orders forbid it. Albanese is urging the Independents to withdraw their motion. It should not be proceeded with. The motion moved by the Member for Denison would have been clearly out of order. Quick check of the standing orders. While we wait for this vote, enjoy the day in a video. A day of frantic racing around after bits and pieces, fragments of a rolling debate. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison just wants to land his lines about the failure of national security. Unfortunately for him, Labor's Joel Fitzgibbon strolls by. The herd with microphones thunder after Fitzgibbon in hot pursuit. I think we can all laugh at each other's absurdities for a moment here. Fellow cross bencher Rob Oakeshott is seconding the Wilkie motion. The House is dividing. The Acting Speaker, Bruce Scott, seemed a little uncertain whether he should call one. The disposition of the chamber was unclear. It's that kind of day. Actually not quite, here is another suspension of the standing orders. Tasmanian Independent Andrew Wilkie this time. He wants a parliamentary inquiry by the joint committee on intelligence into the facts of this case involving the Egyptian man. This incident, Wilkie says, is a cause for very serious concern It's important that someone, sombody get to the bottom of this. This is a matter the parliament should consider. The vote on the suspension of the standing orders was negated. Over, and out. Perfect chamber shot from Mike Bowers. 101 days to poll day. Strategy mode. After Albanese's analysis of the Coalition's dull predictability on the launching of suspensions of the standing orders, Speaker Anna Burke asks if the motion should be agreed to. The House is dividing. What's Albanese talking about? "Wheat for weapons" was a scandal concerning kickbacks that the Australian Wheat Board paid to the Iraqi Governmment in contravention of UN rules. This happened under the Howard Government. And Haneef? Thanks to the Law Council for this quick summary: Dr Mohamed Haneef, an Indian national, was arrested at Brisbane airport on 2 July 2007 in connection with a failed London bomb plot. He was held for twelve days before being charged with providing support to a terrorist organisation. The charge was unsustainable and was quickly dropped. However, in the interim Dr Haneef's immigration visa was cancelled on character grounds - a decision which on review was found to be unlawful. The gag motion failed. Albanese is on his feet. We will not be lectured on national security by the party who gave us wheat for weapons and the party who gave us the Haneef scandal. A comment from #politicslive reader Alf McMillan. Here's Liberal backbencher Kelly O'Dwyer's comment on the attempt to gag the debate. A little advice needed? Christopher Pyne knows where to seek it. From Peta Credlin, Tony Abbott's chief of staff. The Prime Minister, having announced an inquiry into this case by the Director-General of Intelligence, has attempted to end Question Time. Not so fast is the view of the Opposition. Michael Keenan is back at the dispatch box attempting to suspend the standing orders. This is a serious breach of national security demanding a parliamentary debate. Keenan: This is an urgent matter. We are dealing with a grave breach of national security and nobody will take responsibility for it. Anthony Albanese then moves to shut Keenan down with a gag. I move the member be no longer heard. Bells are ringing. Here is the statement Brendan O'Connor made to parliament just prior to Question Time. He has confirmed that his predecessor in the immigration portfolio, Chris Bowen, was in fact briefed about the Egyptian case. O'Connor has previously said otherwise. O'Connor: I was advised that the former Minister for Immigration was not briefed on the matter of the Egyptian detainee. I was subsequently advised, that the former Minister’s office received a submission relating to this matter on 28 September 2012. The submission was not signed by the former Minister. This submission related to the complex issues surrounding the detainee’s capacity to apply for a protection visa. The Department has advised me that the submission held by the former Minister’s office was not returned to the Department by the then Minister’s office. I can confirm that this submission was not provided to me or my office upon appointment as Minister for Immigration and Citizenship. The Department has also advised that this submission, relating to this detainee’s capacity to apply for a protection visa, has not been resubmitted to me. No matters relating to the detainee were raised with me or my office prior to 17 April 2013, as I advised the Parliament last night. The brief of 17 April 2013 related to the movement of this detainee from the Inverbrackie to Villawood detention facility. I also advise the House that the detainee has always remained in detention since arriving in Australia. Tony Abbott is back with a question to the Prime Minister. Given a convicted jihadist was left behind a pool fence, given everyone in the government was in the dark, given the Prime Minister refused to accept briefings on this matter, how is this not a major national security failing of your government? Julia Gillard is asked by the Speaker to ignore the final inference. The Prime Minister says today I have directed the Inspector-General of Intelligence and Securty to examine this case. The Prime Minister continues her answer. The Leader of the Opposition does not know more about this than the Director-General of ASIO. She says she was briefed on the case after the matter was raised in Senate Estimates. Tony Abbott is back to the Prime Minister. When were you, or your national security adviser, informed of this serious security breach? Julia Gillard: The facts are these. The person he refers to was always in detention. I refer the Leader of the Opposition to the statement by the Immigration Minister just before Question Time. Julie Bishop persists with a supplementary to Dreyfus. What action did you take after the briefing on April 24? Did you brief the national security committee of Cabinet? Albanese wants the question ruled out of order. You can't talk about Cabinet is his argument. That would be breaking the law. Speaker Anna Burke says the question is in order. Dreyfus says he won't be talking about Cabinet. Deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop has a question for the Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus. Has the Attorney been advised on the case of the Egyptian by ASIO - when, and what action have you taken? Dreyfus: What a disgrace this opposition is. This remarks prompts generalised uproar. Mr Dreyfus says he was briefed on the case on 24 April. He says ASIO is properly resourced to do security assessments. He points to the ASIO triage system. Is the Opposition saying it knows how to do security assessments better than ASIO? If so how, and what would it cost? Michael Keenan has a question for Jason Clare, Minister for Home Affairs. Why didn't the police brief you on the Egyptian sooner than they did? Clare: I won't second guess the Federal Police about what they think it's approrpiate to brief me on. Jason Clare says when he was briefed, he recommended cooperation with other agencies. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison is continuing to pursue Brendan O'Connor over the Egyptian. Why weren't you informed about this man for seven months? O'Connor ploughs on. At all times our agencies have worked together. This person has been in detention at all times. Morrison persists. If you thought Inverbrackie was ok to detain this person, why did you move him? O'Connor says officials moved him on advice from the agencies. This was the correct course of action. Suspension of the standing orders in the offing perhaps? Manager of government business Anthony Albanese pays a visit to the cross bench. Meanwhile the Shadow Minister for Border Protection Michael Keenan wants Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor to explain why he told him in a letter in May that the assessment of the Egyptian man had started, but told parliament yesterday that it hadn't. Brendan O'Connor says Keenan referred to three cases in the May letter. His response took that into account. O'Connor says the assessment of the Egyptian man had not commenced at the time the letter was sent. Tasmanian Independent Andrew Wilkie comes at the same issue from the left. He's wanting particulars about processing of asylum seekers on Manus Island. Why can't journalists go and check the facility out? The Prime Minister says the government has been open and transparent about the Manus facility. Wilkie begs to differ. Tony Abbott has switched the attack to boats. Given a convicted jihadist terrorist was held in a family detention facility for months due to a clerical error, will the Prime Minister agree her policies have made Australia less safe? Julia Gillard: The Leader of the Opposition is out there trying to create fear. All of this fear raised by an Opposition, that if it was clear and if it was truthful, would admit their policies amount to nothing. The Prime Minister says the Coalition creates fear in the community but won't take legislative action to stop the boats. We make no connection between Treasurer Wayne Swan's answers and Kevin Rudd needing oxygen on the backbench. Hockey is back on the debt cap. Why don't you just fess up, you've made a mess of this haven't you? Why are you leaving it to someone else to clean up your mess? Swan declares that he wants ten questions on this subject. A Dorothy Dixer allowing the Prime MInister to be ebullient about the passage through the House of Representatives of the Gonski package earlier today. Julia GIllard: You would think in this parliament there could be a moment of unity. But unfortunately today, we didn't see that moment of unity today. Speaker Anna Burke. The time for member's statements has expired, in accordance with standing order 43, are there any questions without notice? Here we go for Question Time. Shadow treasurer Joe Hockey opens on the debt cap. It will be breached won't it? Will the Treasurer now apologise for breaking another promise to the Australian people? Treasurer Wayne Swan says Treasury officials have provided face value figures on the debt cap today. Debt in this country is low. Our public finances are in great shape. This (question) just demonstrates how those opposite want to talk down our economy day after day. Having been moved aside by the Prime Minister, Senator Crossin is a big fan of rank-and-file preselection ballots. She's endorsing one now in the Victorian seat of Batman. This must happen. Sky TV journalist David Lipson asks whether she's "forgiven Julia Gillard." Crossin: I'm certainly getting on with what my next challenge in life will be. It's taken a while to get through that. But I know there will be another door. Too much sermonising? Never! Labor's public dialogue with itself concerning the current deficiencies continues. ALP Northern Territory Senator Trish Crossin has bobbed up on a panel on Sky TV. Senator Crossin was recently moved aside somewhat brusquely by the Prime Minister in favour of Nova Peris as Labor's main senate candidate in the NT. Crossin says Labor MPs know what the policies are and what they mean, but voters don't. Crossin: I do think there are some problems with the messaging. We talk about Gonski. People say to me, what's that about? Shadow treasurer Joe Hockey is out now in one of the courtyards responding to the national accounts figures. He's pressed on what the Coalition would do to boost domestic demand and make life better for business. Hockey is asked whether business would thank him for delaying the introduction of the Coalition's paid parental leave scheme (which in its current iteration at least, imposes a levy on business). Hockey: You will see our initiative in that regard prior to the election. I'm not getting into speculation about where we are at. Combet is asked to reflect on what might have been if Kevin Rudd's carbon pollution reduction scheme had passed the parliament. Small laugh from Combet. It's unknowable. Who knows what would have been? My life might have been a bit easier if the CPRS had gone through. Combet is asked whether - like on uranium - the Labor Party will take a stand on environmental grounds, but gradually back track on the carbon pricing mechanism. No, Combet says. This issue has been thrashed out comprehensively within Labor and the labour movement. The carbon price will endure and so will Labor's commitment to it. The Labor Party is committed to tackling climate change. Combet is asked to commit to serving a full term if Labor loses in September. He provides a very elegant non-commital to this question. He's standing, he's committed to fighting, and to Labor winning. I'm still hungry to do more. (That's a bit short of an unequivocal yes.) Combet is asked to substantiate allegations made in his speech that Coalition people have been bullying banks and agencies, like the Clean Energy Finance Corporation. Combet says he doesn't have to reveal private activity. These (interventions) have been quite public. Combet says Tony Abbott in government will not repeal the carbon price. The game has already changed. Combet: Like the floating of the dollar and the opening up of Australia’s economy by the Hawke and Keating Governments, the Clean Energy Future plan is in the great tradition of Labor reforms. It came as no surprise to me that recent polling has seen a turnaround in support for the carbon price. Essential Media found that support for the Government’s carbon pricing policy has increased by six points since January to 43 per cent – at the same time opposition to carbon pricing is down by seven points to also stand at 43 per cent. Another poll found that only 20 per cent of swinging voters wanted the carbon price repealed. The fact that the policy itself is decreasingly contentious, and demonstrably working, is worth careful consideration in the context of politics in future months and years. A scare campaign will only take Tony Abbott so far. He already thinks he has won the next election, but it stretches credulity to contend that a double dissolution election on the issue is already in the bag as well. Tony Abbott knows this. And let’s not forget that he is a first-class political opportunist. His aim is simply to win Government. If he does, my contention is that he cannot and will not repeal the carbon price. Climate Change Minister Greg Combet is now a fair way into his address to the National Press Club. The carbon price is working exactly as intended, Combet says. It creates a powerful incenitve to reduce pollution. As a market mechanism it's ensuring pollution is reduced in efficient ways. The Coalition's policy would pursue higher cost ways of reducing pollution, rather than trusting the efficiency of the market. He suggests the Coalition's policy is a dud. Tony Abbott, Combet says, has been frightening people on low incomes about the impact of the carbon price on the cost of living. Swan is back now to the question he didn't want to engage with earlier - why do you get no credit? He says the government has lost paint politically because it has taken on controversial reforms, like carbon pricing, against substantial political opposition. He's asked does he agree with backbencher Laurie Ferguson that the Prime Minister needs to get more involved in the boats issue? She has been full bore in trying to put together a comprehensive response. We will continue to do everything within our power to get a full suite of policies. Any comment on ill-discipline in caucus? The public who elect us expect us to come here and deal with the big issues. I think its about time that a few people who are out there put a sock in it basically. Swan is making the point in general terms that it would be good for the economy if the Australian dollar started to fall (as it has recently). Swan: I don't speculate about the pathway of the dollar. But he says he always thought the Australian dollar would begin to fall when the US economy started to recover. If you want to get across all the details of the new national accounts data, here's a news wrap. Growth is good. Mike Bowers caught the Treasurer on his way to his media conference. Swan is asked given the strong numbers, why is the government getting no credit? The Treasurer retorts that he'd like to stick to explaining the data for now. He says getting so quickly to a question like that might indicate one of the government's problems. (Presumably he means the fog of media interpretation.) Wayne Swan says there may well be doomsday predictions "from predictable quarters" courtesy of the new national accounts data. But here's his summary. Swan: When you look at today's national accounts I think they are a reminder of what Australia has achieved over the past five years. We have an economy in transition, we have a high dollar which is making that transition harder. But the reality is the economy is in good shape, and the economy is handling these transitons very well. Our economy remains a stand out in terms of the developed would. The Treasurer Wayne Swan is addressing reporters about the national accounts. Growth is good. Friends, one and all. Kevin Rudd, catching up with folks on the sidelines of that Gonski vote, including the man of the moment, Joel Fitzgibbon. And quick as a flash, the Young Liberals have Senator Bishop's "moment" on YouTube. It's fair to say there's a bit of pressure around the traps. Labor senator Mark Bishop went berko in the economics committee late yesterday. Have a listen. It's right at the end of this sound file. (Note to readers: This post has been corrected from the original. I thought initially this committee hearing was today. It was late yesterday. Apologies.)
