First, Labour's record on IT projects of this kind was pretty poor. Second, implementing large IT projects slowly is, on balance, a good idea. And, third, what Labour would actually do about UC remains something of a mystery. All of these are strong points because they are true. So why did Duncan Smith lose? His main problem is that, having stuck so rigidly to the "on time and on budget" mantra, he does not have the grace, humility or dexterity to back down and admit that the timetable has slipped. I suspect it's because he's too thin-skinned and insecure. A minister like Ken Clarke would have pulled this off, dignity intact, quite easily. Guy Opperman, a Conservative MP, says he is at a loss to understand Labour;'s policy. Why is Duncan Smith pursuing his test, learn and implement proposals? Duncan Smith says Opperman is right. He says he has no idea what Labour want. Duncan Smith says in the private sector up to 30-40% is allowed for write-downs and re-writes in IT projects. What is happening within UC is well within this, he says. Labour's Wayne David asks Duncan Smith if he has any credibility. Duncan Smith says he has more credibility than Labour. Duncan Smith accuses Labour of "hypocrisy". They are claiming that all went well under them. But it did not. They wasted billions and billions. Labour's Anne McGuire asks Duncan Smith why he is still in his job, given that he said he was taking such a close interest in the project two years ago. Duncan Smith says that, as result of his intervention, changes were made. Sir Tony Baldry, a Conservative, says Duncan Smith if he heard Rachel Reeves tell the Today programme yesterday that she supported UC. Isn't it strange that she supports it on the airwaves but rubbishes it in the House? Duncan Smith says Reeves also said UC was wasting hundreds of millions of pounds. She has dropped that claim today, he says. If you support a plan, you should support it. But Labour is actually opposed to this, he says. Duncan Smith says he hopes Labour will say sorry by the time of the next election. Labour's Meg Hillier asks what will happen to people on employment and support allowance (a disability benefit). Duncan Smith says this group are known as the "stock". Some Labour MPs complain. Duncan Smith protests, saying this is the term Labour itself used when it was in office. This group will move on to UC in time. Labour's Andy McDonald asks if Duncan Smith or any his ministers tried to influence what the public accounts committee said about UC in its recent report. No, says Duncan Smith. Graham Evans, a Conservative, asks Duncan Smith to agree that UC will save money, and that it is currently handling complicated cases. Duncan Smith says the NAO agrees UC will save money. He does not address the second point. Duncan Smith says Labour is the welfare party because it has opposed every welfare reform measure introduced by the government. Labour's Barbara Keeley asks Duncan Smith if he thinks he has the support of Treasury minister. She quotes the unnamed George Osborne ally quoted in Rachel Sylvester's column in the Times. Duncan Smith says he has the support of the Treasury. David Ruffley, a Conservative, says Duncan Smith has the wholehearted support of MPs on the Conservative benches. Labour wasted £25bn on IT projects. It is right to proceed slowly, he says. Duncan Smith agrees. Labour wasted £13bn on NHS IT, he says. Labour's Glenda Jackson asks when UC will be available for everyone. Duncan Smith says Labour rushed out tax credits too quickly. Some £5bn was lost, and 400,000 people were damaged. Labour's Jenny Chapman asks when UC will be able to deal with people with complicated conditions, like being in a couple. Duncan Smiths says some of the reporting in today's papers is wrong. It was always intended to roll it out in stages. Jacob Rees-Mogg, a Conservative, says Labour should remember what the Bible says about motes and beams. They made plenty of IT mistakes. Duncan Smith says the SNP government in Scotland is in "complete denial" about the cost of welfare. He does not know where they would get the money from under independence for their welfare plans. Duncan Smith says Labour did nothing to improve the quality of life for those trying to find work. Labour want to "nit-pick away about this programme" because they have nothing to say on welfare reform. Duncan Smith says the DWP has carried out an exhaustive impairment review (ie, a review of cost being written off). The write off figure is £40m. The accounts are being published today. Labour's Debbie Abrahams asks Duncan Smith if it is on time, or if Duncan Smith is taking his time. Duncan Smith says Labour MPs do not know what they want. He is taking his time with people on disability benefit because they are vulnerable. Labour want to rush these people onto UC early. Dame Anne Begg, the Labour chair of the work and pensions select committee, says Duncan Smith said originally UC would be digital by default. It isn't. He said it would be in place for all new claimants by May 2014. It won't. Why should anyone believe what he says? Duncan Smith says when problems occurred, the government had to address them. Duncan Smith says UC will roll out without damaging a single person, unlike tax credits. Duncan Smith is responding to Reeves. He says Reeves used to work for a bank. She should know the difference between costs being written off and written down. Does she drive a "motor car"? If she did, she would know that its value goes down every year. Perhaps this "lady" needs a lesson in economics. He says Labour left welfare in a mess. But this government has saved £11bn from the welfare bill already. Child poverty is at its lowest level since the 1990s. And pensioner poverty is at its lowest level for 30 years. Rachel Reeves says Duncan Smith said in September UC would roll out on time and on budget. He made the same point in successive weeks. Yet the goal for getting to a certain level of claimants has been delayed by two years. Some £40m has been written off. And another £90m could be written off.Does this mean a new IT system is being built? When did Duncan Smith decide to delay this for two years? Isn't the problem that that Duncan Smith did not know what he wanted when he started. Will people on UC get free school meals? Duncan Smith is in denial. But doubtless he will deny he is denial. Reeves finishes with the quote from an unnamed minister in today's Rachel Sylvester column: “There are some ministers who improve in office and others, like IDS, who show they are just not up to it." Iain Duncan Smith says this is a major reform that will transform Britain for the better. The government is focusing on its "safe and secure delivery". It is pushing ahead with the culture and business change in job centres. Yesterday he announced his plans for the next stage, and discussed them at length with the work and pensions committee. John Bercow, the Speaker, intervenes. He says the constant heckling is discourteous. Duncan Smith says the DWP will further develop the work started by the Government Digital Service for this project. Next summer the departement will start to deal with universal credit (UC) claims from couples. From the autumn it will start to deal with claims from families. The pilots show 90% of people are claiming online. And 78% say they are confident of their ability to budget on the basis of having monthly payments. Responding to heckling from Labour's front bench, he will not make the mistakes made when tax credits were introduced. The government will test, learn and implement, he says. Rachel Reeves, the shadow work and pensions secretary, asks for a statement about universal credit. Iain Duncan Smith, the work and pensions secretary, spent more than two hours taking questions from the work and pensions committee yesterday about universal credit (UC). I covered it in detail on my live blog. Today we're getting an encore; he's answering an urgent Commons question tabled by Rachel Reeves, his Labour opposite number. It will start in five minutes. Yesterday's hearing received widespread coverage in today's papers. Broadly, the stories focused on two news lines. • The Guardian and others focused on the amount of UC investment being written off. Here's the start of the Guardian's story. The government has increased its initial write-off of a failed IT system for universal credit by £6m to £40.1m, but acknowledged that a further £90m of software is likely to be written down in its value over the next five years. The precise loss to the taxpayer will depend on how much of the existing IT software is retained after it has been merged with a new IT system being developed by the Cabinet Office's Government Digital Service. Universal credit, which brings together six existing benefits, is seen as potentially the biggest change to welfare since the second world war. Ministers had to concede last week that it had fallen behind schedule and would not be completed by 2017 as originally planned. Seeking to explain the £40m write-off, Mike Driver, finance director general at the Department for Work and Pensions, said: "There is no use for the IT code built to run the computer systems. It has no future value. It is not going to generate any future return for the department." • The Independent and others focused on the revelation that UC cannot yet deal with claims from couples. Here's the start of the Independent's story. The computer system for the flagship welfare reform was not yet ready for couples who make a claim, Iain Duncan Smith, the Work and Pensions Secretary, admitted on Monday. He also announced that the Government had written off more than £40m worth of software designed for the new Universal Credit, while a further £90m of equipment would be worthless in five years’ time. In a bruising session with MPs, Mr Duncan Smith said his reforms remained on track despite a series of delays and set-backs. He denied that the launch of Universal Credit, which is due to replace six benefits and tax credits with a single payment, had turned into a “debacle” and insisted the scheme was “doable”. And here's one more item of background reading. It's a Telegraph blog from John McTernan yesterday. Is Iain Duncan Smith a Stalinist? Not in the Stalingrad way – "there are no prisoners of war, only traitors" – but in the mad, bad 5-Year-Plan way? You know, when production was driven by central planning and workers were exhorted to work harder to turn 5 into 4. I ask because I still can’t quite believe his performance on the Today programme this morning. He stated that Universal Credit is "on time". This is his mantra now. He told the House on October 14 that UC "will be on time and within budget". He repeated his assurance on November 18 when he will have known that, under cover of the Autumn Statement, he was going to slip out an announcement that would exclude claimants of ESA from UC ... Ambitious, but clear. What do we know now? No new claimants will be put onto Universal Credit before the next election. So, instead of 12 million people moving to UC over four years, they will all be moved in two years. In fact, according to the Office for Budget Responsibility the migration to Universal Credit will not start until the summer of 2016, and be concluded by the end of 2017. Four years work will be delivered in 18 months. This is either truly Stakhanovite or utterly delusional. Expect many more delays. The Ministry of Justice has announced plans to enable people who get poor service from claims management companies to get compensation. Here's an extract from the news release. In future complaints about claims firms will be handled by the Legal Ombudsman, which holds powers to force companies to pay compensation or provide other forms of redress. The change planned for next year will mean the Claim Management Regulation (CMR) unit at the Ministry of Justice will also be able to dedicate more resources to tackling bad practice by companies in the sector, which specialise in helping people make claims for compensation for accident injuries and mis-sold financial products like Payment Protection Insurance (PPI). In Treasury questions a Labour MP asked whether the government would let the Office for Budget Responsibility audit Labour's plans before the general election. Responding for the government, David Gauke, the Treasury minister, said the government did not want the OBR to become embroiled in political controversy. Iain Duncan Smith, the work and pensions secretary, is responding to the universal credit UQ. It will be at 12.30pm. I will be covering it in detail. There's an urgent question on universal credit. You can read all today's Guardian politics stories here. And all the politics stories filed yesterday, including some in today's papers, are here. As for the rest of the papers, here's the PoliticsHome list of top 10 must reads, here's the ConservativeHome round-up of today's political stories and here's the New Statesman's list of top 10 comment articles. • Tim Shipman in the Daily Mail says ministers are trying to block the publication of a critical report about HS2. Ministers are trying to use emergency powers to ban the publication of a damning report into the £50billion High Speed Rail project. Cabinet Office Minister Francis Maude and Transport Secretary Patrick McLoughlin called on David Cameron to veto releasing a report that labelled HS2 ‘red/amber’ and in danger of failing. They claim it would cause ‘political and presentational difficulties’ and could seriously damage the project. HS2 critics say the gagging attempt is ‘absolutely disgraceful’ and are using Freedom of Information laws to demand that the Project Assessment Review, from November 2011, be published. The Information Commissioner has rejected ministers’ objections and ordered them to release the report. But in a leaked letter to Mr Cameron, circulated to ministers, Mr Maude and Mr McLoughlin urge that an emergency veto be put in place. • Christopher Hope in the Daily Telegraph says David Cameron has angered rightwingers by saying the Foreign Office will take charge of the EU renegotiations. David Cameron has infuriated Conservative right wingers by suggesting he will entrust the renegotiation of Britain's relationship with Europe to the Euro-phile Foreign Office. The Prime Minister made clear he wanted to use the Foreign Office to lead Britain's renegotiation with the European Union describing it as “one of the most expert groups of people I could have”. Eurosceptic Tory MPs said they were dismayed about the comments, with one saying that he had “no faith in the foreign office whatsoever because it is very pro-European”. Asked if he "intended to appoint a negotiator to take forward Britain's renegotiations with the EU", Mr Cameron said: “I have got in William Hague and his team probably one of the most expert groups of people I could have. " • Rachel Sylvester in the Times (paywall) says universal credit is a shambles. There are also tensions within the political and Civil Service tribes. George Osborne, a hard-headed pragmatist, has never understood Mr Duncan Smith’s missionary zeal: he is more interested in saving pounds than souls. Now his allies openly question the Work and Pensions Secretary’s ability to run a major department. “There are some ministers who improve in office and others, like IDS, who show they are just not up to it,” says one minister close to the Chancellor. • Steve Richards in the Independent says that MPs should get their big pay rise - and that we need more MPs able to take a big national leadership role. Another very big part of the problem is that it is completely taboo for leaders to suggest that part of an MP’s job description is to be a figure big enough to devise and implement national policies and become a powerful advocate for them. Instead the qualifications are increasingly related to connections with a constituency and a tireless commitment to the local area. On one level the number of independent-minded Tory MPs elected in the 2010 intake is impressive – backbenchers who care more about speaking for their constituents than they do about becoming a minister. Equally it is a noble ambition to become a social worker, which is how some MPs proudly describe their role to me. Recently I asked a former MP and cabinet minister – now in a much better paid job outside politics – about the main differences. He said that it was almost impossibly demanding to be a cabinet minister and then return to the constituency at weekends to deal with a mountain of local issues, pointing out that in France ministers are spared the additional responsibilities of constituency work. This would never happen here because the connection with the constituency is so highly prized. But the emphasis on the local comes at the expense of the need for MPs to address national responsibilities. Leading, governing, opposing, communicating, advocating, winning elections are increasingly dependent on a small number of MPs on either side who are seen as up to the job. • Janan Ganesh in the Financial Times (subscription) says the ideological differences between left and right are getting wider. And the deepest faultline of all has emerged this year. No longer do the parties merely disagree over how much government should tax and spend the output generated by the market. They also take different views on the market itself. Under Tony Blair and Gordon Brown, New Labour seldom expressed a judgment as to what the private economy “should” look like. As good Fabians, they limited their role to milking capitalism instead of remaking it. Mr Miliband is a different kind of social democrat: happy to intervene in markets, such as household energy, if he suspects they are rigged against the consumer or, in the fuzzier case of gambling and payday lending, if he believes they are not very nice. His proposal for a state-mandated freeze in prices charged by private energy companies would have been laughed out of town by his predecessors, but it does constitute an answer to the existential question that has dogged Labour since the crash became a fiscal crisis: what is the point of the left when there is no money to spend? Mr Miliband believes inequities can be eased through direct market interventions, which cost little or nothing to the exchequer. It is a contentious answer, pregnant with perverse consequences, but it is an answer. And it leaves the ideological space between the two main parties looking wider than at any time since he was a student. • Gideon Rachman in the Financial Times (subscription) says the West is losing faith in its future. What defines the west? American and European politicians like to talk about values and institutions. But for billions of people around the world, the crucial point is simpler and easier to grasp. The west is the part of the world where even ordinary people live comfortably. That is the dream that makes illegal immigrants risk their lives, trying to get into Europe or the US. Yet, even though the lure of the west remains intense, the western world itself is losing faith in its future. Last week Barack Obama gave one of the bleakest speeches of his presidency. In unsparing terms, the US president chronicled the increasing inequality and declining social mobility that, he says, “pose a fundamental threat to the American dream, our way of life and what we stand for around the world” ... If the erosion of living standards continues, how will western voters react? There are already signs of political radicalisation – with the populist right on the rise in both the US and Europe. But, as yet, there is no real sign that the Tea Party in America or nationalist movements in Europe have a realistic shot at controlling the central government in a large nation. The consensus around globalisation also seems to be holding. Indeed this weekend the World Trade Organisation apparently made a breakthrough in the search for a new global trade deal. But while new political movements are not yet ready to smash the established parties in the west, mainstream politicians are having to react to the new economic climate. Rising inequality is increasing the pressure for more redistributive taxes and higher minimum wages on both sides of the Atlantic. Another decade of western economic malaise – or, God forbid, another financial crisis – is likely to see more radical solutions and politicians emerging. Here's Nick Clegg's Christmas card. David Cameron has been tweeting from South Africa. There are two polls around this morning. For the record, here are the figures. ICM for the Guardian Labour: 37% (down 1 point from ICM last month) Conservatives: 32% (up 2) Lib Dems: 12% (down 1) Ukip: 9% (down 1) Labour lead: 5 points (down 3) According to Electoral Calculus, this would give Labour a majority of 64. YouGov for the Sun Labour: 38% (down 1 point from YouGov in the Sunday Times) Conservatives: 33% (down 1) Ukip: 13% (up 2) Lib Dems: 10% (no change) Government approval: -30 (down 5) Labour lead: 5 points (no change) According to Electoral Calculus, this would give Labour a majority of 56. I'm afraid we're not exactly at the centre of the news cyclone today. You may want to take a look at Matthew Weaver's Guardian live blog of the Mandela memorial service. For those of you loyal enough to stick with Westminster, I can promise you ... well, not much. The diary is empty. But that should leave me plenty of time to go through the papers and scour the internet. And you never know what will turn up. Here's the agenda for the day. 11am: A report from the Commission on Civil Society and Democratic Engagement is launched at an event in the House of Commons. The commission is an alliance of charities, and the report (pdf) explains how the lobbying bill threatens their ability to campaign. 11.30am: Danny Alexander, chief secretary to the Treasury, takes Treasury questions in the Commons. Alexander is leading for the government because George Osborne is abroad. 12.30pm: The advisory council on the misuse of drugs publishes its report on ketamine. It will recommend reclassifying it from a Class C drug to a Class B drug. 2pm: Labour hosts a summit on A&E services. 2.45pm: Mark Sedwill, permanent secretary at the Home Office, gives evidence to the Commons home affairs committee. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a summary at about 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: Labour conference - Ed Balls's speech: Politics live blog

fields.headline: Labour conference - Ed Balls's speech: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the Labour conference in Brighton, including Ed Balls' speech
fields.bodyText: That's it from us for the night. Andrew Sparrow will be back in charge tomorrow morning. Have a nice evening. Ed Balls seemed notably relaxed and confident during that interview – perhaps suggesting a newfound confidence in the Labour frontbench. Here are the key points: • Balls said “a really hard and tough look” needed to be taken into the High Speed 2 rail scheme. "I think it's about time we injected a bit of hard thinking into this debate, and if at the end of that you go ahead, good," he said, although his comments suggested he was sceptical that it was worth the £50bn price tag. • Balls suggested strongly that health spending would remain ringfenced under a future Labour government. • Balls said Labour was no longer advocating a cut in VAT because it no longer suited the economic circumstances: “Clearly since June the recovery has begun.” • He said he agreed wholeheartedly with Ed Miliband’s comments that any shadow minister briefing against a colleague should be sacked, and said he had never done that himself. The Damian McBride era was “a different place – it really feels like quite a long time ago,” he said. • He said the government’s help to buy scheme was set too high, but he could see the case for a smaller scheme. But building houses was more important, he said. In a final question, Richards asks about Balls's piano lessons and whether that was the only time his mind was clear of politics. Yes, he says. It's really hard to do one thing with one hand and one thing with the other, he says, so it takes "absolute total concentration". It's fabulous, he says. Richards tries to get him to pre-announce Ed Miliband's policy announcements planned for his speech tomorrow, but of course he won't. And with that it finishes. Balls is asked to make more of a distinction between Labour and the other parties. The questioner suggests funding Sure Start children's centres. Balls says these are one of Labour's greatest achievement and it's terrible to see them being closed and having services scaled back. "I'm with you. This will be a very important part of what we do in a Labour government." He jokes that he'll tell them if they promise not to tell anyone. (I've never seen him this relaxed.) Balls is asked to name some more policies for people to take home. He says he could do his whole speech from memory. It's over 4,000 words. He says the biggest thing he was worried about was whether his towel joke would work. But Harriet Harman said: "Fabulous towel joke. I wish I'd thought of it." He lists a number of policies he mentioned in the speech. Tomorrow you'll get "certainly two more manifesto commitments which are there and good and strong - but that's off the record!" Richards asks if Labour is going to reverse the rise in VAT. Balls says he made that case in 2011 for 2011 - but it would not be right for 2015 as the recovery begins. "Clearly since June the recovery has begun. We're not advocating a temporary VAT cut now." Balls suggests Nick Clegg got off scot-free when he announced his free school meals policy without announcing how it would be funded. "Nick Clegg got away with it... Well, not really because people didn't take it that seriously because they think, 'It's Nick Clegg ... '" Balls has a good joke himself, about how John Prescott used to sit at the back of his battle bus cold-calling random people to ask if they are going to vote Labour. "Once he asked Gordon to join him ... " Balls is asked to give out a list of the policies he announced today to help members make the case to voters. Balls says he will do something similar by Saturday. The questioner makes a good joke about Balls getting a standing ovation and then returning the mic: "Maybe we've all been to too many rock concerts ... but I was expecting two more policies!" Everyone laughs and the questioner says he has been working on that joke all day. "It was very good," says Balls sweetly. Should the ringfencing of health spending continue? He says he doesn’t want to make a promise now but he says it’s very likely. Balls is asked about Miliband's promises that any shadow ministers who brief against other shadow ministers will be sacked. What does he think and has he ever done that? Balls says he agrees with Miliband wholeheartedly and he has never briefed or sanctioned briefing against colleagues. Asked about the help to buy scheme, Balls says £600,000 is a very high figure for a taxpayer guarantee for mortgages. He can see the case for a smaller scheme. But again he returns to the idea of acting on the supply of housing first. He says Osborne thinks helping first-time buyers is a good thing politically, but he doesn't want to build houses because "he's locked into the fiscal path". Balls is asked whether the green message is a vote winner for Labour. Yes, he says. The government through incoherence and lack of vision has united the CBI and Greenpeace on green issues. Let's have a 2030 renewables goal in law, Balls says - and that is a response to a demand from the CBI and Greenpeace. Cameron has "totally ducked this policy agenda in government" - despite widespread support. "I think it's a big opportunity for us." Balls is asked about land tax. Sometimes there are complexities in the tax system that mean people we don't want to pay have to pay, he says. In the end it is about the supply of homes rather than the price of land, he says. Richards opens it up to questions. "I hope this is interesting for all of you. It feels a bit like In the Psychiatrist's Chair," Balls muses (if only). Balls looks back at the summer of 1996 and recalls the Skinner/Baddiel/Lightning Seeds song Three Lions, with its famous refrain "football's coming home" (and I said there was going to be no culture tonight...). Blair and Brown had independently written their conference speeches to end with the line: "Labour's coming home." A row ensued. Gordon grumpily gave way, even though he liked football more than Blair. How could the two of them get to the day before conference without having seen each other's speeches, asks Balls. Miliband showed him his speech for this year's conference two weeks ago. Richards brings up Damian McBride's revelations and asks what Balls reflects on that era. Balls says he was "horrified" by McBride's revelations. But it allows us to look back on this "different place - it really feels like quite a long time ago," he says. Richards notes that, unusually in recent times, Balls and Miliband are an opposition duo with much experience of power. But he suggests Balls and Miliband do not want to push this because "the recent past became so toxic". Balls says he is happy to have a debate about Labour's time in government and the lessons we have learned. People are more worried about where things are going than where they've come from, he says. He suggests it would be ridiculous if he started every interview about reminiscing about what he did in 2006 "like some pub bore". But he acknowledges Labour's frontbench are a group of 40-somethings who are "veterans". "There's a lot of track record and experience," he says. The 1964 Wilson government was similar, he suggests. A more recent parallel is that Margaret Thatcher had been a cabinet minister before becoming PM, he says. "I don't think a responsible chancellor should be the cheerleader for an infrastructure project on this scale without being hardheaded about value for money," he says. He suggests George Osborne would much rather be a political strategist than a chancellor and he acts like it. "I don't think George Osborne is doing his job properly on HS2." That gets some applause. Richards suggests it sends out an incoherent message to oppose the biggest capital spending project in the country and yet push for more capital spending generally. Balls says Labour has had no discussions about announcing it is opposing HS2. "I think it's about time we injected a bit of hard thinking into this debate, and if at the end of that you go ahead, good," Balls says. Money is fungible, he says - every billion you spend on HS2 is not spent on other things. Is it the best project to spend £50bn on, he says. You don't sound very keen on it, says Richards. Balls says he is a Leeds Wakefield MP - you can see some benefits. But journey times alone are not enough, he says. "I'm not meaning to sound un-keen," says Balls. Balls recalls that the Lib Dems changed their policy on the deficit in order to go into coalition with the Tories. We want to build houses, create jobs and keep the economy moving, and we'll be disciplined doing that, he says. Richards asks about High Speed 2. Balls says this is something we have got to really know will work and will give value for money. We supported this, he says, but now costs have gone up and up. There are also questions about the benefits, he says. This is about the use of £50bn of public money, he says. We need to know the costs are properly under control, the benefits are there, and this is the best way to spend £50bn. It needs "a really hard and tough look" - after that they may approve it, he says. Has he already made up his mind? No, says Balls. But the costs have gone up by billions and billions in the last few months, he says. He compares it to the Olympics - which was "fiscally small" compared to HS2. Richards asks if Balls has changed his mind from his 2010 Keynesian views on the economy and the deficit to today's much more conservative approach. Balls suggests Keynes would have approached the 2010 economy by prescribing more spending, but the 2015 case will be very different, because of the mistakes the Coalition has made. "They should have taken our advice in 2010 and 2011, and they didn't." Richards asks if his Keynesian instincts got ahead of his political instincts. Balls says "if you don't make what you think is the right argument, you are letting people down". Balls says he and Ed Miliband, 18 months from the election, have set out more detail of tax and spending manifesto commitments that Cameron did before the 2010 election or Blair did before the 1997 vote. He talks about meeting mothers who said the amount of free childcare they were currently getting was not enough. Richards asks if Balls is trying to take "tax and spend" out of the election campaign. He says it's very hard for an opposition party to win that argument. Balls says if that was his idea he would be drawing the OBR into party politics, which he says he is not. There is a brief outbreak of jargon as Balls hits Richards with a reference to "Barnett consequentials". I'll draw a veil over that. Richards begins by asking Balls about whether the Office for Budget Responsibility will agree to analyse Labour spending policies. Balls says Robert Chote of the OBR supports the idea. He says there is no desire to draw the OBR into party politics. He says the OBR is established enough to take on this new role. He wants exactly the same certification the government gets for its measures. We can change the OBR's charter so it says "governments and prospective governments", but he admits it may need a change in the law. "I've not had a legal opinion on that." Balls says he is surprised that George Osborne has rejected the idea. (Sorry for spelling Steve Richards's name wrong in that tweet below. Very sloppy.) Hi, this is Paul Owen taking over from Andy for the evening. I’m here in a disappointingly bland and windowless conference room in the Metropole hotel – a far cry, unfortunately, from the seedy glamour conjured up by its memorable cameo appearance in The Waste Land. But that’s enough culture. I’m here to see shadow chancellor Ed Balls interviewed by the Independent’s Steve Richards, a perceptive interviewer and commentator who can usually wring some new information out of his victims. This has been Balls’s day at the Labour conference, so fingers crossed Richards will manage to catch him off guard tonight. I’ll live-blog the whole thing here. • Labour delegates have demanded an end to the public sector pay freeze. They applauded Dave Prentis, the Unison general secretary,when he put the demand at the heart of his speech to the conference. More than anything, our people need hope. They need to know now that when elected, our party will end the pay cap which is blighting so many lives. It's not rocket science. If this is a cost of living conference, then the pay freeze must end. No ifs, no buts - a clear commitment to end the Tory pay freeze. And they overwhelmingly backed an emergency motion, passed without opposition, saying the next Labour manifesto should include "a strategy to end the public sector pay cap to help support economic growth, improve services and tackle the cost of living crisis". Afterwards a party spokesman played down the suggestion that this meant the party was committed to lifting the pay cap. Of course Labour supports a strategy to return to growth. As public sector pay caps are currently set until 2016, it is right that this motion was amended before it was debated. Labour Party policy is made via the National Policy Forum. In his speech Prentis also called for the value of the national minimum wage to be lifted so it matches that of the living wage. What is so wrong with a clear, unambigous Labour promise to turn a statutory minimum wage into a statutory living wage. • Sajid Javid, a Conservative Treasury minister, has claimed that Ed Balls' speech contains "debt-funded spending commitments". There was nothing new in Ed Balls’ speech – just more debt-funded spending commitments, breaking his own promise to show ‘iron discipline’ on public spending. Coming after this week’s revelation that there is a £27.9bn black hole in Labour’s spending plans, it’s no wonder nobody trusts Ed Balls or Ed Miliband to take the tough decisions needed to secure a recovery for everyone. • Charlie Whelan, Gordon Brown's former spin doctor, has said that Brown would be "shocked" to learn what Damian McBride had been doing on his behalf. This is what he told the Daily Politics. I had no idea what Damian was up to and neither did Gordon Brown. I do genuinely think that Gordon Brown would be more shocked than anybody by some of the allegations that have been made in his book. • Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, has said that Labour should do more to show that it understands ordinary people, particularly in the south of England, who go to church and like the royal family. (See 4.08pm.) • Vernon Coaker, the shadow Northern Ireland secretary, has said that nationalists must accept that the Good Friday agreement means Northern Ireland remains within the UK until a majority say otherwise. That's all from me for today. My colleague Paul Owen will post an update from the fringe. On her Reality Check blog Mona Chalabi says she thinks Labour's childcare pledge could cost £2bn, and that the £800m bank levy that is supposed to fund it won't cover the full cost. Here's a short afternoon reading list. • Alastair Campbell on his blog condemns Damian McBride. I honestly believe – and I know that this is a big if given how politics and politicians are – that if the big beasts of New Labour had all more or less stuck together, if Gordon had understood the value of the broader team and not just his own, if some of his darker people had been sacked early – or better still never been near the place – there is a chance we would still be there and this wretched coalition government would not be undoing so much of the good that Tony, Gordon and their colleagues did. That is the real crime of the horrible, nasty, vindictive politics and internal backstabbing that McBride, after all the years of GB denials,now admits to, so forgive me if I refuse to bow down before this attempt at Catholic redemption. • Siobhan Kenny at Comment is free says Damian McBride almost cost her her job. My colleagues tell me the problem with the comments is affecting our whole website. They are on the case at Guardian HQ. At a fringe meeting at lunchtime Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, said Labour must do more to understand people living in the south of England. He said that in Scotland it had become unusual for people to vote Conservative, and he said he hoped that people in the south of England, and affluent voters, never felt the same way about supporting Labour. We have to ensure that it never becomes counter-cultural among optimistic and contented voters to ever consider voting Labour. The Conservatives can win an election without Scotland. It is harder for us to win an election without winning a substantial share of votes in some of the 209 constituencies south .... The Labour Party has sometimes talked in the past about respecting diversity and sometimes that's been, rightly, about the extension of civil rights for those with disabilities and the LGBT (lesbian, gay, bisexual and transgender). I think there needs to be a new language about respecting diversity that is about some of those voters who live their lives in a way that is entirely straightforward. They go to church on Sunday and they have an immeasurable sense of patriotism and a real affection for the royal family and much else besides. We have got to have a genuine understanding and attachment to that set of voters. Effectively he was saying Labour should make more of an effort to understand and appreciate those who go to church and like the royal family. The Telegraph's Jon Swaine has found Gordon Brown at the United Nations in New York and asked him about Damian McBride. He did not get an answer. The comments don't seem to be working at the moment. I don't know why, but I'm trying to get that sorted. On her Reality Check blog, Mona Chalabi says that the Conservative costings for Labour's jobs guarantee seem rather selective. And while we're on the subject of Len McCluskey, the Unite general secretary, he seemed to be threatening some kind of civil disobedience when he was speaking at a fringe meeting at lunchtime. He said this. If this government continues to attack us, and continues to try and reduce our rights even further, let me warn them this. If you push us further outside of the law, then be it on your own heads what the consequences are, because we’ve got no intention in Unite or in this movement of allowing laws introduced by the Bullingdon boys to restrict the legitimate and international rights we have to protect our workers ... Conference, if we’re pushed outside of the law then so be it. McCluskey has made this kind of comment before, talking about the prospect of using civil disobedience to resist any potential attempt to restrict labour rights. Earlier this year Len McCluskey, the Unite general secretary, suggested that Liam Byrne, the shadow work and pensions secretary, should be sacked. Since then there has been constant speculation about Byrne's future, and so it is hard to avoid seeing his speech to the conference just now in that context. As I said earlier, it sounded uncharacteristically leftish and combative (partly because he was quite shouty), But it went down well. Here are the main points. • Byrne said the Tories should be ashamed of their record. Once upon a time the Tories told us they cared: all those speeches in Easterhouse. And people gave them the benefit of the doubt. We were promised a Tory party that cared about the poor. We were promised a welfare revolution. We were promised we’re all in this together. Three years on I tell you the jury is in. A cost of living crisis. A million young people out of work. Long term unemployment at record highs. Disabled people living in fear. Child poverty rising. Living standards hammered. A promise that started in Easterhouse has ended with the spectacle of a Tory Minister, Michael Gove, blaming the poor for the temerity to turn up at a food bank. He should be ashamed. • He said Iain Duncan Smith, the work and pensions secretary, was incompetent as well as prejudiced. These Tories have let their prejudice destroy their policies. And just as bad as the prejudice is the incompetence. They say to err is human. But if you want someone to really screw it up you send for Iain Duncan Smith. • He said Labour was inviting Jenny Macklin, an Australian politician, to help investigate how to integrate health and social care. But let’s take Andy Burnham’s idea of whole person care and ask why not bring together health, social care - and the back to work system into one comprehensive service. That’s what Labour did in Australia. Let’s see if we can learn from that here. I’m delighted to announce that Jenny Macklin, a fine Labour politician and the architect of the system down under, is going to help us figure out how. In his speech Ed Balls also mentioned the Andy Burnham plan. He said this. Can we improve care and save money, as Andy Burnham has proposed, by pooling health and social care as a single service, with a single budget and joint management? That was significant because at one stage Balls was known to be sceptical about the idea. Taken together, these two speeches suggest that Labour is more committed to the proposal than when Burnham first floated it, slightly tentatively, in a speech. • Byrne said he said he was setting up a committee to look at ways of rescuing universal credit. The Tories’ system may prove dead on arrival. So we need a better way. So, today we announce our Universal Credit Rescue Committee. And I’m grateful to Kieran Quinn, leader of Tameside, the first pathfinder, for his offer to drive our work. • In the conference hall delegates are debating two composite motions on the cost of living. One of them calls for Labour to abandon the public sector pay cap. The session was opened by Liam Byrne, the shadow work and pensions secretary, who delivered an uncharacteristically leftish and combative speech. I'll post some excerpts shortly. Labour has had an opinion poll lead pretty much since the end of 2010, but as Paul Owen has been hearing, that lead is somewhat “fragile”. There were three reasons for this, said Ipsos Mori’s Gideon Skinner at a fringe event at the elegant Hotel du Vin just back from the Brighton seafront: Labour is losing ground on the electorate’s No 1 issue, the economy, with the Tory lead on this issue increasing to 18 points recently and people’s optimism about the economy rising too; Ed Miliband is “still not convincing the public”; and Labour is relying riskily on former Lib Dems switching to their party, when former Tories making the leap will surely be needed too. Skinner mapped Miliband’s approval ratings – which are not impressive – against those of failed Tory leaders Iain Duncan Smith and William Hague. “His highs have been better than Hague’s and IDS’s but his lows are fairly similar,” he said. He pointed out that Margaret Thatcher had worse ratings as a leader on some measures than opposition leaders James Callaghan and Neil Kinnock so these findings were “not the be-all and end-all … but I think there is something there to be concerned about”. Michael Dugher, shadow minister without portfolio, pointed out that a Lord Ashcroft poll recently had found Labour 14 points ahead in the 40 most marginal seats. And he said that in last year’s US election “[Mitt] Romney was ahead of [Barack] Obama on the economy but Obama was ahead of Romney in terms of standing up for ordinary people” – there was a “very similar trend here,” he said. Dugher claimed that “whenever Ed has exposure his ratings go up. We want more Ed Miliband, not less.” As the election approaches, Miliband would get more such exposure, he said. Skinner had shown the crowd a slide showing that 51% of people said they did not know what Miliband stood for, compared to 33% for Cameron, and Sophy Ridge of Sky, also on the panel, said this was “a troubling statistic”. She agreed that it was dangerous for Labour to rely on Lib Dems switching without persuading any Tories to do so – noting that in byelections since 2010 “the Lib Dems have held up pretty well”. Andrew Harrop of the Fabian Society agreed that “in every key marginal seat we need a few hundred Tories to vote Labour for us to be in a winning position”, adding that “on top of that we need to turn out people who didn’t vote last time” – young people, new voters, and people who have been switched off politics”. He was surprisingly confident that Labour “probably will be the largest party” in parliament after the next election, although it might not win a majority – but interestingly he warned that disputes over union funding of the party could be “a car crash this next six months … it could be so distracting”. Here's some reaction to the Ed Balls speech. From Simon Walker, director general of the Institute of Directors Politicians must understand the central role of business in creating wealth, prosperity and employment. I remain concerned by Labour’s proposals to increase the minimum wage in specific sectors, and requiring firms to take on an apprentice for every non-EU worker they hire is a recipe for disaster. Having said that, Ed Balls engaged with several issues vital to business leaders in his speech. We share his concerns about the extended Help to Buy scheme, which risks pushing up house prices while doing little to increase the supply of homes. Planning reform is the vital missing element here. There are still too many flaws in the system that hold up home-building. It was also significant that he questioned whether HS2 was the best way to spend £50bn. The IoD agrees with Labour that infrastructure investment is essential, but governments must be clear that they are getting value for money. Mr Balls’s commitment to discipline in the public finances is welcome, but will require Labour to clamp down on additional spending pledges. Their rhetoric must be matched by actions. He should be very wary of attempting to achieve deficit reduction through tax rises, which would be deeply damaging to the recovery. From John Longworth, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce Ed Balls identified many issues of concern to business in his conference speech, including infrastructure and growth finance. Yet Labour's policy alternatives on the key long-term economic issues remain embryonic at best. Many policy reviews have been undertaken, but Labour's renewed credibility with business rests on swiftly setting out concrete and clear measures on the key issues facing companies across the UK. And Labour must address worrying hints that they are starting to lose their nerve on transformative investment projects, including HS2, in favour of current spending. From Robin Hillman, a spokesman for the Robin Hood Tax campaign Our public services have been slashed to pay for the financial sector's mistakes, so it is good news that Labour is asking banks to make a greater contribution. But Labour's proposal lacks ambition – the bank levy and one-off bonus tax are little more than small change for the richest sector in Britain. Banks can afford to be paying billions more in tax, money that is desperately needed to shore up public services and create more jobs. From Craig Bennett, Friends of the Earth's director of policy and campaigns The shadow chancellor understands that the only long-term plan which works for Britain has low-carbon growth as its cornerstone. Mr Balls’s promise to let the Green Investment Bank borrow to invest and bring in a 2030 target for clean energy is exactly what we need to restore battered investor confidence. • Ed Balls, the shadow chancellor, has announced that a Labour government would increase the bank levy by £800m to fund the expansion of free childcare for three- and four-year-olds from 15 hours a week to 25 hours a week. Alison Garnham, chief executive of Child Poverty Action Group, welcomed the announcement. This proposal is great news for hard-pressed families struggling with the cost of childcare. Childcare costs are cripplingly high, and while tax credits give important help, most UK parents are still footing the lion's share of the bill. The more hours of childcare parents can get for free, the more it will help with the squeeze on living standards they are facing. • Balls has questioned the wisdom of spending £50bn on the HS2 high-speed rail project, creating speculation that Labour could abandon its support for the scheme before the 2015 general election. • A Treasury minister, Sajid Javid, has said the government will not change the remit of the Office for Budget Responsibility to allow it to audit Labour's spending plans. He was responding to Balls's call for the OBR to be allowed to assess the accuracy of Labour's claims. But Robert Chote, the head of the OBR, said he thought it would be a good idea to have opposition spending policies independently audited. I think the idea of having independent scrutiny of party manifestos in the runup to an election is generally a good idea. That would provide a more informed public debate, and would, for example, help potentially with coming up with coalition agreements afterwards. Certainly, with my former hat on with the Institute for Fiscal Studies, we were involved in trying to do that ahead of the last two general elections. Chote said that the OBR was taking legal advice to see whether changing its remit would require primary legislation. He also said that in the Netherlands the equivalent of the OBR publishes its assessment of opposition plans 12 weeks before the election. For the same to happen in the UK, parties would have to publish their manifestos much earlier than they do now, he said. • Jim Murphy, the shadow defence secretary, has said that Labour would make attacking a soldier a specific criminal offence. • Chuka Umunna, the shadow business secretary, has said Labour would hold an inquiry into blacklisting in the construction industry if the government does not order one. (See 11.56am.) • Alan Milburn, the former Labour cabinet minister, has said that Labour needs to find some "beef" to bolster its policies and should not be conniving with Tory attempts to reduce immigration. As Rowena Mason reports, Milburn said he was really worried about the direction of the party on immigration, shortly after Ed Miliband said Labour would cut the number of foreign workers coming to the UK. We seem to be moving towards a nice cosy consensus that I'm really worried about … our future relies on more immigration not less. If we've got an ageing population, with fewer domestically produced workers, the gap can only be closed if are prepared in a sensible, managed and rational way to encourage immigration not close it down. • David Lammy, the former minister, has told the World at One that Ed Miliband has not yet persuaded voters that he is a prime minister in waiting. You’ll get a constituency of people who switch on to the bedroom tax and welfare issues like that, but large segments of the country who own their own home and want to know how you’re going to run the economy because they don’t want interest rates to go up, or they want their kids to be able to get on the housing ladder ... I think Ed Milband has moved to being an effective opposition leader and has taken bold decisions, like on Leveson and his position on Syria. But he now needs to move into being a prime minister in waiting and I don’t think he’s crossed that Rubicon and that needs to happen from this conference outward if we are to be serious about heading to No 10. • Chris Bryant, the shadow immigration minister, has said that Labour would reintroduce the overseas domestic worker visa to prevent a form of virtual slavery in Britain. As Randeep Ramesh reports, Bryant told a fringe meeting that after the government abolished the visa last year, domestic workers brought into the country to work in homes were "completely tied to their employers". My colleague Mona Chalabi is wring a Reality Check blog about some of the claims in Ed Balls' speech. In particular, she is looking at the jobs guarantee and the childcare pledge. Here's the full text of Ed Balls's speech. The Treasury minister Sajid Javid says the government will not let the OBR audit Labour's spending plans. And here's some more general Twitter comment on the speech. From the BBC's Nick Robinson From the Spectator's James Forsyth As usual, Twitter is the place to go for debate on the great issues of the day. (It was rather an odd remark, and it was not entirely clear what Ed Balls meant. Or maybeI'm just innocent.) For the past three years pundits and members of the public have been saying that they want to hear what Labour would do differently. Today, Ed Balls confronted that head-on, and delivered a lengthy, explicit answer. Gordon Brown was fond of using lists in his speeches and Balls used the same device at least twice, when he was reminding the audience what the last Labour government achieved and when he told them, right at the end, what Labour would do in 2015. It was a checklist for activists, ideal for campaigners who need to be able to answer the "why vote Labour" question on the doorstep. There is a lot of interest, too, in what he had to say about HS2. He has been using the no "blank cheque" line for some time, but to put that up in headlights in a conference speech signals, more clearly than before, that Labour is threatening to pull the plug on the project. (If you want to know why, it helps to look at the polls. Public support for HS2 is falling, more people are now opposed than in favour, and Labour supporters are particularly hostile to the idea.) The chances of HS2 ever actually going ahead are looking a bit slimmer than they were. I'll post some more reaction shortly. Balls is winding up now. Conference, we are not the first Labour generation to face a huge deficit and the need for spending restraint and a country crying out for change. We are not the first generation to be awed by the scale of what needs to be done to transform our country. And as we prepare for the 2015 general election, to be held in the 70th anniversary year of the end of the second world war, let us take our inspiration from the great reforming Labour government of 1945. And he finishes with a practical list for delegates. So in the coming weeks and months, when people ask what would a Labour government do, let’s go out and tell them: Jobs for young people guaranteed. Expanding free childcare. A British Investment Bank. Infrastructure delivered. Green investment unlocked. The deficit down fairly. Tax cuts for millions – not millionaires. Reforming our banks. The minimum wage raised. Our NHS save.d Tackling tax avoidance. Rail fares capped. The bedroom tax scrapped. Building the homes we need. This what a Labour government could do. Let us together make it happen. Thank you. Following Sir George Cox’s review on short-termism, we will change takeover rules and corporate incentives and reform our tax system to stop short-term asset-stripping and support long-term investment. And why not use any revenues from the planned increase in the licence fees for the mobile phone spectrum, expected to be over £1bn in the next parliament, to capitalise the British Investment Bank so that, region by region, we can get small and growing businesses the finance they need to grow and create jobs? And, conference, we will set up an independent infrastructure commission, as recommended to us by the chair of the Olympic Delivery Authority, Sir John Armitt, to end dither and delay in infrastructure planning. • Balls says mobile phone spectrum revenues should be used to capitalise the British Investment Bank. Just look at the British car industry in the 1970s and 1980s. Trying to compete on cost. Cutting back on innovation, quality and skills. Plagued by terrible industrial relations. And now look at the renaissance of Jaguar Land Rover – creating thousands more jobs and exporting round the world. Not by cutting corners, but based on world-class, long-term investment in innovation, skills and supply-chains. Chuka Umunna and I are determined to learn from this success. And I can announce that Mike Wright, executive director at Jaguar Land Rover, will now lead a review for us on how we can help strengthen our manufacturing supply-chains and deliver the skills and innovation Britain needs to succeed. Balls says companies look on the government's industrial policy with dismay. The RDAs abolished. The Heseltine growth review neutered. The Business Bank a damp squib. Apprenticeships for young people falling. Energy policy in chaos. Borrowing powers for the Green Investment Bank postponed. And on infrastructure, dither, delay and inaction. Conference, we cannot succeed as a country with this ‘race to the bottom’, deregulation, laissez-faire and old-style ‘trickle-down economics’. It’s a narrow and defeatist vision. Balls turns to childcare. Childcare is a vital part of our economic infrastructure that, alongside family support and flexible working, should give parents the choice to stay at home with their children when they are very small and to balance work and family as they grow older. So to make work pay for families, we must act. Stephen Twigg set out yesterday how we will guarantee childcare available for all primary school children from 8am to 6pm. But we need to do more for families with nursery-age children too. The banks will fund this, he explains. In the last financial year, the banks paid a staggering £2.7bn less in overall tax than they did in 2010. Over the last two years the government’s bank levy has raised £1.6bn less than even they said it would. At a time when resources are tight and families are under pressure that cannot be right. So I can announce today, the next Labour government will increase the bank levy rate to raise an extra £800m a year. And we will use the money, for families where all parents are in work, to increase free childcare places for three- and four-year-olds from 15 hours to 25 hours a week. For the first time, parents will be able to work part-time without having to worry about the cost of childcare. This gets a strong round of applause. Balls says Labour will encourage employers to pay the living wage. And he says Labour would introduce a 10p starting rate of tax, paid for by a mansion tax. So building on the success of Labour’s Future Jobs Fund – so short-sightedly scrapped by this government – we will introduce a Compulsory Jobs Guarantee for young people and the long-term unemployed. We will fund this by a repeat of the tax on bank bonuses and by restricting pension tax relief for the very highest earners to the same rate as the average taxpayer. And we will work with employers to make sure there will be a paid job for all young people out of work for more than 12 months and adults out of work for two years or more, which people will have to take up or lose benefits. That is welfare reform that works. And conference, in tough times it's even more important that all our policies and commitments are properly costed and funded. The British people rightly want to know that the sums add up. So we will go one step further and ask the independent Office for Budget Responsibility – the watchdog set up by this government – to independently audit the costings of every individual spending and tax measure in Labour's manifesto at the next election. This is the first time a shadow chancellor – the first time any political party in Britain – has ever said it wants this kind of independent audit. It’s a radical change from what's gone before, but the right thing to do to help restore trust in politics. Balls turns to HS2. Under this government the High Speed 2 project has been totally mismanaged and the costs have shot up to £50bn. David Cameron and George Osborne have made clear they will go full steam ahead with this project – no matter how much the costs spiral up and up. They seem willing to put their own pride and vanity above best value for money for the taxpayer. Labour will not take this irresponsible approach. So let me be clear, in tough times – when there is less money around and a big deficit to get down – there will be no blank cheque from me as a Labour chancellor for this project or for any project. Because the question is – not just whether a new high-speed line is a good idea or a bad idea, but whether it is the best way to spend £50bn for the future of our country. • Balls reaffirms Labour's decision not to offer a "blank cheque" for HS2. With our zero-based review, we will make different choices. So we will ask: can we improve care and save money, as Andy Burnham has proposed, by pooling health and social care as a single service, with a single budget and joint management? And, conference, we will repeal the damaging and costly Tory privatisation of the NHS. And we will ask: does it really make sense to have separate costly management and bureaucracy for so many separate government departments, agencies, fire services and police forces? And, conference, we won’t pay for new free schools in areas where there are excess school places, while parents in other areas are struggling to get their children into a local school. Balls illustrates some of the tough decisions Labour will have to take. And with our zero-based review – a review of every pound spent by government from the bottom up – Rachel Reeves and my shadow cabinet colleagues have begun the work of identifying savings so that we can switch resources to Labour’s priorities. But we won’t be able to reverse all the spending cuts and tax rises the Tories have pushed through. And we will have to govern with less money around. The next Labour government will have to make cuts too. Because while jobs and growth are vital to getting the deficit down – something this government has never understood – they cannot magic the whole deficit away at a stroke. So delivering our Labour goals will be harder than at any time in living memory. But it can be done – if we get people back to work and strengthen our economy, cut out waste and focus relentlessly on our priorities, and make sure difficult choices are not ducked, but are rooted in our values, in fairness and in common sense. So, conference, at a time when the public services that pensioners rely on are under such pressure, we cannot continue paying the winter fuel allowance to the richest 5% of pensioners. We won’t be able to reverse the government’s cuts to child benefit for the highest earners. We will keep the benefits cap, but make sure it properly reflects local housing costs. We will have a cap on structural social security spending. And yes, over the long-term, as our population ages, there will need to be increases in the retirement age. Labour will inherit a very difficult situation, he says. And we stand to inherit a very difficult situation. After three wasted years of lost growth, far from balancing the books, in 2015 there is now set to be a deficit of over £90bn. David Cameron and George Osborne’s failure on the economy has led to their failure to get the deficit down, and it will be up to the next Labour government to finish the job. And I need to be straight with this vonference and the country about what that means. The government’s day to day spending totals for 2015-16 will have to be our starting point Any changes to the current spending plans for that year will be fully funded and set out in advance in our manifesto. There will be no more borrowing for day-to-day spending. And we will set out tough fiscal rules – to balance the current budget and get the national debt on a downward path. Balls says the government should not cut infrastructure spending. That is why we have consistently said, it is why the IMF have said, bring forward £10bn of infrastructure investment right now, build 400,000 affordable houses over the next two years, create half a million jobs and thousands of apprenticeships. That is the way to secure an economy that works for all and is built to last. This seems to be a "for now" policy, not necessarily an election commitment. In one of his interviews this morning, Balls said Labour would not say whether Labour would increase capital spending until nearer the election. George, it’s basic economics. If you push up housing demand, but don’t act to boost housing supply, all that happens is that you push house prices up and up. And the end result is that the very people your policy should be helping – young first time buyers – will find it even harder to get on the housing ladder. • Balls says the Help to Buy mortgage guarantee scheme could fuel a house price boom. (Vince Cable has expressed exactly the same worry.) The Tories are on the side of privilege, Balls says. And isn’t it now clear whose side David Cameron and George Osborne are really on? Cutting taxes for hedge funds. Trying to bribe working people to give up their rights. Country suppers at Chequers for Tory party fundraising. Protecting the privileges of the few, while the many work hard and don't see the benefit. For all their claims to be modernisers, with Cameron and Osborne, it's not been “Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?” it’s “Who Wants to Help a Millionaire?” Not “phone a friend”, but “cut taxes for your friends.” Not “50-50” but “winner takes all”. Conference, isn’t it about time they “asked the audience”? Because we know the truth. The country knows the truth. David Cameron and George Osborne. For the few, not the many. Balls is still on the failures of coalition's economic policies. Let us remind them: Prices rising faster than wages for 38 out of the 39 months since David Cameron entered Downing Street. Three years of flatlining. The slowest recovery for over 100 years. A million young people out of work. Welfare spending soaring. More borrowing to pay for their economic failure. The coalition's policies are not working for ordinary families, he says. For millions of ordinary families, worried about how to make ends meet when wages are falling, and prices are going up For the young couples struggling to get on the housing ladder because the chronic shortage of homes is forcing up prices For ordinary working families – the aspirational majority – who work hard, pay their taxes, who want to get on and not just get by, but who are working harder for less as the cost of living keeps on rising This Tory plan isn't working. Balls asks if people felt a little sorry for Cameron during the summer. Cameron was photographed on the beach in his shorts. Balls sympathised. He has been photographed like that. But his wife Yvette said Cameron looked slim. Who could she have been comparing him to, he asks. (That's a joke about his own tum.) Balls turns to the economy. The Tories failed by their own terms, he says. They claimed in 2010 that faster tax rises and deeper spending cuts would secure the economic recovery and make it stronger … They said their plan would make people better off and get the deficit down. And on every test they set themselves, this prime minister and chancellor have failed. Balls returns to Syria, and foreign policy. (In the week when MPs were debating this, he said very little, if anything, in public on the subject.) And, conference, when David Cameron and William Hague now have the nerve to go round saying that parliament's refusal to be bounced into military action in Syria has 'diminished' Britain, let us reply: no Labour government will ever stand aside when terrible atrocities are committed and international law is broken. But, conference, we know what has diminished Britain. Flouncing out of a European summit, leaving Britain isolated and without influence. That's what has diminished Britain. Absurdly comparing Britain to Greece and choking off business confidence and our recovery as a result. That's what has diminished Britain. Stigmatising the unemployed and the low paid and calling them shirkers, driving vans round out streets telling immigrants to ‘go home’, attacking our police and teachers and social workers, peddling the lie that 'Britain is broken'. That's what has diminished Britain. Conference, we know who diminishes Britain. David Cameron has diminished Britain. Balls praises Ed Miliband. Miliband has been Leading from the front – on phone-hacking, banking reform, putting the crisis of the ‘squeezed middle’ on the political agenda before anyone else. And who on Syria had the courage to stand up and say that if the case was sound and the United Nations was properly engaged, Labour would support military action, but that Labour would not support a gung-ho prime minister, putting decision before evidence in a reckless dash to conflict. Conference, Ed was right – and he prevailed. My friend, our leader, Britain’s next prime minister, Ed Miliband. Balls turns to Scotland. With Alex Salmond in a state of total confusion on the single most important economic decision a country can take – which currency to have: first he wanted the euro – saying sterling was a “millstone around our neck” and now he wants independence but to keep the pound all the same. Let us win the argument that we are Better Together in next year’s Scottish referendum. Demonstrating, as Carwyn Jones and Welsh Labour have done so brilliantly, that it is our Labour values of co-operation, solidarity and social justice that best secure our union. Balls thanks Margaret Beckett, who has co-chaired the economic policy commission with him. And he says that he is a member of both Unison and Unite. Next year, in the European elections, Labour should kick the BNP out of the European parliament, he says. Balls says Labour also got rid of Section 28, which paved the way for the gay marriage legislation. And he reminds delegates that he will be in the frontline at the general election. As the Labour and Co-operative MP for Morley & Outwood – majority just 1,101 – the seat David Cameron needed to win to get a Tory majority in 2010 and, because of our hard work and determination, the seat he failed to win … conference, I am up for the battle to come. He mentions the achievements of the last Labour government. The national minimum wage. Our schools and hospitals rebuilt. NHS waiting times down from 18 months to 18 weeks. More apprenticeships. Bank of England independence. Not joining the euro. That last, of course, is one he was particularly associated with. He says no party since the 1920s has gone from losing office to a working majority at the next general election. And he sets out Labour's mission. To show we are ready for the great challenges we will face on spending control and the deficit. To set out Labour ‘s alternative plan to create jobs, make work pay and tackle the rising cost of living. And to secure a mandate to build a One Nation Britain: a strong recovery that is built to last. an economy that works for the many and not just a few at the top. Ed Balls is starting his speech now. In his speech to the conference Chuka Umunna, the shadow business secretary, said that Labour would hold a full inquiry into blacklisting in the construction industry if the government did not set one up. Society working with business for the common good. In that spirit we want people to value their work and we want companies to value their people. That’s why we will act to outlaw zero hours contracts where they exploit people. And it’s why if this government won’t launch a full inquiry into the disgraceful blacklisting in the construction industry, we will.
