Mr Osborne’s move has reopened a Cabinet debate over the previous decision to ring-fence the NHS, schools and overseas aid budgets, meaning any extra savings must be found from the non-protected departments that have already suffered cuts announced since 2010. • Elizabeth Rigby and Tanya Powley in the Financial Times (subscription) say industrialists are complaining that green taxes are pushing them to "crisis point". British industry has warned that green taxes are pushing it to “crisis point” even as George Osborne prepares to cut environmental levies on household energy bills in this week’s Autumn Statement. Leading industrialists from companies such as Tata Steel and Ineos told the Financial Times that green taxes were putting their British plants at a competitive disadvantage relative to those plants’ European competitors. They urged David Cameron and his chancellor to extend their pledge to “roll back” the levies on households to manufacturing plants. British industry has warned that green taxes are pushing it to “crisis point” even as George Osborne prepares to cut environmental levies on household energy bills in this week’s Autumn Statement. Leading industrialists from companies such as Tata Steel and Ineos told the Financial Times that green taxes were putting their British plants at a competitive disadvantage relative to those plants’ European competitors. They urged David Cameron and his chancellor to extend their pledge to “roll back” the levies on households to manufacturing plants. • Tom Bawden in the Independent says the government is changing the definition of fuel poverty. The Government is attempting to manipulate official figures to bring down fuel poverty, it is claimed today. A clause in the Energy Bill will change the definition of the key poverty indicator, reducing the number of English households counted as “fuel-poor” from 3.2 million to 2.4 million overnight. The new definition, which could come into force before Christmas, will instantly reduce the percentage of fuel-poor households in England by nearly a third, from 15 per cent to 11 per cent, according to calculations by MPs on the cross-party Environmental Audit Committee (EAC). “The Government is shifting the goalposts on fuel poverty so that official statistics record far fewer households as fuel-poor,” said the committee’s chair, Joan Walley MP. “The changes to the fuel-poverty definition and target should be stopped unless the Government is prepared to make a public commitment to end fuel poverty altogether.” ScottishPower has now become the last of the big six energy companies to put out a statement responding to the government's fuel bills announcement. It says it hopes to avoid further price rises in 2014. ScottishPower welcomes the Government’s announcement of proposals to reduce the impact of the Energy Company Obligation, an introduction of a universal rebate and other measures. Once implemented early in the New Year, these initiatives will lead to real savings for customers in 2014 and ScottishPower agrees with Government estimates which suggest total savings for households of around £50 for a typical dual fuel customer. Neil Clitheroe, CEO of Retail and Generation at ScottishPower, said: “We are acutely aware of the burden that higher gas and electricity bills place on households and have been working with Government to ease the strain. We are pleased the Government has recognised the impact of green and social levies on rising consumer energy bills and we have worked collaboratively with them to identify a number of individual savings not only to these programmes, but also other rising input costs. “We look forward to working with Government on the detail of these proposals. We will continue to be as open as possible about consumer bills, explaining the impact of input costs on a line by line basis. As always, we will continue to try and avoid further price rises in 2014, but this will depend on whether there are increases in wholesale energy prices or other costs outside our control." The Office for National Statistics has published a report on what has happened to average household incomes over the last 35 years. Here's the summary and here's the full report (pdf). It says that since the start of the downturn median household income has fallen by 3.8%, allowing for inflation. But there is a big gap between what's happened to pensioner households and non-pensioner households. However, when looking separately at non-retired and retired households, the median income for non-retired households fell by 6.4% between 2007/08 and 2011/12, while the median income for retired households grew by 5.1%. While David Cameron has been tweeting about his meeting with the Chinese president. Here's Ed Miliband on the energy bills announcement. David Cameron has been meeting the Chinese president, Xi Jinping. Here's what the Press Association has filed about how it started. The two men shook hands for the cameras in front of their nations' flags before going into talks with small teams of advisers. Xi thanked Cameron for the congratulations he sent after he was elected general secretary of the Chinese Communist Party and President of China a year ago. And he asked the PM to pass on his "cordial greetings and best wishes to Her Majesty the Queen". Cameron responded: "I will certainly pass your regards to Her Majesty. I know that she too wants to see very strong relations between Britain and China and also looks forward to future potential visits between our two countries." Xi reminded Cameron that they first met in 2007, during his visit to Beijing as leader of the opposition, when he expressed the hope that he would return one day as PM. The Chinese president added: "On several occasions, you have expressed a positive attitude of valuing the role of China and your commitment to enhancing the UK's relations with China. I appreciate your position." And he told Cameron: "We have come to a crucial moment in global economic recovery and change in the international landscape. "Both China and UK are major economies in the world and permanent members of the UN Security Council. The relation between our two countries not only concerns our respective development, but also will exert major global impact." Cameron said that his decision to bring six government ministers and a large business delegation with him was "a signal of the importance I attach to the success of China and, I hope, the success of the China-British relationship." Here is is some more reaction to the announcement. From Paul King, chief executive of the UK Green Building Council Make no mistake, this is bad news for people who cannot afford to heat their homes, especially if they live in solid walled properties, and bad news for thousands of construction industry workers who may well be joining the dole queue this Christmas. It remains perverse that the prime minister is attempting to reduce energy bills by slashing the very scheme that is designed to bring them down for good. We have to recognise that some in the coalition have fought hard for a package of incentives to sweeten the pill. Encouraging households to take up energy efficiency measures when moving into a property through a ‘Stamp Duty rebate’ is something we have repeatedly called for. From Richard Lloyd, executive director at Which?, the consumer organisation It's about time the government started getting the cost of energy under control and this will be a welcome step in the right direction for consumers who are struggling with the increased cost of living. It is right to refocus the Energy Company Obligation so that it gives greater priority to those most in need of help with lower cost measures such as loft and cavity wall insulation. But in return for more time to get this right, the suppliers must now commit to greater transparency and to getting their costs down, fast. From Cathy Debenham at YouGen, a renewable energy advice website While we are pleased to see that the elements of the Energy Companies Obligation aimed at low income and vulnerable households have been maintained, it is disappointing that the Carbon Emissions Reduction Obligation is being reduced. This offers help for people living in homes with solid walls or hard-to-treat cavities. They often have the coldest and most expensive to heat homes, and very few people will be able to insulate without financial help. People are already finding it very difficult to get ECO for solid wall insulation, and we believe that help needs to be strengthened, rather than reduced. Just as the feed-in tariff has helped the solar industry to develop and grow - with more people installing solar panels and massive price reductions as a result - we believe that the solid wall industry needs similar support to grow. This should encourage innovation and economies of scale so that it eventually becomes affordable without subsidy. Here's Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, on the government's energy bills announcement. Even after these changes to levies, energy bills are still rising and the average household will still be paying £70 more for their energy than last winter. Any help is better than none, but you can judge this government by who they’re asking to pick up the tab – the taxpayer. The energy companies have got off scot-free. This shows why nothing less than a price freeze and action to reset the market to stop the energy companies overcharging again in the future will do. Energy UK, the trade body representing energy firms, has welcomed the government's energy bills announcement. Here's its statement. This is a good outcome for consumers. The changes will bring a welcome set of reductions to the policy costs which are a significant part of the household bill, have been rising and were set to rise further. Costs are spread in a fairer manner and household bills are reduced. The industry will continue to insulate homes. The parts of ECO that target fuel poverty have been increased and the complicated solid wall installation has a more practical timescale and is made less complex. Companies will be making their own announcements individually about what it means for their customers. Labour is unimpressed by today's energy bills announcement. Here's Alex Belardinelli, Ed Balls's press spokesman. The Tory Treasury twitter feed (an official account) has hit back. Robert Peston's BBC blog on the energy bills announcement explains the changes very clearly. EDF has now responded to the government's fuel bills announcement. It says the move vindicates its decision to announce a 3.9% price increase (which was based on the assumption that the government was going to cut the cost of green levies). The Carbon Brief blog has posted a graph on Twitter putting the cuts to fuel bills generated by today's announcement in perspective. Ed Davey, the energy secretary, has been giving interviews this morning about today's announcement. Here are the main points. • Davey said that the government was saving households £50 a year on average while at the same time "protecting the fuel poor". • He said that energy bills would have been higher if Labour had been in office. For example, under Ed Miliband, they were going to put something called the renewable heat incentive onto energy bills – that would have cost an extra £179. From day one, we took off energy charges from the bill that Labour had wanted, so under Labour energy bills would be much higher than they are now. • He said Labour's plan for a price freeze would hurt the fuel poor because it would lead to a cut in investment. It could also lead to small suppliers going out of business, he said. One of the reasons why Labour’s proposal is economic illiteracy is that if wholesale prices went up during their price freeze, what would happen is the smaller companies - who wouldn’t be able to trade for a loss unlike the bigger companies - would go out of business, and that would reduce competition. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. Four of the big six energy companies have now responded to the government's energy bills announcement. Here is what they are saying. Statement from British Gas British Gas is reducing household gas and electricity prices by an average of 3.2%, which is equivalent to £41 on average off the annual dual fuel bill1. An additional £12 customer rebate for the Government’s Warm Home Discount (WHD) scheme brings a total annual saving of £53 to the average dual fuel customer. Today’s announcement follows the Government’s proposed decision to make changes to the Energy Company Obligation and to introduce a rebate to customers to cover the cost of the WHD scheme. It also takes into account the agreement between the Government and electricity network companies to defer an element of electricity distribution costs in the period from April 2014 to March 2015. British Gas is passing on the benefit of all these changes to its customers in full. The price reduction will take effect from 1 January 2014, and will apply to all customers, whether on variable or fixed tariffs. Chris Weston, the Managing Director of British Gas, said: “British Gas is pleased to be cutting energy bills by an average of £53 from 1 January. We’ve been able to do this because the Government has committed to making changes to the environmental and social obligations that are paid for through energy bills. These changes will now allow us to help more people at a lower cost. Two weeks ago British Gas announced it was increasing electricity bills by 10.4%, and gas bills by 8.4%. Statement from SSE Following the announcement by the UK government that it is to consult on proposals to reduce the future costs associated with the Energy Company Obligation and to facilitate other measures to reduce the cost of electricity and gas for customers, SSE can confirm that it will pass any resulting savings on to customers before the end of the current financial year. On the basis of today's announcement and the planned consultation, SSE plans to reduce its household energy prices before the end of its financial year. The overall impact of the proposed changes, including the rebate which is expected to apply in Autumn 2014, should lead to a typical dual fuel customer benefiting by around 4%. This should equate to a saving of around £50 for a typical dual fuel customer. These changes should remove a significant amount of upward pressures from energy bills. SSE plans to extend its current Autumn 2014 price cap at the new lower levels to Spring 2015, subject to there being no marked and sustained increase in wholesale energy costs, network costs or new policy-related costs. William Morris, Managing Director, Retail, said: "We promised our customers that they would benefit from any cost savings and although there is some detail still to be worked out, we are able to commit to lowering our prices. If the proposals set out today are implemented, it will mean energy costs for a typical dual fuel customer can be cut by around 4%. "We continue to believe that policy costs should be paid through general taxation, not energy bills. The Warm Homes Discount changes are therefore a step in the right direction" Statement from Paul Massara, CEO at RWE npower We welcome today's announcement as an important step in cutting energy costs for our domestic customers. As a result of this announcement we will reduce our prices. We are currently calculating how large this reduction will be, and can assure our customers that it will fully reflect the reduction in the costs to our business. "In addition, in order to give our customers greater certainty with their household budgets, we don’t plan to increase energy prices before Spring 2015, unless there are increases in wholesale energy costs or network charges. Statement from E.ON. Here's an extract. E.ON has not announced a price rise since December 2012 and once again is the only major company to hold off for so long into the winter. The result of these changes [announced by the government today] should mean our customers will pay less next year than otherwise would have been the case. We are working through the details and will provide an update in due course. E.ON can also provide customers with the assurance that it doesn't expect to have to raise prices as a result of social or environmental obligations in the next 18 months. There remains a risk, however, that increases in network charges or wholesale energy costs for example could force a price increase but the company (E.ON) very much hopes that situation does not occur. The news release from the Department of Energy and Climate Change (DECC) about energy bills is unusually detailed. I have mentioned the key points already. (See 8.53am.) Here are nine points from the small print worth flagging up. 1. The £50 a year per household saving is an average. "The value of the benefit will vary between companies," DECC says. 2. The £50 figure applies to England, Scotland and Wales. Separate arrangements apply to Northern Ireland. 3. Mostly the cut is being funded by regulatory changes announced by the government. But firms are also taking "voluntary action" to cut distribution costs, contributing around £5 on average to the value of the cuts. 4. The overall package is carbon neutral. Watering down the Energy Company Obligation (ECO), by giving companies an extra two years to meet its targets (the deadline has been extended from March 2015 to March 2017), actually increases emissions. By the government has announced various energy saving measures, for home buyers, landlords and public sector buildings, to offset this impact. These measures will cost £540m over three years. 5. Around 60,000 homes will benefit from the scheme allowing people to get £1,000 to spend on energy saving measures when they buy a new home. 6. Funding for Green Deal Communities is being extended from £20m to £80m. 7. The Green Deal is being "strengthened, streamlined and reformed". There are more details here, in a separate news release. You could read this as an admission that so far it has not been a glittering success. 8. Energy firms will have become "more transparent" about how much they spend on social and environmental measures. This may require changes to the law. 9. Measures to prevent fraud, particularly relating to loft and cavity wall insulation, are being introduced. Is this an admission that there is a potential problem? This morning the government has announced the full details of its plan to curb energy bill increases. The broad outline of the plans, which will cut bills from what they would otherwise have been by £50 on average, have been known for some days, but this morning all the components of the plan have been set out in a news release from the Department of Energy and Climate Change. Here's an extract: Today the Government confirms it will reduce the current cost of levies by: Establishing a rebate saving the average customer £12 on their bill, for the next two years, worth a total of £600 million. The Warm Homes Discount will continue to help millions of vulnerable households receive a £135 rebate off their energy bill. Reducing the cost of the Energy Company Obligation (ECO), an insulation scheme delivered by major energy suppliers. This will result in £30-£35 off bills, on average, next year. The existing dedicated support in ECO for low income and vulnerable households will be maintained and extended from March 2015 until March 2017. In addition, electricity distribution network companies are willing to take voluntary action to reduce network costs in 2014/15. This would allow a further one-off reduction of an average of around £5 on electricity bills, which energy suppliers will be able to pass on to their customers as well. And the energy firms have been reacting. • British Gas is to cut dual fuel energy bills by an average of £53 from January 1. • Npower says it will reduce bills and that "we don't plan to increase energy prices before spring 2015, unless there are increases in wholesale energy costs or network charges." • Scottish & Southern Energy said it expects a saving for the typical dual-fuel customer of around 4% before the end of March, equivalent to a saving of around £50 for a typical dual-fuel customer. Ed Davey, the energy secretary, has been giving interviews about his announcement this morning. I will post the highlights soon, as well as more detail about the announcement, and more reaction to it, from the energy companies and others. Otherwise, the main event today is David Cameron's "Don't mention the Dalai Lama" tour of China. My colleague Nicholas Watt is travelling with the prime minister. Here's his latest story. The political news diary at home is threadbare. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary at about 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened

fields.headline: Politics as it happened
fields.trailText: Crisis talks after reports of exposure to asbestos in the NBN rollout
fields.bodyText: That was quite a day. Let's take stock with the evening summary. The day dawned with asbestos and the NBN roll out dominating the political news cycle. There was a brief diversion into a bipartisan interlude on the republic between Wayne Swan and Malcolm Turnbull; and on a new administrative headquarters for DisabilityCare to be located in Geelong - but within moments, it was back to a brutal day in national affairs. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten and Communications Minister Stephen Conroy gathered a group of stakeholders for a meeting on the asbestos problems in parliament at lunchtime in order to get to the bottom of which contractors or residents could have been exposed to asbestos during the NBN rollout. Telstra chief David Thodey took responsibility, and the group resolved to set up an independent taskforce to examine the issues. The day rolled forward to Question Time. There were short sharp blows on asbestos. The Coalition sought to pressure the government over the management of the whole mess by asking whether it had pursued this issue before media reports surfaced last week of asbestos-related incidents. Shorten said he had, and produced some letters to back the claim up. The government then returned fire by raising some unfortunate comments Tony Abbott made about asbestos campaigner Bernie Banton - and also highlighting that deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop had once worked as a lawyer for James Hardie. The Coalition changed course and then went on boat arrivals, and national security, raising the case of an Egyptian jihadist held in a low security detention facility in South Australia. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison used the case to suspend the standing orders - he was seeking an independent inquiry into the case. Morrison said allowing a known jihadist into the country, and allowing him to be held in a low security immigration processing facility, raised profound questions about whether Australia's current border measures and security assessments were adequate, given the strain the whole detention system is under with an upswing in irregular boat arrivals. Morrison only just lost this vote, which was resolved 68-69. Such motions require an absolute majority to proceed. The government ran interference in this attack by highlighting comments from Julie Bishop suggesting the Indonesians would in fact turn people smugglers' boats around, even though they are saying publicly they have no intention of doing so. Here, incidentally, is a tear sheet of one of Shorten's letters to Telstra which we chased up after Question Time. Anyway, that was about the sum of the parts. Thanks very much indeed to Mike Bowers for his fine work today, and to readers of #politicslive Let's do it all again tomorrow. If you've been following this issue and our coverage today, and are interested, I'd encourage you to read Kath Viner's blog post in full. You can do so here. Here's the bottom line: It was a long interview. We journalists and editors need to be careful in making sure selections don't distort the overall intent. But we believe we have represented Bishop's comments accurately. Kath Viner: Since all reports of long interviews necessarily select the parts regarded as newsworthy, we think the key issue was not that a selection had been made but rather whether the selected parts distorted the interview on the point selected, or were otherwise presented in a misleading way. Having double-checked with Bishop on Sunday in light of the ambassador's remarks, and having changed the headline in recognition of the subtle but diplomatically significant distinction between an agreement and an expectation of co-operation, the Guardian believes that it has accurately conveyed the substance of Bishop's comments on this issue in the interview. Our editor-in-chief, Kath Viner, has just posted a blog about Lenore Taylor's interview with Julie Bishop today, which has been the subject of controversy around the place today. Kath's blog contains the relevant extract from the transcript of Lenore Taylor's interview with Julie Bishop. I'll post that first, then give you another excerpt from Kath's piece. Ms Bishop tells Lenore that based on her conversations with senior officials, she's convinced the Coalition can work with Indonesia to achieve the policy of turning back the boats. The transcript: Lenore Taylor: “On Indonesia, you always seem to say that you have had lots of discussions with Jakarta and you think you could manage the issue of turning back boats. Why do you think you could manage the issue of turning back boats given what Indonesian officials always say publicly? What do you know that we don't know about how about that could be managed?” Julie Bishop: “Well I am not at liberty to tell you. I have had a number of conversations with high-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials, as has [immigration spokesman] Scott Morrison as has [Coalition leader] Tony Abbott and I am convinced we can work in cooperation with Indonesia to achieve our policy aim.” Lenore Taylor: “So they would take boats back?” Julie Bishop: “I am confident we would be able to achieve what we did in the past. The fact is they are Indonesian boats with Indonesian crew and I am sure we can work cooperatively with them and Lenore, one thing you understand about diplomacy and others do as well is the professional diplomats are paid to present, aah, particular views but what goes on behind the scenes can be quite different – what people say privately can be different to what they say publicly, that’s why I am devoting my time to quiet consistent diplomatic messaging and relationships.” Sky TV is just broadcasting some vision from the press conference earlier today from the Telstra CEO David Thodey. I wasn't able to get down there. It is clear that Mr Thodey is taking responsibility for the asbestos issues. Thodey: We take ownership and responsibility. First chance I've had to share Mike Bowers wonderful frame of the chamber today. Your Bear Pit at work. Turnbull continues: They can't just say this is all Telstra's problem. Telstra is doing this work for, and on behalf of the NBN Co. The attempt to push this all off to Telstra is, frankly, a lawyer's argument. If you are the government, Turnbull says, responsible for occupational health and safety, then you have to be all over it. Turnbull says the correspondence Shorten has released raises more questions than it answers. Shadow Communications Minister Malcolm Turnbull is out now with the media on asbestos. Turnbull: We welcome the establishment of a register, and we welcome the announcement by Telstra that they will undertake an audit. He says there remains a number of questions about the government's knowledge of this matter. We know the commercial deal was done with NBN Co in 2011. The issue of disturbance of Telstra's infrastructure was therefore going to happen on a grand scale. Shorten's concern demonstrates that he knew asbestos was going to be a big issue. He raised concerns - but why didn't he raise his concerns with the Communications Minister? That's a bit odd isn't it? Want the definition of tough day at the office? Mike Bowers captures it. Is there a rough number of incidents, Shorten is asked? He says there have been thirty reported incidents of asbestos breaches since 1996, the vast majority in the past few weeks. Will the NBN roll out slow down, Conroy is asked? June targets? No. September targets? No. The work is ongoing, he says. If there is a material change, NBN Co will advise of that. Did Telstra just fob you off, The Conversation's Michelle Grattan asks Shorten? They wrote back to me, and said they were on top of it. Minister Conroy is insisting that NBN Co are not responsible for any of this during a mildly combative exchange with David Crowe, journalist from The Australian. In response to a question seeking metrics - how many pits, where are these problems precisely - Shorten suggests there is some information about where the problems currently lie, but the government is still seeking further particulars. We need the best possible information Shorten says. For me, he says, it's an unsatisfactory state of affairs that we can't tell residents the full story. Shorten's questioned about a fund. It was discussed but not resolved definitively. He points to a new register tracking exposure to asbestos. This is a very important advance when it comes to future claims. Shorten is defending his actions based on his contact with Telstra in 2009. He says he was reassured by Telstra that all was safe. Should you have pressed harder, Shorten is asked? When Telstra write back to you not once, not twice, but three times .. Does anyone think the Opposition would be tackling asbestos if it wasn't for the politics of the NBN Co? We contacted Telstra. They gave promises. We are the ones driving this process. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten and Communications Minister Stephen Conroy are now reporting back after the talks with Telstra and others earlier today. Shorten says: The group has agreed to support an independent taskforce to examine these issues. Telstra has committed to provide transparent information to people who feel they might have been exposed to asbestos. It will report back to the Commonwealth in a speedy fashion about how it intends to remove asbestos from their pits. Shorten: Everyone has a responsibility in our community to make sure people are safe from the scourge of asbestos. Minister Conroy says NBN Co fully supports the outcomes of the morning. He says there have been a couple of instances where sub contractors to NBN Co have not followed proper procedures. He says there should be no shortcuts when it comes to the handling of asbestos. Conroy is asked whether NBN Co has done enough and whether he should take responsibility. Conroy: I am satisfied that Telstra has put up their hand and taken full responsibility for their work. Should I take responsibility? Well, Telstra has taken full responsibility. Julia Gillard and Craig Emerson, catching up during that division. That's Question Time, over and out. Shadow foreign minister Julie Bishop seeks leave to make a personal explanation. This concerns the interview with Lenore Taylor that we've referred to throughout today. Bishop: At no stage did I say there was an agreement with the (Indonesian government) Bishop is objecting to a headline on the story, and notes that has been amended. Bishop has been under attack from Labor on this story for most of today - and the static has cut across the Coalition's political attack on the border protection theme. And then it was done. The Morrison motion was not carried by the required absolute majority required in such cases. The House is dividing on the motion now. While we wait, here's Morrison, launching the suspension of the standing orders. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor is on his feet to respond. The Prime Minister has remained in the House. Normally she would leave during such procedural skirmishes. Not today. O'Connor says this person has committed no offences while in Australia. He says this man has never left detention. O'Connor says he is happy to provide a security briefing to the Opposition - but when they did that last week, the details were released publicly. This is not on, O'Connor says. Those briefings have to remain in-camera. The person is now in high security detention. The reason why this motion is being brought on today is (they) are trying to scare the Australian people in a way that is really outrageous. O'Connor says the Coalition is long on rhetoric but short on substance. The Opposition has not cooperated with efforts in parliament to slow down the flow of irregular arrivals. He says Abbott pretends he has an agreement with Indonesia to turn back boats when that clearly isn't the case, but he refuses to deal with the Malaysia Solution. It doesn't make sense. Malaysia wants to assist and Abbott won't allow it. I oppose the motion because the Opposition are disingenuous on this issue. They don't want a compact. They want to get in the way of policies recommended by experts. They want to create fear within our community. Morrison telegraphed his intention late last week. In a statement released on Friday, he demanded that the Immigration Minister Brendan O’Connor immediately establish an independent inquiry into the mishandling of the detention of a convicted jihadist terrorist by his department, placed in low security family accommodation for asylum seekers at Inverbrackie in South Australia. The Morrison statement: The Government was grossly negligent in keeping a known and convicted jihadist terrorist in low security asylum seeker family accommodation at Inverbrackie for five months, a facility secured by a perimeter pool fence and located adjacent to a defence force facility. The inquiry must receive full cooperation and access and take evidence from all relevant agencies, ministers and their offices, with usual protocols to apply to sensitive information. The subject of this case is a convicted terrorist whose crimes include premeditated murder, firearm and explosives possession and destruction of property. He was a member of the terrorist organisation Egyptian Islamic Jihad, which merged with Al Qaeda. Morrison is using parliamentary procedure to push for an independent inquiry by a select committee of the parliament into these events. The government, Morrison says, has sat on its hands while a convicted terrorist came into this country. Morrison: That is where the bar has been set. We need an inquiry because this government does not learn from its mistakes. They are light touch on our borders, they are a light touch on national security, and this minister is a light touch. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison is pursuing the case of the suspected Egyptian jihadist detained in a low security immigration detention facility in South Australia despite being the subject of an Interpol alert. We covered this case on the live blog last week. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor says the person concerned: has not been out of detention. O'Connor suggests this person has also been monitored by security agencies. He says his department is working with intelligence agencies to ensure the security assessment system works. The agencies did not fail us. Mr Morrison is pressing for more particulars. Mr O'Connor says he has no advice that this person has been through any process leading towards him being granted a protection visa. There are situations where a person could be determined a refugee but not released into the community due to offences that have been previously committed. Mr Morrison is now attempting to suspend the standing orders to discuss the Gillard Government's failings on border protection and national security. Tony Abbott switches the attack to boat arrivals, and the government losing control of the borders. The Prime Minister swivels the attack around using today's Guardian Australia's interview with Julie Bishop. Gillard declares Bishop has embarrassed Indonesia by suggesting there's a deal on turning boats back, when there is no such deal. Trade Minister Craig Emerson is now backing that point in with a Dorothy Dixer. (Much hollering from the Opposition bench.) Emerson says the Indonesians have made it clear they will not tow back boats. They will not. Climate Minister Greg Combet has been wheeled in to support Shorten on asbestos. He's now shouting about Julie Bishop representing James Hardie before she entered politics. National Luke Hartsuyker asks Shorten how many NBN contractors have so far undertaken asbestos removal? What training have they undertaken? Shorten isn't engaging with specifics. He takes the political riposte. Shorten: I've never represented the companies in court who made the absestos; and I've never insulted the victims of asbestos and campaigners. (By this he means Abbott and his deputy, Julie Bishop.) Willing down there today. Manager of Opposition business, Christopher Pyne, directing tactical traffic. Turnbull is back with the contents of that 2009 letter tabled by Shorten. GIven you clearly knew there were risks associated with asbestos, why didn't you raise it with Telstra between March 2009 and this week? Shorten doesn't engage with that allegation directly. A supplementary now from Turnbull to Shorten. Will you confirm you made no contact with Telstra or NBN Co since 2009 despite being aware of the risks? Shorten responds that he wrote to Telstra on August 4, then November 2 - Telstra wrote back with a detailed response. Of Turnbull's pursuit? Shorten: Tawdry. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten. An apt summary by Crikey correspondent Bernard Keane of a Dorothy Dixer Shorten just asked himself to update the House about today's asbestos talks. Independent Rob Oakeshott wants particulars of the Debates Commission - a new body to conduct election debates for September. Does the Prime Minister support this concept? Julia Gillard: We certainly are committed to a leader's debate commission. Turnbull is back at the dispatch box. He'd like the 2009 letter. Given this letter was written well before Telstra's agreement with NBN Co, what was this letter about? Was it about pits and ducts and asbestos? In what capacity was it written? Shorten ignores the specifics of the question. Shorten: Asbestos should not be a political football. The Workplace Minister has now tabled the letter, written in March 2009. Here's Garrett a few moments ago on indulgence. A Dorothy Dixer now allowing Treasurer Wayne Swan to have a shout about Queensland Premier Campbell Newman not signing on to Gonski. Shouting is ensuing. Shadow Communications Minister Malcolm Turnbull is asking a question of Bill Shorten. Can he inform the House how often he has met with Telstra in relation to asbestos risks and the NBN before the news reports last week? After all, everyone knew there were risks. Mr Shorten says he wrote first to Telstra in 2009 and Telstra indicated it had the issue under control. Shorten says he's met with a group of stakeholders today. Telstra made it very clear at this meeting that they accept they have to do more. I'm pleased to inform the House ... that they all agreed about the outcomes of the meeting we have just had. We saw Telstra accepted responsibility for end-to-end training throughout the system. Telstra also accepted the need to do much better in accepting the anxieties of the community. Turnbull would like Shorten to table the 2009 letter. House of Representatives Speaker Anna Burke says she can't do that. She can only request that of papers the minister is reading from. Opposition leader Tony Abbott opens on the NBN. Does the Prime Minister agree that millions spent on advertising for the NBN would have been better spent on keeping people safe from asbestos? The Prime Minister returns in kind. I suppose we shouldn't be surprised given this is the same man who insulted Bernie Banton on his death bed. (Mr Banton, as asbestos campaigner, died of an asbestos related illness. Mr Abbott once said of Banton, who was pressuring the Howard Government to take action on asbestos-related diseases: "Just because a person is sick doesn't necessarily mean that he is pure of heart in all things." Abbott later apologised for the remark.) The Prime MInister says Telstra, not the government, is responsible for pits and ducts. It being the scheduled hour of Question Time, it is Question Time. Schools Minister Peter Garrett is on his feet on a matter of indulgence. Garrett is clearly emotional. He speaks of the death of Mr Yunupingu. The music of Yothu Yindi spoke to Australians, Garrett says, with urgent political intent. He dreamed that Australians could be better, and he demanded that Australian politics be better, and so it became. This is a day of sorrow and also a day of pride. For now, he's gone, and like so many Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people, gone too young. Liberal Ken Wyatt echoes the words of Garrett. Wyatt says Mr Yunupingu was not only a musician but an educator. He brought Australians together, he participated in public debates about education, he had insight into learning which contributed to innovative approaches to education. To me, and so many others who knew him personally, he was an inspiration. Mr Yunupingu, thank you for making our lives so much richer. Here are the two ministers - Bill Shorten and Stephen Conroy - on the way in to the asbestos talks. Through the crack in the doorway. The asbestos talks. Telstra is believed to be outlining the steps it has taken to contain the situation regarding asbestos at the NBN sites. Man in fedora. As discussed. Oh dear. Now the monarchists are cranky. The Australian Monarchist League has fired off this missive to Malcolm Turnbull, (who, as we've noted, was out this morning, fanning those flames of republicanism) - a letter copied to hundreds of their closest friends in the media. 3 June 2013 Hon. Malcolm Turnbull MP PO Box 545 EDGECLIFF NSW 2027 Dear Mr Turnbull, On this morning's ABC News Breakfast show, you said, in reference to comments made in 1999, that the 'monarchists lied'. The Australian Monarchist League is Australia's largest member-based monarchist organisation and both media and the general public refer to us as 'monarchists'. I would point out that the Australian Monarchist League has never intentionally lied to anyone at any time and has always acted in a truthful and open manner. We would therefore appreciate you issuing a public notice that your comments did not refer to the Australian Monarchist League. Thanking you Yours sincerely, Philip Benwell National Chair Australian Monarchist League Crisis talks on asbestos. Mike Bowers records the opening. Perhaps it's Ms Hanson's tendency to refer to herself in the first person? In the ABC footage of Hanson, a chap in a check fedora and sunglasses is watching on with intense interest. And over in Sydney, another lady with red hair, Pauline Hanson, is maintaining her rage: the voice is characteristically querulous, the vibrato outrage undimmed. (And she's announcing she'll have yet another go at trying to get a spot in parliament this September.) Hanson: The cost of living has become unaffordable. Over at The Conversation, political editor Michelle Grattan has been speaking to Brian Howe, the former member for Batman and a former Deputy Prime Minister. The battle for Batman (the Victorian seat vacated by the retiring Labor man Martin Ferguson last week) is the contest everybody is talking about. And I mean everybody. Cats have views on this pre-selection, and they don't mind sharing them. But I digress. Mr Howe has told Michelle Julia Gillard (who is backing a bloke for this seat, factional mover and shaker David Feeney) should just shut up. Here's a bit from The Conversation: Former deputy prime minister Brian Howe, who held Batman from two decades, has strongly backed local woman Mary-Anne Thomas in the bitter preselection battle for the seat. Howe said Labor should keep its promise to deliver women candidates in a fair share of safe seats, but it was important as well to have as candidate a credible and active local resident in an area where the Green vote was increasing. He also said he was “very disappointed to see the Prime Minister involved” in the preselection. “It’s quite counterproductive for her to be involved. This is not about the election – this is about the future.” Let's stand still and work out where politics has touched down this lunchtime. Asbestos and the NBN rollout has been front and centre in the political discussion. Workplace Relations Minister Bill Shorten and Communications Minister Stephen Conroy are sitting down as we speak with representatives from Telstra, NBN contractors, unions and asbestos campaigners to work out what to do after recent incidences where workers or residents could have been exposed to asbestos. The two ministers will address the media on the sum of those talks after Question Time today - around 3.30pm. The Prime Minister has confirmed that the national heaquarters of DisabilityCare will be in Geelong - the location of two Labor-held seats in trouble in September. There's been a small outbreak of bipartisanship on the republic - Wayne Swan and Malcolm Turnbull have been out promoting a renewed discussion about constitutional reform, pushing through the defeat of the referendum in 1999. Boats, Indonesia and asylum policy is also kicking around courtesy of the Coalition wanting to keep political focus on this issue; and some comments from Shadow Foreign Minister Julie Bishop which appeared somewhat at odds with what Indonesia is saying publicly about the Coalition's policy of turning back people smuggler's boats. The Fair Work Commission has given the lowest paid a weekly increase of $15. Let's try - all the breaking news notwithstanding - and circle back to turning back people smuggler's boats, and whether the Coalition can achieve an agreement with Indonesia on this front Julie Bishop has put out a statement concerning this morning's Guardian Australia story. There is not an agreement with the Indonesians - that's not possible - Bishop asserts she's simply confident that all will be well. I am confident that, if elected to government, the Coalition will be able to work constructively with the Indonesian government on people smuggling issues. However, it is not possible for the Coalition, from Opposition, to negotiate agreements publicly or privately with foreign governments. This is what Bishop said to Lenore in the interview - she suggested the Indoensians understood the Coalition policy, and had been affirmative about it behind the scenes. Professional diplomats are paid to present particular views but what goes on behind the scenes can be quite different – what people say privately can be different to what they say publicly. And we have had wide ranging discussions at the highest levels on this issue. The new minimum wage is $622 per week. The weekly increase is just over $15. The president of the FWC Justice Iain Ross notes in this decision that minimum incomes are falling in relative terms in this country. But he argues that increasing the minimum wage is a blunt instrument in addressing the needs of the low paid. He argues he tax-transfer system can apply more targeted assistance to households. Just to add to the briskness of today, the Fair Work Commission is now handing down its minimum wage decision in Melbourne. The minimum wage will be increased by 2.6 per cent. The president of the FWC says inflation is contained, increases in superannuation are on the way, the carbon price impact on households is not as great as first thought, therefore the minimum wage increase is a bit lower than people may have thought. The Opposition leader Tony Abbott was out briefly on the doors of parliament a little while ago. He was asked about how he intended to implement the Coalition's policy of turning back people smugglers' boats when it is safe to do so. (This issue became sticky for the Coalition last Friday when the Indonesian Ambassador to Australia indicated there was no agreement to turn boats back. Defence officials have already queried whether this pledge is workable in practice.) In an interview with Guardian Australia political editor, Lenore Taylor, Shadow Foreign Minister Julie Bishop counsels that all will be well, whatever the Indonesians might be saying to the contrary. 'It's the relationship stupid.' Here's Lenore's piece. This is an excerpt: High-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials have indicated privately that Indonesia would cooperate with a Coalition government to turn back people smuggler boats insists Julie Bishop, the foreign affairs spokeswoman and deputy Liberal leader, despite the country's ambassador claiming publicly that “no such collaboration will happen.” “I have had a number of conversations with high-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials, as has [immigration spokesman] Scott Morrison as has [Coalition leader] Tony Abbott and I am convinced we can work in cooperation with Indonesia to achieve our policy aim,” Bishop said in a wide-ranging foreign policy interview with Guardian Australia. Tony Abbott is also on the 'all will be well' theme. The Coalition has a strong and constructive relationship with Indonesia, Abbott says. Here's the relevant sections from his press conference this morning. QUESTION: Mr Abbott are you prepared to defy the Indonesian Government to turn boats back there even though their Ambassador on Friday made it clear that they don’t want to accept them? TONY ABBOTT: Look, we’re very confident that we can have a strong and constructive relationship with the Indonesian Government. I’ve had several meetings with the President. I’m met with Foreign Minister Natalegawa. I have a good and strong relationship with Ambassador Nadjib. Julie Bishop obviously spends an enormous amount of time working with the Indonesians, as does Scott Morrison. So we are very confident that an incoming Coalition government won’t repeat the kind of mistakes that we’ve seen from the current government of which the absolute worst was the suspension of the live cattle trade in a state of panic over a TV programme. QUESTION: You’re talking about the willingness to turn boats around now. Is that different to the reality of turning boats around? Will you actually turn around any boats? TONY ABBOTT: The Howard Government turned boats around. It wasn’t a large number, but it was enough to send an absolutely crystal clear signal to the people smugglers and their clients that the game was up and that the Australian Government and people would no longer be played for mugs. QUESTION: How long do you think it will take to stop the boats? TONY ABBOTT: We will make a difference from day one, from day one we will make a difference and obviously we will be judged on our results – should we win this election – at the subsequent one. Garrett has now switched modes to the Gonski school funding reforms. He (like Swan was earlier) is asked about Premier Campbell Newman, saying no. Or no most likely. Garrett has advised Newman to take a leaf out of New South Wales Premier Barry O'Farrell's book. (NSW has signed on to Gonski). Garrett says he doesn't know whether Newman has been pressured by his Coalition colleagues in Canberra: Tony Abbott and Shadow Schools Minister Christopher Pyne. But he knows he should get on with doing a deal. Garrett: It's time Premier Newman put down the cudgels and picked up the phone to begin negotiations. We are absolutely confident we can reach agreement by the end of the month. Schools Minister Peter Garrett is out now paying tribute to Mr Yunupingu, with his ministerial colleague Warren Snowdon. Garrett: I'm wanting to pay tribute to someone who was a beacon for their people. This is someone we will sorely miss. Mr Garrett (who readers will remember had a rock career with Midnight Oil before entering politics) says he knows he speaks for colleagues in the music industry in this public tribute. He says there are still considerable health challenges facing indigenous communities. Snowdon says Mr Yunupingu taught him to have faith. Not the religious kind of faith, but faith in actions being able to make a difference. Garrett is asked about Treaty. Yothu Yindi sang about it. isn't it time to have one? It was an important song. Garrett treads carefully here. The policy of the Gillard Government is to recognise indigenous people in the constitution - that's one step back from a treaty. Garrett backs government policy and inches forward one jot - we need recognition and any other settlements that might be appropriate. He's asked about his favourite memory of the singer. He nominates sitting backstage sitting with the boys dressed up and painted. Mr Yunupingu spoke about culture and reconciliation. Independent MP Rob Oakeshott is reminding media outlets to show respect in the reporting of Mr Yunupingu's death. He means media outlets should conform with the strictures of traditional culture. (Members of the community are often not named after their death, and images of the deceased person should carry a warning before being broadcast). Asbestos was also on the agenda for the hovering reporters. What will the government do to fix up this terrible mess with the NBN roll-out? Swan: We are the Labor Party. We will do everything we possibly can to make sure this is dealt with correctly. The Treasurer was stopped by reporters on his way out of the republican function. He was asked a range of questions, including about the report in The Australian we pointed you to this morning about the Queensland Premier Campbell Newman signalling he won't sign on to the Gonski package of school reforms before the end of June cut-off. Swan had a go at the Queensland Premier, telling reporters this showed Premier Newman was putting the politics of Tony Abbott and the Coalition ahead of the education of young Queenslanders. Here's a clip of Yothu Yindi singing Treaty at last year's ARIAs. (I think the Schools Minister Peter Garrett might have the microphone at one point.) Tributes are pouring in this morning for Mr Yunupingu, who has died of kidney disease. The Prime Minister: We have lost a uniquely talented musician, a passionate advocate for Aboriginal people and a truly great friend. A former Australian of the Year, his music set out challenges for us all in the road to reconciliation. He will be remembered for his tremendous musical achievements, including being added to the ARIA Hall of Fame last year as the lead singer of Yothu Yindi. He will also be remembered as a great educational leader and a champion for the Yolngu people in East Arnhem Land, in the Northern Territory. He leaves a great body of work to inspire us and we will need all of that inspiration, for so much work remains undone. The Leader of the Opposition: Look, he was a great leader of his people. He was obviously a very significant cultural figure to the wider Australian community as well as amongst Aboriginal people and it’s tragic that he’s gone and I guess it’s very sad that he’s gone at such an early age, but this is one of the real problems in modern Australia – too many Aboriginal people die too young. God save constructive bipartisanship. Lovely frame from Mike Bowers of Wayne Swan and Malcolm Turnbull at the republican do. Malcolm Turnbull is making his contribution now. He's explaining why the Queen - a foreign institution, a much beloved one, but a foreign one - is in the Australian constitution. (To veto local laws Britain didn't aprove of). Turnbull says in some respects modern Australia is more independent than the United Kingdom because Australia is not subject to the strictures of the European Union. It will be extremely hard, he says, to change the status quo. There have been no controversial changes to our constitution since 1946. It is very difficult. You effectively have to have overwhelming support and very little opposition. He rejects arguments that if a model to directly elect the Australian president had been put to Australian voters in 1999 it would have been carried at the referendum. It would have failed, miserably. Direct election would have united the Coalition against the reform proposal and split the ALP, he says. There's no way it would have happened, whatever the direct-elect proponents say. Turnbull: I'm very positive about the republican cause. We are not going to fight the election on this issue .. but we should always be alert to, and interested in, our constitution. Turnbull is of the view a proper, deep, informed discussion won't happen in this country until after Queen Elizabeth departs the throne. He says technology helps this conversation - there are more options to engage the community in a discussion about constitutional change. The pre-requisites (of success) are to prepare the ground very very well. There's got to be the sense that this is the issue of the hour. There's no point in having another referendum that's going to be lost. A little bit of audio for those inclined to listen in. Here's a little bit of audio from Swan's speech. The Treasurer is speaking now at the launch of a new book of republican essays. We pointed you towards this story first thing this morning. Swan: We are all here because we have a deep love of our nation. It's always a time to talk about the merits of having an Australian as our head of state. We do need to put some urgency into a respectful national conversation. How can it be that in a modern democracy that one of our citizens can't aspire to be our head of state? Asbestos and the NBN rollout is a major issue in national politics today. Just a couple of look forward points on that. Legislation will pass the House of Representatives today creating a National Asbestos Eradication Agency. This bill has already been debated, but there will be a vote later. This package does not relate directly to the controversy around the NBN rollout. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten will meet a bunch of stakeholders: Telstra, NBN contractors, unions, asbestos campaigners ay 12.30pm to gather information about incidences of mismanagement, and to work out a way forward. Speaking of Turnbull, we note he's been chatting in the middle of the night with Armidale-based blogger Kate Doak. Kate wrote a blog which examines investments Lucy Turnbull made in companies with associations with the NBN rollout. Turnbull says the interests were disposed off some time back. I gather from his spokesman that they dispute some of the material published in Kate's blog. But it's interesting isn't it - this shift in political communication. Politicians don't only have to grind their teeth over mainstream media coverage, the bloggers are now a significant and growing feature of national affairs coverage - a development I welcome. This morning we pointed you to an opinion piece from Deputy Prime Minister and Treasurer Wayne Swan arguing in favour of a republic. Liberal frontbencher Malcolm Turnbull is out on the subject too, but more cautiously than his Labor counterpart. Swan will speak on this theme in a little while. The Australian Monarchist League has bolted out of the blocks, issuing a statement blasting "the Swan/Turnbull alliance" on the republican issue. Philip Benwell, national chair, Australian Monarchist League: So Wayne Swan is on the republic warpath again. Why does he seem to always get it wrong? The people have no interest whatsoever in constitutional change. They have so many other matters on their mind, which, to them, are far more important. This is particularly so at this election where the fragmenting Labor party is possibly not only fighting for its political survival, but its very existence as a 'party of the people. Nature. We laughed. The pigeons of parliament camp on the right side of the building, but they lean left. That's called having it both ways. They are baaaack! (And luckily for me, so is the lovely Mike Bowers, watching through his lens.) From Gillard's statement about DisabilityCare and Geelong. This will create around 300 new jobs once DisabilityCare is fully rolled out from 2019-20. This is in addition to the 120 jobs already announced for the Agency’s Geelong regional office, which will open in time for the launch of DisabilityCare Australia in the Barwon region next month. The DisabilityCare head office will include staff responsible for managing the scheme’s operation, such as national operations management, the CEO and executive support and corporate services. The Gillard Government has made this decision on the location of the head office on the basis of the Victorian Government’s offer of $25 million toward the costs of relocation and establishment for the head office. Prime Minister Julia Gillard has bobbed up in Geelong to announce that the administrative headquarters of DisabilityCare will be located in the Victorian city. Right here, in Geelong. Thank you to everyone. You deserve it. (DisabilityCare is) starting, it's real, roll on 1st of July. There are a couple of marginal seats in that neck of the woods that Labor will try and defend in September. Ford's decision to pull out of its Australian operations affects workers in Geelong. A jobs announcement, in that context, will likely go down well. Tony Abbott says he supports the HQ being headquartered in Geelong. Obviously I support sensible locating of facilitieas in regional areas. Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of politics from the national capital. It’s a very busy news day already. Parliament resumes today. Readers interested in the legislative program for this week can click on this link and access a very comprehensive summary of all the parliamentary business minus the daily rhetoric. Asbestos dominates the news cycle first up. The Gillard Government will this afternoon meet with representatives from Telstra, trade unions and national broadband network contractors following now widespread reports that asbestos is not being handled properly in the NBN rollout. The Workplace Relations Minister Bill Shorten has been speaking to affected residents over the weekend - and has hit the airwaves early this morning. We don’t know who has been exposed and who hasn’t been exposed. What this requires is a first class response. Let’s run through the main political stories in the newspapers and digital sites. Asbestos (news.com) The federal government will discuss the management of asbestos sites with Telstra and unions at a crisis meeting. Workplace Relations Minister Bill Shorten, Communications Minister Stephen Conroy and Assistant Treasurer David Bradbury will meet with groups in Canberra on Monday after concerns were raised that Telstra subcontractors weren't properly handling deadly asbestos uncovered during work on NBN sites. On March 20 an open bag marked "asbestos" was found by residents on a footpath near telecommunications works at Bligh Park in Sydney's northwest. Telstra has stopped work preparing pipes and pits to be used for the NBN rollout and has appointed PriceWaterhouseCoopers as independent advisers to help review the process. Disability Care (ABC Local) Less than a fortnight after Ford announced it was closing its Geelong manufacturing plant, the regional Victorian city has been chosen as the headquarters of the new DisabilityCare agency. All states and territories - except Western Australia - have signed up to be part of the scheme, formerly known as the NDIS. Once DisabilityCare is fully rolled out, the national headquarters in Geelong will employ 350 people, in addition to 120 people in the regional office. Turning back people smuggler boats: Indonesia (Guardian Australia) High-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials have indicated privately that Indonesia would cooperate with a Coalition government to turn back people smuggler boats insists Julie Bishop, the foreign affairs spokeswoman and deputy Liberal leader, despite the country's ambassador claiming publicly that “no such collaboration will happen.” “I have had a number of conversations with high-ranking Indonesian ministers and officials, as has [immigration spokesman] Scott Morrison as has [Coalition leader] Tony Abbott and I am convinced we can work in cooperation with Indonesia to achieve our policy aim,” Bishop said in a wide-ranging foreign policy interview with Guardian Australia. “Professional diplomats are paid to present particular views but what goes on behind the scenes can be quite different – what people say privately can be different to what they say publicly," she said. "And we have had wide ranging discussions at the highest levels on this issue.” Cost of asylum seekers (The Daily Telegraph) The cost of supporting more than 40,000 asylum seekers expected to be in Australia next financial year is set to average more than $70,000 each person. And new break-down of costs this year show taxpayers have spent $1.5 billion maintaining the detention network amid record arrivals which are approaching 22,500 this financial year. Supporting almost 13,000 asylum seekers in the community whose asylum claims haven't been processed has cost $265 million for just 10 months. The cost includes $131 million to support asylum seekers on Bridging Visas who are prevented from working and who are paid equivalent welfare payments each fortnight. Renting homes and furnishing them has cost $18 million this financial year. Cost breakdowns given to a parliamentary budget Estimates committee this week also show charities are accepting hundreds of millions of dollars to care for asylum seekers. Campbell Newman and Gonski (The Australian) Campbell Newman has virtually ruled out the Queensland government's agreement to the Gonski school funding reforms by Julia Gillard's deadline of June 30 and warned that agreement at any time is "impossible" without substantial changes. The Queensland Liberal National Party Premier has told the Prime Minister she misunderstands state funding arrangements and is making misleading public statements about the education funding changes. (Paywalled) 457 visas: Simon Crean off script (AFR) Labor elder and former ACTU chief Simon Crean has called on unions to produce evidence of rorts and malpractice in the 457 visa program, as the Gillard government flagged stricter tests for business and more fines. Immigration Minister Brendan O’Connor said Labor would impose tougher rules on importing foreign workers, requiring businesses to prove all local labour options had been exhausted. More fines will be imposed for breaches. Mr Crean told The Australian Financial Review he could not understand why employers would not be seeking to use Australian workers, as opposed to bringing them in from overseas, and suggested unions had “gone about it the wrong way” in calling for a crackdown on 457 visa rules. “My point to the unions is give me the evidence to show this is happening. If that is happening, that’s wrong, that’s not the intention of the program,’’ he said. (Paywalled) Batman pre-selection (The Age) Cabinet ministers Jenny Macklin and Penny Wong and minister Catherine King are at odds with Prime Minister Julia Gillard over Martin Ferguson's successor in Batman, with the trio publicly backing the need for Labor to select a woman. Ms Gillard is backing Right powerbroker Senator David Feeney, who is parliamentary secretary for defence. She considers him a strong and loyal ally who has performed well. He helped her rise to the prime ministership and remains favourite for the seat. Republican revival: Wayne Swan opinion piece (National Times) After a recent speech in which I called for a renewed national conversation on an Australian republic, I was met with a curious response: ''Why now? Is this really the right moment?'' The Australian republic, it seems, always raises the crucial question of timing. ''When?'' To monarchists, the answer is always ''never''. To the cautious, the answer is usually ''not now'', or at least ''not until Queen Elizabeth's reign is over''. Others argue that we should raise the issue again only after global recession has been conquered once and for all, and people can concentrate on the future free of practical worries. The time may not indeed be right for an immediate referendum, but it is my belief that it is always the right time to argue for the merits of a republic and prepare the ground for future constitutional change. But before we get carried away, fellow republican Malcolm Turnbull is more cautious (Guardian Australia) Liberal frontbencher Malcolm Turnbull, the former leader of the Australian Republican Movement, who will launch the book alongside Swan in Canberra, urges a more considered and cautious approach to ensure success where the 1999 Republican referendum failed. In his forward to the book of essays Turnbull describes the Republican cause as "simply, purely patriotic." But he says it will be more easily achieved when Queen Elizabeth is no longer on the throne and questions the idea that the "sexy celebrity status" of William and Kate, the Duke and Duchess of Cambridge, will be enough to maintain the institution. You can follow all the day’s political developments here on the blog, but you can also follow my periodic updates on Twitter @murpharoo If you want to join in the conversation on Twitter use the hashtag politicslive - then I can bring some of that back to the blog.

tags.description: NBN: latest news on the national broadband network from the Guardian

results.webTitle: The archbishop, the duchess and the politics of poverty | Andrew Brown

fields.headline: The archbishop, the duchess and the politics of poverty
fields.trailText: Andrew Brown: By recruiting Camilla to his credit unions cause, Justin Welby is returning to an old idea that the aristocracy should help directly
fields.bodyText: The news that Justin Welby has visited Camilla, Duchess of Cornwall, to discuss credit unions is one of the oddest stories of the week. Are we really back to a society where the archbishop and the future queen have to work on behalf of the poor because no one else is working for charity? I don't think there's any doubt that debt relief of this sort is now seen as the most urgent thing that the church can do to help the poor. It has been one of Welby's personal causes, which he has driven as hard as he could. But it is also an answer to those on the right who argue that there isn't any real poverty in Britain because almost everyone has enough to eat and shelter. By recruiting traditional Anglicans like Camilla, Welby is returning to an older pattern of Christianity, which may never have entirely died out among evangelicals but had certainly receded from the mainstream of British Christianity. The idea that the aristocracy should help the poor directly as part of their Christian duty was completely unfashionable during the heyday of the welfare state. So far as we know, what's going on here is lobbying rather than a simple gift of money. But even that is unusual. In part this reflects Welby's own background and connections. His mother was at the heart of the old establishment and he has just hired as his personal press secretary Ailsa Anderson, who worked for the Queen for 12 years, and had been her personal press secretary since 2010. So it must come naturally to him to suppose these people still have real power, which should be wielded for the common good. It is also consonant with the tradition of Holy Trinity Brompton, the posh evangelical church whose culture shaped him. HTB may not be terribly successful in its prison ministries or in its attempts at church planting in the poorer and more de-Christianised parts of London, but it does take them seriously, and it is about the only part of the Church of England where that tradition is still alive among the congregation. For the other interesting point about this move is that it actually places Welby well to the left of a lot of Anglican sentiment. One of the most fascinating things to emerge from the public opinion surveys conducted for the Westminster Faith Debates earlier this year was the "values gap" which shows that on political and economic questions the ordinary Anglicans, especially irregular attenders, are a long way to the right of the church leadership. This is true both on issues of national policy, like support for asylum-seekers, and on local matters like support for food banks. I don't know a single instance where the initiative for such projects has come from the congregation, rather than the church leaders. It is a very clerical thing to suppose that the church justifies itself by its service to the wider community. So far as the ordinary, intermittent churchgoer is concerned, the church justifies itself by its service to them. Here, as in other areas, there is a huge difficulty in that the policies that attract and energise the core believers are precisely those that put off the peripheral and occasional ones on whom the church relies just as much. But if he can recruit the most notorious adulteress in modern British history to the cause of righteousness he will have done something to move the church's idea of morality from sex to money and power.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened - Rudd quits as parliament marches on

fields.headline: Politics as it happened - Rudd quits as parliament marches on
fields.trailText: Kevin Rudd quits politics drawing warm tributes as parliament carries on as the Treasurer warns of a US-style debt default over Labor's refusal to agree to a $500bn debt limit.
fields.bodyText: So at the end of only the second working day of Parliament, here is where we are up to: Most normal people (as in not we political tragics) woke to the news of Kevin Rudd's resignation. He did one last portrait for photographers in his office. A number of maiden speeches were delivered, the most notable of which were from re-elected Liberal Mal Brough and agriculture minister Barnaby Joyce, who attracted a billionaire mining magnate mate to the public galleries. Veterans affairs minister Michael Ronaldson announced the details of the ballot for the Centenary of Anzac service at Gallipoli on Anzac Day, 2015. The Prime Minister will lead a delegation of only four elected representatives, leaving 8000 tickets for the rest of Australia. The ballot opens on Saturday. Don't wait for tickets on Ebay. The Government's attempt to increase the debt ceiling to half a trillion dollars was stymied in the Senate, with Labor and the Greens amending it to $400bn. Treasurer Joe Hockey refused to accept the amendment and sent it back up to the Senate like a naughty child, leaving a possible double dissolution trigger three months down the track. The Greens senator Sarah Hanson-Young succeeded in passing a motion to force immigration minister Scott Morrison to produce in timely fashion a broad sweep of documents about ‘on water operations’ that occurred between 7 September 2013 and 14 November 2013. Labor attempted a suspension of standing orders on the basis that the government wasn't what it said it would be. Furthermore, Labor said Scott Morrison could not shed any light on asylum seeker boats for the Parliament or the public. Labor lost. Liberal senator Bill Heffernan, who is implaccably opposed to the sale of GrainCorp to Archer Daniels Midland, managed to kickstart a Senate inquiry into the takeover bid. The lower house will not sit until next next Monday, November 18. Senate estimates start up simultaneously. To all those public servants who will attend, rest up for the weekend. This blog will return on that day. Last but not least, we love your comments but due to my L plates, I failed to turn on the right button. Apologies. Until Monday, adieu. The House has disagreed with Labor's Senate amendment to the debt ceiling on a vote by 82 - 48. So now, the government's original bill which increases the ceiling to $500bn, goes back to the Senate. We are now having a division on the question of Labor's amendment to the debt ceiling. The debate, which has continued over the past 24 hours, has had some pearlers. Joe Hockey on Chris Bowen: Last night I couldn't sleep too well and I wondered who introduced the $75bn debt limit? It was old Bugalugs over here. Labor's Ed Husic on the Coalition: Who are these people who, before the election, were talking about budget emergencies and railing against debt... It is like they have been to one of these RSL hypnosis shows and they have been told, 'when you hear the clap, you will no longer be vandals and you will no longer rail against debt and deficit, and you will say, "we need to increase the debt limit".' This is magic! The Greens Adam Bandt supporting Labor's amendment: The Greens take the approach that most orthodox economists take to debt... Liberals Alex Hawke: If the member for Melbourne represents economic orthodoxy, then I confess to being a radical evangelical—praise God!—because I do not believe the Greens could be associated with economic orthodoxy. After Labor and the Greens amended the the debt ceiling bill in the Senate to $400bn instead of $500bn, it has now come back to the House. Treasurer Hockey is speaking against the amendment. Ipso facto, government is telling Labor and the Greens to shove it. From Mike Bowers on The Global Mail captured fun and games over the table. Katharine Murphy reports: The prime minister has gone in to bat for his friend, the leader of the Catholic church in Australia, George Pell, a man who was “not perfect” but was nonetheless “a fine human being, a great churchman”. Tony Abbott was asked to comment on a report that followed an inquiry in Victoria, which was highly critical of Pell, his attitudes to the problems evidenced in comments before the inquiry and the institutional failures of the Catholic church in stopping child abuse. From his interview with the ABC's Neil Mitchell: Mitchell: As probably Australia’s best known Catholic and a former trainee priest do you accept the church did cover up and move paedophiles around? Abbott: Well, again, I was a seminarian for a couple of years. I wasn’t involved in matters of policy and I wasn’t involved in administrative decisions. So, I just don’t know for a personal fact what was done. I know that it wasn’t handled well. I absolutely know that it wasn’t handled well and I understand that these things probably did happen but I suspect that it wasn’t just the church that didn’t handle these things well. I suspect a generation ago there was this general view in our community that certain things just didn’t happen. We all know now that they did happen. It was hideous, it was gruesome, it cost some people their lives, it cost some people their sanity, it has rightly damaged the reputations of institutions that otherwise deserve our respect and I deeply regret that as I think all decent people do. Labor loses its suspension of standing orders: 53 - 85. House divides for Labor's suspension of standing orders. Morrison accuses Shorten of asking him to undermine the decisions of the joint agency task force, overseeing Operation Sovereign Borders. It is the resolve of this government that puts the steel in the measures that makes them work. Those opposite ... thought the answer was to flip and flop with the wind. They sent a message to people smugglers that their heart wasn't in it ... that they would never have the resolve to carry it off. The policy will always be safer in the hands of the Coalition.
