Morrison says the mistake the Labor government made in the Malaysian people swap is that they announced it before the deal was done. The Coalition would not have made that mistake and will not telegraph information to people smugglers. My memory was the Coalition thought the Malaysian people swap deal was a mistake because Malaysia was not a signatory to the UN Refugee Convention. Speaking to his suspension of standing orders, Bill Shorten is charging that "this is not the government it promised to be". He is ripping into Morrison about his refusal to answer questions on asylum seekers. A choice selection of Shorten quotes: I love those people opposite...no I don't. The military should not be used to do the job of ministers. It's not satisfactory to say a matter relating to boats is on the water operations ...Thank you Decartes. Philosophy at work in your Parliament. Shorten is moving a suspension of standing orders on the Prime Minister's "failure to lead an open and transparent government" as he promised and the failure of Scott Morrison to answer questions regarding asylum seeker arrivals. This is a government that loves to hide. Scott Morrison is up again on boats, "operational matters on water" etcetera. If the minister doesn't know the answer he should be sat down, says Burke. Speaker Bishop says that is not a point of order, and then comes fairly close to cracking up with laughter. She is clearly enjoying herself. There is so much silliness your correspondent misses the next question but essentially a dixer from Fiona Scott to Malcolm Turnbull on the NBN. He's talking about the former communications minister Stephen Conroy's linguistics - Conrovian, a bizarre Orwellian language. Marles tries a question to Morrison about an "on-land" policy. How many Indonesian boats have you bought? Again it's an operational matter. Trust us, says Morrison essentially. The campaign to spread the word of the evils of people smuggling is progressing well. Pyne - self-described "workers' friend" is continuing on the building and construction industry and its union thuggery. Morrison asked by Shorten whether there is any information he can share with the Parliament. Morrison says no, he will ensure the integrity of the Operation Sovereign Borders. Christopher Pyne is asked about the reinstatement of the Australian Building and Construction Commission. (The bill was before the House this morning.) Pyne says construction sites had become: Intimidation, thuggery, lawlessness and violence. Union leaders support Bill Shorten, says Pyne, so when they tell him to jump, he says how high. He won't support the Abcc. Scott Morrison asked by his own side about the reintroduction of temporary protection visas. Then asked by Labor's Richard Marles about incidents between Australia and Indonesia regarding asylum seeker boats. Andrew Wilkie asks Tony Abbott about live exports and penalties for breaches of Export Supply Chain Assurance Scheme. "This trade is important for Australia and it should be encouraged. The department will always investigate breaches. We are satisfied with the rules as they stand and we don't intend to toughen them further." Treasurer Joe Hockey asked by his own side about Coalition changes to tax policies - read carbon tax and mining tax. Hockey talking about failure of the mining tax. Marles asks how many boats have been turned back successfully since the election. Morrison says Operation Sovereign Borders is a very different way of doing things. "One of those differences is the way we handle information..so as not to use information to assist people smugglers." Dance of the Seven Army Surplus Blankets. Tanya Plibersek makes a point of order. "The minister for asked for a number. If the number is zero, just say it." Speaker asks her to withdraw. If it assists the House, says Plibersek. Kevin Rudd says reports on Jessica's candidature is untrue. Warren Truss is asked about the effect of the carbon tax on Mackay Council. Anthony Albanese then asks Truss about a grant under the regional development fund for $411,484 in community infrastructure to Mackay Council. Truss 910 projects regional development grants were not contracted. We have our own election commitments, says Truss. We don't have to honour commitments made by Labor. Shorten asks about Treasurer Hockey's pre-election statement: "If debt is the problem, more debt is not the answer". Shorten wants to know why not give a full and frank Budget update? Abbott: "Everyone knows which party in this house is the party of debt and deficit." Dixer to Abbott on the impact of carbon tax on the Australian economy. It's just like the old days. Bill Shorten asking about Abbott's misleading statements on the debt cap and the fact Abbott claimed he never opposed Labor's attempts to increase the debt ceiling. Shorten has to change misleading and chooses "imaginative" statements. Speaker Bishop warns Shorten he is sailing close to the wind. I'm not imagining, counters Abbott, the debt disaster is real. Question Time coming up... Labor succeeded in amending the debt ceiling in the Senate from the government's planned $500bn to $400bn in a vote 37 to 32. This and other salient facts about the Senate's business can be gleaned from the delightfully named Dynamic Red. Not Katharine Murphy, but the Senate's version of the Live Minutes. I hope no one got too excited about Jessica Rudd's possible candidature. Lenore Taylor reports that the most likely candidate is Di Farmer, a state MP who lost the seat of Bulimba - which covers some of the same suburbs of Brisbane - at the 2012 Queensland state election. Immigration minister Scott Morrison has been ordered to report on-water operations by midday November 18 - next Tuesday. From Katharine Murphy: The Abbott government has been ordered by the Senate to produce documentary material detailing the “on-water operations” it wants to keep secret, in an escalation of the political effort to force greater transparency into the Operation Sovereign Borders policy. The Senate has passed a Greens motion ordering the government to produce any documentation relating to “on-water operations” occurring between 7 September 2013 and 14 November 2013 – by 12pm on 18 November. The Australian government will throw in a further $20m and deploy additional Australian Defence Force logistic support to help the Philippines to respond to Typhoon Haiyan. This brings our total contribution to more than $30 million. Just when we thought we had slid into Post-Ruddernism, there is unsourced talk reported by Mark Kenny and Chris Johnson over at Fairfax about Jessica Rudd running for public office. Cue Twilight Zone music. Re those pesky "on-water operations" details, my colleague Lenore Taylor on journalists' responsibilities to keep asking those annoying questions. Tony Abbott is starting to go further than just refusing to answer questions about his government’s asylum policy. He now seems to be suggesting journalists have some kind of moral obligation not to ask them. Liberal Senator Bill Heffernan has garnered support to continue the inquiry into ownership arrangements of grain handling, examining the proposed $3.4bn takeover of GrainCorp by Archer Daniels Midland. Joe Hockey is expected to make a decision on the takeover on December 17. Heffernan has been pursuing ADM like a dog with a bone and in his second interim report, he concluded that more evidence was required in public hearings from Cargill, Glencore, Toepfer International and the relevant financial representatives from ADM. Heffernan also got support from the Greens to continue the inquiry. Senator Hanson-Young to the immigration minister: "What are you hiding?" Proof of Barnaby's fan club. Former Labor senator David Feeney, now in the seat of Batman vacated by Mar'n Ferguson, has just completed his maiden speech in the House. He was preceded by the National member for Hinkler, Keith Pitt and Labor's Pat Conroy, member for Charlton. Former Howard minister, Mal Brough, is now making his maiden speech on his re-entry into the Parliament. Brough replaced his mortal enemy, the former speaker Peter Slipper in the seat of Fisher . Like Sandra Bullock in the movie Gravity, Brough nearly burned out during his re-entry to the Australian political stratosphere. For good background on Brough, check out Nick Bryant's piece in The Monthly. He is talking about how all MPs should be committed to improving behaviour in the House. He thanked the prime minister Tony Abbott and the member for Leichhardt, Warren Entsch, who he said practically "stalked" him to urge him to rejoin the party. Just casting back to this morn, Treasurer Joe Hockey had a D&M with Fran Kelly on his friend, Kevin Rudd. I totally understand why he is going. He has had a tumultuous political career. He has seen the great highs and the great lows of Australian politics. The most brutal words about Kevin Rudd have come from his own side and the most brutal actions against Kevin Rudd have come from within his own party. I fully respect his decision to go. I understand it. I understand it particularly because I know Therese, his wife, he has got three magnificent children, hugely impressive children. He has got a very young grand-daughter, I know he was feeling the pain of being away from his grand-child. Joe says politics has changed, not in the chamber but on the streets. Politics has changed. It is more brutal. I do not think it is more brutal in the chamber. I think it is more brutal on the streets. Social media has changed things. People feel that they can give you a character reference by screaming across a car parking lot in front of your children which I have experienced. I do not think we should just be gentler in the Parliament, I think as a community we must be gentler to each other. Love a friendship across the chamber. Greens leader Christine Milne is accusing the Coalition of undergoing an "extreme makeover" when it comes to debt. She is reading all for quotes from Abbott in opposition when he argued that Labor should justify its attempt to increase the debt limit. Milne channelling Abbott: The government should be forced to specifically justify this...not sweep it under the carpet. The Senate is debating the government's plans to increase the debt ceiling - universally described as the nation's credit card limit - to half a trillion dollars. Labor has agreed to raise it from $300bn to $400bn but doesn't want to go to $500bn. Treasurer Joe Hockey says he wants to jack up the limit so he doesn't have to come back to the House, i.e. at a later date when it is harder to pin it on Labor. Shadow trade minister Penny Wong says in the Budget forecasts, the $370bn debt is not reached until 2016-17. We are saying before we give you more (than $400bn), we think you should tell Australians why." Threatening to shut down the government for political purposes is a lie." When you think of Parliament protecting things, you may be thinking of the Southern Corroboree Frog. But Communications minister Malcolm Turnbull, has just been on his feet speaking to a couple of bills relating to telecommunications. One reduces red tape on industry and enhances consumer safeguards. The other protects submarine cables. Pffft, you may say but submarine cables are those things that connect voice and data traffic with the world. As an island, we would - to use a technical term - be buggered without them. Even though it wasn't immediately clear from the look on his face, Turnbull was cock-a-hoop about protecting submarine cables and: embracing the excitement of global connectedness. The deputy prime minister and infrastructure minister Warren Truss has made a statement in the house, announcing a review of aviation safety regulation, with the bipartisan support of former infrastructure minister Anthony Albanese. Truss "suspended" the standing orders just so Albo could speak. Truss flagged the review after adverse findings on the Civil Aviation Safety Authority and the Australian Transport Safety Bureau by the Senate and Regional Affairs and Transport Committee. That's obviously what they were talking about when photographer Lukas Coch caught them having a quiet chat in the chamber on Wednesday. Immigration minister Scott Morrison and military head of the government's Operation Sovereign Borders, General, Angus Campbell will have to do the dance of the seven army surplus blankets at the next asylum seeker briefing after the Senate passed a motion to provide more information regarding "on-water operations". Labor and the Greens have just passed a motion to force the government to produce in timely fashion a broad sweep of documents about ‘on water operations’ that occurred between 7 September 2013 and 14 November 2013. It has just passed and means Morrison and Campbell will be defying the Parliament if they don't comply. Watch this space. The man with the best voice in Parliament, industry minister Ian Macfarlane, has just introduced a bill to move the Anti-Dumping Commission from under the umbrella of Customs and Border Protection to the Industry portfolio. Macfarlane says the move will free up customs and border protection to concentrate on "other matters". Notably (though he did not mention it), those infamous "on-water operations". Macfarlane said it was but one move in a suite of measures to strengthen the manufacturing industry. SPC Ardmona will await further advice. Apparently I have started the Ruddy Great Word Game. Julia Gillard tweets Kevin Rudd. The war is over. Good morning everyone - Katharine Murphy breaking in briefly on Gabi's blog.With boats a major issue of this political week, we've been waiting on a couple of items out of the Senate. One of these is a Greens motion to try and force the government to produce documents relating to those "on water" incidents it doesn't like talking about. Labor has been mulling whether or not to support this motion. Labor has confirmed they will support it when it is debated later this morning. The motion seeks to force the government to produce in timely fashion a broad sweep of documents about ‘on water operations’ that occurred between 7 September 2013 and 14 November 2013. If the intel is correct and Labor supports this motion, it will be interesting to see how the government responds given its intention to produce as little information about these events as possible. It has been very much a case of by all means ask, but we don't intend to tell. Legislation continues apace in Parliament. Ninety minutes into sitting and we have had five bills, three relating to setting up the Australian Grape and Wine Authority, an amendment to the Australian Research Council and a bill to set up the Australian Building and Construction Commission. The Abcc was introduced under the Howard government following the Cole Royal Commission into the building and construction industry. It was abolished under the Gillard government and in the lead up to the September 7 election, Tony Abbott promised to reinstate it. Christopher Pyne, representing employment minister, Eric Abetz, is on his feet saying the Abcc will stop "thuggery" in the building industry and: change the lawless culture in the building and construction industry. Treasurer Joe Hockey has been belting the table this morning on Radio National, making threats about cutting services if Labor and the Greens don't tick the increase in the debt ceiling by $200bn to $500bn. If they want to own the cuts to the budget they will own them. I will not put in place a budget that has a bigger debt than the debt limit and therefore the Greens and Labor will be responsible for the very significant cuts that will need to be made. Veterans Affairs, Michael Ronaldson, is announcing details of the ballot to attend the Gallipoli service for the Anzac Centenary in 2015. Only four members of the Australian Parliament will be attending the service to maximise the number of places for members of the services, direct descendants of veterans and the public. The four members include the Prime Minister Tony Abbott, the Opposition Leader Bill Shorten, Ronaldson and his shadow, who at the moment is Senator Don Farrell, though he loses his Senate seat in July next year. The ballot will open at 12.01am on Saturday morning, November 16, and will close at 12pm on January 31. Early lodgement makes no difference to securing one of the 8000 tickets for Australians. There will be specific places set aside for direct descendants, school children, the defence forces and 160 places widows of veterans. The minister says strict conditions will be placed on the tickets, which are not transferable. These will not be available on Ebay. Sharpen your pencils. Parliament has opened at 9am with the maiden (or is that bachelor) speech agriculture minister Barnaby Joyce. Like Kevin, Barnaby's first name is enough. He replaced independent Tony Windsor in the seat of New England when Windsor resigned after having a pivotal yet controversial role in the 43rd Parliament. Barnaby's speech drew mining magnate Gina Rinehart to join his family and friends in the public gallery. He started his speech with his first memory at the laminex table in his high chair. From there, we ranged through his life, Roman philosopher Cicero and his hopes for rural communities. He grew up in a farming community, comforted by the knowledge that his food and fibre-producing district supported the nation. His country childhood was in the 1970s, though he said it felt like the 1950s. This moulded the man. Like him or loathe him - and he does have a capacity to divide - Barnaby is Barnaby. And his entry into the lower house is sure to change the landscape for the National Party. He represents a more muscular rural political force than the softer versions represented by former leader Tim Fischer and current leader Warren Truss. But his conversion on the issue of foreign investment is the first sign he has softened his old-style National beliefs. His reaction to any decision to sell Australian wheat giant GrainCorp on December 17 will be his first real test. Here we are considering our lives as politics' relentless march continues into Post-Ruddernism. The Grate Man quit and his emotional speech evinced a whole lot of warm tributes, from his own side and from the opposite side. It was hard to listen to the speeches without remembering the comprehensive bollocking they had all bestowed on him during his two periods in the prime minister's office. Pure and simple, Kevin Rudd's speech was heartfelt and emotional. And perhaps it was, as Speaker Bronwyn Bishop said, the fact that he stood there stripped bare of his political armour that everyone else gave him the public acknowledgement that they never delivered in the previous decades. Perhaps in the deepest, darkest corner of the souls, there was a touch of 'there but for the grace of God go I'. However we think of Kevin, everyone agreed that his legacy was topped by the apology to the Stolen Generation. As we listened to Labor senator Nova Peris' moving maiden speech and the Liberal's Fiona Scott, who talked about the importance of Indigenous recognition in the Constitution, it is clear that Rudd's apology took the nation a step forward. But Parliament slows for no Chan, so though there were some notable speeches, this blog will try to highlight them later in the day. In the meantime, here is Katharine Murphy's round up and Lenore Taylor's analysis. You can read his full speech here and it even includes the video.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened

fields.headline: Politics as it happened
fields.trailText: Toxic opinion polls and continuing fall-out from the NBN
fields.bodyText: Time to wrap the #politicslive conversation for this evening. Thanks to those who made contributions. Let's do the evening summary. Bad opinion polls for Labor, and concerns about potentially dangerous exposures to asbestos associated with the NBN roll-out, dominated the political day. Labor backbencher Joel Fitzgibbon ventured out early and cracked jokes on breakfast television about what an MP should say on a day where there was wall-to-wall bad opinion polls - making it clear that there were official talking points, he thought them silly, and he didn't intend to parrot them. Kevin Rudd made a couple of public appearances and remarked how tough things were for the ALP. "Let's just be honest about it, it's very, very tough. I think that's the conversation going on right now with all my parliamentary colleagues who, I've got to say, knowing most of them really well, work their guts out in their communities. So it's really tough going." The jittery Labor caucus expressed its concern to Julia Gillard about her political management of the asylum boats issue, and interogated the government's stance on toughening the temporary skilled migration program - the 457 visa. Backbencher Laurie Ferguson warned Labor would be "dead" in western Sydney if something didn't happen on the boats, preferably yesterday.  But Ferguson is of course correct on the substance. Immigration and boat arrivals is always a red hot political issue in western Sydney. Tony Abbott sailed above the fray and went out to visit a steel works in Queanbeyan. He shrugged off questions from reporters about whether the Coalition could actually implement its stated policy of turning back asylum boats - was there, as his foreign affairs spokeswoman Julie Bishop hinted to Guardian Australia, an understanding with Indonesia? Indonesia knew what the Coalition thought was essentially the response. Abbott also shrugged off questions about his record as Health Minister on issues related to asbestos. He was not getting into the fine-print of all that, but could point to policy advances he'd made. Question Time was economy, boats, security and Gonski. Outside the parliament, the Reserve Bank left the official cash rate on hold. After Question Time, Labor kept pressure on Julie Bishop over her comments on Indonesia, and how she represented them to the House. Bishop has now been referred to the privileges committee of the parliament. There were also scraps on the Gonski school funding deal, and on marine parks. The House is just now considering an attempt by the Coalition to disallow the management plans for the Gillard Government's marine parks. I'll bring you up to speed with how that vote panned out when we join each other in the morning. That's about the sum of the parts. Thanks so much to Mike Bowers for his stellar work behind the lens today. See you all on the morrow. The Fitzgibbon performance doesn't get any more comprehensible on reflection. Let's think of that whole sorry leadership episode in March. Caucus is worried by terrible opinion polls. They've been terrible for months, and it doesn't look like changing. Labor is divided about what to do about it. The Rudd camp rises up, but the candidate won't stand. Rudd tells all and sundry that he won't stand. The expectation is he won't stand. So if there's nothing to be done, why keep twisting and turning? How is this rational exactly? After a messy day for Labor, the Finance Minister Penny Wong is clearly not amused by the antics of Joel Fitzgibbon. Sky political editor David Speers updates. Blinded by the light. Laurie Ferguson emerges from the ABC bureau to find fifty of his closest camera wielding mates waiting for him. Labor backbencher Laurie Ferguson has bobbed up on ABC24. Ferguson told the Prime Minister in caucus today that Labor needed to fix the damaging politics around the issue of boat arrivals, or Labor would be "dead" in western Sydney at the September election. Now he's telling ABC24 political editor, Lyndal Curtis, the same thing. Ferguson says decisive action is required, pronto. His argument is Labor is not engaging on this issue and it needs to. He's asked if people are still listening to the Prime Minister. Ferguson says he hopes so. The flow of events was quite quick in Question Time. I want to track backwards for a moment to be clear on the specifics of the contradiction that the Shadow Minister for Border Protection, Michael Keenan, was pursuing during a series of questions to Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor concerning the Egyptian man held in the Inverbrackie detention facility. I've chased a copy of the letter mentioned in dispatches in the chamber. Keenan was told by O'Connor on May 10 the assessment of the man's asylum claim was being progressed by the department. In parliament today, O'Connor suggested the process of assessment hadn't yet commenced. That was the point of all that tooing and fro-ing. (Dotting i's and crossing t's.) Kevin Rudd has been highly visible today by recent standards. As well as backing Queensland in the State of Origin in response to a question about the opinion polls, earlier today Rudd recorded a segment for a TV program with News Limited journalist Joe Hilderbrand down on the forecourt of parliament. TV cameras jogged along side. Much jolly jokes with a bunch of student politicians. No doubt you'll see some of that footage in the TV news packages this evening. Call me Hammo. A transcript of a radio interview between the Opposition leader and Barry Hamilton of Radio 4MK has just hit my inbox. Ham, or Hammo. You be the judge. HAMMO: Well, we’re only 102 days to go until the federal election and the federal Opposition Leader Tony Abbott joins me on the program this morning. Hello Tony, welcome again. TONY ABBOTT: Hammo, it’s lovely to be with you. HAMMO: You must be pretty happy with the latest polls which have come out overnight. It’s not looking good at all for the Federal Labor Government. TONY ABBOTT: Look, Hammo, gee we have an abundance of polls ... Ah look. Just as Albanese predicted, here's Malcolm Turnbull, back in good time for the MPI debate. Another referral to the privileges committee. Manager of government business, Anthony Albanese wants the issue of a no-confidence motion examined by privileges. He's referring to a front page story in The Australian last week that detailed letters to the parliamentary independents testing their support for a no-confidence motion in the government. Albanese says no letters existed at the time of publication. Can this issue be examined please? It's all happening. Peter Slipper, the former speaker, wants the current Speaker to examine whether the parliamentary press gallery is acting as a closed shop - locking out new online entrants, blogs and digital sites, like Independent Australia. Could this be looked into please? Now the Trade Minister is attempting to refer the deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop to parliament's powerful privileges committee. (This follows Ms Bishop's interview yesterday with Guardian Australia's political editor, Lenore Taylor.) Emerson wants an inquiry into whether Bishop misled the House concerning a deal (or otherwise) between the Coalition and the Indonesian Government about turning back people smuggler's boats. Emerson says a transcript of Bishop's comments to Lenore are not consistent with her statements yesterday in the House of Representatives. This matter goes to Speaker Anna Burke. Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor wants to make a personal explanation. He says his statements to parliament and to Michael Keenan are entirely consistent on the matter of the Egyptian held in low security detention. Inconsistency? I reject that assertion. With the clock duly run down, the Prime Minister places further questions on the notice paper. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten has further particulars about Mackay and the asbestos case raised in Question Time. He says the site was inspected under state laws and COMCARE was told about it after the fact. Sharp eyes over in the House of Representatives chamber report the Prime Minister has been hitting the Strepsils this Question Time. Winter of discontent. Ruins the throat. All that. Pass the lozenge. It should be noted that suspension and gag - the parliamentary game de jour - helps the government run down the clock during these procedural skirmishes. The question, for the record, was negated. Liberal Michael Keenan, therefore, resumes his attack. A national security debate should not be gagged. Albanese promptly moves another gag motion. He's arguing the Coalition is playing this procedural card because the Shadow Communications Minister, Malcolm Turnbull, was ejected by the Speaker during Question Time. Bit tricky that, because Turnbull is listed to lead the Matter of Public Importance Debate after this session. Albanese counsels Turnbull can resume the chamber in plenty of time. He's due back in at 3.28pm. The division bells ring out once again. Here they are, voting with the Coalition. Thanks Mike Bowers. The ABC's Sabra Lane notes the cross benchers are voting with the Coalition on this motion. (They generally don't support gag motions on principle.) I'm told Opposition whip, Warren Entsch, is sitting out in the advisers box for this vote. (If the former Labor MP Craig Thomson votes with a Coalition, they pull out a parliamentary number to nullify his vote. This goes back to the days when Tony Abbott declared Thomson a tainted vote.) The Immigration Minister gets another question on the Egyptian jihadist. Brendan O'Connor repeats, more or less, the answer he gave Michael Keenan earlier in Question Time. And he has a slap at the Coalition about the Howard Government's detention record - mistakes made in that period, including the detention of an Australian citizen. Manager of opposition business Christopher Pyne then moves a motion attempting to force Brendan O'Connor to explain inconsistencies in his statement to parliament, and a letter he sent Michael Keenan in May. That doesn't work. Pyne attempts to suspend the standing orders. Manager of government business, Anthony Albanese, then attempts to gag Pyne. Albanese argues there's been no lead up to this as a course of action. The division bells are ringing now. Assistant Treasurer David Bradbury takes a Dorothy Dixer allowing him to smack the Shadow Treasurer, Joe Hockey, about Coalition policy costings. Bradbury points to an article we linked to first thing this morning - an edict from Hockey that spending proposals come past him before being announced - an edict that was repeated again in today's Coalition party room. Bradbury chortles about the Opposition attempting to avoid scrutiny. The Prime Minister is sardonic. We can't have that. The Prime Minister is pointed to a statement by the head of COMCARE that the Commonwealth will be liable for NBN-related asbestos cases. The Prime Minister speaks about Telstra accepting responsibility. She's checked by Abbott who takes a point of order. You were pointed to the COMCARE statement, not Telstra, please be relevant. Gillard: I was asked about responsibility. The Prime Minister says she will deal with these issues respectfully, unlike those opposite. Local Government Minister Anthony Albanese (pictured here with Julia Gillard a moment ago) is at the dispatch box attempting to make the case for Constitutional recognition of local government. Good luck with that. Back to asbestos. The Member for Dawson points to a Telstra site in Mackay that lay uncovered in April despite the health and safety risks. Shorten says he will see particulars about the case from COMCARE. It isn't acceptable. Abbott interjects - hang on, this happened in April, weeks ago, why aren't you across this yet? Dorothy Dixer now to Environment Minister Tony Burke on oceans. This concerns the disallowance motion on the marine park management plans that we foreshadowed earlier. There is much chortling about the shadow spokesman Greg Hunt making an announcement about this on land, in a tinny. Much outrage about the lack of bipartisanship on this issue. Burke is asked about the impact of the disallowance. He points out that the Coalition's motion would get rid of the south east marine zone, which was set up by the Howard Government. Burke: Twenty years of science, all the consultation. If it's about protecting the ocean, this Opposition has no interest. The asbestos questions continue. The head of NBN Co is being quoted. Shorten: I do believe asbestos is a big issue in Australia. Treasurer Wayne Swan is telling the House the Reserve Bank has left official interest rates on hold. The Opposition leader has to pick up where Turnbull left off. Another question to Shorten. Given you took action in 2009 with Telstra about your concerns about asbestos, what advice or help did you give the Cabinet on this issue? Shorten shouts about luddites. He is warned by the Speaker, Anna Burke. Shorten points out he wasn't in cabinet in 2009. He was a parliamentary secretary. Now Shorten is shouting about Tony Abbott abusing abestos campaigner Bernie Banton. His time expires. Abbott is back. Can you confirm you took no further action even though you knew the NBN rollout would disturb all these pits and ducts? Shorten: Goodness me I love a lecture from this pack of hypocrites opposite. We are the first government to put in an asbestos agency. Shadow Communications Minister Malcolm Turnbull has a question on asbestos. Workplace Relations Minister Bill Shorten opens with bluster - attack being the best form of defence presumably. Turnbull seeks a point of order. Could the Speaker please remind the minister he is not currently in a Melbourne pie shop? (Shorten had an unfortunate incident in a pie shop last year .. he got a bit testy with the sales assistant.) Turnbull is ejected from the chamber for his trouble. Shorten ploughs on. Asbestos should be a bipartisan issue. Bye bye Malcolm. Keenan has come back on the Egyptian man. Will the Immigration Minister guarantee that this person will be deported once he's had his assessment for a protection visa? Mr O'Connor says the assessment is yet to commence in this case, and this person will be the subject of normal practices, including a character test and a security assessment. Mr O'Connor then backs up with a Dorothy Dixer on changes to 457 visas. The Immigration Minister says these reforms are consistent with Labor values. Presumably, given the ructions in caucus today, as much for the backbench as for the Coalition, (which has also been critical.) That go well? Julie Bishop in Question Time. While the Treasurer Wayne Swan takes a question from Queensland's Bob Katter, and Trade Minister Craig Emerson implores the Coalition to "put the kids before the Libs" - sheesh - I've back tracked slightly. Presumably Julie Bishop - with the question to the Prime Minister earlier about representations to her by the Foreign Minister Bob Carr - refers to a column in today's Australian by Phillip Adams? Adams: Private polling (not that the figures will be revealed by the PM's praetorian guard) shows that Victorian voters have now deserted Julia Gillard and we're looking at swings similar to those that obliterated the party in NSW: more than 20 per cent in safe seats. September won't be so much an election as an exorcism. It wouldn't help if the Prime Minister guaranteed eternal life. No one's listening. Remember the last days of John Howard? Which is why a lengthening number of Labor heavies have told Gillard, publicly or privately, that she has to go. The heaviest won't admit it publicly, but we're talking the aristocracy as well as the apparatchiks. Bob Carr and Simon Crean have not been alone. But Gillard's not for moving. Having led Labor to the edge of the abyss at the previous election, she's now doing a Thelma, with Wayne Swan as Louise, and pressing on the accelerator. Liberal frontbencher Michael Keenan is now turning back to the case of the Egyptian jihadist - the man who was housed in a low security immigration detention facility in South Australia. Keenan asks what is the status of his claim for asylum? Immigration Minister Brendan O'Connor responds. There has been no assessment in relation to this matter. Deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop wants to know the veracity of a report that Foreign Minister Bob Carr has approached the Prime Minister and asked her to stand down for the good of the party. Can she confirm a report in the media today that she's been asked to quit her job for the good of the party? The Prime Minister: That report is entirely untrue. The Prime Minister is thinking of Queensland. She'd like Premier Campbell Newman to give a Gonski. He would, if only Tony Abbott would let him. The leader of the Opposition is prevailing on Premier Newman to put the politics first and the kids last. Labor's Dorothy Dixers open today on education. Bet you didn't see that coming? Mike Bowers captures the Prime Minister entering the House for Question Time. At the conclusion of some elaborate wordsmithing by Liberal backbencher Scott Buchholtz in the workplace debate, here comes the invective hour. Tony Abbott opens today with a question to the Prime Minister. Can she confirm that a recent NATSEM report showed that most people, 87 per cent of households, will be better off under the Coalition? The Prime Minister: Families will be worse off under his plan to take away the school kids bonus. That's real money, worse off, right around the nation. The House of Representatives is debating changes to the Fair Work Act in the downhill run to Question Time. Get your cup of tea. Almost 2pm. I shouldn't be snippy about Clive Palmer, who is, of course, a political journalist's dream. As opinion polls have been a dominant issue in Canberra today, let me share an interesting snippet I heard recently from north of the border. There's a major poll in Queensland tracking voting intentions that has been in the field for some months. A person close to the data told me that survey had Palmer on 6 per cent Queensland-wide for the federal poll; 7.8 per cent on a state-wide metric, and more popular on the Sunshine Coast. The number in Maryborough was more than 13 per cent. Interesting. The case study that is Clive Palmer this election season: can a high wealth individual blast through the lock the major parties have on politics in this country? Independents Tony Windsor and Rob Oakeshott have strolled out to one of the parliamentary courtyards to tell reporters they don't intend to support any further changes to Australia's labour laws unless there is bipartisan agreement between the major parties. The government is attempting to secure amendments to its Fair Work Act. Talk to the hand is the message from the NSW Independents. This will be one in - all in. Tony Windsor: We are not supporting anything that is not supported by the major parties. Rob Oakeshott has a formulation about red lights and green lights. So where is national affairs this lunchtime? Let's pause and take stock. A new batch of toxic opinion polls has triggered another outbreak of Brown's Cows within the ALP. Labor backbencher Joel Fitzgibbon went on a high rating breakfast television program to effectively defy the Prime MInister's office by laughing at the talking points. Asked about the polls, Kevin Rudd declared somewhat Delphically that he was backing Queensland in the State of Origin. The Labor caucus roiled on boats and 457 visas. Tony Abbott warned his troops that 102 days to polling day was a lifetime, strewn with mountains, and slippery bits. He then took himself to a steel factory in Queanbeyan. Abbott faced questions about whether it was deal or no deal with Indonesia to turn back people smugglers boats. We all know what we all think was Abbott's effective reply. Mining magnate Clive Palmer meanwhile thieved some oxygen - apologies, outlined more of his platform for government - at the front of parliament. Asbestos and the NBN continued to roll through the news cycle. Now, the Labor caucus. Thanks to Lenore. The Prime Minister opened the meeting with a recap of recent events - she'd welcomed the babies in her electorate, and opened the administrative headquarters of DisabilityCare in Geelong. She said Abbott was trying to undermine economic confidence, and Queensland Premier Campbell Newman was playing politics with schools funding. There were statements from MPs. One caucus member reflected that Shadow Foreign Minister Julie Bishop's comments about understandings with Indonesia on towing back people smugglers boats were "dangerous for an alternative foreign minister." Bishop appeared to be advocating a "new isolationism." There were questions on the government's proposed changes to 457 visas, including a question from Kevin Rudd: what was the basis for the claims that employers weren't testing the local market before resorting to temporary skilled migrants? There were also the negative remarks about boats which we have pointed you to in the update at 12.26pm. My colleague Lenore Taylor is now back from the briefings after today's partyroom meetings. The Coalition first. Abbott told his troops they may have felt cheery after today's news reports about poor polls for the government, but 102 days until the election was an eternity. MPs he said, needed to stick to the key messages of hope, reward and opportunity. He added everyone needed to conduct themeselves in a manner befitting a member of parliament. Shadow Treasurer Joe Hockey told colleagues that the Parliamentary Budget Office would take account of all announcements being made between now and the election, so MPs must not make unauthorised announcements. These would be counted in the costings. Announcements must come through him. Shadow Communications Minister Malcolm Turnbull said of asbestos and the NBN, that if Workplace Minister Bill Shorten complained of problems in 2009, why didn't he follow this through in 2011 as the NBN rollout accelerated? Turnbull compared the NBN/asbestos issue with Labor's handling of pink batts - the stimulus scheme that went badly wrong several years ago. While I've been watching Tony Abbott, the first news reports after Labor's caucus meeting this morning are hitting the digital sites. The Australian Financial Review: Phillip Coorey, James Massola and Gemma Daley. A warning to the Prime MInister: stop the boats or we are dead in western Sydney. Labor MP Laurie Ferguson has told caucus that Labor was “dead” in Western Sydney and must do something to address widespread community concerns about boat people. Sources have told The Australian Financial Review that Mr Ferguson, clinging to the Western Sydney seat of Werriwa, told Prime Minister Julia Gillard that silence on the issue was no longer an option and she must go on the front foot and explain to people what was a complex issue. This included exposing the claims by the Opposition that it would stop the boats, claims that Mr Ferguson noted were being increasingly diluted by the Coalition. Several MPs said Mr Ferguson had “belled the cat’’ on the issue which had caused Labor the greatest damage since it relaxed the laws for asylum seekers in 2008 and there was widespread support for his contribution. Mr Ferguson said repeatedly “we are dead” unless Ms Gillard made some attempt to explain to the community what was going on. He said anger was now throughout all elements of the community, including ethnic communities. It is also understood that Mr Ferguson’s brother, Martin, and former leader, Kevin Rudd, were among those who called on Immigration Minister Brendan O’Connor for more evidence that the 457 Visa program was being rorted. They wanted to know where they where being used, where they were needed and where they were being rorted. 102 steps to the summit. Abbott invokes Kevin Rudd's Everest analogy again - winning government is as hard as climbing Mt Everest. 102 days until the election, but who is counting? Tony Abbott. Any slip can be fatal. Perhaps that's why he's heading for the First Aid room? Abbott is asked about media reports this morning negative about his record on asbestos while Health Minister in the Howard Government. We pointed to these reports first up today. Abbott: I'm not going to comment on decisions the department made. Abbott says he can defend his record on asbestos. The government needs to get this problem with the NBN under control so it can do the same is his reasoning. This is a massive asbestos problem if its not handled correctly. Abbott is with Peter Hendy, the Liberal candidate for Eden-Monaro (currently a Labor-held marginal seat). The Opposition leader is here this morning with a message of hope and optimism for the familes and businesses of Eden Monaro. While an incoming Coalition government can't solve all the problems overnight .. It's interesting that Abbott has started to insert this qualification more frequently to his statements about the universe post September 14. Like that Pantene commercial - it won't happen overnight but it will happen. Dangerous in politics, over promising, as Abbott well knows. Abbott is pressed in questions over whether the Coalition has an agreement with the Indonesian Government to turn boats around. (This question is in response to Guardian Australia's political editor Lenore Taylor's interview with Shadow Foreign Minister Julie Bishop yesterday.) Like Julie Bishop, Abbott is suggesting the Coalition has an understanding with Indonesia. Not an agreement, but an understanding. Our policy is to turn boats around where its safe to do so. We are confident that we can have a strong and constructive relationship with the Indonesian Government. We do have a lots of discussions with senior members of the Inodensian Government. It's impossible for an Opposition to negotiate with a sovereign government but we've had very frank discussions. Here he is. Man of steel. Opposition leader Tony Abbott press conference coming up shortly from Queanbeyan. Speaking of the economy, we'll find out later this afternoon whether the Reserve Bank of Australia will cut the official cash rate. Market expectation is no, most likely. While I'm drawing your attention to good pieces around, I'll point you to the latest blog post from Greg Jericho, AKA Grogs Gamut. Today's meaty Grogonomics outing is on debt: should we be worried about government debt? With charts, it's here. Some wonderful pieces around this morning remembering Yunupingu, singer, educator and Australian of the Year, who died yesterday at only 56. Schools Minister Peter Garrett has written for us at guardian.co.uk/australia Here's an extract: The Yolgnu people of north-east Arnhem Land have lived for millennia on the top end of Australia. It is a vast tropical region of striking landscapes, criss-crossed by streams and wetlands, bordered by numerous white sand beaches and offshore islands. Unlike other parts of our country, this was a place Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people never left. It has always been their home. The Yolgnu are strong defenders of their living culture, with campaigns going back as far as the 1960s for recognition of their rights to land. On Monday we lost, as is tragically so often the case, a 56-year-old Aboriginal leader to kidney disease. Yunupingu, from one of the clans of this region, was both an educator and a musician who proved both eloquent and adept at transmitting the strong cultural messages of his people. He had a long-held view about the need for genuine on-the-ground reconciliation between Indigenous and non-Indigenous Australians – a view from which he never wavered. He saw that healing and then building relations between Yolgnu and Balanda (Europeans) was a task that needed urgent effort and action. The Age's wonderful political editor Michael Gordon has also penned his tribute, a must read in my view. Here's a taste. We met at the funeral of his niece, who died too young from a treatable disease and suffered all the more because of her inadequate command of English, her second language. At an airport in north Arnhem Land, men with ochre-painted faces had danced while the women cried, as the girl's body was taken gently from the back of a station wagon to the small plane that would return her to her homeland. ''When they cry, they heal,'' Dr Yunupingu explained to me later, his soothing voice hushed and heavy with his own grief. The next day, he followed the girl by car to her homeland community, some 300 kilometres to the south, for a ceremony that would extend over several days. ''I'm going back to re-educate myself, to spiritually empower myself,'' he told me. BREAKING: Parliamentary press gallery swim team absolutely, comprehensively trounced by the Coalition. Coalition declares omen. This note from ABC political correspondent and swimming maestro, Greg Jennet, explains. Folks, Unfortunate news: our Gallery Swim Team has suffered a mid-winter meltdown.. After an unbroken run of at least five wins, our gallant swim team was swamped by a powerful Coalition side; (Laming, Simpkins, Jensen, Chester, Frydenberg, Hartsuyker, McKenzie.) Of course, exuberant Coalition team members are speaking of their triumph as “an omen”.. Labor (Livermore, Leigh, Bowen, Macklin, Hayes, Perrett, Dreyfus) ground out a determined third. Thanks to; Tom Iggulden, Joe Kelly, Nick Butterly, Jacqueline Maley, Melissa Clarke and Eliza Borrello. Our female representation was at record highs this year. Cheers, Greg Jennett. Opposition leader Tony Abbott is off to the marginal seat of Eden-Monaro for a visit to a steelworks, TV cameras in hot pursuit. Down in Melbourne meanwhile, influential radio talkback host Neil Mitchell is maintaining his rage. AFL and politics. Mitchell has editorialised thus: Two people today are hanging on when they shouldn’t be hanging on. It is painful. Mark Neeld, coach of Melbourne, is like a carcass swinging in the wind. He is finished, and they are delaying the inevitable. More important, but the same thing, is Julia Gillard and her government. They are finished too. The decent thing to do here would be to call an election. Now. And out the front of parliament, mining magnate Clive Palmer, is insisting he can and will be Prime Minister. Palmer: I could spend my time in Switzerland or somewhere like that. But I care deeply about this country. Just another day in national affairs. Everyone talking. All the time. No State of Origin jokes from the rubgy league-mad Climate Change and Industry Minister Greg Combet. ABC Radio National Breakfast host Fran Kelly asked Combet about the polls on her show this morning. Here's the relevant exchange. FRAN KELLY: And just finally Minister, a number of polls out today all suggest Labor is in an irreversible decline headed for a massive defeat. With 103 days left, what can the Government do, what can Labor do to close the gap? Anything? GREG COMBET: Yes. There are many things that we can do. There’s no doubt that we are under considerable pressure as we head towards the election. There’s no news in that, it’s been difficult for quite some period of time. But I do believe that as the election does get closer many of the issues will come into the frame. We’ve been discussing the automotive manufacturing industry for example. There’s a very stark choice to be made on that issue alone, particularly for people in Victoria and South Australia who depend upon it, the better part of a couple of hundred thousand people and their families. The Liberal Party policy will kill the auto manufacturing industry stone dead in this country. It will. It’s not a piece of political rhetoric or hyperbole. Their policy is to take $500 million out of the support for the industry to period to 2015 and place a further $1.5 billion in support after 2015 at risk. Now, no business can invest in those circumstances in the car industry and the Liberal Party policy will see the car industry end in this country in 2016 or so. So it’s a pretty clear choice there and there’s plenty of other clear choices on education, on superannuation, on the tax system, on many things, the SchoolKids Bonus, things that are really important to people. And we’ll be arguing our case I can assure you. Thanks to my colleagues from Network Ten for this audio. Kevin Rudd was stopped by reporters at the Hyatt Hotel this morning, and asked for his opinion on the ball polls - including the survey predicting he would be the last Labor man left standing in Queensland after September 14. Rudd made a joke about the State of Origin. He was backing Queensland. Have a listen. Make of that intervention what you will. MPs are hunkered down in their party room meetings as they do each Tuesday during a parliamentary sitting. Looking ahead, there will be an interesting parliamentary vote later today. The Coalition will try to overturn the Commonwealth's proclamation of a network of marine reserves. The Coalition wants to disallow the government's marine park management plans. The ABC's Alex Kirk broadcast a report previewing this vote on this on the AM program this morning. The Coalition says the management plans have been imposed on communities with inadequate consultation. Environment Minister Tony Burke begs to differ. Mr Burke told Alex Kirk: Their concerns are entirely political. They're playing a game just like they did with the super trawler. Whenever it's a game about the ocean they're not on the side of the ocean. The Opposition are claiming for example that there's been no consultation. There's been five rounds of consultation, something like three quarters of a million submissions in that process. Observers believe the numbers aren't there for the Coalition, but as they say in the classics, time will tell. Liberal Senator Cory Bernardi - described by host Tony Jones on last night's ABC TV Q&A program as a 'social media enthusiast' - wants folks to know he's overhauled his website. As this audio makes clear, he wants the new site to be a meeting place for conservatives. Those who caught Q&A last night will recall Senator Bernardi described himself not as a social media enthusiast (perhaps that was a given) but a healer in the Liberal Party. This contribution is by far my favourite caption for Mike Bowers' lovely opening still this morning. Thanks for playing, Lee Skelton. If you think you can do better, you can leave a comment here on the blog, or join the Twitter conversation using the hashtag #politicslive @murpharoo Mike is @mpbowers Greens deputy leader. Powered by Ladyhawke. (My Delirium not inappropriate theme music for this sitting period). "Leaked internal polling" generally points to an agenda of some kind. Let's stick with the Newspoll for now. The Opposition leader Tony Abbott has picked up three points in this morning's survey on the preferred prime minister measure - and the Prime Minister has lost four points. The Coalition's primary vote is 49 per cent, and Labor's has slipped to 30 per cent. Newswire AAP is calling that a 35 seat loss for Labor if the election was held today. The Abbott Government would take The Lodge with an 8 per cent swing. The poll confirms a trend evident for many months. Issues in play over the past couple of weeks include the botched public funding agreement which collapsed last week - and of course the reports of asbestos and the NBN rollout were prominent as Newspoll went into the field. Fitzgibbon, in continuation. DAVID KOCH: Is there a push to take the party in a new direction now after figures like these? JOEL FITZGIBBON: No, Kochie, that little battle has been done and dusted and the die has been cast and again, we just need to bind together as a party and get on with it. Bind together as a party? I'll leave that thought with you. You can see that vision of Fitzgibbon giving it to the official line if you click on this link. Or here is an audio clip if you want to listen. Not everyone it seems is as intent as the Prime Minister in changing the conversation this morning. Labor backbencher Joel Fitzgibbon (Fitzgibbon went to the backbench after his contribution to Labor's botched leadership coup in March) has appeared on a popular breakfast television program this morning. Helping. Actually, not so much. The host David Koch wants Fitzgibbon's reaction to the bad opinion polls today. Here's the relevant excerpt: DAVID KOCH: Joel, Joel, Joel, are you shattered by this? And can you, are you still going to go to the election with this Prime Minister? JOEL FITZGIBBON: Hang on Kochie, I just brought the manual with me. I’ll see what it says. It says I should say, “polls come and go, but the only poll that matters is on election day”. A bit of translation: the line Fitzgibbon used on TV this morning is the line that is generally issued to MPs by the central communications command in the event of bad polls. It's what they are told to say. Fair to say Fitzgibbon just gave that whole process the bird on national television. Faced with a forest of bad headlines this morning, the Prime Minister is intent early on a change of subject. Let's talk about school improvement. Overnight, Julia GIllard has written to Queensland Premier Campbell Newman in the following terms - c'mon Campbell, what's going on with Gonski? Surely you aren't putting Tony Abbott's political interests ahead of the kiddies of Queensland? There's even a PS. Here's the Prime Minister's letter: Dear Premier Newman, Your state is one of the biggest winners from the National Plan for School Improvement (NPSI). Queensland schools will gain $3.8 billion – around $2.2 million on average per school, over six years. As you know, $2.5 billion of that extra cash would come from the Australian Government and $1.3 billion from your state. A two-for-one deal that will bring huge benefits to Queensland schools. Parents, principals, teachers and the broader community will wonder why you persist in refusing this extra money for their schools. It will be extremely disappointing to Queenslanders if you do not put extra money for schools in tomorrow’s Budget. It cannot be ignorance since you know how much Queensland schools – government and non-government alike – will gain from the NPSI. Your officials have been provided with detailed modelling, and updates, on five separate occasions. Your officials have also had 56 bi-lateral and multi-lateral meetings with my government’s officials. Despite this, you have not responded properly to the offer, and you have not authorised your officials to give accurate information about how you will fund schools next year and beyond. Perhaps it is simply that you do not want to guarantee your schools real funding growth in your Budget. As you know, we’re asking all states to provide indexation of at least 3%. That guarantee is essential. It ensures that schools get the full benefit of new money and prevents your government from using the new federal money to cover up your spending cuts. Perhaps it is because the Leader of the Opposition or members of his team are demanding you put their political interests ahead of the interests of Queensland children. Whatever the reason for your refusal to agree to $3.8 billion extra for Queensland schools, there can be no excuse for your government’s wilful misrepresentation of the scale of investment that is available, and surely there will be no forgiveness for you turning your back on Queensland’s kids. Yours, Julia Gillard Prime Minister PS: Reform is essential. Queensland schools were outperformed by NSW in every NAPLAN measure across every year level in 2012. Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of politics from Canberra. A gorgeous frame from Mike Bowers to open on the blog this morning - a golden parliament. Sometimes a little less golden than this image suggests. Let's get cracking. Unsurprisingly, the NBN rollout and asbestos is still a dominant political story this morning, but it’s been joined overnight by a batch of new opinion polls portending a wipeout for the Gillard Government in September. The coverage of the asbestos story cuts both ways in the papers and digital sites today - there are negative stories for the government and negative stories for Tony Abbott harking back to his days as Health Minister in the Howard Government. The opinion polls lean just one way: goodbye Labor. On our selection. Let’s run through the main stories. Asbestos Labor is fighting off new safety fears over its National Broadband Network as more cases of asbestos exposure during its construction fuel a growing political brawl over the management and cost of the $37.4 billion project. The health risks spread to all mainland states yesterday after the Queensland safety regulator revealed three new incidents and federal authority Comcare warned of an "alarming" spate of asbestos safety breaches by contractors. The Australian (paywalled) Telstra has admitted that up to one in five of its suburban communications pits contains asbestos, fuelling fears the National Broadband Network rollout could expose a big number of workers and households to the deadly fibre. The danger led the Federal Government to call an emergency meeting yesterday with Telstra and NBN officials to discuss the threat. It also invited asbestos advocates, union leaders and Australia's chief health officer to help co-ordinate a national response to public anxiety. The West Australian The NBN asbestos affair is, apart from being a major concern in itself, particularly bad for the government because it triggers memories of the mishandled pink batts scheme. That’s why the opposition is going all out to attempt to pin some blame on Workplace Minister Bill Shorten, despite Telstra accepting general responsibility. The Conversation Telstra tried to fast-track compensation arrangements for its employees exposed to asbestos but was rebuffed by Tony Abbott's own department back in 2001. With the opposition on Monday leading a parliamentary attack on the government over its alleged lack of urgency in addressing asbestos discovered in Telstra junction pits being handed over to the NBN, Fairfax Media has learnt the giant telco wanted to create an independent body to accelerate compensation and sought approval from the Department of Workplace Relations. However, the department, then under the ministerial leadership of Mr Abbott, rejected the plan. Fairfax Media Back in October 2007, when Tony Abbott was Health Minister, he denied asbestos disease sufferers access to subsidised medicine for their illness. Naturally, the asbestos disease sufferers were not happy with the decision. And they organised a petition, imploring Abbott to place the costly drug, Alimta, on the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme. Some 17,000 people signed it. It was arranged that it would be delivered to Abbott’s Sydney office by Bernie Banton, 61, who was suffering terminal mesothelioma. But when Banton showed up, in his wheelchair, with his oxygen tube, trailed by media, Abbott had done a bunk. Banton was devastated and told the media so. He said Abbott had known he was coming. He called the then-Health Minister, now-Opposition Leader and probable future Prime Minister a “gutless creep” and a “disgraceful human being”. The Global Mail Bad polls Julia Gillard's personal support has dropped to a three-month low and Tony Abbott has extended his lead as the nation's preferred prime minister after public outrage over plans to give the Labor and Liberal parties $10 million each in taxpayer funds for electioneering. Satisfaction with the way the Prime Minister is doing her job has fallen three points to 28 per cent in the past two weeks and dissatisfaction has risen from 59 per cent to 62 per cent, the highest since the end of March. And Mr Abbott has stretched his lead as preferred prime minister to eight points. Ms Gillard faced a revolt from her Labor MPs and a public outcry at attempts to quietly agree to public funding changes for political parties last week. The Australian (paywalled) Julia Gillard's home state has abandoned her with as many as six seats in Victoria now at risk of falling to the Coalition, including that of her Attorney General Mark Dreyfus. And Tony Abbott has surged ahead nationally as a better potential Prime Minister, with three separate polls now confirming a worsening electoral crisis looming for Labor as it battles to contain the latest NBN asbestos debacle and more boat arrivals. In what will be a shock for the Prime Minister, an exclusive JWS/Herald Sun poll reveals a 15.4 per cent swing against Mr Dreyfus in the once-safe seat of Isaacs, enough to remove him. If repeated in neighbouring seats, the PM's hopes of retaining the furniture in her home state could be dashed. (news.com) Deputy Prime Minister Wayne Swan looks set to lose his Brisbane seat of Lilley, with internal polling suggesting Labor will struggle to retain any Queensland seats at the September 14 federal election. In a result even worse than the 1996 ''baseball bats'' election, when Labor was reduced to two of the then 26 seats in Queensland, Labor may retain only one MP - former prime minister Kevin Rudd. The Queensland polling, taken in recent weeks in Mr Swan's seat of Lilley, is believed to show Mr Swan's primary vote has collapsed to just 28 per cent, compared with 41 per cent at the last election. Fairfax Media Don’t speak out of school Shadow Treasurer Joe Hockey has issued a gag order on Coalition MPs and candidates talking to the Parliamentary Budget Office over fears that new laws will expose the Coalition to greater scrutiny. But in a bid to crack down on ill-discipline in Coalition ranks, he has banned any candidate or MP speaking about election policy without prior approval from the leader's office or the campaign head office. Fearing that changes to the powers of the PBO, expected before the September election, will put individual members under PBO scrutiny over the total cost of Coalition policies, Mr Hockey has written to all MPs and candidates warning them about straying from official policy line. He has demanded that if any candidate or MP is contacted by the PBO, then the contact must be immediately referred to Mr Hockey's office. (news.com) Asylum boats There was almost no warning. The boat had stopped about 10 minutes earlier. Since then it had rocked gently in the swell, settling lower in the water. Its Indonesian crew shouted to one another, increasingly agitated. On the roof of the open wooden outsize fishing boat, Mohammed Ishaq was shaken awake by another refugee. “Get up, the boat is sinking,” he was told. But even as he stood, the 31-year-old Afghan-born Pakistani felt the deck tilting sharply under his feet. He slid, fell and hit the water. It was 24 June 2012. The boat was 107 nautical miles from the nearest land. Of the 204 refugees aboard, almost all from Afghanistan or Pakistan, 94 would die. (Guardian Australia) You can follow live updates throughout the day here on the live blog, or join the conversation on Twitter using the hashtag #politicslive You can also follow me on @murpharoo

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 30 August

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 30 August
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

results.webTitle: Delhi's new chief rejects security on mission to clean up Indian politics

fields.headline: Delhi's new chief rejects security on mission to clean up Indian politics
fields.trailText: Arvind Kejriwal, whose party is made up of all first-time candidates, vows to fight corruption after shock results at polls
fields.bodyText: The former tax inspector and activist who is to be Delhi's new chief minister, the top elected official in the metropolis, has refused police protection, saying that his "biggest security" is God. Arvind Kejriwal, leader of a new populist political party "dedicated to improving the lot of the common man", announced on Monday that he would form a government to run the sprawling, troubled and increasingly wealthy city of 15 million people. The 45-year-old novice politician, who has vowed to end the "VIP culture" of the capital, also said that new ministers would refuse to accept the large government bungalows that are customary perks of such posts as well as cars that use flashing red beacons to force their way through traffic. Even minor dignitaries in the capital travel with a large police escort, adding to the already acute congestion and enraging ordinary citizens. Kejriwal's Aam Admi (Common Man) party stunned political analysts and established parties when it won 28 out of 70 seats in local assembly elections in Delhi earlier this month. The newcomer to the capital's cutthroat machinations, who launched his party a year ago, beat the former chief minster of the city, a veteran of the ruling Congress party who had dismissed his challenge as "not even on our radar". Congress suffered a catastrophic defeat, being reduced to eight seats. Almost all the candidates of the AAP were political debutants and included a rickshaw driver, a lawyer and a TV actor. Their key pledge was to clean up politics and the endemic corruption that has crippled the provision of public service for the many millions who cannot afford to pay for private healthcare, schooling or even basics such as water. The party's message and symbol – a broom – proved popular with urban voters also struggling with runaway inflation, chronic youth underemployment and slowing economic growth. Kerjiwal, who called the party's victory a historic win, had initially said it would not form a minority government. But after lengthy negotiations in recent days, Congress has now decided to support the AAP in the Delhi local assembly. "It's a great day, a very exciting time," said Aathishi Marlena, a senior AAP activist and policy adviser. Marlena said the challenges were daunting but that the party would meet them. "Tackling the rampant corruption, the lack of transparency and accountability, the problems faced by the common people is a matter of political will and that's what we bring to this." The main opposition Bharatiya Janata party won 32 seats but no other political group was willing to offer it support. Sachin Pilot, a Congress minister, described the emergence of a new political outfit – the AAP in Delhi – as "absolutely astonishing". "For my party of course it has been a setback but generally speaking the fact that indiviudals can come together in a very difficult space and find themselves relevant and meaningful has been a new phenomenon. In the last three or four decades it hasn't happened at all. That's something one has to recognise reconcile and then deal with," Pilot told the Guardian, speaking shortly after the poll earlier this month. Senior Congress officials told reporters that the party's support for the AAP in Delhi would not be unconditional and would depend on the party's performance. The election in Delhi was one of four state elections in the last month, which saw Congress lose heavily. The polls were dubbed a semi-final for the general election due next spring, by analysts. Though India's population remains largely rural, the proportion of seats in the national assembly determined by urban voters has steadily risen in recent years. Some observers see the new middle classes in cities and, particularly, tens of thousands of small towns, as determining what is likely to be a tough battle between the Congress party and the BJP next year. Also crucial are likely to be between 120 million – 150 million first time voters. The AAP has said it now wants to focus efforts on expanding into the huge northern state of Uttar Pradesh, which has a population fo 180 million, and Bihar, another poor northern state. It hopes to become a national party however. Kejriwal's announcement ended two weeks of speculation and fears of a hung assembly in Delhi, a city which has become much richer in recent years but faces huge problems of crime, sanitation, housing and pollution . Though the outgoing Congress government in Delhi had been praised for successfully building a metro system and holding the Commonwealth Games in the Indian capital in 2010, it had also been hit by graft charges and suffered from the general unpopularity of the Congress-led coalition government at a national level. The new government will be sworn in on Thursday. The BJP called the AAP's decision a betrayal. "The AAP accused the Congress of being the most corrupt. Today they have compromised on their principles. This is gross betrayal," said Dr Harsh Vardhan, the BJP's chief ministerial candidate.

results.webTitle: Oh Lord, can't we keep religion out of politics? | Teresa Wiltz

fields.headline: Oh Lord, can't we keep religion out of politics?
fields.trailText: Teresa Wiltz: America's founders insisted on church-state separation. Yet now we have senators and justices prattling about the devil. Save us
fields.bodyText: The devil is making a comeback. Or maybe, if we're to believe a certain group of politicos, he's been hiding in plain sight all along. First, we had Representative Michele Bachmann (Republican, Minneosota) asserting that President Obama's decision to help a segment of the Syrian rebels is proof positive that the four horsemen of the apocalypse would be showing up any day now. Because terrorists. "Rather than seeing this as a negative, we need to rejoice, Maranatha, come Lord Jesus, His day is at hand," she said in an interview with a Christian radio station. Senator Rand Paul (Republican, Kentucky), speaking at the Values Voter Summit over the weekend, declared that there was a "worldwide war against Christianity" – and the president, he said, isn't doing a thing to stop it. And then we had Senator Ted "Shutdown" Cruz (Republican, Texas), at the same conference, using a line from the movie The Usual Suspects – "the greatest trick the devil ever played was to convince the world he didn't exist" – to make a clumsy analogy to liberals. Because leftwingers have "convinced conservatives that we cannot win". Never mind those plummeting Republican approval ratings, senator, Satan made the pollsters do it. Sadly, I've come to expect such "us v them" histrionics from Bachmann et al. They are, after all, playing to their base. So I'd be tempted to dismiss this all as political theater – played-out political theater – if it weren't for that crazy times interview that US supreme court Justice Antonin Scalia did with New York magazine. In it, he talked about the devil. At great length. With no apparent irony: And then You know, it is curious. In the Gospels, the devil is doing all sorts of things. He's making pigs run off cliffs, he's possessing people and whatnot. And that doesn't happen very much anymore … It's because he's smart … What he's doing now is getting people not to believe in him or in God. He's much more successful that way. And then, he told the reporter that since she didn't hold the same belief system, she'd be going to hell, too. Let's hope he was kidding about that. Now, I'm all about freedom of religion. (My own beliefs tend to veer towards agnosticism, with a healthy side of Buddhism.) And I certainly respect the good justice's right to believe in whatever the hell he wants to believe in, even if that means a fervent belief in Beelzebub. But given that Scalia's about to weigh in on a case about public prayer in government meetings, I really wish he'd kept his lips zipped. Religion has no place in politics – and it certainly has no place in the US supreme court. Religion, or the lack thereof, is such a deeply, deeply personal thing. People who think they've got a personal pipeline to the divine make me nervous. Scratch that. People in power who think they've got a personal pipeline to the divine make me nervous. Really nervous. Because religion has been used in this country to justify all sorts of things – slavery being at the top of that list. It encourages revisionist thinking, like the Texas Board of Education's reshaping American history in its textbooks. And unless you've pulled some mystical magic trick, died, crossed over to the great beyond and come back again, how do you really know what God's thinking? If, that is, She exists at all. So, I don't like the role that religion – to be specific, Christianity – plays too often in American politics. It made me cringe during the 2008 presidential election, watching Barack Obama tie himself into knots trying to establish his Christian bonafides. Why should it matter? What if he were, let's say, a devout adherent to the Baha'i faith? Or if he were a committed atheist? No, we weren't created to be a Christian nation, even though the Founding Fathers had Christians in their ranks. When he was president, Thomas Jefferson, a fervent theist, told a group of Baptist ministers government had no business meddling in religion. Moreover, he said, there needed to be a "wall of separation between church and state". What happened to the wall? I like the wall. We need the wall. In fact, let's toss a few more bricks on top of the wall while we're at it – the better to keep the devil from meddling in our political affairs.

tags.description: News about the US Republican party, including comment and features from the Guardian
News about Barack Obama, 44th US president who served from 20 January 2009 to 20 January 2017

results.webTitle: Labour conference - Ed Miliband's speech: Politics live blog

fields.headline: Labour conference - Ed Miliband's speech: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the Labour conference in Brighton, including Ed Miliband's speech
fields.bodyText: Here is a summary of that fringe event. • The lights could go out if Labour introduces its 20-month freeze on energy prices, Ian Peters of British Gas said. “If we have no ability to control what we do in the retail prices” and wholesale prices suddenly go up within a single year “that will threaten energy security,” he said. Asked if that meant the lights would go out, he replied: “I think that is a risk.” • Tom Greatrex, the junior shadow energy minister, rejected this. He said: “I do not accept that that [the price freeze] is likely to produce blackouts at all … I don’t see that as a realistic prospect, as much as it might be a good line for a news bulletin and a good headline.” • Greatrex was asked if Labour would cut the companies some slack if there was a sudden increase in wholesale prices. Greatrex said they would not, and pointed out that when wholesale prices have come down in the past that had not been passed on to consumers. That’s it from us for today. Join Andrew Sparrow tomorrow morning for the final day of the Labour conference. Reg Platt of the IPPR says he does not believe there will be a supply problem brought on by the Labour policy and he says that if the energy companies threaten that, they will deserve a harsh response from the public.
