Tom Greatrex is asked about this idea that the lights might go out. Ian Peters interrupts to say he was talking about “in year market volatility” rather than long-term increases. Greatrex says: I don’t accept that the process of ensuring you have transparency and clarity in the market is going to lead to blackouts … In terms of the fixed period, the price freeze, that is because we would need that period to get that framework in place. I do not accept that that is likely to produce blackouts at all … I don’t see that as a realistic prospect, as much as it might be a good line for a news bulletin and a good headline. Gary Smith of the GMB says he does not agree that the lights will go out because of Labour’s announcement. British Gas’s Ian Peters is asked what he thinks of the Labour announcement. “If we have no ability to control what we do in the retail prices” and wholesale prices go up “that will threaten energy security,” Peters says. Does he mean the lights will go out? “I think that is a risk,” he says. Nick Pearce of the IPPR asks what happens if there is a big increase in wholesale prices. Greatrex says from 2015 we would have to put a short piece of legislation through to establish the cap, and then until 2017 the costs will be frozen. When wholesale costs have come down in the past, Greatrex says, this has not been reflected in consumers’ bills. “During that period [2015-17] it is right to be able to have that freeze,” he says. He says “it’s not about a short-term fix”. Tom Greatrex, the shadow junior energy minister, is up next. He says all the big six energy companies know they have "lost trust with their customers". It’s “vital” that trust is rebuilt, he says. There are significant levels of profit from companies in the generation part of the energy sector rather than the retail part – and these are often the same companies, he points out. Greatrex responds to criticisms that Labour’s policy would threaten investment. Investment recently has “partly fallen away” because of “mixed messages” and “false debates” from the government on shale gas, renewables and nuclear energy, he says. Reg Platt of the IPPR thinktank speaks first, saying wholesale gas costs are largely responsible for high energy prices. Platt says: “It’s very difficult to know what those profits are that are being made, and what you might deem appropriate in the sector.” He predicted Labour’s cap would be “a very popular policy with the public”. Ian Peters, managing director of residential energy at British Gas, speaks next. He says the vast majority of a gas or electricity bill is made up of things his company cannot control – such as wholesale costs, delivery to the home, and taxes. “Energy prices reflect the cost of energy,” he says. Profits are a small proportion, he says. We’re a strong British country creating jobs and investing in Britain’s future, Peters says. Gary Smith of the GMB union goes next. He says energy bills could double over the next few years and “that is a crisis that is facing people in how they’re going to meet that”. Smith says large chunks of the UK’s gas and electricity infrastructure are owned by Chinese companies, and he doesn’t believe they have British interests at heart. He backs Peters on his contention that the main drivers of increased prices are out of the hands of energy companies. Hi, Paul Owen here to take the reins for the next hour or so. I came here to this a conference room on the top floor – more or less on the roof – of the Metropole hotel to hear Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, hopefully talk about Labour’s most eye-catching policy for years: Ed Miliband’s promise to impose a 20-month price freeze on all energy companies. Unfortunately Flint has pulled out and will be replaced by her junior shadow minister Tom Greatrex. But I think it will probably be worth hearing what he has to say. Miliband announced the new policy in his conference speech today, saying: “The companies won't like it because it will cost them money. But they have been overcharging people for so long because the market does not work. And we need to press the reset button." To my mind this policy has the potential to have real “cut-through” with voters and may even have some impact on the outcome of the 2015 election. Labour's promise to increase the number of hours of free childcare is the same, in my opinion. • Ed Miliband has delighted his party with a well-delivered conference speech that contained a promise to freeze gas and electricity bills for almost two years if Labour wins the general election. It was the one announcement in the speech not trailed in advance, and it is the probably most eye-catching example of his desire to tame "irresponsible" capitalism through robust regulation. For Labour activists who have been looking for populist policies to sell on the doorstep, it is a godsend. But it's a risky move too. Energy companies claim that it is unfair, and that it will curb investment. They may, of course, be exaggerating. (The plan will cost the industry around £4bn. Utility companies complained bitterly about the £5bn windfall tax proposed by Labour before the 1997 election, but in the event it was imposed with little fuss). But there are other dangers too. What happens if the Tories respond with fuel payments targeted at those on low and middle-incomes (which would allow David Cameron to criticise a price freeze benefiting the rich)? And what happens if energy prices start to fall, undermining Labour's key election offer? • Ed Davey, the Lib Dem energy secretary, has suggested that Miliband's policy could lead to "widespread blackouts". Energy UK, the trade body for the industry, has also suggested that energy companies could stop investing in Britain in response to Miliband's threat. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. My colleague Paul Owen will post an update from the fringe later. Here are the best quotes from Ed Miliband's speech. • Miliband said his main theme was that Britain could do better. I want to start today with the simplest of thoughts. An idea that has inspired change for generations. The belief that helped drive us out of the Second World War and into that great reforming government of 1945. An ambition that is more important now than it has been for decades. An emotion that is felt across our country at kitchen tables every night. A feeling that is so threatening to those who want to keep things as they are. Words that are so basic and yet so powerful, so modest and yet so hard to believe. Six simple words that say: Britain can do better than this. Britain can do better than this; we are Britain, we are better than this. • He said he believed that listening to others, and understanding their problems, was the most important thing a leader could do. [Miliband's mother] is the most patient, generous person I have met in my whole life. And she taught me never to be contemptuous of others, never to be dismissive of their struggle. Now she was teaching me a lesson of life. And some people will say, ah yeah but you have to leave decency behind when it comes to politics. Well I say they are wrong, because only if you reach out and listen can you do the most important thing a leader can do, the most important qualification in my view for being Prime Minister. Only then will you have the ability to walk in the shoes of others and know who to fight for, whoever your opponent, however powerful they are, guided by the only thing that matters: your sense of what is right. This is what I believe, this is where I stand, this is the leadership Britain needs. • He said the key problem for Britain was that ordinary people were no longer benefiting from growth. Now what I’m about to tell you is the most important thing I’m going to say today about what needs to change about our country. For generations in Britain when the economy grew the majority got better off. And then somewhere along the way that vital link between the growing wealth of the country and your family finances was broken. This is, this goes beyond one party or one government. It is more important to you than which party is in power, even more important than that ... They used to say a rising tide lifts all boats, now the rising tide just seems to lift the yachts. Now I say this to the people of Britain. If I were you I wouldn’t even take a second look at a political party unless they make this their central defining purpose because your future depends on it. Your children’s future depends on it. Britain’s future depends on it. I say we are Britain we can do better than this. • He said this problem was linked to David Cameron's approach to what Cameron calls "the global race". The cost of living crisis isn’t an accident of David Cameron’s economic policy it is in his economic policy. Let me explain why. You see he believes in this thing called the global race, but what he doesn’t tell you is that he thinks for Britain to win the global race you have to lose, lower wages, worse terms and conditions, fewer rights at work. But Britain can’t win a race for the lowest wages against countries where wages rates are pennies an hour and the more we try the worse things will get for you. Britain can’t win a race for the fewest rights at work against the sweat shops of the world and the more we try the worse things will get for you. And Britain can’t win a race for the lowest skilled jobs against countries where kids leave school at the age of 11. And the more we try the worse things will get for you. It is a race to the bottom. Britain cannot and should not win that race. • He said the Tories were only interested in those at the top. So when they tell you the economy is healing, that everything is fixed, just remember, they are not talking about your life, they are talking about their friends at the top. That is who they are talking about; it is high hopes for them. And every so often you know the mask slips doesn’t it. The other day a man they call Lord Howell, he was I think their advisor on fracking at one point… There is nothing funny about that. He said it was wrong to frack in some areas but it was ok in others, it was ok in the North East of England because he said, and I quote ‘it was full of desolate and uninhabited areas.’ In one casual aside dismissing one who le region of the country. Let’s tell these Tories about the North East of England and every other part of Britain. People go out to work. They love their kids. They bring up their families. They care for their neighbours. They look out for each other. They are proud of their communities. They are proud of their communities. They hope for the future. The Tories call them inhabitants of desolate areas. We call them our friends, our neighbours, the heroes of our country. They are fed up of a government that doesn’t understand their lives and a Prime Minister who cannot walk in their shoes. We are Britain, we are better than this. • He said Labour would freeze gas bills. Take the gas and electricity companies. We need successful energy companies, in Britain. We need them to invest for the future. But you need to get a fair deal and frankly, there will never be public consent for that investment unless you do get a fair deal. And the system is broken and we are going to fix it. If we win the election 2015 the next Labour government will freeze gas and electricity prices until the start of 2017. Your bills will not rise. It will benefit millions of families and millions of businesses. That’s what I mean by a government that fights for you. That’s what I mean when I say Britain can do better than this. • He said Labour would get house building up to 200,000 a year by 2020. There are 9 million people in this country renting a home, many of whom who would want to buy. 9 million people - we don’t just have a cost of living crisis, we have a housing crisis too. In 2010 when we left office there was a problem. There were one million too few homes in Britain. If we carry on as we are, by 2020 there will be two million too few homes in Britain. That is the equivalent of two cities the size of Birmingham. Wave got to do something about it and the next Labour government will. So we’ll say to private developers, you can’t just sit on land and refuse to build. We will give them a very clear message - either use the land or lose the land, that is what the next Labour government will do. We’ll say to local authorities that they have a right to grow, and neighbouring authorities can’t just stop them. We’ll identify new towns and garden cities and we’ll have a clear aim that by the end of the parliament Britain will be building 200,000 homes a year, more than at any time in a generation. That’s how we make Britain better than this. • He said Labour did rescue the NHS. When we came to office there were waiting time targets of 18 months that were not being met, when we left office there were waiting time targets of 18 weeks that were being met. When we came to office there was an annual winter A&E crisis, when we left office the people had A&E services they could rely on. When we came to office there were fewer doctors and nurses, we when left office more doctors and nurses than ever before. And when we came to office people said well the health service, it was a good idea in previous generations but I don’t really believe it will be there in the next, and we left office with the highest public satisfaction in the history of the health services. Yes friends, we did rescue the National Health Service. • He said he would happily fight David Cameron on the question of leadership and character. But the next election isn’t just going to be about policy. It is going to be about how we lead and the character we show. I have got a message for the Tories today: if they want to have a debate about leadership and character, be my guest. And if you want to know the difference between me and David Cameron, here’s an easy way to remember it. When it was Murdoch versus the McCanns, he took the side of Murdoch. When it was the tobacco lobby versus the cancer charities, he took the side of the tobacco lobby. When it was the millionaires who wanted a tax cut versus people paying the bedroom tax, he took the side of the millionaires. Come to think of it, here is an even easier way to remember it: David Cameron was the Pr ime Minister who introduced the bedroom tax, I’ll be the Prime Minister who repeals the bedroom tax. You see here is the thing about David Cameron. He may be strong at standing up to the weak, but he is always weak when it comes to standing up against the strong. That is the difference between me and David Cameron, so let’s have that debate about leadership and character, and I relish that debate. Here's the full text of Ed Miliband's speech. I'll post the best quotes in a moment. Angela Knight, chief executive of Energy UK, the trade association for the energy body, told Sky News a few minutes ago that Ed Miliband's proposal could lead to energy companies pulling investment out of the UK. Possible the first thing that might happen is a bit more serious in some respects. Much of the money that is required to invest in energy in the UK comes from overseas. They will be looking at these sorts of statements and thinking do I go and invest this money in a country where I don't know if I'm going to get a return because there are price freezes around, or do I go and invest it in a country which perhaps thinks in a rather different manner. Nigel Farage, the Ukip leader, has responded to Ed Miliband's speech. (He didn't like it.) The Labour leader’s speech was an extraordinary confection promising Britain a return to the divided, bankrupt country of the late seventies. Economically, environmentally and constitutionally inept and destructive, at over an hour, it may the longest political valedictory speech in our history .... [His plans] to freeze gas prices until 2017, without saying how he could possibly achieve this in a free market while adhering to the carbon cutting craze that is forcing energy prices up by channelling vast amounts of money into unreliable, inefficient green technologies such as wind. Labour’s idea to create an Energy Security Board does little more than bring back memories of candles after dark and burning braziers at street corners. Here's Guardian Comment is free panel verdict, with contributions from Diane Abbott, Jackie Ashley, John McDonnell, Melissa Kite, Hopi Sen and David Clark. From Craig Bennett, Friends of the Earth's policy and campaigns director Freezing energy bills will certainly be welcomed by cash-strapped households, who face rocketing prices due to the soaring cost of gas and bumper energy company profits. The only way to deliver an energy system we can all afford is by urgently investing in an ambitious and comprehensive energy efficiency programme and weaning the nation off increasingly costly fossil fuels - and this must be a priority for an incoming Labour Government. Ed Miliband was right to put the environment at the heart of his conference speech - he must now put it firmly at the centre of Labour's policy-making to realise his goal of one million new green jobs. Ed Davey, the Lib Dem energy secretary, has put out a statement about Ed Miliband's plan to freeze energy bills. He suggests that it could lead to power cuts. Everyone wants to help with the cost of rising bills, which is why Liberal Democrats have cut income tax by £700 for working people. But Labour’s plan is a promise that won’t work. When they tried to fix prices in California it resulted in an electricity crisis and widespread blackouts. We can’t risk the lights going out here too. Fixing prices in this way risks blackouts, jeopardises jobs and puts investment in clean, green technology in doubt. The Institute of Directors does not like the speech. This is from Simon Walker, the IoD's director general, on the plan to penalise developers if they do not build on land with planning permission. The obstacle to house building is invariably the bureaucratic nightmare of trying to get a project off the ground. Taxing developers who are not building is no substitute for simply making it easier to build. This approach risks making it even less attractive for developers to build, whilst ignoring the urgent need for planning liberalisation. And this is what he says about the proposal to freeze energy bills. We should think very, very carefully before piling more distortion on an already grossly distorted energy market. Price controls only add greater uncertainty to companies who we need to take the financial risks of energy investment. It was also a great shame not to hear anything about the potential benefits of domestic shale gas exploration, which must feature as part of the UK’s long-term energy strategy. From Frances O'Grady, general secretary of the TUC This was a defining speech that rightly focussed on the living standards crisis and went on to offer hope. At its heart was a clear break from the view that Britain succeeds by reducing the rights, pay and prospects of people at work. More importantly the clear pledges made today and during the rest of this week showed that there will be a real choice at the next election – no-one can now say that the parties are all the same. The right were never going to like this speech. Here is reaction from two groups on the libertarian fringe of politics. From Matthew Sinclair, chief executive of the TaxPayers' Alliance When the Government fixes prices it always ends in a disaster for consumers. Ed Miliband is sticking by the green taxes and expensive subsidies that drive up the price of energy, so at best this new policy would just store up massive price hikes for another day. At worst it could create a crisis and force the Government to bail out the sector. If he really wants to help families struggling with their bills, he should promise that the next Labour Government would scrap the taxes and regulations that will otherwise keep making energy much more expensive for British consumers. From Philip Booth, editorial director at the Institute for Economic Affairs Ed Miliband clearly does not understand the root cause of rising living costs. His speech set out an array of government interventions. It is precisely these kind of interventions which have seen the price of food, energy and housing reach such burdensome levels. His tinkering policies will do little for families struggling to make ends meet. Unfortunately the Labour Party has not recognised that the most effective way to reduce living costs and unleash economic growth is to reduce taxes and ease regulation. In his speech Ed Milband defended England's union with Scotland with a story about a Labour delegate from Glasgow having to receive emergency hospital treatment in Liverpool. The SNP's Angus Robertson thinks trying to use the NHS to make a unionist point will backfire. For Ed Miliband to use the NHS as a reason to attack independence shows an astonishing lack of knowledge both about the National Health Service and about Scotland. The reality is that Scotland is already independent in terms of health policy, and it is precisely because NHS Scotland comes under the remit of the Scottish Parliament, rather than Westminster, that we are able to protect and develop the founding ethos of our health service, in stark contrast to the privatisation and fragmentation happening south of the border. This was a real blunder by Mr Miliband which plays right into the hands of the case for Yes, because it demonstrates that the best decisions for Scotland are decisions made in Scotland. And as experts have confirmed, exactly the same cross-border healthcare arrangements that NHS Scotland is obliged to have now with counterparts in the rest of the UK and overseas would apply in an independent Scotland. And here's some more union reaction. From Paul Kenny, leader of the GMB It was a very polished performance, with the welcome theme that Britain can do better. Millions of people know that life can be better. That was a completely different Labour leader to the one we saw three years ago. No prompts, no plastic performance - he has grown into being a prime minister. From Steve Murphy, general secretary of construction union Ucatt Our current housing crisis is a result of a failure to build a sufficient number of homes especially council housing for decades. This is the first time that a political party is seriously addressing this crisis which is affecting Britain socially and economically. From Lord Kinnock, the former Labour leader A brilliant speech, wonderfully delivered and, most importantly, I thought it was fundamental, practical patriotism that will resonate in the country and it's really what Ed means. From Dave Prentis, the Unison general secretary [Miliband] showed he understands what people are going through. He offered firm commitments to help hard-pressed families. He gave a vision for the many, not the few. From Joss Garman, deputy political director of Greenpeace As energy secretary, it was Ed Miliband who introduced new pollution rules for power stations and pushed for more ambition at the global climate talks in Copenhagen, so it was good to see him once again putting climate change back at the heart of his pitch to the country. His promise that his government would completely remove carbon pollution from the energy system and freeze energy bills will mean he has to face down the powerful energy companies, but public opinion is on his side. Together with his pledge to create a million new green jobs, he has set a new bar for the coalition parties to meet. From John Longworth, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce Ed Miliband is right to say that Britain should be in a 'race to the top'. While it was refreshing to hear a leader speak about major economic issues, he did not set out a clear vision to move Britain's economy from good to great. What's more, his speech contained more sticks than carrots for business. The way to have a high-wage, high-value economy is to encourage business investment and to build an enterprise-friendly environment. While some of his proposals pass this test, many others do not. What's more, he has made significant spending commitments, but it is not clear how the country will create the wealth required to pay for them. From Peter Hain, the Labour former cabinet minister It was a barnstorming speech, a virtuoso performance. I don't know any other politician in Britain who can deliver without a note that kind of rousing speech of that length of time and tell a story about what Ed Miliband's Britain will be like - a fairer Britain and more prosperous Britain for everybody. From curbing electricity and gas prices to cutting business rates to small businesses to abolishing the reviled 'bedroom tax', all of these policies - building hundreds of thousands of more houses - will resonate with every one of the concerns in every household in Britain. From John Cridland, the CBI director general Businesses will view the proposals on tax and energy as a setback for Labour's pro-enterprise credentials. Rising energy bills are tough on families and businesses, but the proposed energy price freeze will deter much-needed investment and is at odds with Labour's pledge to decarbonise the economy and create a million green jobs. The main reasons that bills are going up is the combination of rising wholesale prices, the cost of policies needed to keep the lights on and the move to a low-carbon economy. Here's Len McCluskey, the Unite leader, on the speech. People at home urgently wanting to know what a Labour government will do for them will certainly have a better idea today. They will know that this is a leader and a party that is on their side. Ed Miliband should never be afraid to articulate a courageous vision for this nation because that is what these extraordinary, fearful times urgently need. Voters are hungry for something better for Britain than foodbanks, tattered wages and Wonga-nomics. They want to hear about homes, that their children will have a future in this country, about a fair day's wage for a fair day's work. They need Labour to stand for a better life for the people of this country than the worry and insecurity this miserable government offers. It was good to hear Ed mention the role of unions positively in delivering the minimum wage, challenging in-work poverty. This is what we do day-in, day-out. Here are the key points from the speech. • Miliband says Labour would freeze gas and electricity bills for 20 months after 2015 general election. • 16 and 17-year-olds would be given the vote. • By 2020 200,000 new homes would be built a year. • Business rates would be cut for small businesses. • All primary schools would have breakfast clubs or after-school clubs. • Minimum wage would be strengthened. Here is some Twitter reaction from journalists and commentators. The energy companies have been reacting to the speech already. Snap Verdict: Labour delegates wanted a biggie and they got one. Many policies announcements have little "cut through" with the public, but the energy price freeze surprise at the heart of the speech will probably do the trick. Even if the Conservatives retaliate somehow (as they surely will), it will appeal to voters, and it will signal a determination to use the power of the state to strong-arm corporate Britain in a way the coalition (or New Labour) could not or would not match. Miliband was not using notes, and the speech was more conversational than a conventional leader's conference. But that worked, and may impress people who have doubts about Miliband's power as a communicator. The speech was also rich in hope and optimism (unlike much of what has been heard from the conference platform this week), and his character challenge to David Cameron was the most effective he has delivered. More later. But first I will round up some other reaction .... Our sentiment tracker showed a steady climb over the course of Ed Miliband's speech, with just one major dip into negative territory, at around 2.45pm, possibly around the time Miliband said Labour would not be able to spend money it does not have. PO We had a few internet problems here, and I may have missed some, but I think Ed Miliband only said three of our Milibingo terms: one nation, cuts and unions. I didn't hear any of the others: infighting, recovery, credibility, unemployment, macho or coalition. Let me know in the comments if I'm wrong. PO Miliband says that when people said at the dawn of the industrial revolution that Britain could be better than this, they were right. Those that said at the darkest hour, during the war, that Britain could be better than this, and they did. And the same applied when the battle for equality was being fought at the end of the last century. Britain can be better than this, he says. He will lead a government for Britain. Miliband says there is going to be a big fight in the run up to the general election. Think about the people, he says. I don't want to win this fight for Labour. I want to win it for Britain. Miliband condemns the Home Office for sending the "go home" vans to areas where immigrant families have lived for years. We are better than that, Miliband says. Miliband has a message for the Tories. If they want a debate about leadership and character, "be my guest". When there was a choice between Murdoch and the McCanns, Cameron sided with Murdoch. He sided with the tobacco industry. And he cut taxes for millionaires. And he was the PM who introduced the bedroom tax. Miliband will be the PM who abolishes it. Cameron may be strong at standing up to the weak. But he is weak at standing up to the strong. Miliband has a story about Scotland. Cathy Murphy lives in Glasgow. She was ill. But she came to the Labour conference in Liverpool in 2011. She fell ill, and had to have a serious operation. She had it at a world-leading hospital in Liverpool. She has to return every six months for a check-up. The doctors do not worry that she is from Scotland. They care for her anyway. He invites the audience to applaud. And he says he wants the Liverpool hospital to carry on treating patients like Cathy. Miliband turns to devolution. Devolution works, he says. Carwyn Jones is a brilliant first minister of Wales. And Johann Lamont is doing a brilliant job fighting the SNP. Miliband says he would like to hear more from young people. It was gay and lesbian young people who led the fight for gay marriage. And young women are saying the battle for equality is not won. They are not satisfied that 30% of Labour MPs are women. They want it to be 50%. And they are right. And they are not satisfied that women are still subject to violence and harassment. And they are right. Let's give the vote to 16 and 17-year-olds, he says. Miliband says he wants ordinary workers to be at the heart of the party. Leadership matters. But in the end change comes from the bottom. He wants to make Labour a mass membership party. Let's make ourselves truly the people's party again. Unions: Labour’s best frenemies. PO Miliband says he has explained how Labour can rescue the economy and society. Now he wants to talk about rescuing politics. It is the bit they have all been looking forward to - party reform. Change is difficult, he says. But Labour cannot just be a party of 200,000. It should be a party of 500,000, or 600,000. It is possible because of the unique link with the unions. Miliband says he does not want to end that link. He wants to mend that link. Miliband says Labour really did rescue the NHS. The government is responsible for what is wrong with it now. And Labour has to rescue the NHS all over again. (This gets what seems to be the largest round of applause of the speech.) Miliband says Labour does not believe in a race to the bottom. It believes in a race to the top. (Another glitch. I've missed a bit.) Miliband says the next Labour government should deal with the scourge of low pay. The minimum wage has been falling in values. Labour must do something about this. Some sectors, like banking, pay the living wage. But other sectors need to pay more. That's why government will strengthen the minimum wage. Miliband says firms will have to provide apprenticeships if they want to hire skilled foreign workers. And companies that train their workers will be able to "call time" on their competitors who do not do this. Miliband says to win the race to the top Britain has to support small businesses. New jobs will come from small firms. In the future most of the new jobs will come from firms that don't yet exist. The government has cut taxes for big businesses, but put them up for big ones. He says that's not right. Labour would take money from big business to cut taxes for small businesses. It will cut business rates for them in 2015, and freeze them for the next year. One nation Labour stands for small businesses. Cuts: Ed will cut small business rates, he says. PO Miliband turns to the environment. In 20 years' time his children will be asking if his was the last generation not to get climate change, or the first to get it. George Osborne says it's a matter of what you can afford. He's right. Britain cannot afford not to take climate change seriously. Miliband says Britain should be in a race to the top. But Labour is not going to be able to do this easily. You think opposition is tough? Government is harder. Labour will not be able to spend money it does not have. And, if he said it would, people would not believe him anyway. Miliband says it is not the low achievements of the Tories that annoy him. It is their low aspirations for Britons. The Tories are only interested in their friends at the top. Think of Lord Howell, the Tory adviser on fracking. He said it was wrong to frack in some places, but okay in others, like the north east, which is "desolate". Let's tell people about the north east. People love their kids there, they go out to work, they are proud of their communities, they look after their neighbours. The Tories call them desolate. We call them friends, and the heroes of our country. Miliband says he would advise the British public not to even take a second look at a party unless they acknowledge this. We can do better than this. Do the Tories get it? He invites the audience to respond. They shout no. Miliband says when he was growing up living standards were rising. People could afford to go on holiday for the first time. They used to say a rising tide lifts all boats. Now a rising tide just seems to lift the yachts. We need to understand how we got here. This bit is the most important thing he will say today, he says. For generations, as the economy grew, everyone benefited. But then the link between growth and the majority broke. This is more important to people even than who is in power, he says. (I've missed a bit, because my computer crashed, but I'm up now.) Miliband has just told stories of meeting people who like Britain, but who believe Britain can do better than this. Britain can do better than this, he says (twice). And Britain will be better than this, he says (again, twice). Mililband is now on to the political challenge. Britain can do better than this ... We're Britain, we're better than this. Are you satisfied with a country where people are working harder for less? People listen only to the powerful. Miliband is not satisfied. We're Britain.We're better than this. One nation: he stole it from a Tory, and he’s never giving it back. It’s Miliband’s catchphrase: one nation. PO She said Ed was attractive. He was not geeky, she said. And she said he was dressed casually "and had style". Miliband was pleased with this. But then he found out she was concussed. Anyway, he says to her: "You've made my year." Ed Miliband starts by thanking someone from the bottom of his heart. Not Justine - though he does want to thank her too. And not his mum. But a woman called Ella Phillips. She rode past him on his bike. She fell off. He helped her. And she said he was "an action hero." Ed Miliband has arrived in the hall. On the BBC Andrew Neil asks Nick Robinson asks if Ed Miliband will announce a 20-month energy freeze. Robinson says he has been told various things on an embargoed basis. But there will be an announcement about energy, he says. A random selection of Labour tweets from the hall ... Lord Ashcroft, the former Tory deputy chairman, is in the hall to watch the speech. (He has been speaking at a fringe meeting.) He's been tweeting. You can watch the speech live at the top of this page - and register what you think of it by pressing plus and minus on our "sentiment tracker". Just refresh the page to see them both. Ed Miliband will be speaking shortly. He is expected on stage at around 2.15pm. He is speaking without notes, and the party is not releasing a text in advance. That means that I will have to blog it in reported speech. Overnight Labour briefed two announcements from the speech, the house building plans and the business rate cuts for small firms. But we're expecting something more. The BBC's Andrew Neil has just posted this on Twitter. • Lord Adonis, the Labour former transport secretary who is leading Ed Miliband's growth review, has urged the party not to abandon the HS2 high-speed rail project. This is what he said at a Progress fringe event. We cannot as a party preach long-termism and not practice it ourselves. We have got to be very clear about that. We are the party that started HS2. I published the plan three and a half years ago. We set the whole thing out, we set out the rationale, including capacity. You have got to in politics, you cannot say that your principles and all that apply to other people but they don't apply to you when short term political advantage might rear its head. We have got to stick with this. It's very important. • Organisations representing large businesses have criticised Miliband's plan to cut business rates for small firms by abandoning a planned corporation tax cut for large firms. The CBI said the proposal undermined Labour's pro-enterprise credentials and the Institute for Directors said it was "a dangerous move for Labour to risk our business-friendly environment". But the Federation of Small Businesses and the British Retail Consortium welcomed the move. • Maria Eagle, the shadow transport secretary, has said Labour would put the interests of rail passengers before profit to "end the racket on our railways". As the Press Association reports, Eagle highlighted a range of proposals to stop customers feeling like the system is "always trying to rip you off" and said Labour would introduce a "new deal for passengers". Among the changes Labour would bring in is a drive to end the confusion around peak and off-peak tickets, changing the law to make sure passengers are offered the cheapest ticket no matter where or how the buy it, and ending the inflexibility on advance tickets which means passengers cannot get the following train even if it is empty. • Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary, has said energy companies would be forced to do their business in an open market under Labour. As the Press Association reports, she said if Labour won power in 2015, the party would prevent power companies doing their trades behind closed doors. She said there is currently no incentive for energy companies to keep prices down, telling the Labour Party conference in Brighton that power stations would no longer be run by the companies that supply gas and electricity directly to consumers. • Diana Holland, Labour's treasurer, has suggested that Miliband's reform to the union link could damage the party's finances. She delivered the warning in her speech to the conference. There are also important discussions about reforming membership and affiliation to the party. In all the discussions going forward, and about finding the best way, as your party treasurer I hope that whatever your point of view, everyone bears in mind the need to stick to the proven success of our party's finance strategy ... We cannot and must not jeopardise the hard-won financial stability of this party. Let us never forget as we develop the next steps for our party two very important truths - one, that the principles and values that unite us are far greater than the tactical and organisational differences that can divide us. • ITV have released a ComRes poll showing that 61% of people say Miliband does not come across as an election winner. Some 17% said he did, and 22% were not sure. • Gordon Brown was initially unimpressed by Barack Obama, Damian McBride has claimed in his memoirs. As Paul Owen reports, the book by the controversial former political adviser was published on Tuesday after a high-profile serialisation in the Daily Mail, with McBride's revelations about how he smeared Labour ministers gaining the most attention. But Power Trip also contains revelations about Brown's views of Obama, David Cameron, Rupert Murdoch – and the Mail's own editor, Paul Dacre. • Iain Dale, McBride's publisher, has been involved in a scuffle with a protester distracting attention from an interview that McBride was conducting. Paul Owen will be moonlighting as a bingo caller throughout Ed Miliband’s speech. He writes: The Guardian’s ruthless literary satirist John Crace has identified nine key words and phrases Ed Miliband is likely to weave deftly into his speech this afternoon. Click here for your interactive bingo card and you can tick them off when he does so. Here’s the full list: • One nation • Infighting • Recovery • Credibility • Cuts • Unemployment • Unions • Macho • Coalition I'll be following the speech and calling the words in this blog, as well as @gdnpolitics. Click here to get your bingo card. Good luck. Here's some more reaction to the plans being announced by Ed Miliband today. Cutting business rates for small firms From Helen Dickinson, director general of the British Retail Consortium The UK business rates are the highest property taxes of any EU country and lead directly to vacant shops. A consensus is emerging that the system is no longer fit for purpose and requires total reform. We therefore welcome this focus on supporting small businesses and high streets and the recognition that the cost of business rates has become unsustainable for retailers. With more than one in ten shops currently empty across the country, a complete reform of the system is required. We are keen to discuss these proposals in detail with Labour to understand how they might form part of this more fundamental root and branch review. From Alexander Jackman, head of policy at the Forum of Private Business There has been a lot of talk on the cost of living at this conference so we are pleased that Ed Miliband will use some of his speech today to highlight some of the rising costs of doing business. Taking money from a proposed business tax cut to fund a different business tax cut isn't an ideal way to fund the policy but importantly it recognises a significant rising cost facing many British small businesses. Building 200,000 new homes a year From Mike Quinton, chief executive of the National House Building Council, a body that sets standards for new homes. We welcome Ed Miliband's commitment to support the UK house building industry. Despite encouraging signs this year of a broad based recovery, there remains a critical shortage of new homes being built. Labour is right to focus on policies that will support the building of more new high quality homes and NHBC will work with all Parties to develop good housing policy. However, there is still some way to go before house building returns to pre-recession levels. As part of the Labour plan to promote more house building, developers would be penalised if they did not build homes on land granted planning permission. (See 11.49am.) At a fringe meeting at the conference, Sir Steve Bullock, London Councils' executive member of housing, backed the call for a crackdown on "landbanking". It’s frankly unacceptable that developers are hoarding land which could be used to build much needed homes for Londoners. With London’s population set to top nine million by 2021, we need to think radically about policy options to boost supply. Cracking down on landbanking could add 30,000 homes to London’s housing stock. London Councils say that in July 2013 there were 124,247 homes in London with agreed planning permission that had not been built. And the final shadow cabinet speaker this morning was Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary. The text of her speech is on the Labour website. Here are the main points. • Flint said Labour would introduce the "most radical, comprehensive reforms since energy privatisation" into the energy market. The big six energy firms would be broken up, she said. The days where a company generates energy, sells it to themselves … and then sells it to us … Those days will end ... Just as the banks will have to separate their investment and trading arms from the high street branches, so we will make the energy companies separate their production from the companies that supply your home. • She said Labour would make energy bills clearer. And let me say one more thing about the bills you will receive… Under Labour, on every bill you will see one standing charge and one unit price. Simple. Straightforward. Easy to compare. Easy to switch. • She said Labour would set up an energy security board "to identify our energy needs, secure investment for the future and keep the lights on". Maria Eagle, the shadow transport secretary, gave what may have been the best received speech of the morning. On policy, it was relatively meaty. The full text is on the Labour website, and here are the main points. • Eagle confirmed that Labour would impose a "proper cap" on rail fares. • She identified a string of measures that could be introduced to end what she described as "the racket on our railways". But we need a new deal for passengers too. No more talk of Super Peak fares, meaning your season ticket wouldn’t even be valid on every train. No more stretching peak time, when it’s actually about stretching profits. No more confusing tickets, but the exact time you can use it printed on the ticket. No more inflexibility when you book in advance, so you can’t get the next train – even when it’s empty. And if you do have the wrong ticket on the train, take off the price you’ve already paid from the cost of a new one. No more single and return journeys costing the same. Not just in one pilot area after 2015, as the government plans, but across the network. No more charging more at the ticket office than online, just to provide another excuse to close them. No more rip offs at ticket machines, but a new legal right to be offered the cheapest fare regardless of how or where you buy a ticket. No more inflation-busting increases in the cost of leaving your car at the station, when it’s just another way to clobber commuters. No more ripping people off with internet charges, just because you can’t afford to travel First Class. And isn’t it time that all trains had wifi in the 21st century? So let’s require it in franchises. • She said that Labour in principle supported HS2. This is what was in the text. So, as Ed Balls rightly says: we support the idea of a new north-south rail line but, if costs continue to rise – and the value for money cannot be demonstrated, we will have to ask if this is the right priority for £50billion pounds. But, as Rob Merrick points out, she added a line. • She identified a string of measures that could be introduced to boost cycling. And she said that she supported what Norman Baker, the Lib Dem transport minister, was trying to do on this. But Baker was being blocked by the Tories, she said. [Baker's] tried to get his Tory bosses to take cycling seriously. But while they’ve set out a plan to spend £28 billion on roads, he’s secured just £38million a year to support cycling. And conveniently forgotten the three wasted years that followed his decision to axe Cycling England and its £60million a year budget. Come off it, Norman: On ya bike. So, here’s what we need to do:clear goals to increase cycling. Separated routes. Redesigned junctions. Phased traffic lights. Cycling Safety Assessments for all new transport schemes. Restored targets to cut road deaths and serious injuries. Duties to support Active Travel, as Labour introduced in Wales. 20mph zones, the default in residential areas. Long term support for teaching safe cycling. Space on trains. Secure facilities at stations – required in rail contracts. Sentencing guidelines reviewed. Tough new rules on HGVs. Supporting cycling. Increasing numbers. Improving safety. • She said she wanted all InterCity trains to look the same. (Presumably this means that Virgin Trains' red carriages would get a repaint under Labour.) So, instead of all this waste, let’s reduce costs in our railway. Save money by bringing a fragmented industry together. With responsibilities currently spread across the Transport Department and multiple separate bodies, brought within a reformed and more accountable Network Rail. Save money by ending wasteful repainting and rebranding of trains and stations with every new contract. Restore a coherent InterCity identity to national train services, regardless of public or private operator. In his speech to the conference earlier Hilary Benn, the shadow communities secretary, set out more details of Labour's plans to build more homes. The text is on the Labour website. Here's an extract. First, we must admit that we can’t carry on saying on the one hand “where are the homes for the next generation?” and on the other “please don’t build them near me”. Nor will we get more homes by top-down targets. Councils and communities must take that responsibility but they need more power to be able to do so. Communities should know where land is available. That’s why we will ensure developers register the land they own or have options on. And where land is not brought forward for homes, communities should be able to do something about it. And when communities have given planning permission they should be able to say to developers: we’ve given you the go ahead so please get on and build the homes you said you would. And if you don’t then we’ll charge you and, if you still don’t, we’ll sell the land on to someone else who will. Secondly, there are areas in the country where councils and communities see the need for more homes but there just isn’t the land to build them on. So the next Labour government will give those communities a new ‘Right to Grow’, allowing them – if they want – to expand and ensuring that neighbouring areas work with them to do so. Thirdly, conference, it’s time to build new communities – new towns and new garden cities. That’s what the great Attlee Government did as they started to rebuild Britain and we need that same spirit again. So we will invite local authorities to come forward, and in return, we will make sure that they get the powers and the incentives they need to acquire land, put in the infrastructure and build. Build those new communities. You can read all today's Guardian Labour party conference coverage here. As for the rest of the papers, here are some articles I found particularly interesting. • Janan Ganesh in the Financial Times (subscription) explains the difference between Ed Balls and Ed Miliband. Mr Balls is a conventional social democrat. He believes the market is the least bad way of making economic decisions. He wants to tax and spend its proceeds for egalitarian ends – lavishly, for sure – but does not aspire to interfere with its workings. He does not fancy himself wiser than the price mechanism. He is a leftwinger who has read his Adam Smith. Mr Miliband is more corporatist. He thinks the left over-relies on redistribution. He wants to intervene earlier – to “predistribute” – by making businesses narrow pay differentials, train workers and empower unions. He is happier to second-guess the market. This intellectual difference between the two men manifests in a thousand ways. Mr Balls values the City as a financial centre and source of revenue; Mr Miliband seems to regard it as a barely necessary evil. Mr Balls would expand London’s airport capacity to meet demand; Mr Miliband prefers the environmental case against. Mr Balls tends not to sermonise about what the economy “should” look like; Mr Miliband scolds betting shops, payday lenders and other “predatory” businesses. Even in their political styles, Mr Miliband is abstract and Mr Balls remorselessly practical. In the arcane typology of the British left, Mr Miliband is “pamphlet Labour”, all discursive academia and moral certitude, whereas Mr Balls brings the campaigning grit of “leaflet Labour”. Only by grasping these differences can anyone appreciate Mr Balls’ usefulness to his party. • Rachel Sylvester in the Times (paywall) thinks Ed Miliband is still bound by loyalty to his father. It is no coincidence that Ralph Miliband’s gravestone in Highgate Cemetery, which stands just a few yards from Karl Marx’s tomb, is engraved with the words: “Writer Teacher Socialist”. Indeed, according to a family friend, one reason Ed decided to stand against his brother David for the Labour leadership was because he thought he would protect his father’s political legacy more faithfully than his more Blairite sibling. Just as Ralph emphasised the importance of the Left working outside Parliament to create a “new social order in which democracy, egalitarianism and co-operation” took hold, so Ed wants a “new politics” based in communities rather than the Commons to bring about his One Nation ideal. The influence of the older Miliband — who opposed regime change through military intervention, even the overthrow of the Pol Pot regime in Cambodia — may also be evident in his son’s decision to block action in Syria. Although his head knows that he must stay in the centre, Mr Miliband’s heart beats to the left. Socialism, he has said, is a “response to injustice” and “a tale that never ends”. He fears betraying his father, whom he describes as his “lodestar”, a man whose approval both he and his brother have always craved. • Benedict Brogan in the Telegraph says Labour figures are worried that the Tories are beating them at spin. Every conversation in Brighton, it seems, includes an admission that the Conservatives are winning the propaganda war hands down. If Craig Oliver and Lynton Crosby could hear what the shadow cabinet are saying in the bars, they would blush: “Their attack unit is ruthless. Their messaging is consistent and disciplined. They are beating us.” This is not something said often in Westminster – but for the past six months or so, the Labour high command has been noticing that a widespread perception of Tory incompetence lags behind the reality. The Conservatives may complain about their own machine, but the Opposition is arguably the better judge. And they are definitely hurting. There is no argument that Labour has had a terrible summer. Doubts about its leader are, if anything, strengthening. The party rages that it has the better arguments on a wealth of issues – but that the Tories are more adept at distorting events to suit their purposes. There is even talk of creating a rapid-rebuttal unit, modelled on the one that helped Bill Clinton to his first win in 1992. • Owen Jones in the Independent writes the speech he thinks Miliband should deliver. • Laura Pitel in the Times (paywall) says Unite is boycotting the Labour gala dinner. Ed Miliband’s biggest trade union backer is to boycott his gala dinner in the latest rebuff to the Labour leader. For the first time in years, Unite will not pay for a table at tonight’s event, where Mr Miliband is guest of honour. Nor is it holding its usual conference party. A source at the union said that Unite was taking a low-key approach in Brighton, adding: “We’re not sulking, just watching.” It is partly a protest at the growing corporatisation of the conference, which has become an important money-spinner for Labour. The symbolism is clear, however, with tension between Mr Miliband and Len McCluskey, the Unite General Secretary, over the Falkirk row. • Oliver Wright in the Independent explains how party conferences have become glorified trade fairs. Electoral Commission records show why, exposing just how lucrative this source of income has becomes for our political parties. In their most recent accounts the Conservatives reveal that they raised just £747,000 in membership fees last year – but £4.2m from conferences. Labour raked in £3.37m from so called “commercial activities”, including conferences, while even the Liberal Democrats raised £1.4m from events. One senior party official admitted that what was once a forum to debate and decide party policy has now become little more than a “glorified trade fair” with almost as many lobbyists as delegates. The truth is that while big donations to political parties have been cleaned up and made more transparent, the same cannot be said for the money made at party conferences. These companies and lobbyists do not pay for passes or fund events out of the goodness of their hearts: they are buying access and the possibility of influence. In the conference, in the living standards debate, a delegate has just given a speech in her dressing gown. She was talking about a protest against the "bedroom tax" involving people wearing their dressing gowns. David Sparks, leader of the Labour group on the Local Government Association and leader of Dudley council, spoke in the conference hall earlier. He said it was only a matter of time before some councils in the UK went bust. I've served 37 years [in local government] ... I can honestly say that the situation that faces local government now has never been more precarious in all that time. Under this government and under this secretary of state, if things carry on as they are, a conservative estimate, with a small c, will show that local councils have had a reduction, on average, overall, over the last four years, of 42% in their grants. The financial black hole facing local authorities is widening each year at the astronomical rate of £2.1bn. It is only a matter of time in this country before some councils cease to be viable. And if anyone thinks that's impossible, just look across the Atlantic to Detroit and see what can happen when a large council runs out of money. One section of Damian McBride’s memoir Power Trip confirms that “Gordon considered the Queen more important than [Rupert] Murdoch” – which is reassuring, sort of, writes Paul Owen. When a Treasury civil servant accidentally sent a “daft joke about the Chinese” to the entire Treasury press list, Brown was worried about “relations with the Chinese – not least Rupert Murdoch’s then-wife Wendi Deng” and felt “the Treasury should show no mercy”. But after McBride reminded him that this civil servant wrote Brown’s annual note on horse-racing for his audience with the Queen, Brown’s position shifted, “definitive proof that Gordon considered the Queen more important than Murdoch”. Murdoch gets one other mention: in the wake of the 7/7 attacks in London, “there were concerns at the highest level in the company – ie Uncle Rupert – about whether Gordon sufficiently grasped the seriousness of the security situation, and whether he could be trusted to maintain the strength of Blair’s relationship with the Bush administration”. Partly in response to this, Brown became “a whirlwind of activity” after the failed 21/7 attacks two weeks later, McBride writes. Damian McBride's publisher, Iain Dale, has been involved in a scuffle at the Labour conference. The Press Association has the full (and rather absurd) story. Verbal battles over Damian McBride's memoirs at the Labour conference escalated into actual fisticuffs today, as the ex-spin doctor's publisher stepped in to stop a protester disrupting the publicity drive for his controversial memoirs. Iain Dale, of Biteback Publishing, was involved in a scuffle with the man on the Brighton seafront in an effort to stop him appearing on screen behind McBride during a series of media interviews. The protester - a familiar face outside party conferences, where he regularly appears with banners opposing smoking or nuclear energy - managed to get himself into shot as McBride spoke to ITV1's Daybreak. But Dale decided to take action, grabbing the man's rucksack and physically hauling him out of the way of the cameras, and the pair grappled on the pavement as Mr McBride's interview continued. The barking of the protester's dog - which eagerly joined in the commotion - could be heard by TV viewers as the struggle continued. But the terrier - carrying placards reading "No Nukes" on its back - failed to live up to the loyalty expected from dogs, jumping up and biting its owner on the rear. The protester doggedly attempted to make the best of the situation, holding up his banner reading "No Nukes - Radio Active Dust Cancer Epidemic" to photographers who were busy recording the scrap. After a few moments, the pair separated and dusted themselves down, and the protester went back to trying to edge his way into view of the cameras. Writing on his blog, Dale - also a broadcaster on LBC 97.3 radio - joked: "I knew I shouldn't have had three Weetabix this morning." Dale has written more on his blog. Labour used to advocate a temporary cut in VAT as a means of stimulating the economy. But Ed Balls did not mention that in his speech yesterday, and at a fringe meeting last night he confirmed that the idea had been dropped. This wasn't a surprise. In a speech earlier this year he said that Labour proposed the idea when the economy was in recession and that, as the economy recovered, the balance of advantage would shift, and the case for other measures would grow stronger. The Lib Dems have claimed that this is "rank hypocrisy". This is from Stephen Williams, a Lib Dem Treasury spokesman. Labour have spent the last three years aggressively attacking the Coalition for raising VAT. Now they admit they won’t reduce it. This is rank hypocrisy from a party that has no answers to the big questions of the day – how to fix the economy, create jobs and help people with the cost of living. Paul Owen is reading Damian McBride’s memoir Power Trip, and he has found an anecdote about David Cameron that rather plays into his “chillaxing” image as a keen holidaymaker. McBride paints Cameron as rather callow after both Brown and Cameron had had to cancel their holidays because of the 2007 foot and mouth outbreak. During a phone call “Cameron asked Gordon almost pleadingly when he thought things would stabilise sufficiently so Gordon could go back to Dorset and Cameron could go back to Brittany. His exact words were: ‘I can’t go away until you do, and we won’t get a holiday at this rate. But you really have to go away first.’” Brown apparently responded to this call by telling McBride: “That was personal, that was private. You don’t say a word about that to anyone,” which was very saintly of him. Cameron betrayed this trust by giving a series of TV interviews criticising Brown based on information from the phone call, McBride claims. McBride says that from the moment Cameron emerged from the 2005 Tory leadership contest, he and Brown were worried. “If they [Cameron and George Osborne] were serious about modernising the Tory party; if they could succeed in neutralising the economy and public services as dividing line issues by agreeing with us on our tax and spending plans; and if – as was evident – they had the image and freshness to go with it, then they represented a real threat to Gordon’s chances of succeeding Tony, and then of winning any subsequent election.” Interestingly McBride says he rejected “the main Labour attack lines” of focusing on Cameron and Osborne’s poshness, preferring to try to paint them as “incompetent and weak”, and as “kids” who weren’t up to the job. That approach had a double value: they were “at the same time sending a not-so-subtle message to those on our own side who were giving David Miliband the glad eye”. (That approach eventually culminated in Brown’s “no time for a novice” jibe at both Cameron and Miliband.) But he praises Cameron for his response to the expenses scandal, saying the Tory leader “seemed remarkably well-prepared for the emergence of the story, and calm and decisive in his response”. Brown, by contrast, was “not only taken completely by surprise by the revelations, but also thrown into a total tailspin” by the Daily Telegraph’s decision to focus on him in its initial coverage. He attacks Cameron’s decision to block Brown as head of the IMF once he’d left Downing Street on interesting grounds, calling it not just partisan but “also tremendously short-sighted”. Why? Because “having Gordon run the IMF would have effectively neutralised the organisation’s influence on the 2015 election. Any criticism in the IMF’s reports on the UK economy could simply have been dismissed by Osborne with a ‘What do you expect?’ shrug.” The Federation of Small Businesses also likes the Labour business tax proposal. Here's the news release (pdf). And this is from John Allan, the federation's national chairman. The Federation of Small Businesses (FSB) welcomes the focus by the Labour leadership on this critical issue for small businesses. It affects thousands of our members across the UK and is an issue we've lobbied on for some time. Whilst we welcome today's announcement, the long-term solution is one that requires root and branch reform, including of the Valuation Office Agency. The system is clearly no longer fit for purpose. We know that many firms are struggling to pay business rates and FSB research suggests seven per cent of members pay more in rates than rent - and that cannot be right. In addition many small businesses are being forced by local authorities to pay the rates when it is beyond their means. But the Association of Convenience Stores likes Labour's business tax plans. This is from James Lowman, the ACS chief executive. Reducing business rates for smaller shops on high streets and in neighbourhoods would benefit tens of thousands of convenience stores. We will work with opposition and the Government to make proposals for tackling the rates burden workable. Immediate action is vital, but it must be accompanied by a commitment to a fundamental review of the business rates system. Paul Owen has got his copy of former Gordon Brown adviser Damian McBride’s memoir Power Trip, and he is busy speed-reading his way through it. Strangely the Daily Mail’s serialisation did not include McBride’s comments about that paper and its editor Paul Dacre. Indeed a Mail piece on Brown and the royal family misses out a few lines about McBride feeding a splash to the Mail about Brown wanting to fund a statue of the Queen Mother for the Mall. But disappointingly what McBride has to say about Dacre is quite tame. McBride compares Brown’s “very strong” relationship with Dacre with his relationship with Tony Blair – “bar all the shouting” – in that “because all their meetings took place behind closed doors … no one was ever quite sure how they interacted with each other in private, or how open, warm or personal they were with each other.” McBride says he never heard of any Mail journalist being instructed to “go soft” on Brown because of this relationship, and “Gordon never told me we that we were to put any particular scoops in the Mail’s direction”. He tells the story of Brown being invited into the Mail offices to tell its senior journalists about his ideas about Britishness, and McBride “couldn’t help but admire the way that Paul Dacre rocked back in his chair and let” his columnists attack Brown’s ideas, “as if thinking: ‘You want intellectual debate; here it is. You want to know what the Daily Mail thinks; we’re going to tell you.’” McBride does say Dacre “effectively scuppered one of my greatest plans”: a plan to cut the 30-year rule for releasing government papers to 15 years, and then rush out papers for all the years up to 1995 so that if Labour won the 2010 election promising to be “the only government ever to publish the secrets of our own time in office while still in power”. Brown asked Dacre to lead a review into the subject, but the eventual plan the Daily Mail chief came up with was slower and exempted the royal family, “which defeated some of the long-term news value”. And now the Institute of Directors has also criticised Labour's business tax announcement. This is from Simon Walker, the IoD's director general. This is better politics than it is economics, and it creates a false distinction between small and larger businesses when in reality, all companies benefit from a more competitive environment. Whilst the IoD welcomes any commitment to reduce the burden of business rates, Labour’s plan to finance this by abolishing the proposed cut in the main corporation tax rate is not a price worth paying. The main corporation tax rate is paid not only by multinational corporations and FTSE100 companies but by medium sized companies and smaller firms. The government has spent three years telling the world that we are open for business, and reductions in corporation tax have been a key part of that strategy. It’s a dangerous move for Labour to risk our business-friendly environment in this way, at the same time as announcing a new bank levy. Globally, corporation tax rates are falling and it is imperative that the UK has an attractive ‘headline’ corporation tax rate low enough to attract much needed foreign direct investment into the UK. This will reduce unemployment, increase economic growth and, as a consequence, increase tax revenues. Labour is going to eliminate the debt by June 2016. That's the party's debt, not the country's. Diana Holland, the party treasurer, is presenting a report to the conference now, and she has just said the party is scheduled to pay off its debt within three years. Another business leader has criticised the business tax announcement in Ed Miliband's speech. This is from John Longworth, the director general of the British Chambers of Commerce. Labour must realise that you can't rob Peter to pay Paul. Business rates are an iniquitous tax and a drag on business – hitting companies of all sizes long before they make profits. Ed Miliband is right to look for ways to ease the business rates burden, as the BCC has urged the Treasury to do for years now. Yet we question why a freeze or cut in business rates for smaller firms should be offset by a delayed reduction in corporation tax. To create an environment where companies can thrive, both business rates and corporation tax rates have to be contained, and the broken business rates system fundamentally reformed. The notion that you can offset cuts in one tax with changes to another doesn't deal with the real problem – the fact that the business rates system is bust and in need of reform. Ultimately, companies of all sizes need to be clear on taxes and rates bills, so that they can generate jobs and wealth with certainty. Sir John Armitt, who chaired the Olympic Delivery Authority and who conducted a review of long-term infrastructure planning for Labour, told the Today programme that he would be disappointed if Labour cancelled HS2. I would be very disappointed if that was happening in this case. I think we’ve had political consensus around HS2. It’s a very large project. It requires a political consensus frankly to be delivered at the end of the day and I think the value of HS2 to the country long-term is still absolutely certain and I think is understood by senior politicians in all the major parties. However, he also said that he did not think that what Ed Balls said about HS2 in his speech yesterday indicated that Labour was planning to scrap the scheme. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. For the record, here are today's YouGov GB polling figures. Labour: 40% (up 3 points from YouGov in the Sunday Times) Conservatives: 32% (down 1) Ukip: 12% (no change) Lib Dems: 10% (down 1) Labour lead: 8 points (up 4) Government approval: -27 (no change) According to Electoral Calculus, these figures would give Labour a majority of 100. If the Labour party thought that announcing a tax cut for small business would win the approval of the business community, it's in for a disappointment. John Cridland, the director general of the CBI, has just been on the Today programme, and he said he was not impressed. He complained that the cut in business rates for small firms was being funded by an increase in corporation tax for large firms (or, more accurately, the reversal of a planned cut). This sent out the wrong message, he said. Some of the tax changes that we hear that Mr Miliband will propose are not particularly pro-business. Reducing business rates for small companies is a good thing to do, but I would not do it by increasing taxes on investment, on research, on job creation, which is what will happen if you put up corporation tax ... I just think it's divisive to take from one part of the business community to give to another. The key point, though, is what it says about the Labour party's pro-enterprise credentials. It is giving a signal that taxes are going up for some businesses. Actually, we are fighting for our share of global markets. We are getting back to the notion that we earn our corn around the world by getting on planes to far-flung countries to export. Whether you are small, medium or large, you need to invest as a business and grow as a business, and higher taxes don't do that. Overnight the Conservative business minister, Matthew Hancock, put out a response to the stories about what Ed Miliband would be saying in his speech. Here's his statement. This is yet more economic incompetence from the same Labour politicians who got us into a mess. Ed Miliband is calling for a tax rise on business that would cost jobs. And because he would reverse our planning reforms, the only way Ed Miliband could deliver his promise to build more houses is to spend more and borrow more. Tax rises on businesses and the same old Labour policy of more borrowing and more debt would undermine the recovery. And it's hardworking people who would pay the price with their jobs, higher taxes and higher mortgage rates. Harriet Harman, Labour's deputy leader, has been giving interviews this morning. Here are the key points. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome and the Press Association. • Harman said Labour had been making good progress since Ed Miliband became leader. If you look at the actual polls where people are voting, since 2010, since we lost the general election, actually the momentum with Ed Miliband’s leadership has been with the Labour party. We have won 1,950 new councils seat and that’s all around the country, and if you look at actual party membership, which has been on a long trajectory of falling across all the political parties, since Ed Miliband became leader we’ve increased our membership by 14%, whereas with David Cameron his party membership has fallen by 40%. • She said Labour was announcing plans to help small firms because they were the "engine of the economy". It's a bit of a cliche but it's absolutely true that small business is the engine of the economy. And they have really struggled after the austerity and the credit crisis and they haven't been able to borrow money from banks, and people haven't had enough money in their pockets to keep small businesses going. A lot of small businesses say they are paying as much in their business rates as they pay in rent. They have suffered really more than big businesses and one of the problems with the cost of living for businesses is that business rates have gone up. So what we're doing is, instead of giving a cut for big companies on corporation tax, we're using that money instead to take that money off the cost of small businesses rates. • She said that Damian McBride could not be believed because he was a self-confessed liar. The one person who is not worth believing because he’s admitted he’s a liar is Damian McBride, so I don’t think we need to be told by him who did or didn’t know what’s going on. According to Nick Robinson on the BBC, delegates at the Labour conference are going to be given a "One Nation Economy" book at the end of Ed Miliband's speech summarising the party's growth policies. This week (as the end of Ed Balls' speech yesterday showed) Labour is very keen refute the claim that it does not have any ideas. Leaders of modern political parties need a suite of skills - being able to communicate well on TV, leadership, team management, authority at PMQs etc - and being able to deliver a powerful, set-piece speech is probably no longer an essential part of the job requirement. Very few people actually watch these speeches in full. But a powerful speech can still shape the political debate for months, or even longer, and, for all the sniping about Ed Miliband's leadership, conference speech-making has been one of his strengths. He served his political apprenticeship under Gordon Brown (a great believer in the importance of politics by speech-making) and his last two Labour conferences speeches have been unusually good. Most party conference speeches are quickly forgotten, but Miliband's 2011 speech, which included an attack on "irresponsible" capitalism, generated a real debate, and last year's speech launched "one nation Labour", a branding exercise that lifted party morale. Miliband is speaking this afternoon. Can he pull off another biggie? We've had some briefing on the speech already. Here's the start of Patrick Wintour's story in today's Guardian. Ed Miliband will propose on Tuesday to reform Britain's dysfunctional housing market by building 200,000 new houses a year by 2020, the end of the next parliament. The Labour leader's move is a response to a collapse in UK housebuilding, which has stoked house-price inflation, and will see him set up a commission designed to curtail the planning logjams preventing houses being built in sufficient numbers. Miliband will use his set-piece address to the Labour conference in Brighton to announce that Sir Michael Lyons, the respected local government finance expert and former BBC Trust chairman, will lead a review designed to ensure that an incoming government he leads will be able to swiftly draw up housing reform legislation. The Labour leader also intends to use his speech to ask voters to recognise that Britain can be better – and to ask themselves in 2015 whether they are better off than five years ago, an echo of the famous question asked by the Republican Ronald Reagan in the 1990 presidential election. I'll be covering the speech in detail, and, of course, all the reaction to it. 9.30am: Conference opens. Conference arrangements committee report. 9.40am: Diana Holland presents the party treasurer's report. 10.15am: Debate on stronger, safer communities, and living standards and sustainability, with speeches from Hilary Benn, the shadow communities secretary, Maria Eagle, the shadow transport secretary and Caroline Flint, the shadow energy secretary. 11.15am: Debate on Britain's global role. From 12.30pm: Fringe meetings, including Hilary Benn on Labour and the north, Arnie Graf, who is advising Labour on community organising, on winning votes, Maria Eagle and Ken Livingstone on transport, and Lord Glasman on Britishness. 2pm: Ed Miliband delivers his conference speech.
