Mr Irvine looks a bit thin lipped. If I was a betting person I'd venture that he's not entirely happy. But he declines to rise to the bait. He says only: The government follows a policy of not commenting on operational matters. I don't want to get into a discussion (of recent events). It's not appropriate for a public servant. One of my colleagues was downstairs a little while ago and caught one of those only in Canberra moments. The Opposition leader Tony Abbott was jogging past Aussies Cafe to go to the gym, as he sometimes does in the afternoon after Question Time. At one of the tables was Bob Ellis - the author whom Mr Abbott sued for defamation several years ago concerning some allegations in the Ellis book, Goodbye Jerusalem. There was, my informant noted, a slightly uncomfortable moment as the two crossed paths. Mr Ellis was having afternoon tea with a couple of ALP folks. He was heard to remark to his tea companions that today, "we'd reached a tipping point" concerning the man on his way to the gym. Presumably this was a reference to Mr Abbott's difficulties today on the political funding deal. Mr Ellis is a bit of a fixture around parliament on big political days - how he materialises at such moments is part of the Ellis mystery, or perhaps, mythology. Senator Brandis has now concluded his line of questioning. As we begin to wind down, here's a fun GIF of the Prime Minister's reaction to the sandwich toss at Lyneham High from earlier today - captured by Mike Bowers. Thanks to The Global Mail. There's a bit of stereo going on now. The Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison is, with colleagues, having a press conference to raise this case that we've referenced on the live blog this week of an Egyptian man held in low security detention in South Australia despite being the subject of an Interpol alert. The case has featured in Mr Abbott's Question Time attacks this week on Labor's national security credentials. Senator Brandis is also pursuing Mr Irvine on the same matter in the estimates committee. Mr Irvine says at no stage was this person let out of detention. Light touch assessment. Senator Brandis has picked up Mr Irvine on his description a moment ago of the nature of ASIO's security assessments. Mr Irvine says anyone who goes into community detention gets an ASIO assessment. They go through the triage process when they are on the track to get a protection visa. My figures say in 2010-11 about 11,000 people have received the full assessment, he says. Senator Brandis: We used to assess everyone who came in. Now he says the number of boat arrivals has gone up, and the number of threat assessments have gone down. That must create a greater risk that something is going to be missed. Mr Irvine says in reducing the number of people to be assessed ASIO uses rigorous criteria to determine who should get the additional assessment. Senator Brandis: But there are probalaistic judgments. Mr Irvine: Yes. Senator Brandis: There's a risk somebody is going to slip through. Mr Irvine: It is always a matter of risks and probabilities. But he says the triage system works to put intelligence resources in the right place. Mr Irvine is detailing current procedures for security assessments for would-be asylum seekers following the influx of boat arrivals over the past few years. He speaks of a triage system. The ASIO boss says 10 or 15 per cent of people heading towards a visa proceed to full threat assessment. It varies. That figure can go up and down. Mr Irvine says when there were less arrivals, there were more comprehensive threat assessments. He says thus far this financial year there have been about 3,000 assessments. Let's look in on a senate estimates hearing. ABC correspondent Naomi Woodley has been watching proceedings. Shadow Attorney-General George Brandis is asking questions of the ASIO head, David Irvine. The current topic is security assessments of asylum seekers. Not often stuck for words. This, however ... Apparently, all these fresh faced youngsters are in. The Prime Minister has placed further questions on the notice paper. We have a batch of personal explanations. Mr Abbott is contesting the Prime Minister's assertion during Question Time that he hasn't attended security briefings while Opposition leader. He lists all the dates of his security briefings. The Nationals leader Warren Truss is raising a matter of privilege. Mr Truss is objecting to a claim from Independent Tony Windsor that his opponent in the seat of New England, Senator Barnaby Joyce, has taken a donation from mining magnate Gina Rinehart. This isn't true, Mr Truss says. Surely this untruth is a breach of privilege? Mr Truss says Mr Windsor's statement was made with malicious intent. Speaker Anna Burke allows Mr Truss to table his supporting documents. Manager of government business Anthony Albanese, objects. What's the matter of privilege exactly? He says Mr Truss has enaged in the breach of process that he just complained about. Here's Mr Windsor's reaction, captured by Mike Bowers. Mr Pyne isn't sold on Mr Garrett's response. He moves a procedural motion requiring Mr Garrett to speak for three minutes in order to complete his answer. All those in favour, Speaker Anna Burke asks? (Loud ayes.) Against? (One, lone, no rings out in the chamber.) Everyone laughs. Mr Garrett shouts on for several minutes. Are we there yet? Shadow Treasurer Joe Hockey. Gracious. A portfolio question from Christopher Pyne. Normally Mr Pyne is more intent on his role as Manager of Opposition Business. Mr Pyne is asking about funding for a particular school in 2014. How much will MacKillop College get next year? Schools Minister Peter Garrett is pointing again. Jabbing with his finger. The indexation commitments included in the plan for school improvement ... will see additional investments flow from 2014. Now Ms Bishop has been tossed out of the chamber by Speaker Anna Burke. Ms Bishop is back with security checks and asylum seekers. How can a security check be adequate if a suspected terrorist is allowed to be held at a low security detention facility? The Prime Minister is going through the criteria for security assessments. Ms Gillard says a report from the intelligence committee that Mr Abbott has quoted from several times this week found the security system was adequate. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison, getting the early plane home. The Member for Wentworth does raise a very serious issue. The shadow communications spokesman Malcolm Turnbull is asking about the exposure of NBN workers to asbestos - a story we pointed blog readers to earlier today. The Prime Minister is responding. We take all of this very seriously. There are no shortcuts in dealing with asbestos. Ms Gillard says COMCARE is investigating to ensure safety standards are being met. She says the asbestos problem may cause delays to the roll out of the NBN in some sites. The NBN rollout wil continue in other places. Deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop is back with boats and security checks. Is the Prime Minister confident that Australia's light touch security checks for "illegal" boat arrivals are adequate? The Prime Minister: The government is satisfied by present arrangements. Ms Gillard has a blast at Mr Abbott on the way through for not asking the security question today. She refers to a media report this morning that suggested Mr Abbott had failed to avail himself of security briefings whilst Opposition leader (until his interest in them this week.) Woah. Schools Minister Peter Garrett. Scaring the children. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison has a question for the Prime Minister. Why are you making life hard for police who want information about asylum seekers? Mr Morrison has been pursuing this issue for the past couple of days. The Prime Minister tells Mr Morrison that work is underway and ongoing between federal immigration officials and NSW police on the matter to which he refers. Mr Morrison is having a shout, and the House of Representatives Speaker Anna Burke asks him to leave the chamber. Transport Minister Anthony Albanese is pursuing variations on a theme. The Coalition don't like public transport. Public broadcasters. Anything with the word public in it. They don't like the public. Off the economy. The asylum boats are back. Liberal Michael Keenan wishes to know why there's not enough police in Indonesia. The Prime Minister hints there might be an announcement on that subject soon. Speaker Anna Burke has, meanwhile, asked the Shadow Health Minister Peter Dutton to leave the chamber. Mr Abbott, thinking of the children. Another Gonski question. Will no-one (apart from Labor) think of the children? Education Minister Peter Garrett is on his feet, thinking of the children. The Canberra children. ACT chief minister Katy Gallagher is being congratulated for her vision. Labor's Dorothy Dixers continue on the theme of education and Gonski. Mr Swan says the Coalition, by opposing the reforms, is stealing from the future. He says Mr Abbott has been trying the thug the premiers, pressure them against signing on to the Gonski package. Mr Swan: We know he likes the biff. We know he's been going the biff on the Liberal premiers. Manager of Opposition business Christopher Pyne takes a point of order. I know I'm the only person left in the country left listening to the Treasuer .. Mr Pyne is counselled not to abuse points of order. Shadow treasurer Joe Hockey is continuing the economy theme. Mr Hockey raises the new report from the OECD we mentioned in our wrap of the news first thing this morning. Mr Hockey cites new taxes and regulations. Things are going badly aren't they? The question is to Treasurer Wayne Swan, who says: Those opposite come in here day after day, talking down the economy. They want to generate a sense of uncertainty. We've got an economy which is good nick, but it is an economy in transition. Meanwhile, out on the internet, Queensland's Bob Katter is hosting his own Q&A. Mr Abbott has opened on the economy. A change of topic from the national security theme seen for most of this week. Labor's first Dorothy Dixer is about the ACT saying yes to the Gonski agreement. It being 2pm, Question Time is underway. I'm with cartoonist Cathy Wilcox on this one. With character tests and truth telling the order of the day in national politics, have a read of Canberra blogger Paula Matthewson's thoughts on whether truth matters in politics anymore. Paula, who is part of our Comment is Free network, (and blogs as Drag0nista), has responded to a column Lenore Taylor wrote this week about the post truth election. Truth? Well, it's not so important Paula reasons. The sad truth is that we expect politicians to lie: it is simply part of what they do. While we denounce the lies of politicians we’d never vote for, we forgive the untruths of those we support. Trust is the make-or break issue. Paula argues that competency is the issue voters care most about. Guardian Australia’s political editor Lenore Taylor wrote that we’re heading to a post-truth election. This might have come as a surprise to the many political observers who consider truth to be a quaint artefact from a golden age of politics that may not have actually existed. Truth, or its absence, has not been a deciding factor in politics for a very long time. Nor will it play a definitive role in the 2013 federal election. That's a sad thought for Factinistas. No currency in truth. Interested in thoughts about this, here, or on Twitter. You can follow me @murpharoo and join in the daily conversation using the hastag #politicslive Here is Guardian Australia political editor Lenore Taylor's lunchtime news wrap of the row over public funding. The Coalition has forced its leader Tony Abbott to back down on a written agreement he gave to Labor that his party would vote for a deal to increase public funding to political parties. The party’s leadership group made the final decision to overturn the deal – which Abbott promised to support in a letter to Labor just six days ago – on Wednesday morning after a revolt by the backbench, senior members of the shadow ministry and state divisions of the Liberal party organisation. Brisk old day in national affiars. Let's take stock with the lunchtime summary. A rebellion from the Coalition backbench has killed a package designed to boost public funding for the political parties ahead of the September election; and make political donations more transparent. Mr Abbott's leadership group in effect told the Opposition leader that support for the package would be untenable, given the strong public backlash against the package. The bill, expected to be introduced to parliament today, has been withdrawn. Labor was determined to make sure that it didn't end up holding the baby. Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus released a letter showing Mr Abbott had agreed to the package on May 24. The government then flipped the whole imbroglio to being a character test for Mr Abbott: can you believe anything this man says? Manager of government business Anthony Albanese was tasked with landing this message. The parliamentary crossbenchers chimed in with some reinforcement - all the while arguing that the Gillard Government needed to press on with real disclosure reform. Labor is leaving its options open on that score. In other news, the ACT Government has signed up to the Gonski changes. And a young man has thrown a sandwich at Ms Gillard during a visit to Lyneham High School, copying another sandwich toss in Queensland a couple of weeks back. Getting Canberra over the line on the Gonski agreement is actually more difficult than it sounds. The ACT public school system is one of the strongest in the country. The tussle was over whether or not the Gonski changes would make Canberra schools worse off. Different story in some other jurisdictions. In any case, big Canberra and little Canberra have come to terms. While Twitter goes nuts on the sandwich, let's stick to some nuts and bolts on the Gonski announcement. Here's an excerpt of Ms Gillard's statement: Building on the already strong public investment in the ACT, (the Gonski agreement) will bring the total public investment in ACT schools to around $5 billion over the six years from 2014 – with around $190 million extra for ACT schools over this period. This extra resourcing will mean that all ACT schools are funded to at least the level of the new Schooling Resource Standard (SRS) by 2019, as well as ensuring that schools across all sectors benefit from new more stable indexation arrangements over this period. In simple terms, these indexation arrangements mean all schools in the ACT – Government, Independent and Catholic – will see fair funding growth each year. Schools already funded at, or above, the new SRS will see their funding grow in real terms. In addition, the Gillard Government has committed to grow its school education spending by 4.7 per cent per year from 2014 into 2015 and throughout the agreement. In return, ACT has agreed to grow its own school budget by 3 per cent per year from 2015 onwards. The results of ACT students are among the best in the country – and that’s why the Gillard Government is also announcing today that we will provide $26 million over six years to establish a Centre for Quality Teaching and Learning at the University of Canberra. This will help all education authorities in the ACT to continue to be at the forefront of school reform in this country. Just to confirm gravity works. What goes up must come down. Was it salami? Jam? Hard to tell. Concerning reports this morning about workers on the NBN rollout being exposed to asbestos (we linked to this story first up), Ms Gillard says she has received assurances from Telstra that action is being taken to ensure the safety of contractors. She's then asked whether Senator David Feeney should replace the retiring Martin Ferguson in the seat of Batman. The preselection process isn't even underway. I haven't got any comment. The PM is asked about the collapse of the election funding agreement. Ms Gillard rounds on Tony Abbott: There's a huge gulf between what Mr Abbott says and what Mr Abbott does. If Mr Abbott says he won't cut to the bone, you can't believe him. Prime Minister are you disappointed that you had another sandwich thrown at you? A question from a reporter. Ms Gillard: They must have thought I was hungry. Nice riposte. The Prime Minister has left the kids and is now speaking to reporters. Ms Gillard welcomes the ACT signing on to the Gonski reforms. Canberra is the second jurisdiction after NSW to agree to the school funding package. Ms Gillard: I'm delighted we've been able to reach agreement with the ACT. The ACT will get $26 million for a new centre of teaching excellence at the University of Canberra. Ms Gallagher says today's deal will ensure that no Canberra schools are worse off. Clever, that Mike Bowers. See the flying sandwich in the right hand side of the frame, near the Prime MInister's elbow? What's your view on social media at school? Is this a trick question, Ms Gillard wonders. The Prime Minister is asked to reflect on being the first female leader of Australia. She doesn't think about it exhaustively. It strikes her as inconvenient when she stumbles in her heels. Ms Gillard says frequent school visits have given her feedback that young girls are interested in the pathway she's created. If doing this job has that effect, then however good or bad the day is, then that's a good thing. I'm hearing a whisper out of the pack at Lyneham that the PM copped another sandwich on the way in to the school. Remember this happened a few weeks ago in Queensland, and there was a media frenzy for about 24 hours? Oops, the bell has just rung in the library. But Ms Gillard is continuing with her Q&A. Hello Lyneham. The Prime Minister arrives at the campus in north Canberra. Little reflection on resilience. If you are an insecure person, you would be buffeted by incoming negativity, the Prime Minister says. If you are confident, it doesn't worry you as much. Digital natives .. Ms Gillard is speaking of iPads. What is the successor to the iPad? Er .. dunno. But there will be one. Ms Gillard is unfurling an anecdote from when fax machines were a newfangled invention in offices. I wonder if these kids have ever seen a fax machine? Here's the Prime Minister over at Lyneham High School. Ms Gillard is taking some questions from the students. She looks slightly worried about the possibilities. This visit is to announce that the ACT has signed on the the government's Gonski reforms. Ms Gillard is with ACT Chief Minister Katy Gallagher. Speaking of data blogs, I'm so delighted that Simon Jackman will be bringing his somewhat mind bending number crunching expertise to our election coverage at Guardian Australia for 2013. (Can you bend minds and crunch numbers at the same time? Probably not .. but you know what I mean.) Simon was a contibutor to the Huffington Post for last year's presidential election, and won plaudits among his fellow political stats wonks for making predictions as accurate as the New York Times pundit, Nate Silver. Silver, as political tragics know, has a rock star following. Simon has done some number crunching for his first blog this morning. Can Labor win the September election? Hard to see how on current poll trends is his broad conclusion. You can read Simon's blog here. If you are interested in the topic of political donations in Australia, who gives what to whom, let me just remind you we have this informative datablog which will give you an insight. I'm a supporter of public funding for political parties. I think more public funding and less private donations is a recipe for a cleaner system less vulnerable to influence by a small group of wealthy individuals or corporates. More public funding also allows more professionalism in party organisations that in this country at least, largely run on the smell of an oil rag. I'm also a fan of a low disclosure threshold for political donations, and frequent public disclosure. Thus ends my sermon. Greens leader Christine Milne is out on the gospel truth theme. If you can't take Mr Abbott's word from written agreements, what else will he rip up? The crossbenchers are now flipping this into a trust and character issue for Mr Abbott. Bit deft that, given they didn't support the deal in the first place. Tasmanian Independent Andrew Wilkie says he's glad the proposal is dead. But he says there is an urgent need to bring down the disclosure thresholds for political donations. Independent Tony Windsor says parliament needs to get stuck into regulating associated entities. It's possible for donor to make big injections to entities which then flow through to candidates, but this largesse is never declared. The origins of the money remain obscured. I see some some folks on social media are going back over Mr Abbott's famous interview with the 7.30 Report's Kerry O'Brien on the subject of the gospel truth. Mr Abbott: The statements taken as gospel truth are carefully scripted remarks .. Mr Albanese has stepped into the place recently vacated by Mr Abbott. He says the government will now examine alternatives to the package it proposed to the Liberals. We'll consider our options, and we'll have discussions with other representatives in the parliament. I'm of the view, and have been for some time, that transparnecy is important in the political process. The Manager of Government Business is going where he would go in such circumstances. This goes to (Mr Abbott's) character. It is very hard to have discussions with someone who even when (an agreement is) in writing, walks away. We were prepared to negotiate an outcome in good faith. He says the agreement was in black and white. I think there should be public funding of political parties. The ideal would be removing the ability of people or companies to donate to political parties. I think public funding is needed. Transparency is needed. Mr Albanese says without public funding, millionaires own politics, as they do in the United States. He says the principle of one vote one value, and equal participation in democracry, is undermined by large donations, particularly large anonymous donations. It's not clear whether Mr Abbott was rolled by his leadership group. It's not clear what's happened here. I was told last night they were in a difficult position. Here's some audio from Mr Abbott's press conference for readers interested to have a listen. Lively, that press conference. Mr Abbott has arrived for his press conferece. We have listened to the public. We've learned. This bill is dead. The Leader of the Opposition says there have been good faith discussions between party officials on public funding. He says there is an argument for public funding. There is an argument. It would make political parties less dependent on donations. But Mr Abbott says the package just couldn't proceed. We are the fag end of a very contentions parliament. He says there's a budget emergency. It was always going to be "a very big ask" to tell the public we are cutting services for you, but not for us. Mr Abbott agrees he had seen a draft of the proposal. We certainly hadn't see a final copy. Were you rolled by your leadership team? Mr Abbott rejects that characterisation. He says the Coalition informed the government at 7.30pm last night that the Opposition needed more time to consider the proposal. He says there was little support for the measure in his leadership team. The public has spoken, and the bill is dead. Prime Minister Julia Gillard is off, meanwhile, to a school in the Canberra suburb of Lyneham. She'll address the media after that visit. Timing switcheroos, just for the information of people watching this story unfold on several devices! We can't linger with the seat of Batman however. The funding deal has gone nuclear in the building. Manager of government business Anthony Albanese will address reporters shortly on the collapse of the agreement. Mr Abbott will front the cameras in ten minutes. We'll follow this story throughout the day. Readers with us yesterday will know that Martin Ferguson, the former Labor Cabinet minister, announced he would retire at the September election. Quite an emotional day in the parliament. Politics being politics, the story today is who gets Mr Ferguson's seat. The ABC's Simon Cullen is looking into that. Mr Dreyfus said if journalists had any further questions on the Coalition's position, they should be directed to Tony Abbott. Mr Abbott's intentions changed evidently after the bull run by the backbench last night. The government's intention this morning is to not go down on this one alone. Let the record show that the Coalition agreed to this deal. Mr Abbott says in the letter of 24 May: I am satisfied with the agreement reached and indicate the Coalition's intention to support the legislation and to deal with it, as requested, before the end of the sittings. The Opposition leader says he has been provided with a near final draft of the bill and will leave settlement of the final legislation with the shadow minister. The day has opened at a brisk pace on the election funding deal. Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus has held a press conference where he's blasted the Opposition for backflipping on the agreement. He's circulated a letter, dated May 24, from the Opposition leader Tony Abbott, agreeing to the package. Here's a partial recording of Mr Dreyfus at the press conference. He walked off without taking questions. Good morning, and welcome to our live coverage of politics from Canberra. Mike Bowers' opening picture this morning is of Queensland cattle producers, in town with Queensland MP Bob Katter. Lovely frame isn't it? All hat, hungry cattle. As we thought late yesterday, a deal between the major parties on public funding for elections, and new thresholds for the disclosure of political donations, seems to have collapsed under the weight of objections from Coalition MPs. The backlash against the agreement to increase public funding for political parties in time for the September election was intense. There was already trouble in Labor ranks. Late yesterday saw a major breakout by Coalition MPs. The legislation was due to be introduced to parliament today. Given the dissent, the Coalition has asked that the bill be pulled. The collapse of that package is the main story in the political news cycle this morning. Here’s the report from Sydney’s The Daily Telegraph: Prime Minister Julia Gillard's plan to hit taxpayers with a $60 million bill to fund election campaigns has been torpedoed after the Coalition abandoned its support for the legislation. Liberal sources confirmed last night there was overwhelming momentum to ditch what had previously been bipartisan support for the deeply unpopular plan. The Coalition informed the government last night that it had withdrawn its support. Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus was still hoping the legislation might be voted on next week but admitted Labor "won't be proceeding" if the Opposition dropped its support. Ms Gillard is feeling the heat, with former government whip Joel Fitzgibbon admitting "members everywhere" would be fielding phone calls and emails from angry constituents over the legislation."Every MP will be getting complaints in their electorate offices ... this week. I certainly am," he said. Let’s run through some of the rest of the issues in the newspapers today. Asbestos safety breaches: NBN Federal workplace safety regulators have ordered work to stop on parts of the National Broadband Network while they escalate an investigation into asbestos safety breaches near homes being connected to the $37.4 billion project. Workplace safety authority Comcare told The Australian last night that it had issued stop-work orders at several sites and expected to issue more as it examined "poor standards" over asbestos hazards. Bill Shorten won’t back a repeal of the carbon price Employment Minister Bill Shorten has indicated that should he lead Labor after the federal election, he would not be willing to roll over to Tony Abbott and allow a Coalition government to abolish the price on carbon. In an email to a concerned lobby group, Mr Shorten said two recent articles which suggested he would lead Labor after the election and readily assent to the abolition of the troublesome policy were “misleading’’. “For the record, I told the journalist who asked me about carbon pricing that I’d always supported the government’s carbon pricing policy and that hasn’t changed,’’ he said. “A carbon price is the best way to do something about climate change, so we can pass on the planet to our kids and grandkids in the best shape possible.’’ He says the election poses a “real choice” between competing climate change policies. He tells the group, Lighter Footprints: “Feel free to tell your friends I absolutely support Labor’s smart and fair carbon pricing policy.” Mr Shorten was responding to an email from the group’s convener who wrote: “We read that the ALP is considering (post a lost election) dumping its position on a price of carbon and possibly the ETS and we lose whatever hope we (have) in this government. “Don’t do it, even if you are thinking like this.’’ After the boom: OECD The OECD has for the first time cast serious doubt about Australia’s future after the resources boom, joining the growing fears among experts about how to manage the economic transition. In an unusually blunt report, the Paris-based group forecast below-average growth this year and acknowledged the pending September election is creating uncertainty in the economy. The report added to mounting evidence global investors are reassessing Australia’s previous resilience. And here's a couple of pieces of analysis you might enjoy. With national security and asylum boats being such a dominant theme this parliamentary week, here’s an analysis from Mike Seccombe of The Global Mail. Economics long ran on the conviction that people were “rational actors”, that they made decisions which maximised their personal advantage. It’s a rubbish theory of course, and politics proves it. We are now in the throes of an undeclared election campaign in which a central issue – asylum seekers – is one which has little relevance to the lives of average Australians. And the disproportionate focus on it is in sharp contrast to that on issues of vital importance to huge numbers of people, and the future of the country as a whole. Jonathan Green has also written a column for ABC online on the public outrage about the orphaned election funding deal. Parties that were connected to their grass roots, that nurtured their popular support and drew both ideological and financial comfort from it, would not be compelling the taxpayer to fund their base administration. For those who might have had misgivings, who may have wondered whether politics was still a noble pursuit of public service or just a self-serving shadow of its former self, each passing day seems to provide evidence of a faith betrayed.

results.webTitle: Viktor Yanukovych: can the great survivor of Ukraine politics hang on?

fields.headline: Viktor Yanukovych: can the great survivor of Ukraine politics hang on?
fields.trailText:
The president weathered the Orange Revolution, but will need Moscow's support to survive this year's protests
· It is time to go, opposition leaders tell president
fields.bodyText: Viktor Yanukovych is the great survivor of post-Soviet politics in Ukraine. He made a comeback from one revolutionary defeat. His main rivals are either tarnished or in jail. He is both courted and threatened by the Kremlin, wooed by Brussels. He had an extremely hard and poor upbringing in the industrial wastelands of eastern Ukraine. He is now a very wealthy man with a large estate outside Kiev. The young people thronging the streets and squares of the Ukrainian capital and other cities are baying for his head, identifying the president as the embodiment of what they do not want their country to be. Jailed twice for assault as a youngster, Yanukovych has thrived in the thuggish world of Ukrainian politics. He will not go easily. "Yanukovych's behavior became the crucial factor," commented Lilia Shevtsova of the Carnegie Russia thinktank in Moscow. "He is trying to ensure his reelection in 2015. This primary motive has guided his actions. He concluded that European integration would not guarantee him victory and decided to fall back on Putin's formula of preserving power." Since his refusal to commit to Europe and instead opt for Russia at an EU summit in Lithuania last Friday, the president has been surprisingly quiet. Hundreds of thousands have commanded the streets of Kiev and occupied official buildings. He has failed to respond in words, except to issue a statement at the weekend saying he was deeply indignant at the riot police attacks on peaceful protesters at dawn on Saturday. But public speaking has never been his strongest suit. Yanukovych presides over a dysfunctional, deeply divided, highly corrupt, extremely poor country positioned pivotally between Russia's western frontier and the EU's eastern flank. Parts of western Ukraine used to be Poland and before that part of the Austro-Hungarian empire, while the Russian-speaking east and south belonged to Moscow and remain culturally Russian. There is no national consensus on what this country should be. A native Russian speaker who only learned to speak the country's first language in his 50s out of political expediency, Yanukovych has long been seen as of the old regime, Moscow's man. The balancing act has been tricky and he has had rough moments with the Kremlin. But as demonstrated last week, when push comes to shove, Yanukovych is much more comfortable with, and has more to benefit from, the type of politics practised in Moscow compared to the systemic reforms that would have flowed from the political association agreement and free trade regime with the EU that he ditched in Vilnius. "This is a revolutionary situation, in a technical sense," said Andrew Wilson, Ukraine expert at the European Council on Foreign Relations. "His natural instincts are to attack." It may turn out to be harder to unseat Yanukovych than it was in the Orange Revolution of 2004-5. He himself ignited that revolt by trying to steal an election. He lost, giving way to Viktor Yushchenko, who proved an ineffectual president embroiled in endless intrigues with the then prime minister and fellow revolutionary, Yulia Tymoshenko. Yanukovych made a comeback, replacing Tymoshenko as prime minister. He then beat her to the presidency in 2010. A year later she was in jail. Outside observers, unlike in 2004, however, gave the 2010 contest a clean bill of health. In the middle of his five-year term, Yanukovych is clearly in trouble. The question is how much? Can he weather the protests, grind the demonstrators down, rely on his secret police and security forces, on some of the most powerful oligarchs in the country who bankroll him and control much of the media? Can he depend on Vladimir Putin in Moscow to shore him up? Can he secure the loans needed to forestall Ukraine going broke? In 2004 the security forces split between the revolution and the old regime. Yanukovych has already lost several MPs and his chief of staff, Serhiy Lyovochkin, has defected. Together with the energy oligarch and one of Ukraine's richest men, Dmytro Firtash, Lyovochkin controls Ukraine's biggest TV station. Firtash is also believed to "own" up to 10 MPs who could tip the balance in bringing down the government, if not the president. Yanukovych is one of several leaders, all strongmen, all relatively popular, all winning elections, and then using their mandates to polarise and divide, reward their cronies and punish their rivals; control freaks who fear and despise independent institutions and vibrant civil societies. There are similar situations in Russia, Turkey, and Hungary. The leaders are different, specific to their own cultures, but share many characteristics and employ similar methods of control and coercion, buying loyalty and penalising opposition and resistance. If you are a Russian, a Turk, a Hungarian, or a Ukrainian right now, according to the powerful government chiefs, you are either with us or against us. In 2004 the Orange Revolution achieved critical mass, as did the protests in parallel crises in Serbia in 2000 and Georgia in 2003, and toppled the ancien régimes. The Kremlin studied the events closely and learned its lessons. Orange is a term of abuse, conjuring up fear and ridicule in Putin's Moscow. A street victory in Kiev will be a defeat for the Kremlin. "The key pressure point is the parliament," said Wilson, anticipating an attempt to muster a vote of confidence and bring down the government. "That would leave Yanukovych dangerously isolated. Russia has very destructive, coercive powers. They won't work in the long term." But in the short term, that raises the stakes in Ukraine. Russia's interest in preserving the status quo makes the situation more dangerous, perhaps increasing Yanukovych's chances of survival.

results.webTitle: Jeremy Hunt could net £17m if pre-politics business sells

fields.headline: Jeremy Hunt could net £17m if pre-politics business sells
fields.trailText: Hotcourses' sale to Inflexion would propel health secretary into the ranks of most successful entrepreneurs in the Commons
fields.bodyText: Jeremy Hunt could be in line for a windfall of up to £17m should Hotcourses, the online directory of courses he founded 17 years ago, be sold to Inflexion, the private equity firm behind the Jack Wills preppy clothing brand. The proposed deal, valuing the whole company at about £35m, would cement the health secretary's reputation as one of the most successful entrepreneurs in the House of Commons. Other wealthy figures in the cabinet include defence secretary Philip Hammond, who made millions in nursing homes and other ventures. David Cameron and George Osborne were also rated among the most wealthy in a study last year by Wealth-X, though in both case their riches were largely inherited. Although Hunt stepped down as a director of Hotcourses in 2009, he is still the largest shareholder, with a 49% stake. His interest in the firm is held via a "blind trust" which means he is prevented from having any day to day control of his stake. Inflexion is understood to be in a period of exclusive discussions over an acquisition of the business, although it is not certain whether a sale will go through. Hunt, Hotcourses and Inflexion did not reply or declined to comment. The firm, which went by the name Elms Hunt International until 2001, is still run by the health secretary's childhood friend and co-founder, Mike Elms. The latest accounts show the business made a pre-tax profit of £2m on a turnover of £6.5m for the seven months to July 2012. Elms said that represented a 17% rise in sales and a jump in profits of more than 50%. One of Hotcourses' biggest contracts is a deal to run a website for the British Council, promoting courses in the UK. The council is a quango sponsored by the Foreign Office to promote cultural and educational opportunities abroad. It is part-funded by government grants. Hunt has insisted the success of Hotcourses came only after Elms and he had pursued a string of failed ventures. Among their false starts were a scheme to export marmalade to Japan, building children's playgrounds and a tourist guide idea. The two businessmen had also set up a political PR firm, Profile PR, later renamed Heckler, in the early 1990s. Hunt has recalled how the pair eventually stumbled on the winning formula that became Hotcourses after they had been publishing student university guides, quickly spotting it as a bigger business opportunity. On his website, Hunt reminds his constituents in South West Surrey that he understands the hardship of starting up a new business. "A lot of people who start their own business do so because they think it's a good way to make a lot of money, but when you start it becomes simply a matter of survival. It's a daily struggle, which is why small business people tend to be very down to earth and practical." The eldest son of Admiral Sir Nicholas Hunt, the health secretary was educated at Charterhouse private school and Magdalen College, Oxford, before turning to business and latterly politics.
