Clegg says politicians should not provide parenting lessons to people. Parenting can be very frustrating. He chooses not to make his children. Q: What about the occasional smack with a wooden spoon, as Mary Berry [the Great British Bake-Off judge] did? Clegg says he is reluctant to comment. He does not know what happened. He does not want to get judgmental about what parents do. But of course they should not inflict violence on their children, or break the law. Q: Would you intervene if you saw someone smacking a child in a supermarket? Clegg says he has pulled his children away from something. Of course parents use force like this. But he won't adjudicate on something hypothetical. Clegg says all party leaders are agreed on MPs' pay. Public consent matters to MPs more than in almost any other job. At a time when the government is capping public sector pay at 1%, it would be hard, "to put it mildly", to justify paying MPs more. A pay rise along the lines proposed today would be "incomprehensible". But this is not set in stone. The proposal needs to be reviewed in 2015. And while we're on the subject of the Cameron/Obama/Thorning-Schmidt, the photographer who took the picture has written an interesting blog about it. He says Michelle Obama wasn't in a grump. Suddenly this woman pulled out her mobile phone and took a photo of herself smiling with Cameron and the US president. I captured the scene reflexively. All around me in the stadium, South Africans were dancing, singing and laughing to honour their departed leader. It was more like a carnival atmosphere, not at all morbid. The ceremony had already gone on for two hours and would last another two. The atmosphere was totally relaxed – I didn’t see anything shocking in my viewfinder, president of the US or not. We are in Africa. I later read on social media that Michelle Obama seemed to be rather peeved on seeing the Danish prime minister take the picture. But photos can lie. In reality, just a few seconds earlier the first lady was herself joking with those around her, Cameron and Schmidt included. Her stern look was captured by chance. I took these photos totally spontaneously, without thinking about what impact they might have. At the time, I thought the world leaders were simply acting like human beings, like me and you. I doubt anyone could have remained totally stony faced for the duration of the ceremony, while tens of thousands of people were celebrating in the stadium. For me, the behaviour of these leaders in snapping a selfie seems perfectly natural. Clegg says even in death Mandela was acting as a catalyst, enabling people to overcome their differences. Q: Would he have found the fake signer funny? Clegg says he suspects he would. Q: There is a picture of you talking to Bill Clinton. And Ed Miliband is watching you, and "looking daggers". Clegg says he knows Clinton a bit. They have met several times. They met in London recently, with his wife Miriam, and Clinton recommended a book called The End of Power. Clegg was telling Clinton that his wife had read it. Nick Ferrari asks Clegg if he should be worried about Clinton recommending a book to him called The End of Power. Clegg laughs. No, he says. He is very confident of staying in power. Q: What do you make of Cameron's Mandela selfie? Clegg says some people are unhappy about it. But it was "friendly, lighthearted joking around by the three of them". He would not get too censorious, he says. The mood at the event was very celebratory. Clegg asks the caller what he thinks. The caller says he did not mind it. Q: Did you feel jealous you were left out? No, says Clegg. He was sitting in a different section, and did not know what was going on. The first question is about Nelson Mandela's death. Q: The BBC's coverage was over-powering. On Thursday night it cleared its schedules to cover Mandela. Yet there was a horrendous storm going on. Shouldn't the BBC have covered that more? Clegg says he does not think politicians should tell the BBC or other outlets how they should run their news. But Mandela was extraordinarily unique. It is not surprising the editors wanted to devote so much time to him. Nick Clegg is about to start his LBC phone-in. You can listen here. Sir Ian Kennedy, chair of the Independent Parliamentary Standards Authority, has used an article in the Times (paywall) to warn that it will be a disaster if MPs reject the proposals to increase their pay being published by Ipsa this morning. Here's the Guardian's story about this. And here's an extract from the article. Parliament was clear in the wake of the expenses scandal that it was wrong for MPs to set their own expenses, salary, pensions and benefits. That remains a position hard to argue with. We all know that MPs made a mess of these things for decades; the result was a disaster. We were given the job of tackling this issue independently for a reason. You are not independent if all you do is bend to the will of the government or the Commons ... We are in no doubt — MPs’ pay needs a one-off uplift. Whatever measure you choose — including international comparisons and historic trends — they all lead to the same conclusion: MPs’ pay has fallen behind. It needs to catch up. We will announce a one-off pay rise. Thereafter MPs’ pay will move with the pay of the rest of us. That’s a sensible way forward, which we will implement after a further review of conditions in 2015. George Osborne delivered his autumn statement a week ago today. Normally the media spends at last 48 hours scrutinising it in detail. But that did not really happen this year because the announcement of Nelson Mandela's death on Thursday night effectively knocked the autumn statement completely off the news agenda by Friday morning. Never mind. Today the Commons Treasury committee will spend the morning quizzing Osborne on the small print. For background, here are a couple of things worth reading. • The 10-page Commons library note summarising the contents of the autumn statement (pdf). • An analysis of the autumn statement from Paul Johnson, director of the Institute for Fiscal Studies (pdf). I will be covering the hearing in detail. Here's the agenda for the day. 8.45am: Elizabeth Truss, the education minister, and Boris Johnson, the mayor of London, make an announcement on childcare in London. 9am: Nick Clegg hosts his Call Clegg LBC phone-in. 9.30am: The Care Quality Commission publishes details of a national maternity survey. 9.30am: Primary school league tables for England are published. 10am: George Osborne gives evidence to the Commons Treasury committee about the autumn statement. 10am: The Independent Parliamentary Standards Authority publishes its plans to increase MPs' pay. 2pm: John Bercow, the Commons Speaker, leads tributes to Nelson Mandela at an event in Westminter Hall. 2pm: Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, speaks at a General Medical Council conference. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a summary at about 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Tuesday 20 August

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Tuesday 20 August
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Monday including some in today's paper, are here.

results.webTitle: Ukip has chanced upon the neglected part of British politics | Alex Andreou

fields.headline: Ukip has chanced upon the neglected part of British politics
fields.trailText: Alex Andreou: To understand the appeal of Ukip it helps to think about the four quadrants of the political compass. Who else covers the authoritarian right?
fields.bodyText: What most political parties' election teams wouldn't give to know precisely what makes a person consider voting Ukip. Facile explanations about the surge of Ukip support being exclusively about Europe have been comprehensively dismissed in extensive research commissioned by Lord Ashcroft. It showed that Britain's relationship with Europe was the top issue for only, roughly, a quarter of the people considering Ukip. And yet the surge in support is a phenomenon that remains, to paraphrase Churchill, a riddle, wrapped in a mystery, inside the enigma that is Nigel Farage. The results of a new YouGov poll conducted on behalf of the thinktank Class provide some surprising clues. In a series of questions relating to a variety of areas, from energy prices and rent control to public ownership of railways and banking reform, the poll found that voters who intend to back Ukip supported state intervention, consistently and by a significant margin. These results are in marked contrast to the lower percentages seen among Conservative supporters and are closer to Labour voters. For instance, 73% of Ukip supporters chose the proposition "the railway companies should be run in the public sector" as expressing their views, compared with 79% of Labour and only 52% of Conservative. This pattern is broadly replicated. "The government should have the power to control energy prices": 83% Ukip, 86% Lab, 60% Con; "The government should have the power to control private sector rents": 50% Ukip, 56% Lab, 32% Con; "The energy companies should be run in the public sector": 73% Ukip, 79% Labour, 52% Con. YouGov warns that people "should be careful about reading too much into a sub-sample", but notwithstanding that caveat they can be "confident in saying that Ukip voters do seem to be more supportive of price controls and nationalisation than their rightwing image might suggest". How does this help our understanding of the current political landscape? The concept of the "political compass" is useful here. The idea behind it is simply this: a horizontal, linear left-to-right nexus of economic ideology is no longer sufficient to express the complex relationship between parties and the voters they court. It is necessary to consider a vertical axis of libertarian to authoritarian (or statist). This, in effect, creates four quadrants, as the figure below shows. Broadly speaking, the way in which each of the three main parties presents itself places it in one of three quadrants (albeit converged towards the centre) as an offering; Conservative in the libertarian right, Liberal Democrat in the libertarian left, Labour in the authoritarian left. I say this fully accepting that during this era, in which opinion polls seem to determine leadership rather than the other way around, all parties cherry-pick policies which are distinctly outside their quadrant. I also accept that the way in which a party portrays itself before an election and the way in which it acts in power, are often incongruous – the Liberal Democrats representing for many the most obvious example at present. It would seem a possibility worth considering that Ukip has planted itself within the authoritarian right quadrant, which was previously largely unrepresented in British politics. This may explain why research into byelections found that a notable slice of Ukip support hadn't voted at all recently. Certainly, there seems to be an element of general disillusionment with traditional politics. However, it would be fascinating to explore further this notion that Ukip may have chanced upon this fourth quadrant. I say "chanced upon" because I don't think there is any element of political strategic genius at work in this formulation. Rather, as I've explained before, Ukip flagrantly pilfers the most unpopular policies from both Labour and the Conservatives and frames them in a negative manner, making its manifesto a "bucket list" for the annoyed and offering easy, uncosted solutions. Scared of immigrants? Vote Ukip. Insecure about the financial crisis? Vote Ukip. Hate the smoking ban, energy companies, HS2, Brussels, travellers, burqas, tax, Boris, debt, wind farms, bankers, quangos, foreign aid, crime, Abu Qatada, tuition fees, lazy people, Muslims, foreigners, the hunting ban? Vote Ukip. The result of that scattered approach, however, may be that Ukip has somehow stumbled upon virgin territory that perfectly fits their nationalist identity; the vote of people naturally leaning towards the authoritarian right.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened - Tony Abbott returns to the parliamentary fray

fields.headline: Politics as it happened - Tony Abbott returns to the parliamentary fray
fields.trailText: The prime minister is back from Chogm for the second sitting week in parliament dominated by debt and carbon tax
fields.bodyText: Just to recap this rollicking old day: The Guardian, in partnership with the ABC, revealed documents which show in 2009, Australia bugged the phone of the Indonesian President and other senior government officials. In Parliament, Tony Abbott said all governments gather information and all know every other government gathers information however the Australian government uses all information "to help our friends and not harm them". The Parliament continued to debate the carbon tax repeal bills. Senate estimates heard a range of delicious details from the processes of the Coalition's recruitment "star chamber", the final resting place of John Howard's chesterfield sofas and the fact that every government change-over requires a change of crockery service for a mere bagatelle, i.e. $80,000. Senate estimates also gleaned that we generous taxpayers are funding a luxury mansion in Canberra, rented for the incoming prime minister before the election. When Abbott chose more modest digs, seems taxpayer was lumped with the on-going lease. Secretary of the department of prime minister and cabinet Ian Watt was revealed to be a fan of the Queen, ordering the raising of two portraits in his department. Assistant education minister Sussan Ley repeatedly told the Parliament, under questioning from her shadow Kate Ellis, that the government had no plans to means test the childcare rebate. In spite of Ellis' urging Ley would not utter the promise. On Tuesday, the fun continues with more estimates and more Parliament. Good night and good luck. Katharine Murphy reports: A senior official has left open the prospect that parliamentary communications in Australia could be monitored by US intelligence through a “back door” provided by Microsoft operating systems. Eija Seittenranta, who is responsible for ensuring network security in Australia’s parliamentary IT systems, told the Senate finance and public administration committee on Monday that no specific action had been taken to secure the parliamentary network against surveillance by US agencies under the Prism program. You may remember that Tony Abbott is essentially minister for women with Michaelia Cash assisting. Queensland Labor Senator Claire Moore is using Senate estimates to question whether moving the office of women into the Dpmc compromises its policy objectives. Senior department officer Rebecca Cross says on the contrary, the moves give the office more weight. Senator Faulkner is questioning department official Elizabeth Kelly, who confirms two portraits of the Queen were hung in the department of prime minister and cabinet. They have a stock of portraits apparently but since the Abbott government came to power, one has been hung in the executive area and one in the amenities area. Faulkner: What was the reason for it, was it to curry favour with the PM? Kelly: The portraits were put up at the request of the secretary of Dpmc. Faulkner: So it was Dr Ian Watt? Was that a result of his interior decorating nous? Kelly: The secretary, at an all staff briefing, said it was his decision and not as a request. Faulkner: It was a departmental decision? Kelly: Yes Senator. Faulkner: I was wondering whether there had been a staff revolution because there had not been a portrait of the Queen. AAP is reporting: Taxpayers are forking out $3000 a week in rent for a luxury Canberra house that Tony Abbott has chosen not to use. The department of Prime Minister and Cabinet (Dpmc) leased the property as a temporary replacement for The Lodge, which is undergoing a major refurbishment. Senior DPMC official Elizabeth Kelly said the department signed a 12-month lease on August 31, a week before the September 7 federal election. It signed even though neither Abbott nor then Labor prime minister Kevin Rudd had been able to inspect the property due to their election campaign commitments. Kelly says the department had wanted to find accommodation "comparable" to The Lodge. But after the election, Abbott opted instead to stay in a modest flat at the Australian Federal Police training college in Barton. Liberal senator Cory Bernardi asked about the property during a Senate estimates. "So $3000 a week for a property that how many people are living in now?" he asked. "The property is vacant, senator," Ms Kelly said. Ms Kelly said the department was notified shortly after the election that Mr Abbott was not going to use it. Since then, the department has been negotiating with the property's owner to get out of the lease, but has not yet succeeded. "We're still in those negotiations," Ms Kelly said. It's understood the property has already cost taxpayers more than $30,000 and the full 12-month lease would cost taxpayers about $156,000. Labor senators Penny Wong and John Faulkner are questioning Leader of the Senate, Eric Abetz, on how the new government recruited people for 420 positions in the Coalition ministry. Specifically, they were honing in on the membership of the government staffing committee which provided advice on the appointments. Abetz says the special minister of state Michael Ronaldson and social services minister Kevin Andrews were on the committee - also known as the Star Chamber - but he would not "gratuitously haul" other names through the Hansard. Of course the name Labor was looking for was Peta Credlin, Abbott's chief of staff - known for having a vice-like grip over appointments - but Abetz was not going to give her up. (Credlin got a few honourable mentions earlier in the day for requesting a portrait of the Queen for the prime minister's office and works to the Pmo dining room and kitchen.) Abetz says the committee advises the prime minister of recommendations on appointments though he was not sure whether Abbott ticked them off. Abetz is getting testy when Faulkner urges him to respect the precedent that previous governments have "fully answered" questions on recruitment process for political staffing. Penny Wong: I just want to know who? Eric Abetz: All you need to know is it was decided by the prime minister....All the correspondence was signed off by the chief of staff of the prime minister. There it is. Liberal MP for Hindmarsh Matt Williams has just finished his maiden speech. Joanne Ryan, the new Labor MP for Lalor is giving her maiden speech, speaking to the former "bonza local member" . Julia Gillard remained one of us. Our colleague at Fairfax radio is reporting Kevin Rudd will hand in his resignation this week. Anthony Albanese is giving a personal explanation because he claims the communications minister Malcolm Turnbull misrepresented him on the NBN Co corporate plan. I didn't see it, says Albo, let alone fail to release it. Justice minister Michael Keenan is outlining the government's anti-gang policies. Bill Shorten asks Abbott: How many boats has the government bought and how much money has been set aside to buy boats? Abbott says the important thing is how many boats has the government stopped from coming to Australia. The boats are stopping. That is the most directly relevant thing. And with that, QT is over. Richard Marles asks why Morrison is prepared to talk to newspapers and not the Australian Parliament. Marles says in a report in The Australian, Morrison said the boat buy back funding had been reallocated. Morrison does not answer. Shorten asks the prime minister: have you dumped the boat buy back policy? Abbott says the government stands by "all our policies". Members opposite are just a little bit embarrassed. Bruce Billson is asked about a voluntary code of conduct under the Food and Grocery Council. Scott Morrison is asked how much money has been set aside for buying back boats in Indonesia. Morrison says the government is deploying "a full arsenal of measures and we are funding those policies". I call that value for money. Sarah Henderson asks communications minister Malcolm Turnbull about the government's plans for the National Broadband Network. The biggest barrier to broadband is not technology, says the minister, it's affordability. Turnbull says Labor did not give a lot of information about the NBN including the advice that NBN Co was going to result in a $31bn loss. Turnbull says the calculations were made on a beer coaster - "a coaster that should be elevated to Labor's pool room of horrors". Turnbull appears to be having a ball, suggesting the pool room should also include the lazy susan from the restaurant where Shorten allegedly plotted Kevin Rudd's downfall. Speaker Bishop says "there is no shame in having vociferous exchanges". Bill Shorten to Tony Abbott: The Opposition is seeking to elicit a promise not to means test the childcare rebate. Abbott says Ley has answered the questions. The government is determined to do whatever we can to improve the childcare system, he says. Speaker Bronwyn Bishop is objected to Shorten holding up newspaper clips headlining the Coalition's promises not to means test the rebate. Props are not allowed in Parliament. Immigration minister Scott Morrison on the patrol boats gifted to Sri Lanka. Assistant minister for education, Sussan Ley, refuses to promise not to means test the childcare rebate, only saying the govt has "no plans" to means test the rebate. WA MP Don Randall is asking education minister Christopher Pyne what plans the government has to extend local decision making in schools. Pyne says independent public schools are transformational schools in Western Australia. Treasurer Joe Hockey has urged Labor to support the repeal of the mining tax. Ellis asks Ley to guarantee that no family will be worse off after the Productivity Commission review into childcare. Ley says the government has no plans to index the childcare rebate - whoops, make that no plans to means test the childcare rebate. Adam Bandt asks if it is true Australia tapped the phones of the Indonesian president. The prime minister says: All governments gather information and all know every other government gathers information. The Australian government never comments on intelligence matters and that has been the long tradition of Australian governments. The Australian government uses all information to help our friends and not harm them. I will never say or do anything to will damage the close relations with Indonesia, which is our most important relationship. Foreign minister Julie Bishop and trade minister Andrew Robb not in QT today. First question from Bill Shorten on the Coalition's commitment to the car industry. Tony Abbott says the Coalition saved the car industry from Labor's Fringe Benefits Tax changes and will repeal the carbon tax. Second question on to the prime minister on how the repeal of carbon tax will reduce Australian power bills by $200 a year on average. Labor's Tanya Plibersek asks Abbott when about his comments in Sri Lanka on torture. Abbott says the government deplores the use of torture. Agriculture minister Barnaby Joyce asked about the effect of the carbon tax on the sugar industry. It's costing millions, says Joyce. Labor's Kate Ellis asks Sussan Ley about childcare. Ley says "everything" is under the purview of the Productivity Commission inquiry. "I have no plans to make changes prior to the review." Question time coming up in the House. Stephen Brady, official secretary of Governor General, is before the Senate finance and public administration legislation committee, otherwise known as Senate estimates. Lunchtime kids. Parliament has been rocked by revelations in The Guardian and the ABC about spying on the Indonesian President. The government has set a time limit on the second reading debate on the carbon tax repeal bills to continue no further than Thursday. Debate is now taking place in the House. Senate estimates have considered a range of things, including the cost and sensibility of changing crockery sets at every change of government. There has been a refugee rally outside the building. Car industry representatives visit Parliament to lobby both sides. Greens leader Christine Milne says Snowden's revelations shows how far Australia has slipped down the US path. Milne says notwithstanding Tony Abbott's rhetoric on Indonesia's friendship: This is not the way you treat your friends. Milne says all Australians should ask what Australia is doing on behalf of the United States' government. What is the justification for trying to spy on the phone calls of the president or his wife? Tasmanian independent and former intelligence officer Andrew Wilkie applauds Edward Snowden in regard to the latest revelations in The Guardian that Australia listened in on mobile phone calls by the Indonesian president. I have no doubt (Snowdon) acted in the public interest. I pay him my compliments. Good on him. Some capabilities must be kept secret but it is in the public interest that a light is shone on how these agencies do business. Bill Shorten says Labor will vote to repeal the price on carbon if the Coalition works to address climate change but so far he has seen no evidence of that. I'm glad we are sticking to our guns. Proving it's going to be one of those days, the National Party member for Dawson, George Christensen will table a motion today proposing the ABC, Screen Australia and other film funding bodies consider offering finance to to bring Dr Who down under. In the interests of greater love in the building, it attracted bi-partisan support with Labor's Graham Perrett and Andrew Liegh joining Christensen and Liberal Alex Hawke. The government won the division to set the debate on the carbon tax repeal debate by 85 to 56. Opposition Leader Bill Shorten is on his feet speaking against the carbon tax repeal bills suggesting Labor will always support laws to tackle the issues of the future. Tony Abbott is lining up with the militia of climate change deniers. Back to a crock of crockery. Labor senator John Faulkner wants to know happens to the old sets of crockery - given they changed over every time a government changes. He suggests we could feed whole armies with the old sets of crockery coming out of Parliament and wants to know how many old sets are floating around the basement. There are "hundreds of partial sets" of crockery. "Perhpas someone could be tasked to put it all together," says Faulkner. Carol Mills of the department of parliamentary services, says: It would be a good thing to do. We found places so scattered where they are stored and different in design it is not easy to create acceptable sets. Leader of the House Christopher Pyne has moved to gag debate on the carbon repeal bills. His Labor shadow Tony Burke is on his feet, suggesting Pyne is trying to "deny people from even having their case heard" on carbon tax repeal bills. A vote is underway now. BREAKING: Australia's spy agencies have attempted to listen in on the personal phone calls of the Indonesian president, Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono, and have targeted the mobile phones of his wife, senior ministers and confidants, a top secret document from whistleblower Edward Snowden reveals. Greens senator Scott Ludlam is questioning the IT officer for parliamentary services regarding vulnerabilities in technology in the building. Through Guardian's revelations on the National Security Agency, we know that "backdoors" were installed in servers such as Microsoft. Ludlam wants to know whether any action has been taken to secure computers and other devices in Parliament. Firewalls and other security gates have been installed, says one of the officers but will provide further details on notice. Well punters, you may be surprised to learn that on a change of government, we taxpayers kindly stump up for a complete changeover of crockery and cutlery. After all, it is a matter of Labor/Liberal germs, no returns. Labor senators Faulkner and Wong are questioning parliamentary services on the $80,000 cost to make the change. Faulkner says while he realises prime ministers may have "wielded the odd knife", he was unaware a whole changeover was necessary. He can understand the odd replacement for breakages. Mills says it has been the protocol. It appears there is a memorandum of understanding between parliamentary services and the department of finance on where the money comes from. We would have thought a good old fashioned wash was all that is required. Any suggestions on the crockery styles of the various parties? Senator Faulkner appreciates there is a lot of movement when government changes. He wants to know the cost of the furniture changes and renovations as well as whether they are in keeping with the "heritage of the building". This all harks back to the criticism of former prime minister John Howard for bringing in chesterfield lounges, which were not considered by Labor to be in keeping with the design and heritage of the new Parliament house. Parliamentary services department secretary Carol Mills says upgrading works since the election has cost $62,344, which includes carpet replacement, patching and painting in both the offices of the PM and Opposition. Senator Faulkner compliments Tony Abbott for maintaining the renos in keeping with the heritage but wants to know where Howard's chesterfields went. Unlike Elvis, the chesterfield lounges never left the building. "They are in an office," says the Mills rather mysteriously. Murmur, murmur. It seems social services minister, Kevin Andrew has taken ownership of the chesterfields. Faulkner wants to know who dropped the ball on the chesterfields and whether minister Andrews would be taken for re-education on the heritage of the building. Secretary of the department of Parliament Carol Mills is facing questions from Labor Senator John Faulkner about 361 terracotta pots which have gone missing from Parliament House. The pots, which take two big blokes to move, have "gone west" as Faulkner says. Mills can't explain but says she has put in place a system to ensure it doesn't happen in the future. Public servants may be checking online auction sites at this very moment. Senate estimates continue apace. Senator Lisa Singh is asking about the Coalition's opposition to the appointment of ABC journalist Barrie Cassidy to the board of the Old Parliament house. Attorney General George Brandis says while he did not wish to reflect on Cassidy's reputation, he found the appointment "irregular". Notwithstanding reports on Friday that Tony Abbott was poised to veto the sale of GrainCorp to Archer Daniels Midland, the prime minister ruled out any intervention on the weekend. It was entirely a matter for the Treasurer Joe Hockey. No matter what happens though, Abbott fully appreciates the importance of foreign investment as does Sri Lanka, where the prime minister was speaking. Australian businesses including Iluka mining and Crown Casinos are hoping to invest in Sri Lanka. I know the Sri Lankan Government is very happy to have those investments. We are happy - very happy - to have foreign investment in Australia. It does have to be the right investment, not the wrong investment. At the weekend, the government announced an inquiry by the Productivity Commission into child care. On Sunday, Tony Abbott said the announcement followed through on an election commitment, which sought to develop a system that was more responsive to the needs of parents in a 24/7 workforce. The government had not had a "serious look" at the childcare system for 20 years and it was needed, said Abbott, to enhance participation and boost productivity in the workforce. No doubt, the first lady Margie, who runs a childcare centre, will have lots of advice on the topic. In the meantime, Sarah Murdoch was in Parliament speaking about child and youth well being. In honour of our former prime minister Kevin Rudd, the ABC has been inspired by our very own Mary Hamilton to develop a series of hand Gifs. Rudd, who announced his retirement last week, was a master of the hand gesture and here is the collection for your viewing pleasure. Lower House is opening now. Labor's Chris Hayes is talking on white ribbon day, a campaign to combat domestic violence. Foreign affairs minister Julie Bishop and defence minister Senator David Johnston announced this morning they are off to Washington on November 20 for the 2013 Australia-United States Ministerial Consultations (Ausmin) hosted by US Secretary of State John Kerry and Secretary of Defense Chuck Hagel. Ausmin is a forum for strengthening bilateral cooperation on foreign policy, defence and strategic matters between Australia and the United States. While we are considering matters Brandis, George has declared himself minister for national security after what he said was a "serious error of judgement" by former prime minister Julia Gillard in suggesting the 9/11 decade was coming to an end. Clerk of the Senate, Rosemary Laing, is telling estimates about the Attorney General George Brandis' famous bookshelves. Laing says the shelves cost $6957 which she thought was reasonable value. Given that those shelves were groaning under the weight of tomes on the Tudor histories and the biography of Trotsky, the Senate could hardly shoot down to Ikea to fill the void. Good morning, Here we are the second week of sitting in the 44th Parliament. Prime Minister Tony Abbott has been in Sri Lanka for the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting, commonly known as Chogm. Australia has given Sri Lanka an early Christmas present of two retired Australian bay class patrol boats to "assist in combatting people smuggling operations". The move has drawn the ire of Labor and the Greens, who have said the move may stop the Sri Lankan government stopping genuine refugees seeking asylum. Abbott: I'm not saying that no-one who comes to Australia is ever going to be found to be a refugee. Of course I'm not saying that. What I am saying about Sri Lanka is that a great deal of progress has been made. Labor's Tony Burke: There may be some people who claim to be directly seeking asylum. Greens leader Christine Milne: 'I am devastated and heartbroken at the thought of Australia assisting a disgraced government to suppress and control its citizens.'' Immigration minister Scott Morrison has been up this morning answering his critics, pointing out the former Labor government was also considering providing patrol boats to Sri Lanka. Now I don't recall any hubbub at the time about that from the government and members of the Labor Party. The House will sit as normal today but a whole lot of nervous public servants have filled the Parliament for the first day of Senate estimates hearings. Right now, Labor's chief inquisitor Senator John Faulkner is questioning officials from the department of the Senate. After a weekend which saw rallies in support of action on climate change, Leader of the House Christopher Pyne has again urged Labor to respect their mandate as the House continues to consider the carbon tax repeal bills this morning. Opposition leader Bill Shorten said while Labor is prepared to compromise, they are not willing to do nothing. Science is on our side.

results.webTitle: Demos and the politics of science

fields.headline: Demos and the politics of science
fields.trailText: James Wilsdon: The think tank Demos is 20 years old this week. What contribution has it made to science policy?
fields.bodyText: The think tank Demos is celebrating its 20th birthday this week, and yesterday published a book of essays marking 'Twenty Years of Ideas'. At last night's launch, Geoff Mulgan, Demos' founding director, reflected on the fact that although Demos is often portrayed as having influenced the early years of New Labour, many of its ideas - for example, around behavioural economics, happiness, cultural policy and the coproduction of public services - have only recently entered the political mainstream. In his opening essay, Geoff Mulgan recalls how "Twenty years ago I and a band of collaborators set up Demos on a wing and a prayer, and £5,000 in the bank." Their motivation was frustration as much as idealism: a sense that society, culture and technologies were changing in a multitude of ways, but political institutions were failing to keep pace. As Geoff says: "That spirit was summed up in the name we chose. Demos was about re-empowering the 'demos', about spreading power, and promoting the idea that democracy itself needed innovation." I joined Demos in 2001 and ended up staying there for seven years. In all that time, there was rarely a dull moment in the Demos office. Occasionally we had to scrabble around to pay the wage bill or keep the lights on, but mostly it was huge fun working alongside such a talented bunch of colleagues, who delighted in taking a creative and at times iconoclastic approach to the big policy questions of the day. In my piece in the 20th birthday collection, I describe how we tried to apply Demos' core ideas to science and technology policy. The origins of what became Demos' science programme date back to the summer of 2003. That July, Tony Blair played host in London to the Progressive Governance conference, an annual schmoozefest for centre-left leaders and thinkers. Bill Clinton, Thabo Mbeki, Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva and Helen Clark topped the bill, and 600 participants from thirty countries took part. The conference was originally conceived as a celebration of the international reach of 'third way' thinking, but these ambitions had been derailed by the decision to invade Iraq in March 2003, and the fierce debate that this provoked on the left and among the wider public. As a result, the conference had an uneasy air: like a party where the Blairite hardcore were determined to keep dancing, even though the music had stopped and the house lights had been switched back on. Demos wasn't one of the organisers of the conference – Peter Mandelson and Policy Network were firmly in the driving seat – but Tom Bentley (then Demos' Director) and I were asked to draft two of the policy papers that framed the agenda (and which Tony Giddens subsequently edited and published as a book, The Progressive Manifesto ). With controversies over genetically modified (GM) crops and the measles, mumps and rubella (MMR) vaccine still fresh in people's minds, my paper (co-authored with Rebecca Willis) took technology, risk and the environment as its theme. It reflected on lessons from GM and MMR for the development and diffusion of other emerging technologies. We took nanotechnologies as a particular focus: a fast-growing research field ripe with scientific, technological and economic potential, but where recent critical noises from, among others, Prince Charles, Bill Joy (the cofounder of Sun Microsystems) and a handful of environmental groups had led some to predict that it could become 'the next GM'. Our paper sketched a more progressive politics of technology, able to steer a sensible, precautionary path between uncritical technophilia (displayed at times by New Labour), and the outright rejection of certain technologies by the green and anti-globalisation movements. "How", we asked, "can the potential of new technologies be harnessed to wider projects of economic and social renewal without giving rise to such negative and polarised responses? Are there new approaches to the development and diffusion of new technologies that are more responsive to social and ethical concerns, and can move the site of public debate further upstream within research and development processes?" It was an example of Demos doing what it often does best: acting as an intermediary between the worlds of academia and policy, by providing a digestible summary of arguments which scholars (in this case, those working on the sociology of risk and in science and technology studies) had made over recent years. It was a topical theme: the following year, these issues would receive a more detailed treatment in The Death of Environmentalism, by the US environmentalists Ted Nordhaus and Michael Shellenberger, which led to the creation of the Breakthrough Institute, and provoked a debate about green attitudes towards new technologies which continues to rage today (for example, over recent remarks by the green campaigner Mark Lynas in support of GM crops). The novelty of the Demos work was in elaborating the idea of 'upstream public engagement', a phrase that Brian Wynne, a sociologist of science, had used in a couple of academic papers, but which had not yet found its way into the policy discourse. The idea was simple: rather than waiting for new technologies to be so advanced that development and commercialisation trajectories were fixed (which was arguably one of the causes of public disquiet over GM crops and foods) the locus of public engagement should move 'upstream' so that broader dialogue and debate could help to inform the direction of funding and policy for research and innovation. This was also the only time I've benefited from prime ministerial peer review. Having circulated a first draft, we were surprised to receive a three-page handwritten commentary from Tony Blair, written as he modestly put it "from the perspective of a policymaker grappling with these issues". He touched on lessons from MMR, and conceded that: "In respect of issues like GM, it is possible to proceed more deliberatively. But it needs real public engagement and it needs the science to be more accessible. Unless it becomes so, even the most 'public' debate can be a mixture of the dedicated, the expert and the cranky." Encouraged by the reception for this paper, we decided to write a fuller account of the case for upstream public engagement, which Demos published as See-through Science in 2004. This prompted a supportive editorial in Nature and sparked interest (and some opposition) from science bodies, research councils and policy makers in the UK and further afield. We even managed to get the idea of 'upstream engagement' included in Gordon Brown's 10-Year Framework for Science and Innovation, which was published that same year. See-through Science became the first in a series of Demos projects on the politics of science and technology, which included The Public Value of Science (2005), Better Humans? (2005) Governing at the Nanoscale (2006), The Received Wisdom (2006) and The Slow Race (2006). We also facilitated several public engagement exercises (including Nanodialogues (2007)), and broadened our international scope to analyse science and innovation policy in emerging economies (through the series of Atlas of Ideas studies on China, India, Brazil and South Korea). It can be hard for think-tanks to claim responsibility for particular impacts, given that their role is often to diffuse and persuade others to take ownership of ideas. But, couched in all the usual caveats, I think Demos' science programme had an influence in at least two ways. First, it helped to build a consensus among UK science policy makers and research councils that public engagement had a constructive role to play. This unlocked new sources of funding (for example, the Sciencewise programme) and encouraged a flowering of public dialogue exercises which continue today, particularly in emerging fields like synthetic biology and geoengineering, where the notion of 'upstream engagement' remains helpful. Second, Demos helped to open up science and innovation policy to more diverse voices. Ten years ago, the field was largely the preserve of research funders and learned societies (with a handful of academic centres like SPRU at Sussex University and PREST at Manchester University). None of the mainstream political think-tanks worked on these issues. Now, the terrain is far noisier and more contested, with a host of organisations, commentators and bloggers regularly piling into debates over science funding, communication and the social and political dilemmas raised by new technologies. Demos played a small but significant part in this shift. James Wilsdon is professor of science and democracy at the University of Sussex, and worked at Demos from 2001 to 2008, first as head of strategy, then as head of science and innovation. He is on twitter @jameswilsdon.

results.webTitle: Boris Johnson speaks to Conservative conference – politics live blog

fields.headline: Boris Johnson speaks to Conservative conference – politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the Conservative conference in Manchester, including George Osborne announcing his 'work for benefits' plan in his speech, and Ukip's Nigel Farage speaking on the fringe
fields.bodyText: Well, that was a slightly underpowered performance from the London mayor, although only by his own standards – I had to sit through speeches by transport secretary Patrick McLoughlin and Michael Fallon, the small business minister, this morning and believe me I don’t recommend it. Politically, his key message seemed to be that a vote for Ukip was a vote for Ed Miliband. Policy-wise, he repeated a call for more fiscal autonomy for local government. But his call for more house-building chimed with a key policy announced by the man he spent the most time mocking, Ed Miliband, whose Labour party promised to build 200,000 homes a year by 2020 last week. But the crowd got what they wanted: Boris catching himself out exaggerating the completion of the Crossrail project and murmuring that Brussels wives were “bumping off their husbands with poisoned profiteroles”. They seemed contended as they filed out into the Mancunian evening. And that’s what I’m going to do now too. Join Andrew Sparrow from the crack of dawn tomorrow for more live coverage, including Boris’s main conference speech in the morning, and other contributions from minsters Iain Duncan Smith, Michael Gove and Jeremy Hunt. Enjoy. What is the definition of a millisecond, Boris asks? It is the time it takes for Ed Miliband to carry out the orders of union boss Len McCluskey. What is the definition of a milimetre? The distance Miliband will deviate from union orders. What is the definition of a millipede? “Some sort of leftwing insect.” His message to Mrs Farage is that she should not vote for Ukip because if so the country will sleepwalk to a Labour government. “Ukip if you want to. David Cameron is not for kipping. Unless of course he is at his sister-in-law’s house.” (That’s a reference to this amusing picture.) Another partial standing ovation as Boris concludes his remarks with the promise the Tories will win in 2015. Last year in London Conservatives showed that we could win again, Johnson says (meaning himself). One thing that would help Conservatives in urban England would be to give local government more responsibility for the tax that is raised in their cities, he says. That would help answer the question: “What have the England got out of devolution – huh? The Scots have got fiscal autonomy. The Welsh have got … whatever the Welsh have got.” The No 1 issue for young Londoners is housing, Boris says. “The best solution always and everywhere is to build homes”, as he has done, he says. But he needs to do more, he says. He says he has published his “2020 vision” explaining areas of the city, “a Michelin guide for the salivating investor”. He says he has “virtually got agreement from the Treasury” to “four track” the West Anglia line, “creating a new commuter railway”. He mentions a number of other improvements to the London Underground currently under way. The cable car near the O2 “is the only piece of transport infrastructure in London” that will cover both its capital and revenue costs in the next six years – unlike HS2, he says. In London, the Crossrail train system "is virtually complete ... Well, no, it's not virtually complete; that's an exaggeration". But it's on time, he says. He says Yvette Cooper, the shadow home secretary, made a "ludicrous comparison" between London and Rio. "Great respect to Rio, I know you can be complacent about crime", but the murder rate in London is down to the lowest level since 1969, "which I think is before Yvette Cooper was born," he says. You are 20 times more likely to be murdered in Rio, Johnson says. You are more than twice as likely to be murdered "in sleepy old Brussels! Heaven knows what they're getting up to in Brussels. Bumping off their husbands with poisoned profiteroles ..." Boris opens proceedings by explaining how Nigel Farage's wife recently invited him to address the Ukip conference. But he didn't want to because Ukip might put "Miliband in power!" he says. Boos from the crowd. He says Ed Miliband and Ed Balls belong to the Costa Concordia school of economic management, and apologises if that is tasteless. The crowd love it. Boris appears to a partial standing ovation. The video starts with the union flag and the "word" "Bozza-riffic". Boris is shown cycling, making speeches about the Olympics, doing the Mobot, making a speech about gangs, playing tennis, looking quite red in the face, promising that Battersea Power Station will be redeveloped, promising to cut CO2 emissions, and scoring a basket with his back turned, while the Killers' All These Things That I've Done plays on the soundtrack. Paul Goodman of ConservativeHome is introducing Boris Johnson now. He says there is going to be a promotional video next. Ukip leader Nigel Farage's rival evening fringe event seems to have been memorable too: The title of tonight's event is "Destination signs from Manchester - how London drives the UK economy", which is not the kind of message that usually attracts a sell-out crowd in Manchester, it's fair to say. Borismania seems to be taking hold ... Hi, Paul Owen here taking the reins for the next hour or so. Last year Boris Johnson appeared at his fringe rally from the back of the hall like Bono while delirious Tories chanted: "Bo-ris! Bo-ris!" George Osborne and Michael Gove do not get this kind of reception - and neither does David Cameron, a fact that was widely noted that year, when speculation Boris was planning a coup was at its height. Such speculation has calmed down for now, but the Tories haven't, much, and tonight they are queued up as far as the eye can see to glimpse the roguish demagogue in person. What can I say? I'm looking forward to it myself. Politics aside (an approach of which Johnson would doubtlessly approve), and when he is on form, Boris is the most entertaining and charismatic politician in Britain today - although Nigel Farage seems to be trying to give him a run for his money. I'll be live-blogging it here. • Labour has said that no one will believe George Osborne's pledge to keep the budget in surplus from the middle of the next parliament. Responding to the key announcement in Osborne's speech (see 1.29am), Rachel Reeves, the shadow chief secretary to the Treasury, said: "[Osborne's] failure on growth means that far from balancing the books by 2015 as he promised, borrowing is now set to be £96 billion. And for all the warm words about capital spending he is cutting it in 2015." • Theresa May has confirmed that the government will bring forward new laws to tackle modern-day slavery and end the "disgusting trade in human beings" that is carried out in Britain. • Andy Burnham, the shadow health secretary, has accused the BBC of providing only "cursory" news coverage of the march by an estimated 50,000 people in Manchester against austerity cuts to the NHS. • Gawain Towler, a Ukip press officer, has apologised to a journalist covering Nigel Farage's visit to the conference after describing her in a text message accidentally sent to the wrong person as being "of some form of ethnic extraction". • Chris Grayling, the justice secretary, has said that details of the Conservatives' plans to get rid of the Human Rights Act will be published in the new year. That's all from me for today. My colleague Paul Owen is taking over now. He will be blogging from Boris Johnson's fringe meeting at 6pm. Here's Frances O'Grady, the TUC general secretary, on George Osborne's speech. Having been forced to face up to the UK’s cost of living crisis, all the Chancellor offered today was more the of the same failed austerity policies that has made families thousands of pounds worse off. The chancellor has shown his priorities by using the proceeds from a bedroom tax that is forcing disabled people out of their homes to give a minority of married couples an extra £3.85 a week. Ordinary families will now be worried how they’ll be made to pay for the unfunded fuel duty freeze. The Help to Work proposals are an admission that the work programme – which is helping just 1 in 25 disabled people into work – is failing. But instead of offering positive support, such as a job guarantee to help people into proper paid work, the chancellor has chosen to punish a small number of struggling long-term dole claimants. Here's the text of the speech from Chris Grayling, the justice secretary. And here are the key points. • Grayling said that in the new year he would publish a document explaining the Conservatives' plan to get rid of the Human Rights Act. And a draft bill would be published later in 2014, he said. We will scrap Labour’s Human Rights Act. We will make sure that with legal rights go legal responsibilities. Our Supreme Court should be in Britain and not in Strasbourg. And a future Conservative Government will do whatever it takes to make sure it is. • He said he would fight attempts to create a single justice system for Europe. There is a very clear intent in Brussels to build a single justice system for Europe. With European laws, European punishments, European judges. With European decisions over issues that which have always been the preserve of our courts. Even a European Justice Minister with the power to dictate the way justice works right across Europe. I have just one thing to say to that proposition. No. It doesn’t have to happen. And it won’t happen on my watch. • He said he was going to review the way prisoners qualify for automatic early release. How many of you are frustrated by the idea that when a judge tells an offender they’ve got ten years, in fact it means they are guaranteed to only spend five years in prison? No matter how they behave or what they do on the inside, whether they have tried to turn their lives around or not … it means five years, and then automatically they’re out. Do you know, the last Labour Government actually went so far as to enshrine in law the automatic release for all prisoners given fixed sentences? As I've said before, I don’t like the concept of automatic early release. I don’t like the idea that from the off, a serious offender knows he’s only going to serve half his sentence. Looking at that issue is my next task. • He said he had banned Sky TV across the prison estate. (Who said the Tories never take a stand against Murdoch?) • He said that from today the rules were being changed so that "if a prisoner deliberately smashes up his cell, he’ll pay the bill himself". Here's some more reaction to George Osborne's speech. From Stewart Hosie, the SNP Treasury spokesman The last thing Scotland needs is a lecture from a Tory Chancellor whose talk of achieving a budget surplus is fatally undermined by his own failed austerity agenda that is choking off prospects for sustained growth. We have had too many fantasy forecasts from Mr Osborne already. Securing growth and getting the economy moving is also the best way to cut the benefits bill. A clear majority of people in Scotland want decisions over tax and welfare to be in the hands of the Scottish Parliament, not Westminster, and only a Yes vote in next year’s referendum will secure that. FromAlexander Jackman, head of policy at the Forum of Private Business The speech was high on ambition and optimism about the economy, a credible economic plan that was well articulated. What it lacked was a slew of new policies for small businesses, largely because the government feels that its current ones are working. The fuel duty freeze was very welcome, but we don't want the money for this taken from other business subsidies or tax rises. The chancellor’s actions over last few years have earned him the right to promote the government’s success, but many businesses still need continued government backing to take advantage of the economic uplift. From Mark Littlewood, director general at the Institute of Economic Affairs George Osborne is still failing to tackle government spending sufficiently. Despite a return to growth, it is likely the government will continue to run a deficit until the end of the decade. The Conservative Party is also failing to produce a credible plan to address rising living costs. Rather than the dangerous Help to Buy scheme, the government should be focusing on why the cost of housing is so high in the first place. Supply-side measures such as planning liberalisation and deregulation should be adopted immediately. Only measures like this will ensure that the hardworking people they wish to represent are able to see their pay packets increase and living standards improve. My colleague Damian Carrington has posted his thoughts on George Osborne's speech on his environment blog. Here's an excerpt. Believing one thing while arguing another is, unfortunately, a skill many politicians have at times to deploy. But in this case, Osborne's wilful blindness to the green economy, despite it ticking every box he demands for every other economic policy, is doing great harm to the "Britain of the future" that he said on Monday he wants to build. Paul Owen has just been to see Francis Maude, the Cabinet Office minister, who had some interesting things to say on devolution and the decentralisation of power. Maude said that “most people’s idea of devolution is devolution to the level at which they themselves operate”. He used the example of the Scottish government, which has “undevolved services to the Scottish government level”, the police and fire service for example. “Alex Salmond’s idea of where all power should reside is with him,” Maude said. He said devolving power was hard. “People talk about letting go of power from the centre. That’s like letting go of the chewing gum that’s stuck to your hand. You have to peel it away … Genuinely you have to push the power away.” He said he was keen to revive the idea of elected mayors. “I would just do it.” Even in those areas where voters had rejected the concept through referendums? “Probably not – that might look tactless,” he conceded. But he added: “We think it’s a good thing to have [elected] mayors – they make local government much more vivid … much more directly accountable.” The same was true of elected police and crime commissioners, he said, although he was not surprised the turn-out for these elections had been so low. “It was a mistake to have the elections not at the time of the local elections.” He could not remember why that decision had been taken. “Some coalition-related reason … It wasn’t Vince on this occasion, but it normally is,” he added, in one of the Tory conference’s many gratuitous digs at Lib Dem business secretary Vince Cable. Here are the main points from Theresa May's speech. You can read the full text here on the Conservative website. • May said the immigration bill being published soon would make it easier for the government to deport people, including criminals from the country. First, we’re going to cut the number of appeal rights. At the moment, the system is like a never-ending game of snakes and ladders, with almost 70,000 appeals heard every year. The winners are foreign criminals and immigration lawyers – while the losers are the victims of these crimes and the public. So we’re going to cut the number of appeal rights from seventeen to four, and in doing so cut the total number of appeals by more than half. Last year, human rights were cited in almost 10,000 immigration appeal cases. So the second thing we will do is extend the number of non-suspensive appeals. This means that where there is no risk of serious and irreversible harm, we should deport foreign criminals first and hear their appeal later. And third, the Immigration Bill will sort out the abuse of Article Eight – the right to a family life – once and for all. This is used by thousands of people to stay in Britain every year. The trouble is, while the European Convention makes clear that a right to a family life is not absolute, judges often treat it as an unqualified right. • She said the European court of human rights made here "crazy". I was told a story by one of our immigration officials who was there when [Abu] Qatada finally got on the plane. As the official signed off the last of the paperwork, Qatada looked at him and asked, “is Crazy May flying with me?” I admit I was crazy – crazy with the European Court of Human Rights – and I know I wasn’t the only one. Here was a foreign terror suspect, wanted for the most serious crimes in his home country, and we were told time and again – thanks to human rights law – we couldn’t deport him. • She said that if Britain had to leave the European convention on human rights "to fix our human rights laws", it should do that. • She said she would publish changes to police stop and search rules before the end of the year. We cannot ignore public concern about whether it’s used fairly. There are more than a million stop-and-searches recorded every year, but only about nine per cent result in an arrest. If you’re black or from an ethnic minority, you’re up to seven times more likely to be stopped and searched than if you’re white. And according to the Inspectorate of Constabulary, more than a quarter of stop and searches might be carried out illegally. I’m concerned about this for two reasons. When stop and search is misused, it wastes police time. And when it’s used unfairly, it does enormous damage to public trust in the police. We’ve just completed a public consultation into stop and search, and I will announce changes in policy by the end of this year. But today, I want the message to go out from this hall that nobody should ever be stopped just on the basis of the colour of their skin. • She criticised the Lib Dems' stance on immigration bonds. The latest policy [the Lib Dems are] fighting is immigration bonds. It’s a simple idea – the government should be able to take a £3,000 deposit from temporary migrants and return it when they leave. If they overstay their visa, they’ll lose their money. Bonds were in our manifesto at the last election. But the Lib Dems suddenly announced that it was their idea. Then they said they were against them. Then they said they were for them – but only to help more immigrants to come here. Now they say they’re against them after all. They were for them, then they were against them… then they were for them, and now they’re against them. • She said the government would change the law so that the starting point for anyone convicted of murdering a police officer is a whole life tariff. • She said she was taking action to deal to deal with the "minority" of police officers who were not honest. The vast majority of police officers are driven by the best possible motives and they do fantastic work. But I’m not prepared to allow a minority to erode public trust in the police. So we’re creating a national register of officers who’ve been struck off, we’re making sure officers can’t avoid disciplinary hearings by retiring early, and we’re beefing up the Complaints Commission so that, for serious cases, the police will no longer investigate themselves. • She confirmed the government would soon publish a modern slavery bill. Without the EU, a Britsh prime minister would only have the clout of a "senior senator from Arkansas" in the US, Kenneth Clarke, the minister without portfolio, told a fringe meeting at the Conservative conference. He was talking specifically in the context of the EU/US trade talks, but also making a wider point. Here's the quote. I can go to Washington and lobby congressmen and I probably have the standing of a junior senator from Arkansas. That probably overstates it - I'm sure it does. The prime minister probably is only the senior statesman from Arkansas. When he gets down to this kind of thing, the nitty gritty of pork barrel, interest rates, public procurement ... you need the clout of the European Union. It's the very attraction of the European market, it's the politics of the European Union, America's key allies, which enable us to look the Americans eye-ball to eye-ball, agree with the ones that share our views on open trade and economics but punch above our weight in negotiations to make sure, as I'm trying to ensure, British interests are looked after in the course of the whole thing. Gawain Towler, a Ukip press officer, has been accused of using "racially insensitive" language. As the Evening Standard's Nicholas Cecil reports, Towler mistakenly texted a photographer — rather than a member of the team waiting for Nigel Farage in Manchester — with a phrase referring to the Evening Standard’s Kiran Randhawa. He said: James, my fault but I told the Standard that Nigel would be arriving at approx 10.30 this morning. They have called and I expect a snapper and a female journalist (of some form of ethnic extraction) at Piccadilly. Am sorry. Possible more than one snapper as they hunt in packs. After this was revealed, several Tory MPs condemned the phrase he used. Nick de Bois, secretary of the 1922 backbench committee, said: “There’s no room in any walk of life, let alone politics, for this type of racially insensitive language.” Towler subsequently apologised to Randhawa. He said that he was going to use the phrase BaME (black and minority ethnic) but that he thought this would not be clear. “If in any way Kiran is upset, I’m terribly sorry," Towler said. "I’m finding this absolutely absurd because it is so evidently alien to who I am and what I am." Nigel Farage, the Ukip leader, said that Towler was not being racist and that he is married to an Asian woman and that they have a mixed-race daughter. My colleague Mona Chalabi is looking at the cost of the fuel duty freeze on her Reality Check blog. She thinks it is probably not quite as significant as it sounds. Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, was speaking about the prospects for the 2015 election at a fringe meeting at lunchtime. My colleague Randeep Ramesh was there. He's sent me this. If the prime minister has a praetorian guard, Jeremy Hunt is likely to be found among their number. Smooth-talking and slinky-suited the health secretary took to outlining the Cameroon strategy in the light of the emergence of Red Ed. Speaking to a packed fringe, organised by the Forty Group – those MPs with the narrowest of majorities, he spoke of a five point plan for candidates to rout the opposition. The most striking was the Tory recasting of Ed Miliband. Hunt said that the message about the Labour leader had to shift from him being "hopeless to being dangerous" as the public has sympathy for “hopeless people because they are seen as underdogs”. It was a backhanded compliment and as well as an admission that Cameroons may have underestimated Miliband. However Hunt was keen to reclaim the ground lost the Labour's “cost of living agenda”. First there was the obligatory reference to “hard working families”, a term so over-used that Matthew Parris quipped the three words should run together as one. Hunt, undeterred went on, to say that candidates on the doorstep had to change the message from the “Tory party being best for the country to being best for you and your family.” However for Tories sitting on tiny majorities the message was that the ground war could be won in part by charisma. Hunt said in his mind strong local campaigns were worth 2,000 votes – crucial in any marginal. However in the next big test, the European elections next year, Hunt warned would be “very difficult” with voters able to punish the government as “cost-free politics”. Hunt was creaseless in dealing with activist concerns. HS2 got Hunt's vote because it would benefit northern cities and prove the Tories were not “in hock to London and the south east”. That Tory high command would not be getting into a fist fight with Nick Clegg and Labour over the mansion tax, which Hunt was against, as it would give the other parties a chance to say the Tories were “on the side of people with £2m houses”. Tellingly he said Conservatives would not be following the Republican party into oblivion by giving the impression that immigants should “not be here. We have to be very careful in the way we talk about immigration”. Hunt's lining himself up as a thinker in Tory ranks, pushing the idea of the Conservatives as the party of workers with the opposition backing freeloaders. While activists were left with much to ponder, the impression was that the health secretary had much more to say. Here is some reaction to George Osborne's speech From Rachel Reeves, the shadow chief secretary to the Treasury After three wasted years, George Osborne still has nothing concrete to say on helping families with the rising cost of living. The few things he has said are already unravelling. His panicky announcement on fuel duty turns out to be just an aspiration if he can find savings elsewhere. This is on top of £1.6bn of unfunded commitments already made by ministers during the party conference season. The Institute for Fiscal Studies has said the marriage tax break will help just 28% of married couples and only 15% of families with children. And his work scheme turns out to be less ambitious than Labour's compulsory jobs guarantee. As for George Osborne's pledges on capital spending and the deficit, nobody will believe a word he says. His failure on growth means that far from balancing the books by 2015 as he promised, borrowing is now set to be £96 billion. And for all the warm words about capital spending he is cutting it in 2015. From business From Simon Walker, director general of the Institute of Directors We welcome the renewed commitment to deficit reduction, and the ambitious aim to generate a surplus in the next parliament. The British government has only run a surplus in seven of the last 50 years. Breaking government addiction to debt and achieving a surplus in public finances is the most important ambition any administration can have. From John Longworth, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce It was heartening to hear George Osborne bring the debate back to our incomplete economic recovery, and the importance of getting the fundamentals right for the future. If Britain's economy is to go from being just good to truly great, the focus the chancellor exhibited in his speech must be maintained relentlessly for years to come. On the fuel duty freeze From Professor Stephen Glaister, director of the RAC Foundation Transport is the single biggest area of household expenditure bar none and our own research shows that 800,000 of the poorest households are in transport poverty, spending a quarter or more of their income on running a car. This proposal will be welcomed but let's not forget that the Chancellor still takes 60% of the price of a litre of fuel in the form of taxation. From AA president Edmund King Two thirds of drivers still have to cut back on journeys or other household expenditure due to the high cost of petrol so a freeze on duty would be welcome. However, it is worth remembering that every time there is a spike in fuel prices the Chancellor brings in money due to the 20% VAT rate on petrol and diesel. This is not exactly a give-away as even with a duty freeze the Chancellor is still raking in approximately 60% of the pump price in duty and VAT. On the "work for benefits" plan I posted some reaction to this earlier - see 11.11am. Here's some more. From Alison Garnham, chief executive of the Child Poverty Action Group [This is a Groundhog Day welfare policy, with the same old headline-candy rhetoric about getting tough on claimants, but nothing addressing the root causes of failure of the labour market to provide enough decent jobs. We already have an intensive regime of support called the Work Programme, and today's announcement is an admission it's failing; but instead of putting it right the plan is to shunt the people it's failed onto yet another scheme afterwards. Andy Burnham, the shadow health secretary, has written to Lord Patten, the chair of the BBC Trust, to ask why the BBC gave so little coverage to the pro-NHS, anti-austerity march outside the Conservative conference in Manchester yesterday. His letter is on the LabourList website. Here's an extract. I attended the event and was proud to walk alongside doctors, nurses and other front-line NHS staff from all parts of the country who had given up their Sunday in the hope of making their voice heard. From my observation, NHS staff made up a significant proportion of the large crowd. It was therefore a real surprise to me to return home to find what I consider only cursory coverage of the event on BBC news bulletins. As far I could see, there was no specific coverage and it was only mentioned in the wider context of Conservative Party Conference. There was no explanation as to why people were there in such large numbers nor direct interviews with participants to find out what had prompted them to travel so far on a Sunday. By any reckoning, this was a major national protest and it seems to me that the BBC’s coverage did not reflect this. Indeed, other major news channels seemed to reach a different editorial judgement, covering the story in more depth and interviewing participants. Theresa May, the home secretary, is speaking in the hall now. She has just said the next Conservative manifesto will include a pledge to scrap the Human Rights Act. I'll post a summary when I've seen the full text. • George Osborne has signalled that public spending will be at the heart of the 2015 election campaign by announcing that the Conservatives are going to propose rules saying the government should run a budget surplus when the economic is growing. The Tories are challenging Labour to say whether they would back the plans, which would require further cuts to welfare spending. Osborne also announced that he intends to freeze fuel duty until the general election. You can read his speech in full here. • Nigel Farage, the Ukip leader, has appealed to Conservatives to vote Ukip in next year's European elections at a conference fringe meeting. He also became embroiled in a row with the Conservative Eurosceptic Bill Cash, who said Ukip could cost the Tories up to 60 seats. • Osborne has said that Conservative candidates will not be allowed to form local pacts with Ukip. • Theresa May, the home secretary, is preparing to announce sweeping new curbs on the appeal rights of illegal migrants, foreign prisoners and others facing deportation. • Patrick McLoughlin, the transport secretary, has said London-based critics of the HS2 high-speed rail scheme should "stop moaning". • Boris Johnson, the London mayor, has launched a campaign to take powers from Whitehall to allow the capital and other major English cities to have greater financial freedom. As the Press Association reports, the "historic and significant" plan would give the leaders of England's biggest cities powers over tax and spending similar to those devolved to Scotland. The cities, representing around half the population of England, are demanding control over property taxes including council tax and stamp duty in their areas, rather than allowing the money to flow to Treasury coffers. London has joined forces with the "Core Cities" Birmingham, Bristol, Leeds, Liverpool, Manchester, Newcastle, Nottingham and Sheffield in the power grab. Johnson said: That London's government is joining with England's largest cities to call for change is an historic and significant move. It's a partial but positive and practical answer to the conundrum about English devolution and I believe it is good not just for the cities involved but for the country at large. • Alan Duncan, the international development minister, has said that David Cameron's defeat in the House of Commons in Syria has had "serendipitous" effects. (See 2pm.) • Owen Paterson, the environment secretary, has said there would be some advantages to an increase in temperature predicted by the United Nations including fewer people dying of cold in winter and the growth of certain crops further north. Outside the conference centre Nigel Farage, the Ukip leader, has been attracting a huge amount of attention at the Bruges Group fringe where he is speaking. Here are some Twitter highlights. Paul Owen reports: The international community is now in a “better place” on Syria due to the government’s losing its parliamentary vote on taking military action last month, international development minister Alan Duncan has said. Speaking at a fringe event at the Conservative conference in Manchester, Duncan said the UK would have been “perfectly justified to take action after an international treaty had been breached by the use of chemical weapons” apparently by the Assad, but “I think we are perhaps now in a better place than we might have expected to be for a number of reasons”. He said that as a result of the loss of the vote Russian “involvement in this chemical disarmament process should to a large extent guarantee their no further use.
