results.webTitle: Cameron and Miliband at PMQs: Politics live blog

fields.headline: Cameron and Miliband at PMQs: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including David Cameron and Ed Miliband at PMQs.
fields.bodyText: • A poll has shown that the British are less likely to support a military strike against Syria than the French or the Germans. Here's an extract from the ComRes news release. The French are more willing than the British or the Germans to launch missile strikes on Syria, but even there, only one in three people backs bombing. One out of five Germans is in favour of missile strikes, while the number is even lower in the United Kingdom, at 16%. (So, we're now less bellicose as nation than the French or the Germans? Truly, this is a defining moment for the national character.) • Boris Johnson, the mayor of London, has announced that he will consult on plans to impose a charge on lorries in London not fitted with equipment to protect cyclists. Here's an extract from his news release. Under national legislation, many HGVs, such as supermarket delivery lorries and the like, are fitted with sidebars or low skirts which protect cyclists from being dragged underneath the vehicle and crushed. However, construction lorries, tipper trucks, waste vehicles, cement mixers and certain other forms of HGV are exempt from these and other safety requirements. The rising number of such vehicles in London's building boom is a serious hazard to the growing number of cyclists, who now make up almost a quarter of all rush hour traffic in the centre. Of the nine cyclist deaths involving HGVs in 2011, seven involved construction lorries. The proposed London Safer Lorry Charge is partly modelled on the successful London Low Emission Zone, which charges up to £200 a day for commercial vehicles that do not meet tough emission standards. Anyone who fails to pay the charge faces an even higher penalty. The proposed charge would not cover buses, smaller commercial vehicles, cars or motorbikes. • The Department of Health has announced a £1bn IT investment programme. Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, said he wanted the NHS to go paperless by 2018. The headline £1bn figure includes £500m from the DoH, some of which has already been announced, with the rest coming from local health units. • Britain has fallen from eighth place to 10th place in the World Economic Forum's Global Competitiveness Index. Chuka Umunna, the shadow business secretary, said: "David Cameron took office promising to make Britain the best place in the world to start a business but things are getting harder, not easier, for our wealth creators under this out of touch government." That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. Here's an afternoon reading list. • Ally Fogg at Comment is free says the GMB decision marks the start of a battle for the soul of Labour. What we are seeing played out here is a battle for the very soul of the Labour party. It is easy to forget that it was formed precisely to give trades unions, the only meaningful representatives of working class people, a democratic voice in the parliamentary system as a bulwark and counterweight to the wealth and power of the ruling classes. While union membership has plummeted in recent decades, union engagement still, at least in theory, serves that function. Without it, British party politics in the UK will be left entirely in thrall to the pockets of ultra-rich individuals and corporate interests ... To a larger extent, it is an inevitable consequence of the victory of global capitalism in co-opting and enclosing the process of politics to its own ends over the past four decades. It is possible that the next few weeks, beginning with the union congress in Bournemouth followed by the Labour party conference in Brighton, will map out not only the legacy of Ed Miliband as Labour leader, but the future of the democratic left (if such a thing still exists) for decades to come. • Hopi Sen on his blog says that, while Labour criticises the lobbying bill for the way it will curb campaigning on charities, the party is planning to change its own rule in a way that would restrict the campaigning activities of pro-Labour groups. Yet, Labour friends, could I also remind us that we face our own unintended consequences? Unless decided otherwise, this Conference will see a motion to regulate the activity of organisations which seek to influence the Labour party. Here’s what the motion says: “Political organisations not affiliated or associated under a national agreement with the party, but whom engage in internal activity, shall be required to: i. Notify the national party of all legally reportable donations received. ii. Transfer 50% of all donations received beyond the first £25,000 per annum to the national Labour Party .... Now, this motion has widely been interpreted as simply an attempt to limit the activity of Progress. I am a member of the Progress strategy board, so might be expected to oppose it on those grounds. Yet in its alleged attempt to circumscribe Progress, the motion also restricts the activities of many other organisations. It is full of unintended, harmful, consequences. • Hélène Mulholland at the Guardian profiles Charles Walker, the Conservative MP with obsessive-compulsive disorder. Walker, 45, was part of a seminal political moment in June 2012 as one of four MPs to make the personal political in a Commons debate on mental health, tabled and led by fellow Conservative Nicky Morgan. He declared himself a "practising fruitcake" as he described living with obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), which for him manifests itself mainly in doing things in multiples of fours. He says he has never regretted the decision to go public. • David Cameron has suggested that failure to respond to Syria's use of chemical weapons could result in "armageddon" for its people. Speaking at PMQs, he confirmed that Britain will not take part in a military strike. But, in response to a question from the Tory MP Sir Peter Tapsell about the consequences of intervention, he implied that not responding to the chemical weapons attack could be disastrous. You have to put the Armageddon question the other way, which is that if no action is taken following President Obama's red line and if no action is taken following this appalling use of chemical weapons, you have to ask yourself what sort of Armageddon are the Syrian people going to be facing? Cameron also criticised Ed Miliband for not backing the government's motion on Syria last week after the government said that its motion, if passed, would not sanction a military strike. • Cameron has said that Britain will use all of Britain's diplomatic "muscle" to bring both sides together for peace talks in Syria. • Cameron has said that he has written to the new Iranian president, Hassan Rouhani, in an attempt to improve relations between London and Tehran. I have written to president Rouhani to raise a series of issues that need to be settled between Britain and Iran, and above all we need to see progress on what president Rouhani himself has said is important, which is trying to come to an agreement where Iran gives up the idea of nuclear weapons and in return we see some relief on sanctions. However, when Miliband and other Labour MPs pressed him to do more to involved Iran in talks relating to Syria, he also expressed caution. We have seen what this country, Iran, has been capable of in the recent past so we should go into these sorts of discussions very, very cautiously ... If we believe there's just some magical key to the Syrian conflict by suddenly adopting a totally different posture towards Iran, I don't think we will be making a very good decision. • The GMB union has said that it will cut its funding to the Labour party by more than £1m. Alan Johnson, the Labour former cabinet minster, told the World at One that he hoped the move was not just "petty retribution" [We can't] go on living a lie with these millions of people signed up as levy-paying members of the Labour party and treated as if they were members. I call them the ghosts in the machine. That’s bad for the party, it’s bad for the trade unions and Ed Miliband is absolutely right to seek to change this. And I just hope that this isn’t a piece of petty retribution by a trade union, and I would think that the GMB, as the great union I know they are, would be above that. • A senior UN official is in the UK to scrutinise the impact of the bedroom tax on the human rights of people in low-income households, it has been revealed. • Chris Grayling, the justice secretary, has has sounded the death knell for Dartmoor prison, one of Britain's oldest and most famous jails, and ordered the immediate closure of four others, including the historic Reading jail, where Oscar Wilde was incarcerated. As Alan Travis reports, in a major shakeup of prisons in England and Wales, Grayling has also ordered a feasibility study into building a second "superjail" in west London and confirmed that the first new £230m 2,000-place facility will be built in Wrexham, north Wales. Justice ministry officials said discussions would begin to end the lease on the 200-year-old Dartmoor prison, which opened in 1809 to hold Napoleonic prisoners of war. It was recently described by a chief inspector of prisons as the "prison that time forgot". (See 11.47am.) • The Ministry of Justice is preparing to announce significant concessions this week in its proposals to reform criminal legal aid, it has been revealed. As Owen Bowcott reports, speculation is growing that the government will back down on unpopular plans to introduce price-competitive tendering in contracts for solicitors to represent defendants in courts and police stations. • The Electoral Commission has revealed that candidates in the elections for police and crime commissioners spent a total of £2.1m in the final four weeks before the polls. As the Press Association reports, the 191 hopefuls paid out an average of £11,220 in the month before the elections in November, during which time spending and donations over £50 were monitored. Data published by the Electoral Commission showed that the candidate who spent most was Craig MacKinlay in Kent, paying out £98,751.46 for a campaign that was ultimately unsuccessful. The winning candidate, Ann Barnes, spent £64,676. • Sir Nicolas Bratza, the former president of the European court of human rights, has said that Britain's international reputation is being tarnished by political attacks on the Human Rights Act. • Lord Stern, the economist and climate change expert, has said that David Cameron's claim that fracking in the UK can drive down domestic gas prices is "baseless". • The Department for Work and Pensions has spent hundreds of millions of pounds on software for its flagship welfare project that may not be fit for purpose, the Guardian has been told. • Yvette Cooper, the shadow home secretary, has accused the government of adopting a "complacent" response to today's National Audit Office report showing that the Border Agency's focus on full passport inspections has led to other checks being reduced. In a Commons urgent question, she said Border Agency staff were not trying to catch stowaways. Mark Harper, the immigration minister, said Britain had "one of the most secure borders in the world". • Rachel Reeves, the shadow chief secretary to the Treasury, has said in a speech that average earnings in real temers have fallen by almost £1,500 a year since 2010. She also called for data on household disposable income to be published quarterly, alongside GDP figures, instead of annually, so as to improve the quality of public debate on living standards. • Cameron has said that he will raise Russia's anti-gay legislation when he meets Vladimir Putin at the G20 later this week. He made the announcement in a written answer. The government shares concerns about the growing restrictions on LGBT (lesbian, gay, bisexual, and transgender) freedoms in Russia. British officials at all levels have been lobbying on the issue of the LGBT law since the first regional anti-gay propaganda law was tabled in 2012. I have personally expressed my strong concern about human rights in Russia when I met President Putin in June and I will be raising my concerns on LGBT freedoms when I meet President Putin in St Petersburg this week. • Downing Street has confirmed that the Tory MP Jesse Norman has been dropped from the Number 10 policy board for not supporting the government in last week's Syria vote. (See 11.57am.) • A British soldier fired a volley of bullets into the "twitching" bodies of Iraqis after a firefight, a public inquiry has heard. As the Press Association reports, former army private Duncan Aston told the Al-Sweady Inquiry he also saw a fellow private stamp on the head of a dead Iraqi, and other comrades punch and kick a detainee. But he did not report the incidents, because he did not want to "grass" on his friends, he said. PMQs Verdict: Well, that was all very civilised. Predictions (from me and others - see 9.02am) that we were in for a nasty one turned out be be wide of the mark. Mostly they were talking constructively about the way forward in Syria, with Ed Miliband pressing for more engagement with Iran and David Cameron sounding cautious. Cameron was trying to needle Miliband by repeatedly stressing his "revulsion" at the use of chemical weapons (implying Miliband does not feel the same - Miliband rejected this firmly), and he criticised him for dividing the Commons last week. But these were muted attacks and the overall impression was one of the two leaders being grown-up and sensible. Given the enormity of what happened six days ago, and the passions it generated (see 9.02am), this is odd. Perhaps it's just a reversion to the norm, where foreign policy is, by fact and convention, a matter for bipartisan consensus. But perhaps it is because Cameron and Miliband are not entirely sure yet about how the Syria crisis will play out and (to be crude) which of them on this is going to "win". Last week's vote was obviously a humiliation for Cameron. But there is evidence some Labour figures are clearly uneasy about where their party has ended up on this (for more on this, read Mary Riddell in the Telegraph today) and Miliband was at pains today to stress that his MPs did not vote to shirk their responsibilities, only to avoid the rush to war. (What Labour MP not shirking their responsibilities might entail is not yet clear.) And Cameron was making the point that, while he will not ask MPs to vote for military intervention, he personally will be with the Americans in spirit when the missiles fall. This came out most clearly in Cameron's reply to Sir Peter Tapsell. Here are the quotes. Tapsell: As the Syrian tragedy has unfolded, I've always had the Armageddon question in the back of my mind .. if the Americans illegally bombard the Assad forces, and Assad legally invites the Russians in to degrade the rebels, what will Nato do? Cameron: The first point I would make is that we would never support illegal action. We debated and discussed this at some length last week and it is not the case the only way action can be legal is a UN resolution. We would only support action that would be legal, we would only support action that was proportionate. As I've said, Britain would not be taking part in this action but in a way you have to put the Armageddon question the other way, which is that if no action is taken following President Obama's red line and if no action is taken following this appalling use of chemical weapons, you have to ask yourself what sort of Armageddon are the Syrian people going to be facing? Labour's John Mann says wages have fallen by £1,500. Cameron says times have been tough. But for Labour to complain about this is like the arsonist complaining about the effects of the fire. That's it. PMQs is over. I'll post a summary in a moment. Julian Lewis, a Conservative, asks if Cameron will sign the contracts for the first two Trident replacement submarines before 2015 so that the Lib Dems cannot blackmail the Conservatives on this issue. Cameron says he agrees with Lewis about the importance of a Trident replacement. A Conservative-only government would go ahead with the replacement, he says. Labour's Barbara Keeley says the cost of school uniforms has risen to £285 as academies have insisted on branded uniforms. At some schools 70% of parents have had to take out loans to fund them. Cameron says he supports schools being able to have a robust uniform policy. Labour is raising this issue because it does not like free schools, he says. At some point it will have to admit that it is wrong on this. Cameron says Labour has "ranted and raved" about the spare room subsidy (his term for the bedroom tax). Will Labour reverse it? When no reply is forthcoming, he says Labour has nothing to say, and is weak too. Sir Peter Tapsell, a Conservative, asks what will happen if America illegally bombs Syria, and Syria then legally invites the Russians in to "degrade" the rebels? What will Britain do? Cameron says the government will not act illegally. And he says you should put the question the other way round. What will happen if the West does nothing? Labour's Stephen Doughty says working people will have lost £6,660 in real terms by the time of the election. Cameron says the only way to get living standards up is to get the economy moving. Cameron says Labour don't want to hear what he is saying on the economy. Labour's Lilian Greenwood asks what the government is doing to stand up to energy companies. Cameron says the government has legislated to force firms to put people on the lowest tariffs. Cameron says that at some stage Labour will have to admit that it was wrong on the economy. Even today Ed Balls is proposing more borrowing, he says. Bridget Phillipson, the Labour MP, asks if every two-year-old promised a nursery place under the government's new scheme will be able to take up a place. Cameron says the government is funding the scheme. Cameron says the government made tough decisions on the economy. Labour ducked every one of them, he says. Asked what he is doing to help food banks, Cameron says the government has allowed benefit offices to publicise them. Food banks wanted this under Labour, but Labour blocked the idea because they thought it would create bad publicity. The Conservative Jesse Norman asks about cancer funding in rural areas. Cameron says NHS England is looking at this. He also mentions the cancer drugs fund, which is "sadly" not followed in Wales. Labour's Joan Ruddock says an American strike now would squander the opportunities presented by the new Iranian leadership. Cameron says he respects the Commons vote. Britain will not play any part in the American action. But President Obama set a very clear red line. Asking him to step away from that would be very perilous, he says. Ruddock has a track record of supporting peace, he says. (She used to run CND.) But the government needs to take a tough line on chemical weapons. Cameron says the number of workless households is down. But Labour has opposed all the government's welfare reforms, he says. Labour's Jeremy Corbyn asks Cameron if he can be more positive about building better relations with Iran. Cameron says building a relationship with someone depends on what they do. The Iranian government was complicit in the smashing of Britain's embassy. Cameron says he has reached out be writing to the new Iranian president congratulating him on his election. Labour's Margaret Beckett asks why so many groups, including ConservativeHome, oppose the lobbying bill. Cameron says there is a lobbying bill run by the unions. Labour do not want the unions bought within the law. Snap PMQs Verdict: No winner, because Cameron and Miliband were in more of a collaborative mode than a combative mode. But there were interesting attempts to win the spin battle over last week's vote. More later ... Miliband says no one is doubting the need for revulsion. But peace involves talking with those with whom we disagree. Will the government talk to Iran? Cameron says a Foreign Office minister will be talking to Iran at the UN. But we should remember what the Iranians did to our embassy. You need to talk to the refugees, he says. Miliband says no one disagrees with that. But how are we going to bring the parties together? There is support across the country for Britain doing all it can to bring peace. Last week's vote was not about Britain shirking its responsibilities.It was about preventing a rush to war. Cameron says he accepts the results of the vote. His only regret was that he thought it was not necessary to divide the Commons on a vote leading to another vote. But Miliband thought that was necessary. Miliband says the revulsion at the chemical weapons attack is shared on all sides of the Commons. What is Cameron doing with the Syrian National Council to make the peace talks in Geneva happen. Cameron says the government is supporting those elements of the opposition that want a pluralistic Syria. But the government also supports training the moderate rebels. Britain must stand up for those Syrians who want freedom. Miliband says there is no difference between the two sides of the House on the need to stand up for the Syrians. The House has approached this so far in a "calm and measured way", he says. Isn't there a case for immediate talks between countries backing the rebels and countries backing the regime (ie, Iran in particular, he implies). Cameron says he has had repeated discussions with President Putin (another supporter of the Syrian government). But those involved in the conflict have to recognise that it is in their interests to take part in talks. Bashar al-Assad also needs to recognise that talks are in his interest, because he cannot win. But we must not give up our revulsion at the chemical weapons attack. Ed Miliband starts by also congratulating the Duke and Duchess on the birth of their baby. At the G20, will Cameron try to get other countries to match the UK's aid commitment to Syria? Cameron says he will do this. Britain has a very proud record on aid, and it is the second largest aid donor to Syria. Miliband says the civil war and the refugee crisis are having profound consequences in the Middle East. (He's been reading his brother's FT article.) What more can be done? Cameron says he has seen the problems for himself. A refugee camp in Jordan is now one of the biggest cities in the country. Britain is spending "serious money". But at the end of the day there must be a solution to the crisis. Chris Pincher, a Conservative, says the economic news is good. Labour should abandon Plan B. Cameron agrees the news has been good. All the figures on construction, manufacturing and services are going in the right direction. That's because of the tough decisions taken by the government. David Cameron starts by congratulating the Duke and Duchess of Cambridge on the birth of their son, Prince George. Next Monday MPs will be able to offer their own congratulations when the Commons debates a motion on this. Not long now. The Conservative MP Jesse Norman is leaving the Number 10 policy board because he abstained in last week's Syria vote instead of backing the government, Isabel Hardman reports at Coffee House. She thinks he will be back. I understand that the decision was made more in sorrow than in anger, and sources hope the path back for Norman will be a quick one. An announcement on his replacement will be made shortly. The policy board hasn’t, as I revealed in the politics column last week, gelled brilliantly yet, but colleagues found Norman impressive to work with. He is unlikely to be on the backbenches for long. Tories speaking to Benedict Brogan have given a rather different impression. Here's an extract from Brogan's morning email on Monday. Dave has to work out what to do with those who 'missed' the vote (Jesse Norman, they're looking at you. My, but they are cross with him...) Chris Grayling, the justice secretary, has announced a series of changes to the prison estate. • Four prisons are closing: HMPs Blundeston, Dorchester, Northallerton and Reading. • A plan to build a super-prison in Wrexham, holding 2,000 offenders, has been confirmed. • A feasibility study into replacing Feltham Young Offenders institution with another super-prison has been announced. • Three prisons are having their functions changed: HMPs Downview and The Verne and HMYOI Warren Hill. • HMP Dartmoor will also close, but not any time soon because the lease has a 10-year notice period. • Plans to build new blocks at four prisons - HMPs Parc, Peterborough, the Mount and Thameside - have been confirmed. The press notice about the changes is here. And the parliamentary written statement, with slightly more detail, is here (pdf). And here's some reaction. From Sadiq Khan, the shadow justice secretary This announcement demonstrates how poor the government's decision in 2010 was to cancel Labour's sensible rolling programme of prison modernising which would have provided cheaper prison places much earlier. New prison places don’t appear out of fresh air to replace those being closed now. It takes a stable rolling programme of investment with many years of planning and construction. This would have meant modern cheaper prisons being open before closing down more expensive and ineffective Victorian prisons. The Tory-led government have already closed twelve prisons since 2010 with the loss of over 4,000 prison places. Yet just this week, we've been reminded of the growing scale of overcrowding in our prisons, showing how all the promises of rehabilitation revolution by the government have come to nothing. From Peter McParlin, the Prison Officers Association chairman The announcement today is short sighted and will have a significant impact on the public sector prison service. Prison officers and their families will once again face the uncertainly of where they will be working in the future. The government prison closure policy is cuts driven and does nothing for the rehabilitation revolution. These prisons have played a pivotal role in the criminal justice system and this announcement is an absolute disgrace. From Juliet Lyon, director of the Prison Reform Trust Closing small local prisons and replacing them with super-sized jails will not reduce crime or make communities safer. The millions secured for new-build prisons could be more effectively spent on robust community service, treatment for addicts and care for people who are mentally ill. Smaller prisons tend to be safer and more effective than larger establishments, holding people closer to home and with a higher ratio of prison staff to prisoners. Prison ought to be an important place of last resort in our justice system, not a giant economic regeneration or job creation scheme. A United Nations official is investigating the "bedroom tax" (the cut in housing subsidy for people in social housing deemed to have too many bedrooms) to see whether it is compatible with the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, the Press Association reports. Here's an extract from the PA story. The impact of the so-called "bedroom tax" on the human rights of low-income households is being examined by a senior United Nations official. Special Rapporteur on housing Raquel Rolnik is on a two-week tour of UK cities where she will meet tenants affected by the policy as well as officials, campaigners and academics. Under the Government's welfare reform, social tenants deemed to have more bedrooms than they need have had their housing benefit reduced since April. Ministers say it tackles an unfair "spare room subsidy" not available to private-sector renters and suggest it will save around £500 million annually as part of the deficit-reduction strategy. But it has sparked protests across the country with critics claiming it is forcing families into poverty and will increase the benefit bill by pushing people into the private sector. Rolnik, who will reveal the initial findings of the unprecedented inspection next week, said the UK faced a "unique moment" when the challenge of providing adequate housing was "on the agenda". Article 25 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights includes housing as part of the "right to a standard of living adequate for the health and well-being of himself and of his family". "The UK has voiced its commitment to human rights on repeated occasions, and this mission will give me an opportunity to assess in-depth to what extent adequate housing, as one central aspect of the right to an adequate standard of living, is at the core of this commitment," she said. "The UK faces a unique moment, when the challenge to promote and protect the right to adequate housing for all is on the agenda. "In doing so, special attention would need to be given to responding to the specific situations of various population groups, in particular low-income households and other marginalised individuals and groups." The visit - at the invitation of the Government - takes in London, Edinburgh, Glasgow, Belfast and Manchester and will include sessions in local communities. A UN spokesman pointed out that the UK was signed up to a number of international treaties which protect the right to adequate housing and non-discrimination. The final report will be presented in Geneva by the Special Rapporteur to the UN Human Rights Council in March 2014. You can read all today's Guardian politics stories here. And all the politics stories published yesterday, including some in today's paper, are here. As for the rest of the papers, here's the PoliticsHome list of top 10 must-reads, here's the ConservativeHome roundup of today's political stories, and here's the New Statesman list of top 10 comment articles. • Lord Stern, author of the Stern report on climate change, tells the Independent that David Cameron's claims that fracking could bring down the price of gas are "baseless". In an interview with The Independent, the respected economist said he was puzzled by the prime minister’s claim this month that “fracking has real potential to drive energy bills down… gas and electric bills can go down when our home-grown energy supply goes up”. “I do think it’s a bit odd to say you know that it will bring the price of gas down. That doesn’t look like sound economics to me. It’s baseless economics,” said Lord Stern, chair of the Grantham Research Institute on Climate Change at the London School of Economics. Gas is a commodity that can be traded on the international market, meaning that it will be sold to the highest bidder, whether inside or outside the UK. As a result, any shale gas boom in the UK would be unlikely to have an impact on the world price, Lord Stern said. Lord Stern also criticised the Government for encouraging a rush into fracking without a thorough analysis of all its potential ramifications. • Andrew Grice in the Independent says the Policy Exchange thinktank will investigate for the Conservatives how the minimum wage could be raised without jobs being lost. David Cameron is being urged by key advisers to boost the minimum wage amid growing evidence that low paid workers have seen their living standards decline because it has failed to keep pace with rising prices in recent years. The move would be a major U-turn by the Conservatives, who opposed Labour’s decision to bring in the minimum wage 15 years ago, warning it could cost up to 2m jobs. Policy Exchange, a think tank with close links to Mr Cameron, is to study how the £6.19 an hour minimum wage could be raised without forcing companies to shed jobs. One idea is to encourage firms to raise it by offering them lower national insurance payments in return. The think tank will report later this year but it is likely that any pledge of a higher wages floor would be included in the Tory manifesto at the 2015 election. Supporters include Jo Johnson, the Tory MP who heads the Downing Street Policy Unit, and Oliver Letwin, the Tories’ policy chief. This is a follow up to a Newsnight story on this theme. • John Bingham in the Daily Telegraph says more than one million families have been forced to sell their homes over five years to pay for residential care. • Roger Boyes in the Times (paywall) says Cameron should be wary of relying on Angela Merkel to help him in his calls for EU reform. Although Ms Merkel is a shoo-in for re-election, it is not clear what coalition will emerge after the September 22 election. If her present partner, the Free Democratic Party polls less than 5 per cent, it will be out of parliament. That would make a coalition with the Social Democrats the only option. Although their leader, Peer Steinbrueck, is an avowed Churchill fan, he is against what he calls à la carte EU membership, and will push for a much closer axis with François Hollande and will almost certainly back the spiky left-wing federalist Martin Schulz to be the new head of the Commission. That surely would be an adrenaline shot for those in the UK who want to leave the EU. Finally, Mrs Merkel has dropped hints to another biographer, Nikolaus Blome, that she considers ten years in office long enough, that 60 is a good age to start something new. She is 61 on July 17, 2015, and will have had a decade as Chancellor. If her party is to find a new candidate for the 2017 general election, that is the moment to go. You could see it in the TV duel this week: the slog of managing first the financial, then the eurozone crisis, has flattened her, drained her of strength. So, here’s the nub: Mr Cameron may be building his Europe in-out strategy on the back of a political friendship that is not made to last. That’s something else to worry about. Here's some more Twitter comment on the GMB decision. On the Today programme Rachel Reeves, the shadow chief secretary to the Treasury, told the Today programme that she was "confident" that more union members would sign up as party supporters under the reforms being proposed by Ed Miliband. Most of the money that the Labour Party receives comes from small donations and members. Of course we welcome the support that we get from the trade unions but this is a decision for the GMB. Ed has spoken about the need for greater transparency and openness in that relationship and we are going to be doing more to reach out and get affiliations from individual members directly to the party. Since the last general election, membership in my constituency party in Leeds West has increased by 50%. We've got more members, more trade union activists getting involved in the party because they like what Ed Miliband has to say. So I am confident that more people will sign up, get involved in the Labour Party, and come out campaigning. For the record, here are today's YouGov GB polling results. Labour: 37% (down 3 points from YouGov yesterday) Conservatives: 33% (no change) Ukip: 13% (up 1) Lib Dems: 9% (no change) Labour lead: 4 points (down 3) Government approval: -31 (down 2) According to Electoral Calculus, these figures would give Labour a majority of 46. And here's Channel 4's Michael Crick on the GMB announcement. GMB officials are now briefing that they could cut their funding to Labour even more. It's worth pointing out that Unite's general secretary, Len McCluskey, has also been dropping strong hints that his union might want to abandon Labour. I posted this excerpt from the interview that John Harris conducted with McCluskey for Saturday's Guardian yesterday, but, for anyone who missed it, here it is again. [McCluskey] mentions the four senior Labour figures who quit Labour in 1981 and founded the Social Democratic Party. "There were things happening in the Labour party that they profoundly disagreed with, and they left and created their own party. Now, that's politics for you…" And does he think that might happen to the people he calls Blairites? "Or it could be the outcome for those individuals on the left of the party who believe, 'Well, we're getting nowhere.'" Including him? "I'm the general secretary of Unite. In our rule book, we're affiliated to the Labour party. And for that to change, our rules would have to change. We're not there yet. But the Labour party has no God-given right to exist. Ed Miliband's challenge is to demonstrate to ordinary working people – including trade unionists – that it's their party." This is a big moment, in other words. "It's absolutely huge." And he doesn't rule out Unite eventually taking its ball elsewhere? "I wouldn't rule anything out," he says. "In extraordinary times, extraordinary things happen. In my view, students of politics in 50 years' time may well look back on this period and realise just how incredibly important it was." Though he is working hard for a Labour victory in 2015, he thinks this will be possible only with "a radical alternative to austerity". If the Tories win, he tells me, "I fear for the existence of the Labour party. None of us know what would happen after a defeat of that nature. And it won't necessarily be the normal process of the leader stepping down, and a new leader taking over." As he says, extraordinary times. McCluskey has dropped vague hints before about Unite leaving Labour, but this was his clearest hint yet that he's considering the idea. And here's some more Labour reaction to the GMB decision on Twitter. From Chris Byrant, the Labour MP From Barry Sheerman, the Labour MP From Paul Richards, the Blairite Labour former special adviser From Owen Jones, the leftwing commentator From the New Statesman's George Eaton From Dan Hodges, the commentator and former GMB official who has just left the Labour party Tom Watson, the Labour MP and former campaign coordinator for the party, says the GMB decision makes this a "sad day". He has explained his thinking in a blog. Here's an extract from his blog. If this is the beginning of the end of that historic link, it is a very serious development that threatens a pillar of our democracy that has endured for over one hundred years. Some will scoff but they are fools to do so. That party card stands for something more than confirmation that an annual direct debit has been processed. Over the next year we have been asked to consider a change to the constitution of the Labour party, though no detailed proposals have been revealed. I’m not opposed to reform but I will fight very hard to retain the fundamental link between the party and Labour movement. PMQs often gets quite testy, but at lunchtime we're unlikely to hear anyone echoing the Number 10 aide who described Ed Miliband last week, ahead of the Syria vote, as a "fucking cunt and a copper-bottomed shit". (It's very feeble invective, a combination of the banal with the archaic. Malcolm Tucker would do much better.) But, still, relations between Miliband and David Cameron are at a low and PMQs should certainly be even more edgy than normal. And the GMB's decision to drastically cut its funding to Labour should come up too. Here's the Press Association story, and here's the statement from the GMB. The GMB central executive council (CEC) has voted to reduce its current levels of affiliation to the Labour party from 420,000 to 50,000 from 2014. This will reduce the union's basic affiliation fee to Labour party by £1.1m per year. It is expected that there will further reductions in spending on Labour party campaigns and initiatives. GMB CEC expressed considerable regret about the apparent lack of understanding the proposal mooted by Ed Miliband will have on the collective nature of trade union engagement with the Labour party. A further source of considerable regret to the CEC is that the party that had been formed to represent the interest of working people in this country intends to end collective engagement of trade unions in the party they helped to form. The CEC also decided to scale down by one-third the level of its national political fund. As well as leaving Labour even more strapped for cash than it is already, this represents something of a snub to Miliband. He was hoping that, after Labour starts taking money only union members who have made a conscious decision to opt in to Labour affiliation, the unions would to their best to encourage their members to sign up. Instead the GMB has decided to short-circuit that process by assuming that 370,000 of its members won't want to fund Labour. As Norman Smith has been reporting on BBC, GMB members are due to have a vote next year on whether to carry on having a political fund (which pays for wider campaigns, as well as donating to Labour) and the GMB decision may be designed to lessen the chances of members voting to scrap the fund. The GMB news only broke this morning, and I'll cover more reaction as it comes in. Here's the agenda for the day. 9.30am: Harriet Harman, the Labour deputy leader, gives a speech to an IPPR seminar on challenges facing older women. 10am: The Electoral Commission publishes spending figures for the police and crime commissioner elections, as well as a report on PCC election spending rules. 10.30am: Owen Paterson, the environment secretary, gives a speech to the Local Government Association rural conference. 10.30am: Boris Johnson, the mayor of London, unveils plans to tackle HGV-cycle safety with a transport minister. 12pm: David Cameron and Ed Miliband clash at PMQs. 12.40pm: MPs begin a debate on a Labour motion on living standards. At some point today Rachel Reeves, the shadow chief secretary to the Treasury will also be discussing living standards in a speech to the Resolution Foundation. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary after PMQs and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: Tony Abbott's election victory and Labor's braindead politics | Aditya Chakrabortty

fields.headline: Tony Abbott's election victory and Labor's braindead politics
fields.trailText: Aditya Chakrabortty: Australia's economy is an enviable state, yet Abbott pummelled the Labor party to its heaviest defeat in a century. How?
fields.bodyText: Has anyone come up with a word for "completely dismaying yet utterly unsurprising"? Because we need one to sum up what happened in Australia this weekend. On Saturday, Tony Abbott was elected Australia's new prime minister. What's he like? In a private memo from 2007 published by WikiLeaks, the then-US ambassador to Australia described him as "a polarising rightwinger" with a "propensity for insensitivity and controversy". Climate change, in his book, is "complete crap". This is the man David Cameron welcomed to the fold with the tweet: "It'll be great working with another centre-right leader." Then there's Abbott's woman problem. He has wondered aloud: "What if men are, by physiology or temperament, more adapted to exercise authority or to issue command?" That was back in 1998; during this campaign, he praised a female candidate for her "sex appeal". After the inevitable barrage of complaints, he backtracked, or tried, by describing his colleague as "not just a pretty face". Abbott is a Catholic; going by these comments, you might surmise that his patron saint is David of Brent. Unsurprisingly, he has never been especially popular – even among his own Liberal party, which awarded him the leadership by a wafer-thin majority. His approval ratings are regularly dismal and one pollster dismissed him as "unelectable". So how did Abbott pummel the Australian Labor party to its heaviest defeat in a century? It's a sad story, and one that holds lessons for leftwingers around the world. From Gordon Brown to José Zapatero, the global financial crisis has claimed many social-democratic leaders. But the striking thing about the economic record presented by Labor's Kevin Rudd at the polls is how strong it is. Australia is now enjoying its 22nd consecutive year of growth: during the Great Recession, the country managed to avoid any recession at all. How? By warding off a global downturn with what Nobel prize-winning economist Joseph Stiglitz described as "probably the best-designed stimulus package of any … advanced industrial country, both in size and in design, and how it was spent". Using the war cry of "go early, go hard and go households", Rudd chucked a total of A$52bn (£30bn) into the housing market, into refurbishing schools and rolling out broadband and other public works. He even doled out money, in the spring of 2009 giving the typical single worker A$900. From the IMF to the OECD, the major international institutions all lined up to clap. The result is an economy in enviable nick, especially compared with other rich economies. Last week's GDP report showed Australia's national income rising at 2.6% a year – the kind of growth rate George Osborne would love to fight an election on. Unemployment has been remarkably quiescent and take-home pay for the average worker is rising. Sure, there are niggles – the country's mining boom seems to be drawing to a close, and the housing bubble remains a worry. But think about the wreckage the banking crash left elsewhere in the world and you can see why they call Australia the lucky country. So, having pulled off such a feat, what did Labor do? Rather than crow about such smart state intervention, it practically disowned its triumph. Rudd – who once described himself as "basically a conservative when it comes to … public financial management" – and his Treasury team were soon promising to get the government budget back in surplus. This was "braindead economics", according to Steve Keen, economist at the University of Western Sydney. Even after its stimulus spending, Australia last year had among the lowest debt-to-GDP ratios in the entire developed world. But it was also braindead politics, playing right into the hands of Abbott's scaremongering over the public finances. What Labor were effectively telling voters was: judge us not on our successes, but on the benchmarks set by our opponents. If that sounds perverse, that's because it is. But, really, it's in line with what social democratic parties around the world have done in the wake of the crisis. Consider: Gordon Brown and Alistair Darling help pull Britain out of an economic nosedive; then draw up a law to force themselves to halve the budget deficit. Barack Obama saves Wall Street but barely lays a glove on the bankers. François Hollande takes charge and does pretty much the same budgetary botch as Sarkozy was planning. When Lehman Brothers fell over five years ago this month, there was much fanciful talk about how this was a "progressive moment". Brown indulged in such rhetoric, and so did Rudd, penning 7,000-word essays on the evils of neo-liberalism. But there was no recasting of economic models, or even a serious debate about how things ought to change. The best social-democrats managed was to save capitalism for the capitalists. And now that the five-year panic is giving way to another phase, the social democrats are being shown the door. That story holds for Rudd and his managerial Keynesianism. What filled the vacuum left where a political philosophy ought to be was bitter rivalry with Julia Gillard (and you know how that worked out), flip-flopping over carbon pricing and a race to the bottom on immigration policies. The result was last weekend's wipeout. So what is the synonym for acute disappointment and predictable defeat? My knowledge of compound German nouns being a bit rusty, let me suggest this: contemporary social democracy.

results.webTitle: G8 summit: Politics live blog

fields.headline: G8 summit: Politics live blog
fields.trailText:
• Obama and Putin prepare for crunch talks on Syria
• Turkey summons UK ambassador over Guardian revelations
• Obama hails NI peace process as blueprint for peace
• Cameron welcomes US-EU trade talks
• Tax campaigners issue warning over developing countries
• Rolling coverage of the G8 summit in Lough Erne
fields.bodyText: • David Cameron and Barack Obama have formally launched plans for an ambitious US/EU trade deal. As Larry Elliott reports, negotiations will begin in Washington next month amid hopes of a speedy agreement that will help the world economy grow more rapidly. Announcing the launch of talks, Cameron said a successful deal would add up to £100bn to the EU economy and up to £85bn to the US economy. "This is a once-in-a-generation prize and it must be seized," the prime minister said. Here's the statement on the deal from the European Commission. • Downing Street has said that Cameron has not given up hopes of an agreement on Syria despite signs that there is a gulf between Russia and other G8 members over proposals for a peace conference. Obama and Vladimir Putin, the Russian president, began bilateral talks on the subject at 6.30pm, and all G8 leaders will discuss it at dinner tonight. Cameron's spokesman said the prime minister's view was that "it is essential we get a conclusion on Syria which is a political transition". The spokesman went on: He has always been clear that this is a complex problem, it is no more or less complex than it was last week. But the important thing is that work continues to ensure that we get that political transition that he has outlined. From the prime minister's view, if you look at the Syrian conflict thus far, we are talking about something around 93,000 people having lost their lives, about the use of chemical weapons - these are unacceptable things and it is important therefore that the international community works together to ensure that we get a political transition and that we bring this dreadful conflict to an end. • Bashar al-Assad has said that any decision by the West to arm the Syrian opposition could lead to "the direct export of terrorism to Europe". (See 4.07pm.) • Sir Malcolm Rifkind, a Conservative former foreign secretary and chair of the intelligence and security committee, has played down the idea that arming the Syrian opposition could lead to weapons getting into the hands of al-Qaida. This is what he told the World at One. The Syrian national coalition, the people who might receive the weapons, they're the people who need them and don't have them. Why should they remotely be interested in handing over weapons that they desperately need to people who are their sworn enemies, the al-Qaida people, who are not part of their national coalition and already have got arms because they received arms from their various supporters in the international community? But Boris Johnson, the Conservative London mayor, has reaffirmed his opposition to arming the rebels. This is what he told reporters in London. If there is anybody I would trust to take this decision it is, of course, David Cameron, but I think what we need to see is a pretty clear way of helping democracy in Syria, helping the good guys without putting weapons into the hands of maniacs and fanatics. • South Africa and Russia have joined Turkey in expressing concern about the revelation the British spied on other countries at the G20 summits in London. That's all from me for today. I'll be blogging again from the summit tomorrow. Peter Robinson, Northern Ireland's DUP first minister, and Martin McGuinness, his Sinn Fein deputy, have been commenting on the summit. My colleague Henry McDonald has sent me this. First minister Peter Robinson said President Obama had "very strongly advocating" a shared future. From a tourist point of view the pictures beamed out from Lough Erne will "entice people to come to Northern Ireland." Martin McGuinness said President Obama "hit all the right buttons" in his speech particularly his line that racism had caused so much damage to the United States of America. McGuinness said the G8 leaders looked to Northern Ireland as an example for conflict resolution. The deputy first minister said the attitude of the young people who listened to the Obama speech showed that "they are up for this peace process." The Associated Press newswire says that, if Barack Obama and Vladimir Putin want further talks at the summit, they won't have far to walk. If President Barack Obama and Russian President Vladimir Putin want some extra quality time together at the Group of 8 meeting, they don't have to go far. The U.S. cottage at the Lough Erne resort where the summit is being held is just a few yards from the Russian cottage, the closest U.S. neighbor of all the G-8 delegations. Upon arrival, Obama stepped out of his limousine and wandered over toward a cottage bearing the Russian flag. The cottages overlook a picturesque lake, and Obama took in the sight, chatted with resort staff then returned to his own cottage. The proximity is all the more remarkable given the tension over how to deal with Syria between the two leaders, who were meeting on the sidelines of the summit Monday evening. Obama just announced the U.S. will supply military support to Syrian rebels fighting in a civil war. Russia has been supplying arms to the regime of Bashar Assad. Here's a G8 reading list. • Kirsty McNeil, a former adviser to Gordon Brown, says in the Telegraph that the G8 will not achieve much because David Cameron has not put in enough work. Enniskillen is no Gleneagles for one very simple reason: David Cameron hasn’t put in the work. Downing Street staff, used to the Gordon Brown approach to breaks (when he said he’d finished a book on holiday he normally meant he’d written one, not read one), could be forgiven for feeling relief at the gentler pace of our chillaxing PM, but there’s no doubt we are paying a price for it abroad. Getting a global summit to deliver is 25 per cent showmanship, 25 per cent brinkmanship and 50 per cent long, hard graft on the detail. This Prime Minister is notoriously allergic to the third ... The issue facing David Cameron is that he both started his preparation for the G8 late and has failed to seize opportunities to build rapport with other leaders. Granted the honour of chairing the UN Secretary General’s High Level Panel on what should replace the Millennium Development Goals, David Cameron didn’t even turn up. So despite some welcome movement on nutrition and transparency, the 2013 G8 is a missed opportunity. The British international campaigning sector is the best in the world, but for some reason it has repeated the 2005 mistake of focusing disproportionately on the UK. For his part, Cameron has not been able to drive forward a global deal on tax because he left putting his own house in order until Saturday – far too late to get other countries to fall in line. • Gary Gibbon on his Channel 4 blog says David Cameron is constrained on Syria because he knows he would lose a Commons vote on arming the opposition. One EU member diplomatic source said the prime minister looked like he’d been trying, along with President Hollande, to get President Obama to sign up to arms for Syria, only to discover he couldn’t deliver anything himself – it all sounded a bit like a man who drags his friend to the bar only to discover he’s got no money himself to buy a round. • Mark Mardell on his BBC blog says President Obama is wary of committing himself over Syria. President Obama's first engagement is a speech in Belfast when he'll talk about the achievements of the peace process. He is likely to reflect that US involvement helped heal the wounds. He probably won't say that peace is only possible when people have had enough of war - when carrying on fighting is worse than not winning everything you want. But that is a sobering thought for those worried about Syria. The Irish conflict took about 300 years to work itself out if you take the long view, and three decades if you take a shorter perspective. In the press room at the G8 I'm sitting opposite some very helpful and friendly colleagues from the BBC. I'm glad they're here. But the Telegraph seem to think there are too many of them in Fermanagh. One in three of the British journalists covering the G8 meeting in Northern Ireland is from the BBC The Daily Telegraph understands that 45 out of the 150 British journalists covering the G8 are from the BBC Critics suggested the number of BBC journalists was not in keeping with the austere message which Prime Minister David Cameron is trying to impart from the meeting. Here are some of the arrival pictures. David Cameron and Angela Merkel. Cameron and Francois Hollande. Cameron and Vladimir Putin. Cameron and Barack Obama. (Apologies to those who have had enough of pictures of Cameron. But at events like this the pictures are deemed important. Summit organisers think like set designers.) Here's the latest from Henry McDonald on the summit security. The PSNI has only dealt with one minor incident so far on the steets. Commenting on the policing and security operation today, Assistant chief constable Alistair Finlay said: “The President of the United States delivered his speech this morning at the Waterfront Hall and all of the World leaders arrived safely in the Lough Erne Resort this afternoon where they have commenced their schedule of meetings. The policing and security operation continues to progress very well. In Belfast this afternoon a small group of protestors made their way from the Falls Road to Belfast City Hall and a 19 year old man was arrested for a number of offences outside the City Hall including disorderly behaviour. To date there has only been two arrests – one in Belfast and one in Enniskillen over the past two days. The G8 leaders are now talking about the economy. Michael Anderson is David Cameron's special envoy for development issues. RTL, a French website, says the French culture minister, Aurélie Filipett, has condemned Joe Manuel Barroso for his comments about the French demand for a cultural opt out from the US/EU trade deal. (See 12.19pm.) Here's the key quote from Filpett. Ces propos sont absolument consternants. Ils sont inacceptables ... C'est la Commission européenne qui est aujourd'hui isolée dans sa logique ultralibérale ... Certains nous ont traité de réactionnaires. Ceux qui ont traité la France de réactionnaire devraient se souvenir que la France n'était pas seule dans ce combat. Notre position, elle n'est pas défensive, elle n'est pas conservatrice et encore moins réactionnaire, c'est une position résolument moderne. Nous allons continuer notre mobilisation, il ne faut pas baisser la garde. And here's a translation. I've used Google Translate and tidied it up a bit. These comments are absolutely appalling. They are unacceptable ... It is the European Commission that is now isolated in its ultra-liberal position ... Some have labelled us reactionary. Those who have called us reactionary France should remember that France was not alone in this fight. Our position is not defensive, it is not conservative, and it is even less reactionary, it is a modern position. We will continue our engagement, we must not lower our guard. The Telegraph's Christopher Hope has been tweeting about the arrival photographs. (See 4.25pm.) According to one of the pool reporters who was in the room when Cameron et al delivered their statements on the US/EU trade talks, there was another reason, besides possible contrived informality, for the fact that all four leaders were not wearing ties. (See 3.49pm.) "The tent where they gave their statements was extremely hot," he writes. My colleague Mark Tran has also written a good account of why the clampdown on tax evasion could be so important for developing countries. This morning David Cameron was able to wheel out the pope to back his position (in general terms, rather than in detail) on tax, trade and transparency. (See 12.31pm.) Now campaigners, who are pushing him to go further, have retaliated with their own moral authority figure. Save the Children has released this statement from Kofi Annan, the former UN secretary general, saying that the G8 should establish public registries of beneficial ownership and that it should make sure developing countries are not excluded from new standards for automatic exchange of tax information. Here's an extract from the statement. In this time of change, the G8 can make a difference. For example, company ownership – as well as revenue flows – can and must be made completely transparent thereby shining a light on a widespread method for hiding corruption. The G8 must establish registries on ownership of companies and trusts in all tax jurisdictions – and the registries need to be publicly available. Closely linked, tax avoidance and evasion are global issues that affect us all. The impact for G8 governments is a loss of revenue. But in Africa, it has direct impact on the lives of mothers and children. Throughout the world, millions of citizens now need the G8 leaders to take action on tax avoidance. Not just because of the palpable injustice of enormous tax avoidance by major multinationals including many headquartered in G8 countries. But also because to do so could save many, many lives around the world. Tax authorities in all regions struggle to prevent the erosion of their tax bases, but Africa struggles more than most. A G8 deal on the automatic exchange of tax information must include African authorities, giving them full access to relevant tax information. If you are looking for more background on the trade, tax and transparency issues at the summit, and why public registries and information exchange are deemed important, here are three useful pieces of background reading. • An article in Prospect by Paul Collier, the developmental economist who has been advising Cameron on these issues. • A briefing from Global Witness called Anonymous companies (pdf). • A House of Lords briefing paper on the summit (pdf). • An Economist feature about the summit. At the media centre they are now showing footage of the G8 leaders formally arriving at the summit. It's a bit confusing because they have already arrived. They have been holding bilaterals and three of them, Barack Obama, Jose Manuel Barroso and Herman Van Rompuy, have already joined Cameron for the EU/US trade deal announcement. But someone must have sent them all out again because, one by one, they are walking up a grey path, with the lough in the background to be greeted by Cameron. Cameron takes them inside, and then has to nip outside for the next arrival. He's like someone hosting a party, trying to fill up the glasses in the kitchen only to have to keep rushing out because someone has rung the doorbell. Still, the pictures look good. South Africa has expressed concern about the revelation that Britain spied on foreign governments at the G20 summits in London in 2009. My colleague Julian Borger has sent me this. The South African foreign ministry, the department of international relations and cooperation, has issued a statement voicing its concern over the Guardian revelation of a GCHQ operation to bug the ministry starting in 2005, and calling for a British investigation. The statement says: "The South African Government has noted with concern various reports following a story published by the Guardian newspaper in the UK on allegations of spying on G20 country Ministers and senior officials. "We do not yet have the full benefit of details reported on but in principle we would condemn the abuse of privacy and basic human rights particularly if it emanates from those who claim to be democrats. "We have solid, strong and cordial relations with the United Kingdom and would call on their government to investigate this matter fully with a view to take strong and visible action against any perpetrators." Syria's Bashar al-Assad has said that if the west supplies arms to the Syrian opposition, this will lead to "the direct export of terrorism to Europe". He made the comment in an interview with the German paper, the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung. Here's the key quote in German. Q: Die EU hat ihr Waffenembargo gegen Syrien nicht verlängert, aber noch keine Entscheidung zur Lieferung von Waffen an die Rebellen getroffen. A: Ich kann nicht behaupten, dass die Europäer auf der Seite Syriens stehen. Es gibt Staaten, die gegenüber dem syrischen Staat eine feindselige Haltung einnehmen, insbesondere Frankreich und Großbritannien. Die anderen Staaten, in erster Linie Deutschland, stellen rationale Fragen zu Waffenlieferungen an Terroristen. Was würde geschehen? Erstens, Syrien würde noch mehr zerstört. Wer würde den Preis zahlen? Das syrische Volk. Zweitens, die Europäer liefern Waffen und wissen, dass sie diese an Terroristen liefern. Manche unterscheiden zwischen „guten“ und „schlechten“ Kämpfern, so wie sie vor ein paar Jahren zwischen „guten“ und „schlechten“ Taliban sowie einer „guten“ und „schlechten“ Al Qaida unterschieden haben. Ist das vernünftig? Wenn die Europäer Waffen liefern, wird der Hinterhof Europas terroristisch, und Europa wird den Preis dafür zahlen. Terrorismus bedeutet hier Chaos; Chaos führt zu Armut; und Armut bedeutet, dass Europa einen wichtigen Markt verliert. Die zweite Folge wäre der direkte Export des Terrorismus nach Europa. Terroristen werden kampferfahren und mit extremistischer Ideologie ausgerüstet zurückkehren. Für Europa gibt es zu einer Kooperation mit dem syrischen Staat keine Alternative, auch wenn das Europa nicht gefällt. And here's a translation, from Google Translate, with a little tidying up from me. Q: The EU has not extended its arms embargo on Syria, but has yet to make a decision about arming the rebels. A: I cannot say that the Europeans are on the Syrian side. There are countries that adopt a hostile attitude towards the Syrian government, especially France and the UK. The other states, mainly Germany, ask rational questions about arms shipments to terrorists. What would happen? First, Syria would be even more destroyed. Who would pay the price? The Syrian people. Second, the Europeans deliver weapons and know that they deliver these to terrorists. Some distinguish between "good" and "bad" fighters, as they made a difference a couple of years ago between "good" and "bad" Taliban, and a "good" and "bad" al-Qaida before. Is that reasonable? If the Europeans deliver weapons to Europe's backyard, Europe will pay the price. Terrorism is causing chaos here, chaos leads to poverty, and poverty means that Europe is losing an important market. The second consequence would be the direct export of terrorism to Europe. Terrorists will return to fight learn and equipped with extremist ideology. For Europe, there is no alternative to cooperation with the Syrian government, even if Europe does not like it. Forget the economics of the US/EU free trade deal, of TTIP as the Americans call it (Transatlantic Trade and Investment Partnership). On Twitter, it's the shirts that are getting all the attention. Here are the four leaders making the trade deal talks announcement. It was relatively informal. Not one of them was wearing a tie. David Cameron was not even in a jacket. Cameron says he and other G8 leaders will take questions at the end of the summit. Herman Van Rompuy, the president of the European Council, is speaking now. He says economies all over the world are becoming more interdependent. This will make the world safer, he says. The Atlantic is not the past, he says. It is also the future. Negotiations will not be an easy ride. But with flexibility and creativity, he is confident that the talks can succeed. Barack Obama, the US president, says it is wonderful to be in Lough Erne. He is grateful for the hospitality he has received. He says America will host the first round of negotiations in Washington next month. The US/EU relationship is the largest in the world. It makes up half of the world's GDP. This potentially ground-breaking partnership would strenghten those ties, he says. He is pleased the EU as a whole supports the move. In the US the deal has also been welcomed, in Congress and in the business community. There are going to be sensitivities on both sides, he says. But he thinks they can build an economic relationship as deep as the security relationship. This deal will be a priority for his administration, he says. It is important to get it right. Occasionally leaders are going to have to intervene to get through some logjams, he says. But he is confident they can succeed. Jose Manuel Barroso, the president of the European Commission, is speaking now. He says the negotiating teams will start meeting next month. It will be a powerful demonstration of the EU's determination to shake up the rules, he says. The EU wants to make rapid progress. The current economic climate requires firms to do more with less, he says. Barroso says he wants legislators, in particular the European parliament, to be supportive. A free trade deal would be good for the whole world, he says. Integrating two of the largest economies in the world won't be easy, he says. But they will succeed. This will write the next chapter of "our common history". David Cameron is now making a statement about the US/EU trade deal. He says it could be the biggest trade deal ever. When it was first discussed at Camp David last year, there was some doubt as to whether it could be launched at Lough Erne, he says. This is a "once in a lifetime opportunity", he says. The formal welcome to the G8 summit at the Lough Erne golf resort will be taking place shortly. In a piece in the Financial Times at the weekend, Mathew Engel described it as one of the most unlikely venues ever for a G8. Lough Erne is an appropriate venue in another respect. The reality of this sumptuous-looking setting is all too similar to most of the countries the leaders represent. Beneath the surface luxury, the operation is broke. It went into administration two years ago and is up for sale for a knock-down £10m. The purpose of a G8 summit may be unclear to most people. It is clear enough round here: this is the biggest property marketing exercise in history. Surpassed only by Silvio Berlusconi’s choice of earthquake-stricken L’Aquila in 2009, Lough Erne must be the second-most improbable setting in the 38-year sequence of these events. Even a few years ago, the Troubles made this border region feel too dangerous for any world leader – most golfers too. The 1987 Remembrance Day bomb four miles away in Enniskillen, which killed 11, was among the most notorious of them all. But a local entrepreneur, Jim Treacy, had a vision: a five-star resort in the middle of nowhere. In late 2008 it became reality, just as the Irish and global economies caved in: less than two years later his bankers, the Bank of Scotland, pulled out of Ireland, north and south, and called in their loans. For a more conventionial piece on the resort, Bert Archer wrote a travel article about it for the Globe and Mail. Amnesty International is being allowed through the gates of the summit to deliver a letter to Vladimir Putin. As Henry McDonald reports, the group will tell the Russian president to release the punk-protest band Pussy Riot, two of whose members are still in jail. A number of Amnesty activists will wear balaclavas similar to the ones the band don when they go on stage. And here's some more from the Press Association on David Cameron's visit to the Enniskillen integrated primary school with Barack Obama. The leaders - both wearing blue ties but with Mr Cameron in a blue shirt to the president's white - first signed their names on a G8-themed patchwork quilt. Obama wrote "Dream Big Dreams" while the Prime Minister added "With thanks for all you do" under his signature. Moving on to the classroom, they were treated to a show of the youngsters' knowledge of the summit - including one who told the president the leaders were made up of four prime ministers, one chancellor and three presidents - and asked them about what they saw as the priorities for the G8, being told hunger and world poverty. Mya Binnon read out a poem written by the class to mark the visit, titled Fermanagh Is. It read: Fermanagh is wonderful, Fermanagh is great, Fermanagh is hosting the G8. Earlier David Cameron, the British prime minister and the host of the G8 summit, said Russia must join the west in seeking a democratic transition from the autocratic government of Bashar al-Assad, "so that people in Syria can have a government that represents them, rather than a government that's trying to butcher them." Let's be clear - I am as worried as anybody else about elements of the Syrian opposition, who are extremists, who support terrorism and who are a great danger to our world. The question is what do we do about it? My argument is that we shouldn't accept that the only alternative to Assad is terrorism and violence. We should be on the side of Syrians who want a democratic and peaceful future for their country and one without the man who is currently using chemical weapons against them. In Enniskillen, a couple of miles from the summit venue, security is good natured but extremely tight. The main road leading to the Lough Erne resort is closed to all but a few local farmers, with police cars blocking country lanes or back roads leading anywhere near the site. A stretch of Lower Lough Erne, in which the hotel sits on a small island, has also been closed to all boats, blocked off from the river that feeds it by a string of yellow buoys policed by officers in inflatable dinghies. On the two small bridges leading out of the town to the north and west, police officers in black boiler suits, body armour and green PSNI [Police Service of Northern Ireland] caps stand 3m apart - one of the bridges had 24 officers spaced along its sides, with three armoured Land Rovers parked on the pavement. 8,000 officers are on duty over the summit, 3,600 of whom have been seconded from forces in mainland UK. Stretches of the major road on the route from Belfast have been lined with yellow bollards to prevent vehicles stopping. At 10am, a fast motorcade of five motorcycle outriders, five blacked out MPVs and two armoured Land Rovers swept along the Loughshore road towards the venue. There was no indication of who was inside. Locals, if anything, are a little bemused at the fuss. "There's an awful lot of money being spent for nothing," said one elderly woman, who had come into town to use the bank but declined to give her name. "We're not going to see any of it, of course. The poor will still be poor. I'm a pensioner, and I'm not going to see anything of it." But others were more positive. "Fair play to the organisers, it's definitely been very well done," said Andy Bracken, behind the till of fishing equipment shop Home Field and Stream.
